questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Nikki and i shared this interview, we had 3 questions each:

Nikki
If hired, what would be your priorities during your first few weeks on the job?
survey the environment, understand current pain points, get feedback on what's working well/not
What do you think is the most important skill for a sales enablement manager to possess?
being OK with being wrong (loved this response)
What are some of the most effective strategies you’ve used to help a sales team succeed?
flipping the narrative, going with an opinion (a POV) rather than stating things about our product (again, loved this response)

Aasim
How do you measure the success of your sales enablement strategies?
 he named several quantifiable (time in stage) and qualitative metrics
Provide an example of a time when you successfully guided a sales team through a difficult period.
showed high EQ here, market is tough - not a lot sales people hitting quotas - seems seasoned that he'll help us weather a difficult period if one come up. 
Describe a situation where you had to quickly adapt to changing circumstances in order to accomplish a goal.
as a PMM, his team didn't historically own SKO and were asked to own it with 3 weeks notice - Edwin walked us through how he did it: come up with agenda, get input from stakeholders (that can be opinionated), and ultimately deliver a great SKO...well, because there was no option not to

Overall, a very strong candidate - his responses are in-line with what i like in a candidate: get to know the problem, have experience navigating a similar problem, show high EQ and perseverance.  He has both PMM and enablement experience so his desire to pursue enablement is A+. i.e. a mindful, career trajectory in mind.

Strong Yes!

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Excellent experience from Braze and Datadog.
- Experience enabling sellers on technical products (spoke about challenges at DD around enabling new sellers across multiple of their product lines)
- Articulate and enthusiastic
- Spoke about generating assets at Braze including positioning and demos
- Experience working closely with developers to help quantify the value of a CI scanning product to customers (and enabled AEs on how to communicate this value); came up with a proxy for how to determine the volume of flaky tests and how to quantify that into dollars saved
- Spoke about prioritization on what to enable AEs on and how he ensures "nice to haves" are accessible but not overwhelming

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Edwin seems to have a very good understanding of the sales enablement process. He would be a good addition to Semgrep. Notes from the interview:
Talked about "CI visibility - Flaky tests" product at Datadog
Discovery lead to a rough ROI
Worked with developers
Competitive data - Google and Spotify
Target audience AEs


Talked about pain points convergence, and which products should AEs must know about and which products/features are good to know.
Talked about MQL -> SQL, improving ACVs.

Why Yes and not a "Strong Yes"?
- Would've loved if he talked about knowing the ICP when I asked him about what would you like to know in your first 15 days at Semgrep. He did not talk about ICP at all. That said he's a 3.5/4 for me (to be accurate).

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I was impressed with Edwin. His experience is relevant to the challenges we're facing today and he can help to build guardrails around potential challenges we'll see in the future. Collaboration is going to be important with this role given the number of BUs and stakeholders they'll engage with and Edwin seemed very collaborative. I thought a bit about risk because it wasn't front and center in the interview and see he spent less than two years at his last three companies. I'd like to understand what will it take to keep him past two years? Otherwise, I am a yes. 


If hired, what would be your priorities during your first few weeks on the job?
Meet with everyone
Understand high level problems -> how did they manifest?
There could be challenges in MM, SMB and ENT - where are they? and then break them down to their basic form. Hows the sales motion today for each of these segments?
Would prioritize challenges by Creating a matrix that shows the smallest work required with largest impact and then confirm with stakeholders before moving forward

What do you think is the most important skill for a sales enablement manager to possess?
The ability to know when and why they're wrong. Have an opinion, but be okay being wrong especially when new info is introduced
Arrive to the conversation with a valuable, malleable option and listen
Doesn't want to make big decisions quickly - wants to listen and understand before introducing operational efficiencies

What are some of the most effective strategies you’ve used to help a sales team succeed?
Technical sales can be scary. At Braze he worked with the sales team to flip the narrative, lead with a story and provide a differentiated experience
Push sales to think about qualification in a stronger way. Pushed leadership to think about disqualification vs qualification as a means to better qualify.

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Timeline: Would love to start mid-May, no deadlines on TC's end

Has done both, Bynder calls and emails and using sales tools. Inbound, would run disco calls and pass to AE to tease out information - how many campaigns do you wish you could release. What would outcome of digital asset management do for marketing team at prospect. 

Stytch, more passing through, hot potato, as soon as get meeting pass to AEs. Noticing with SMB section, it can be hard to fit through so many API calls and sifting through to get the diamonds in the rough - does it make sense? 1 SMB AE only at the time, so they had to be thoughtful about ensuring they did enough on their end given the single AE badnwidth. 

Flexing into whatever is needed, have to be moldable esp in an environment where things are changing a lot. Has done both. 

Tech aspects in resume. Learned some in school, took some coding classes, through major would use tools like powerBI and R to do optimization problems. Swift Python learned out of curiosity. Covid was an interesting time, wanted to learn - make cool apps. The curiosity was the driving force there. 

Started in operations, thought that is what TC want to do. Operations and SC did not pan out to be what TC thought it was. Switched into sales from talking to mentors. Lessons were to be had about that experience - learning repeatability & continuously improving which are quintessential to sales. 

Bynder learned core tenants of what is a good sales pitch, how to handle objections, create concise methods to stick out - using various methods. Email, call, LI message, focus on getting people to talk. Wanted to hone in on team that is energetic and excited. Stych felt like a good choice, on a smaller team 1/3 BDRs had two sequences in use. Felt like it was evergreen, without being told was able to chart out who are we reaching out to, why don’t we have a sequence for CTO, VP of Eng, thought it was fun coming in and getting name out there - felt excited about product, and just bc someone says no well why aren’t you interested? What do we not have? Figuring out that piece. Was not cold calling, analyzed Gong calls to find common objections, what are value props we say over and over, and how do we become prescriptive. Made a cold call playbook, not using that avenue. That was helpful, got him to be up close and personal with content - talk tracks using, helping in terms of being able to speak eloquently and on the fly. Working with PM why are we releasing b2b product suites, speaking with eng what its he product and Marketing why better than competition and weave that into something concise and compact for prospects to see. Laid off - but for next app wants to be somewhere for a long time, grow with company, learn product and be around smart folks. Try to learn from very interaction, loved that knew nothing about identity and access management asking million questions and felt like curiosity as piqued. 

Working out in the open is paramount - getting feedback on what you’re doing and how you’re doing it. Or, this is working really well.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion
3 - Hire


The candidate appears to be a culture fit for the company and I received good signal on values alignment. They gave a number of detailed examples throughout our conversation that demonstrated a growth mindset, thoughtful self-reflection, and effective cross-team collaboration even under difficult circumstances. I believe the candidate was sincere in their answers and that they thrive in a small company environment like ours where individuals have many opportunities for growth and impact. Prior experience working in a developer-focused security space (identity management) is a plus. I was surprised at the level of technical depth of some of the questions they asked but expected some more customer or business centric questions. For these reasons, my conclusion is 3 - Hire.


Raw Notes:


Agenda
Intros
Why Semgrep?
Culture & values
Questions for me?


Chris intro
Known founders for over 10 years
Working at Semgrep for past 2 years
Lead the infrastructure engineering team


Daniel intro
born and raised in Bay Area
Working in sales, learned how to craft a message and communicate how products would provide value to customers
Amazon - Didn’t enjoy the work environment, thought smaller might be better so gave it a try
Found my place at Stitch, sadly part of recent force reduction


How did you find out about Semgrep?
Wanted to stay in developer focused area, specifically security
Read about Semgrep on Topstartups.io and also in some tech news


What did you like about the culture at Stitch?
Started there when company was about 50
Really enjoyed open culture
Immediately felt welcomed and like it was a good fit
Jazzed to be there on Monday morning and so was the team
Admired cross-pollination between teams. For example developers would attend go to market meetings to stay in the loop
Opportunity to learn and grow
Grew to 65, then 20 cuts


What would you change about the culture?
More transparency, especially around things that are going to adversely affect the company
Felt like leadership didn’t communicate the need for course correction soon enough before having to do force reduction
Wish I had some time to make changes before it was too late


How did the culture compare to Amazon?
Felt a lot more valued at Stitch than at Amazon
People were so used to people at Amazon breathing down their neck, everyone was on edge
Learned that super aggressive tactics aren’t effective in the long run


Making the transition to a small company was hard because I had learn new ways of communicating
Really trying to cultivate a mentality of “here’s the problem I’m seeing and I need your help to get to a solution”
Also, mindset shift from “I’m right!” to “How do I know if I’m right?”
At a big organization, seems really hard to do something great when everyone marching in different directions (VPs, Directors, and EMs all seem to have very different goals)


At Stitch, felt strong alignment over sales strategy - example “compete and beat” for a quarter against competitors like Auth0


Giving Feedback
Amazon - operational setting, do the most with as few resources as possible and team was seen as pure cost
Didn’t have enough headcount, team started falling behind on metrics
Gave feedback that goals were unrealistic given current staffing and advocated for 2 additional headcount
Was intimidated because early in his career, giving feedback to people 10-15 years older and with long tenure at the company in front of many peers, felt scary but was glad to have done it


Getting Feedback
First month at small company
Manager feedback that he’s not getting results
Course correct: worked together to figure out how to do better
Reviewed sales calls, identified pivot points in the conversation, retroed how to adjust in the future
Impact: significantly better numbers is following months


Mutual Respect - what does that mean to you?
Believe everyone has something to add
Learning each others strengths and weaknesses and utilizing them
Being comfortable saying something if you see something wrong, expecting that others do the same
Continually be improving


How do you build trust in the first days, weeks?
Meet everyone in the org
Learn the people that make up various groups (marketing, sales, revops) and how those groups mesh at this company
Being proactive about asking for feedback
Being vulnerable and owning mistakes


Mission Driven - what about our mission resonated with you?
Background in operations, the premise of stopping problems earlier in the process made intuitive sense
Got interested in identity management space working at Stitch
Semgrep is similar lens into the security space but at a different angle


Impact
Being effective in the role means getting more customers realizing the value of Semgrep
Measured through more logos won in deals but also increased community engagement


What’s the hardest you’ve ever worked and why?
Amazon was a meat grinder - wanted to put in a little extra effort though
Three departments, differing goals, distrust between them caused problems
His team “Return to Station” goal: reduce returns


Felt the departments gave an overweighted importance to what they contributed and underweighted the contributions of other departments
Came in early to debrief with the other departments to understand their perspectives and pain points
Became an informal mediator between the teams and slowly worked to “whitelabel” concessions that teams weren’t willing to give one another directly
Impact: improved relationships between teams led to reduced “waste” (lost items in warehouse) by 3% - HUGE number for operations passing $50k+ product daily


What’s your long-term arc post-Semgrep?
Hoping to stay with Semgrep for 5+ years
Goals are to learn as much as possible about cold starting a sales engine which seems both really hard but also exciting
Long-term goal is to be a revenue leader (CRO) at an early-stage startup


Questions for me?


Surprised to see Swift as experimental, it seems like there are a lot of iOS developers out there (learned Swift during Covid). How do you decide which languages are GA?


How do you decide if a rule is broad enough to not just be used by a single customer but all customers?


What’s one project that you’ve said yes to and no to? why?


How do you keep focus with so much going on?


How do you think I would work cross functionally at Semgrep?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I enjoyed my discussion with Daniel and am an emphatic yes!

I believe Daniel's will be a success given his experience in Supply Chain management, his drive for repeatable and continuous improvement, and that he has mentors. He's the first BDR to-date that spoke to having a mentor. 

Daniel shared mid-term and long-term goals....another first for my BDR interviews. I was impressed that he has a plan both near and long term. 

Finally, he asked some of the best closing questions, one of which was, "How does a BDR tease out this information before the discovery call?" He asked this after I shared that I am working with my AE's to better investigate prospects prior to the discovery call.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Manager



Relationship History
Can you describe the candidate's day-to-day work or responsibilities?
Happy to hear he is making progress. 
Will caveat the whole conversation - Daniel and I worked together for a short time, 25% of the company laid off. Was let go as well. Overlapped for the last ~2 months. 
Was Daniel’s direct manager, recruited and hired him and brought him over. Day to day was really outbound heavy and outbound focused. BDR on a team of 5, day to day task was going after and finding businesses for Stytch to sell to. Always the first one in, and the last one to leave. Great work ethic. After fully ramping, rep with most activity on the team. Sending emails, LI, even testing out Looms. Very impressed by the way he came in and really wanted to succeed. 
Communication Skills
Have you ever had difficulty communicating with the candidate? 
Team got along well, all open to helping one another and it was a nice cohesive team unit that Daniel worked well within. If there was a team meeting, Daniel is someone Shane could lean on - can you present, pitch, etc. Always willing to share what is and is not working. In some instances you can come in new and find something successful and keep to self - he definitely did not do that, was very open and wanted to help others. 
How does the candidate handle feedback? Can you provide an example?
Actually very open to feedback, would continually push to do weekly email reviews of content. Was very open to feedback, wanted to get better and wanted to accelerate own path to success. Not someone to shy away. Also great to see the first week or so, sat down and Daniel wanted to set goals he wanted to achieve first 30, 60, and continue to elicit feedback. Can be direct with him. 
Strengths and Opportunities
In what areas do you think the candidate could improve or develop further? (see below)
What challenges would be beneficial for the candidate to experience in order to continue learning and growing professionally?
One of the areas for growth is, he did a great job of upping his written word. Came in, and bc what he had done previously going after markers, at Stych targeting engineers - had to help him with tone to make sure is on brand for engineers vs. marketers. 
Naturally funny, great to work with - once the meeting starts Daniel is locked in. 
Performance
How has the candidate handled rejection or difficult situations in their sales role?
Had gotten a few warm maybes - tell me more about the product, or this does not seem interesting why would I waste my time. Strategize about how to respond to that. Wants to work to figure out how to turn this round, had a few nibbles - does not know if it turned into an opp. Saw the beginnings of him being able to object, handle and strategize on how to counter and come back. 
Growth Trajectory
In what ways has the candidate developed professionally or technically since you first knew them?
Looking ahead, what areas should the candidate focus on to continue growing?
As the candidate's potential future manager, do you have any suggestions for how we can best support their success at Semgrep?
Don’t be afraid to push him - not overwhelm, but put a lot on his plate. He proved he can handle it, he has the aptitude to grow. When it comes to the technical side of a product, ensure you’re spending time to get his product knowledge ramped up. Marketing software, Stych was an API technical, selling into technical personas, etc. So he understands ICP, and can construct solid outbound outreach. 
If you could hire this person again, would you?

Yes

candidate_data.company: Lockheed Martin Missiles and Fire Control
candidate_data.title: Lead Software Engineer / Engineering Leadership Development Program

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Was a referral, so I chatted with Seth early on. Passed the homework. We had a good conversation - no cultural/behavioral flags. He was very understanding of the situation, never cut me off, gave in depth answers without probing.

questions.question: What is your timeline (e.g. do you have any deadlines we should be aware of)
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: What are your compensation expectations?
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Seth and I chatted while I was walking to the office - the commute in took 2-ish hours, so I wasn't able to get set up. 

Lockheed - internal research -> development lead. Small team, rapid integration bw 2 systems for customers. Same idea of do someting, throw together and experiment to see what customers say and get their feedback, then iterate and push that out quickly.

With LH was able to get out to demo live with customer. Did a lot of traveling - Hawaii, Guam, etc. Demos on ground with customers figuring out what they want. Lots of experimenting, especially with military customers. Need to figure out how to meet customer needs, and be quick about it. 

MS in the AI/ML realm. Does not have in depth experience with LLMs, has messed around with ChatGPT. Approaching this as an opportunity to learn quickly, absorb as much as possible. Solid on the eng side.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I'm a bit lukewarm because Seth only has experience at Lockheed Martin (very different culture from us) and they just started getting into ML. But I also saw no reason to not give them a chance.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Coderpad - https://app.coderpad.io/YXFMKA3W/playback#886

One of the best interviews I've had for the package registry question.

Pros - great communicator, collaborator, broke down the question really well, tested incrementally, and solved the question (and follow-up extension) before time.
Cons - Nothing really. Just that they have been doing a lot of C/C++ lately so might need a lil time to ramp up on Python codebase or just our stack in general. I am not sure how comfortable they'd be in a full-stack role but that's about it. The interview itself was flawless. 

As we finished early, got a chance to chat with them longer and have an extended QnA session. Seemed very excited about the role, and also asked meaningful questions about the team and company direction. Shared their experiences in the past and described how they have worked on smaller teams and projects at Lockheed in the past. Plus, they are ready to start as soon as possible. Overall, seems like a great fit to me, but will leave it on others to do more screening.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Seth seems like a decent candidate! Kind of a reserved and uncontroversial personality. I struggled a bit to get interesting info out of him but with that being said, he did answer everything respectfully, nicely, (and conservatively). The concern I have I guess is Lockheed is soooo different from us and it's the only place he ever worked at. And he did not really say anything bad about Lockheed, either, so he's kinda happy there. But any time I probed it seemed like he'd also be okay with a Semgrep-like startup, and a role that involves a bunch of product work (I explicitly asked about this part). I guess I'm just concerned because Seth gave me no almost no strong opinions at all.

questions.answer:
Pros: 
* Strong iterative mindset, talked about past projects where he worked with senior leadership to ship an MVP, against push back. And in the product design question, talked about iterating in order to et to a final solution. Considered how it'd impact users & used that to inform his final solution.
*Strong communicator - clear & easy to follow. Didn't have to ask follow up questions, directly answered my questions. I'd feel very comfortable putting him in front of a customer for a demo. 

Cons:
* Didn't get a strong sense if he's excited about us over another company/why he's passionate about security. Would be good to dig into in the founder interview!

Seth took over from Chris

Chris was overexplaining a lot and Seth could catch things a lot quicker than others, Seth was setting up gitlab CI on a classified network and was a lot faster than Chris thought

Majority no, one of them wrote most of the code, and there was tension, but realized seth wasn't doing it in a combative way

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
﻿@Adam Berman﻿

Max has a lot of the qualities you are looking for. Still early in career but focused on moving into security with plans to become a Security Researcher at some point. He attended b-sides and visited our booth. Familiarized himself by looking at our website and documentation. 


Current role/why are you looking? 
-At Anchain. spend time on hackmedia and found opportunity there. they build security for web3. not a lot of tools and like working there. currently doing audits of smart contracts. 
-joined as intern. proposed CTF project at Anchain to help them and him. 
-has been there for a year. working as SWE. help gov investigators invest smart contract. compliance and concerns. malware analysis. 
-role is going away from security focus that he is more interested in. 
-been wanting a more security focused job. able to write code but want to focus on security and vulnerability


Why security?
-beginning of 2020, played first CTF event. Got top 200 players and was really fun. playing competitions to be better and get to know more people in the field.
-there are unique approaches to security. always something new to learn. approach you take is about poking holes and unconventional ways to solve. spurs his curiosity.


What is important to you in your next role?
-looking for a role that he can learn. challenging. 
-learn and apply new skills to do the work. working with more experienced people as well. 
-tend to get tunnel vision so learning from others and their perspective is important.
-want to have an impact with preventing vulnerabilities in existing code. liked this aspect in current job


What is your knowledge of/familiarity with CVE?
-know the term. know there are security researchers out there that will find the vulnerability and disclose to vendors. CVE goes public. 
-gaml rules. proof of concepts. 
-others will research and post blogs. identify them. 
-separate from a weakness since they are more specific. 



Have you done any side projects or participated in security activities?
-CTFs. 
-attend B-sides. 
-speaker at RSA conference; hack group looking for speakers and he submitted a workshop on smart contracts. went really well and enjoyed it. 


When was the last time you learned a new concept and what is the process that you undertake?
-back in Feb. CTF team decided to play dice CTF. 
-there was a Binary exploitation. simple for experienced CTF but he had trouble with it as did rest of team.
-weren't able to solve the challenge but afterwards, looked at write-ups. able to write solution based on their instructions. more of a trial and error way.
-worked with disassembler before. had previous assumptions which were incorrect. worked with team to consider other options. 


Can you share the last time you felt challenged and what you did?
-for CTF project. no experience with marketing or leading a team. 
-since he was the only person interested in it, was a great learning experience
-to achieve goals with software role he neglected the sales and marketing side. but not able to ignore for CTF.
-reached out to marketing lead and they provided suggestions. 
-did best to take in account of details. event planning stuff that he had to lead. manage the expectations and goals for the event. getting x amount of people. keep track and review event. got less people then expected and less sponsorship then expected. 
-CTF is public facing and learning focused. bring to a meetup. invite university students and developers to drive engagement with dev community in smart contracts. 


Where are you at with other opportunities? 
-not currently interviewing. 


Compensation 
$40/hr. reasonable for contract. 
able to come into the SF office.


Visa status
-eligible.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Referral from Diptendu.

Good call overall. Checked off a lot of the boxes. Is interested in security career, can understand C/C++, Python and has used it to create a toolkit (pretty recent though, January 23), pursuing more certifications, and wants to join a startup so he can continue learning. 

How familiar are you with Semgrep?
-Static analysis to find bugs. writing code and common mistakes to correct. 

Tell me about your experience?
Fidelity IT Analyst 6 months. worked with AWS, redis, kafka, citrix. Semgrep would have been helpful in the role. His job was to review policies and make sure they align.
-identify issues in system. work with those that configured it. minor config mistakes or permission issues. identify what policy is set. create report and validations to report to stakeholders.
-cross func partners; any dev team and cybersecurity team. 
-example of work; worked on AWS IAM audit. there were many people that had permissions and weren't removed post project completion. identified access vulnerabilities and reported out findings. 

Just graduated from the Master of Cybersecurity program. read previous calls as part of studies. 

What do you know about CVE; 
-sets of rejection rules
-know how to read rules. but haven't created any rules. knows what to find/look for. 

What programming languages are you familiar with?
-C/C++, Python and Bash. above beginner level. worked on window/linux tool kits in Python.

What is important to you in your next role?
-would like to get into position and learn. like a startup. having conversations with 2 other companies. completing more certifications. 
-Future: want to go into cybersecurity engineering. Security engineering and leading his own team one day. not interested in risk management.

Current job search status?
-speaking with 2 other companies. they are risk management roles that he's not interested in. 

Sponsorship?
EAD starts on June 15. Can't work until then

Located in Boston and able to go into the office.

Questions?
How much research are you able to do?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion
I have overall concerns on not just the if Tanya is able to transition from her "She/We hack purple" brand, persona, and company, but if she really has the right experience and aptitude to lead our Community strategy. I found myself wondering if she had every really successfully done this at a company before - there were a lot of "I could have, I need to, I should have" but not many examples of where she executed and accomplished this. Whether that is hiring and onboarding a Dev Rel team member, community team, etc. There wasn't a lot of thought put into her team strategy and charter 

I also didn't get a good sense that she had worked with a true marketing team nor did she effectively communicate how she might think about that partnership if she were to come on board. I rephrased the question 3 times to see if she could get to an explanation or though-process, but instead she spoke about her own experience getting asked to lead the team followed with "I didn't know what I was doing and they didn't set me up for success". 

Overall, her communication was long and verbose. She didn't practice active listening and therefore her answers never really answered my questions. She spent a very long time speaking about her poor experience with her previous company and I wish we had an opportunity to speak more about the role and how she could apply her experiences, ideas, and brand. 

I also found the way she communicated somewhat off-putting - As a person that might act as a face to our brand, some of her comments and thought in our conversation lacked tact and distasteful - "I would never go to Chicago - there's nothing there. No offense if you're from Chicago" (PS. I'm from Chicago). "I have a lot of famous friends' "Everyone comes to my things so that they can see me"- Came off as arrogant and high-ego.

She had no questions for me.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion
I would rate more of a 3.5! 

Tanya has great experience in many big-name security firms (Microsoft, IANS, Canadian Government, etc) and has experience in SAST and DAST. She's very humble, has given many presentations that have been referenced multiple times, and would be great to work with! 

Although she wasn't able to fully complete the small coding challenge, much of her job description has nothing to do with coding. 
Positives
* Very humble and friendly
* Has ideas on how to improve our SAST product and what is desired in SAST tools
* Has experience in using many SAST tools, also has extensive experience in different fields of security (Appsec, pentesting, cloud security, etc)
* "Shifting everywhere", not just shifting left (aligns with our values)
Negatives
* Wasn't able to fully complete the coding challenge given in the interview (explain what the code below is doing). However, she doesn't have python experience, and had a good idea of what most of the code was doing.
This is also not an integral part of her future job description, and she was able to voice out many of her thoughts. 
Follow-Up Questions
Not specified
Raw Notes
Tell me about yourself:

- long line of software engineers, starting working professionally in 97
    - played music in bars, entertainment, professional comedy
- started working in security after band person introduced them
- ran security for Canada, pentested PM’s website
    - Canadian government doesn’t have money for training
    - OWASP project leaders get free training
- People started sending her plane tickets after a while
- Microsoft — asked Tanya if she wanted to work for them, cloud security focus
    - learned how giant big organizations work
    - everyone really cared about security
    - working with people with SD → security pipeline, getting buy-in
- Messaging is so important
- SAST
    - reachability — for SCA
    - static analysis — antipattern matching, taint analysis
        - speed
        - every tool has false negatives
        - nextgen tools
        - CI/CD, comparing results
        - DAST to confirm SAST res
    - tanya — wrote a shifting left like a boss
    - shifting everywhere
- TP/FP ratio:
    - it depends
        - if security doesn’t matter too much — building fast as compared to
    - 95% of these security situations don’t need to be perfect
    - most people don’t need perfect security, just need pretty good security
    - fixing highs, criticals, maybe mediums
    - chance that you are facing an advanced-enough attacker to do this is pretty low
    - shift across industry → 100% secure → breaking things bc we are building fast

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
4 for senior, 3 for staff (not saying I think he should be staff, just giving this for comparison because I've included it in other interviews): 

They did almost everything the rubric looked for. Not familiar with OCaml, but able to dive in by asking the engineer questions. Broke down the problem in a very principled manner, specifically engaged with me to give me a chance to voice my suggestions, and was careful not to make assumptions. At the end, when I wanted to implement it, he brought up without prompting both that we should talk to the team before making architectural changes and that we should consider whether this feature request should actually be completed.

Some things I liked outside of the actual interview:
Excited about "fast, lightweight static analysis"
Noticed that Semgrep could be good for developers as well as security, though we focus on security. Asked me about initiatives there
Was interested in our room for experimentation and how we got feedback

Fun fact: both interviewees zoomed in on the presence of "patterns" in the new rule. Nobody else has ever pointed to that before.

Rubric:

Part 1: 
Infinite loop 
Solved the problem (4 = worked it out from info given, 3 = figured it out with a hint, 2 = were guided to it, 1 = didn’t get) 
Identified the “make failing” as an infinite loop in a test case - 4
Understood that recursion was potentially problematic - 4
[Optional] Saw the exact call that was causing the infinite recursion - 3
Behavioral choices (4 = always, 3 = usually, 2 = sometimes, 1 = never) 
Focused on the relevant code, did not try to understand everything - 4
Avoided making assumptions - 4
Asked the engineer questions rather than trying to read the code themselves - 4
Kept the engineer as the active participant (corollary: communicated what they wanted clearly) - 4
Made strategic use of print statements and deleting code to isolate the problem - 4 (no print statements but similar isolation)
Responded with encouragement when the engineer declares themselves stupid - 4
Part 2: Logic error Solved the problem (4 = worked it out from info given, 3 = figured it out with a hint, 2 = were guided to it, 1 = didn’t get) 
Understood the difference between pattern and pattern-inside - 4
Understood how information about the operators was stored in the rule tree - 3
[Difficult] Recognized the error as a fundamental problem with the engineer’s design - 4
Behavioral choices (4 = always, 3 = usually, 2 = sometimes, 1 = never) 
[Difficult] Challenged the engineer to explain their design choices - 4
Dug deeper on what they needed (and no more) to find the problem - 4
Made strategic use of visual representation of the rule type - 4
Part 3 : Deciding what to do 
Solved the problem (4 = did without additional prompting, 3 = did after a prompt, 2 = were guided to it, 1 = didn’t do) 
Received an estimate from the engineer for how long they think it would take - 1
Set a time limit to cost a potential solution - 4
[Difficult] Asked about edge cases resulting from a syntactic change like this - 3
Did not ask about edge cases, but talked about the need to test syntax on other users
Helped the engineer reach out to get the support they need to solve this issue - 4
Constructed an honest and communicative response to the user with the engineer - 4
Behavioral choices (4 = always, 3 = usually, 2 = sometimes, 1 = never) 
[Difficult] Sought to understand the engineer’s estimates (junior engineers are bad at estimation) - N/A
[Difficult] Tried to root cause the engineer’s reluctance/hesitance/nervousness - 1
[Difficult] Considered the impact of the engineer’s choices on customers + the team - 4
Encouraged the engineer to be independent - 4
Responded to disagreement respectfully (but fairly) - 4
Overall: psychological safety 
Made the engineer feel valued and comfortable - 4
Made it easy for the engineer to ask questions and express concerns - 4
Easy to interrupt or didn’t need to be interrupted (concise, left pauses, etc.) - 4
Paused to check in that the engineer is following - 4
architectural choices 
You might get a sense of how they think about architecture based on the interview. This is a place to leave notes on that, if applicable
talked about checking with the team before changing the type

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Amarin has relevant PL/PA education and also some work experience. His most recent work at Google isn't particularly relevant to us. But he understands dataflow analysis concepts quite well and he could be a good fit to contribute to Semgrep's dataflow features like taint analysis. Overall impression is positive, no red flags. Assuming he does well in the other interviews (particularly in the type system one) then I think he could be a strong candidate.

Good:
* PL/PA experience.
* Says "I don't know" when he doesn't know.
* Knows some static analysis, understand context-sensitivity, flow sensitivity, may/must, etc.
* He has an understanding of taint analysis and could figure out what to do to implement such an analysis.

Not so good:
* Not very interesting "lessons learned" to discuss (he didn't consider pointers in the toy language that he used for his PhD).
* Comparison between "textbook" Gen-Kill dataflow and Datalog not very deep, didn't mention easy of spec which is supposedly the big gain with Datalog I think? Nonetheless he makes points that do make some sense.
* Not much OCaml experience.

Experience:
* Studied PL/PA during MSc: type systems, theorem provers, lambda-calculus, static analysis, points-to via Datalog (!), etc.
* PhD, worked on loop optimizations, such as reordering to improve cache locality.
* Worked in a Huawei lab, did some dataflow analysis there (live-vars).
* Worked in start up already.
* Work in Google not PL/PA related (profiling tools, Linux perf).

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Good candidate with interesting background in profiling (worked at Google
on the pprof team, which is a super cool profiling tool) which could be
useful for all the profiling work Emma is doing to improve performance.
Was also honest about being laid off from Google and it was super
nice he learned OCaml in the last week to Ramp up! However he didn't
solve fully the type inference hence the 3.

pro:
 - solved typecheck quickly
 - learned OCaml during the last week to prepare for this interview!
   and was pretty good in it (used many OCaml idioms, knew already OCaml a lot)
 - wrote some tests
   
cons:
 - struggled a lot on the type inference
 - was blocked a few times, I had to help; waited too long to
   start coding

--- RAW DATA

** me

** him
PhD UIUC, compiler, David Padua, Darko Marinov (strong people)
Google, profiling team (they owned pprof)

main interest = making tools to help software engineer (nice! like me)
honest, laid off by Google, so look for cool companies to join

** typechecking: start 9:10

learned OCaml in the last week to rampup :) (youtube Cornel lectures)
"if I have to join Semgrep, I'll have to learn OCaml, so simpler I do it now"
(did some Haskell a few years ago, so not too hard to ramp up)

but remember 'rec', 'function' for pattern matching, Map.Make, |>, Nice ramp up!

quick AST definition and typecheck
mistake on Minus (different than Plus)
forgot variables :) hence good idea to write a test

9:20: start write simple test (and problem with variables)
nice test, it worked!

** type inference 9:28

go through many examples
env_output evolutions until type variable
unify environments
got idea of unify

go through more examples (good)
struggled
I helped a bit, through examples

** questions for me

roadmap/vision for semgrep
timezone
best thing you like:
worst thing at semgrep

*** code

(*
3 + 

// Valid, has type int
"hello" + " world" // Valid, has type str
3 + "5" // Invalid, no implicit conversions and + not defined like this
3 + a, {a: int} // Valid

string, int

+ : int, int -> int or string, string -> string (addition or concatenation)
- : int, int -> int
toString : int -> string
toInt : string -> int

*)

type expr =
| IntLiteral of int
| StringLiteral of string
| Plus of (expr*expr)
| Minus of (expr*expr)
| ToString of expr
| ToInt of expr
| Variable of string

type ty =
| IntType
| StringType
| NewType of string

module VarMap = Map.Make (String)

let rec typecheck env = function
| IntLiteral _ -> IntType
| StringLiteral _ -> StringType
| Plus (e1, e2) ->
  let ty_e1 = typecheck env e1 in
  let ty_e2 = typecheck env e2 in
  if ty_e1 = ty_e2 then ty_e1 else failwith "Plus did not typecheck"
| Minus (e1, e2) ->
  let ty_e1 = typecheck env e1 in
  let ty_e2 = typecheck env e2 in
  if ty_e1 = IntType && ty_e2 = IntType then IntType else failwith "Minus did not typecheck"
| ToString e ->
  let ty_e = typecheck env e in
  if ty_e = IntType then StringType else failwith "ToString did not receive type int"
| ToInt e ->
  let ty_e = typecheck env e in
  if ty_e = StringType then IntType else failwith "ToInt expects a string"
| Variable v -> VarMap.find v env

let print_ty = function
| IntType -> print_endline "int"
| StringType -> print_endline "string"

open Printf
let _ =
  let env = VarMap.empty |> VarMap.add "i" IntType |> VarMap.add "s" StringType in
  print_ty (typecheck env (IntLiteral 5));
  print_ty (typecheck env (StringLiteral "x"));
  
(*
(3 + a) + (b + 4), {} // Empty variable bindings - what must be true?
  a -> either int or string
  a -> int
(3 + a) + ("foo" + a) // Invalid 
a, {} // int or string 
(a + b) + 1 , {} // int, {a -> int, b -> int} 
newtype {a->int, b->newtype} {newtype->int} int 
newtype -> actual type
env_output = {"a"->IntOrStringType, "b" -> IntOrStringType}
env_output = {"a"->(NewType "a"), "b" -> (NewType "b")}
(a+b)needs to be int
newtype "a" + newtype "b"

(a + b) + 1

a --> Newtype "a", { a -> NewType "a"}
b --> NewType "b", { b -> NewType "b"}

a + b --> NewType "a", { a --> NewType "a", b --> NewType "a"}
(a+b) + 1
1 -> Int, {}
(a+b) + 1 --> Int, { a --> Int} 

*)  

let typeinfer env exp =
  let unify v t env = failwith "" in
  match exp with
  | Variable v ->
    if VarMap.mem v env then
      (VarMap.find v env, env)
    else
      let new_ty = NewType v in
      (new_ty, VarMap.add v new_ty env)
  | Plus (e1, e2) ->
    let ty_e1 = typecheck env e1 in
    let ty_e2 = typecheck env e2 in
    match (ty_e1, ty_e2) with
    | (IntType, StringType) -> failwith "erorr"
    | (StringType, IntType) -> failwith "error"
    | (NewType v , IntType) -> (IntType, unify v IntType env)
    | (NewType v, StringType) -> (StringType, unify v StringType env)
    | (NewType v1, NewType v2) -> (NewType v1, unify v2 (NewType v1) env)

Notes:
- EM at Okta, through Auth0 acquisition
- Interim EM role, then to TL, now full time EM
- Why Semgrep?
    - Tom reached out, made him interested, then found out about us from the Brendon zombie campaign
    - Really likes our approach, pragmatic security
    - Has really done some research on us, knows about us vs dependabot
    - Wants to work at a startup, wants to be really bought into a mission, wants to feel like their work is meaningful and leading to something
- Communication
    - Director in his org realized that he has a bias toward nice, conflict averse people
    - Zack and his manager are the more strong willed folks
    - Built something with NextJs, wanted a cool routing feature, but it led to a weird undefined behavior
    - Made this argument to his manager, while also trying to see what his manager was seeing so he could learn and figure it out
    - Manager didn’t want to be too confrontational, so the manager didn’t push back in a helpful way
    - Short term, they just went with his manager’s decision, but long term learned that the important part is to dig into the content, not who’s side it is
- Feedback
    - Initially, would just take a task, on his own, just sit down and get it done himself
    - Not getting enough feedback, not giving folks a venue to give feedback
    - Pointed out while progressing from senior to senior II
    - Being proactive about communicating and soliciting feedback
    - More senior people are more willing to give feedback, has to push junior folks to give feedback
- Mentorship
    - Very tailored to an individual, understanding who they are, what they need
    - Digging into technical side and soft skill side
    - Senior person, no need to mentor her technically, but trying to help her with her communication, giving good feedback on creating a narrative
    - For the junior person, giving her space to think through technical decisions, making sure she has good relationships with other engineers on the team
- Growth
    - “I want to be able to do anything”
    - There are core things that power web products that he has no idea how they work, wants to learn more about them
    - Wants to be a product focused person, not just an engineer, wants to understand the business side of things as well
    - Lots of transparency, both ways, with manager
    - Wasn’t getting useful feedback from manager in first role, b/c there were so many re-orgs
    - Wants honesty about what being effective means, what feedback can help him do better

# Presentation

Raw Notes:

- Year long lull post acq.
- The first project was to merge marketo instances for auth0 + okta
- Long in the making, pulled in quite late (3 months)
- Simple things: same privacy policy across the board, point forms to new backend bits
- More complex tasks, with top of funnel impact

- Start by figuring out the dependencies
- Identify the low hanging fruit
- Doing 2 week sprints, 3x = 6 weeks, bit of buffer for testing, fixes, etc
- Harder part is depending on other people, have to get details from the Okta teams.
- Marketo teams are also still not aligned.
- Test env had gaps, have to do some in prod.
- Splitting out wishlist items vs must do
- A/B test as soon as possible.

- Team has a junior, senior, senior II
- Junior was on a different project, but new senior person with no context is the same problem
- Think about this as cascading dependencies - put the less experienced folks on the things where they can learn + grow but don't block other things.
-

# Tech
- Marketo is the connection between Marketing and Sales
- Marketo you can do whatever you want with leads.
- Massive queue.

- Build vs Buy -> if you can afford marketo don't build it.
- Not quite as granular as AWS. Don't need to have the expertise to build building blocks just know how to pick the one that fits for you.

- Marketo doesn't tell you if you have invalid form submissions
- If you don't send a required field, maybe spelling mistake on form field
- Just gets silently rejected somewhere later on.
- Solve the problem by figuring out how to get the team access
- Side effect of the queueing systems

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
3 for Senior 1,
I'm open to a senior 2  role if others champion him on technical fronts.

Zack is somewhat out of his depth with backend things, but not in a bad way.
I like that he acknowledges that finding a frontend role in a team with full stack work as a good path towards building that skillsets.

Given they were honest with me on this front, I assessed them from that angle and I was pretty happy with everything I saw.
Reasoned through things well, took clues well and it was obvious they knew more than they trusted themselves to say.


Raw Notes
- Good assumptions about scale, request count today

# Runs on the backend, how do we protect ourselves
- Is this a DDoS Protection thing

- with no protections, user could press the button as much as possible.
- what does multiple responses even mean, would be a bad experience
- would make sense to support multiple requests if they have two windows open
- with a single instance, disable the run button while it's running (already there)
- limitation is the keyboard shortcut already bypasses this
- would the person implementing the button disabling remember the shortcut
- the user could always just send requests
- theres no auth today, but even if they can get auth today
- frontend is quick, but won't really cut it.
- if we have a ton of users, what are we actually trying to handle
- when is it a distributed problem vs not
- ddos protection -> cloud flare
- at some point identify who is sending a request
> I asked: are you describing rate limiting
- haven't done this before
> how do we implement it in theory
- need to store some kind of state about the user
- most naiive solution -> relational db, but has slow read/write
- keep track of requests in the window and make the decision.
- want fast reads, probably using some sort of a cache
- probably use something like redis, has constant time lookup
- writes are less important that reading for performance 

# Interfaces
- don't think its UI
- expect we mean between different components
- one big distinction is backend vs frontend
- REST api pattern is a good place to start
- Fetching user info if they're logged in
- Fetching some rule info that was saved previously
- Balance between shape of the data vs frontend needs
- POST can result in side effects.
- Data contract
- If we have to do specific formatting for the frontend its a smell -> backend and frontend are tightly coupled
- Rule + Sample code are constants, build around those
- GET list of rules
- GET a specific rule
- rule is stored somewhere the backend
- rule itself
- metdata
- what about versions? (need more info)
- POST endpoint
- doesn't always have to mutate,
- in this case we don't mutate but we do extra work
- need to send everything to run
- always post, because having the backend fetch it would.
- GraphQL vs REST
- rest is quite involved schema wise, needs versioning
- graphql gives you just what you want
- eg if I need a 4th field in a rest world have to add it

# Mobile friendly
- unified -> maybe the features aren't sensible for mobile but lets assume that they are
- difference is in styling
- rendering, browser has css
- use media queries to decide what to show/hide/resize, etc
- how do you make the UI make sense on a phone screen
- 3 panes don't make sense 
- half and half will result in very little screen space
- make the default to hide the rule list when you're editing a rule
- if you plan ahead - components can cater to both
- once you get outside css its get more complicated
- downside becomes having to maintain two
- very case by case when to break things into multiple
- not being able to easily read through a component to understand what happens is an indicator
- hopping around too much is a problem
- functional components help with this a bit
- literal size or many conditionals are also smells
- ask "why" a lot.

# milestones
- running rules from the browser without save
- add rate limiting

- what is the importance of the problems
- why is rate limiting a goal
- is the stablity thing regular
- why not save first
- assume the playground is important to the funnel
- rate limiting
- backend/infra piece -> need to setup the cache, add the logic to the load balancer
- frontend bit, show an error to make it clear whats going on
- can do these in parallel
- define the contract (is it 429, something else) -> frontend person is unblocked
- frontend fixes will be quick, so if we have the data that it will help then do those
- api stuff
- from post with rule id + code
- to post with rule def + code
- in theory you can just check which field is passed in the request
- how do we know if a rule is valid?
- aim for small frontend changes (payload is changed)
- backend work 
- feature flags or versioning
- safest way is an entirely new endpoint

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Strong 4. I think Zack was awesome and would be a great addition to the team. He seems to know his stuff, and I can tell that he'd be able to catch up really quickly if there's something he doesn't know.  The only negative is that we'd have yet another Zack on the team.

questions.question: Did the candidate bring up tradeoffs?
questions.answer: Yes

questions.question: Conclusion
questions.answer: Strong hire

questions.question: Clear communication
questions.answer: Zack was clear with his communication and was very articulate with his comments.

questions.question: Respect & collaboration
questions.answer: No red flags here, Zack was very respectful and collaborated on feedback throughout the interview.

questions.question: Did the candidate demonstrate alignment or misalignment?
questions.answer: No red flags

questions.question: Demonstrates expertise
questions.answer:
Zack demonstrated his expertise in multiple ways throughout the interview:
Called out that we can have memory leaks by setting state within an async call in a useEffect
Dug through the unchanged lines of code that were collapsed to point out multiple potential bugs
Articulated that we're going to have multiple flickers and loading states with how we're loading the data

questions.question: Distinguishing between large/small issues
questions.answer: Zack started the interview off by pointing out the larger issues, saying "this is what needs to be fixed before merging", and then moving onto the smaller issues in the PR what were more UX related.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Definitely excited by Zach's attention to detail in this interview. I think he could be a great addition to the frontenders team. My only open question is if he's senior 1 or 2.

questions.question: Did the candidate bring up tradeoffs?
questions.answer: Talked a little bit about copying code and the tradeoffs there with component maintainability.

questions.question: Did the candidate demonstrate alignment or misalignment?
questions.answer: No red flags. The questions he asked at the end of the interview revolved around the tech stack and how we work. He talked about how the company he's at has slowed down (since acquisition I think?) so it seems he wants to be somewhere that isn't bogged down by process. Leads me to believe he is driven by impact.

questions.question: Clear communication
questions.answer: No red flags.

questions.question: Distinguishing between large/small issues
questions.answer: Zach started out the interview by prioritizing what he would talk about and started with the bigger issues.

questions.question: Respect & collaboration
questions.answer: Would ask for clarification on things and revise his feedback accordingly (sometimes an explanation of why we did something a certain way would make his comment no longer relevant, which he addressed).

questions.question: Conclusion
questions.answer: Strong Yes for senior 1, not sure about senior 2 (would need to see how he does on the other interviews).

questions.question: Demonstrates expertise
questions.answer: Zach has a great attention to detail and caught lots of small bugs with loading states, memory leaks, and even an off by one error in the pagination. Jesse poked more on his thoughts on testing, and he talked about snapshot vs interactivity testing (which I believe he admitted to not having very much experience with). When Jesse asked about how to fix the memory leaks, I don't think his explanation was correct - he said if the call fails then there's a memory leak, and was a little hand wavy about how to check when a component unmounts, but that's all very google-able info so not really a bit deal. He could still identify the memory leak and that's more important

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Great conversation around our mission and culture. He's very aligned with the kind of company and engineering culture around autonomy and ownership.

Do the candidate's interests and values align with R2C?
4. Extremely "get stuff done", high impact, wants to work somewhere where he can move the needle, make an impact, be creative, solve problems, and work with people who enjoy what they do. “I want to go to work and be happy and go to work with people who are happy to be working on what they are working on”



Which technical project did the candidate describe? (Not graded)
Described a project where he transitioned an extremely complex and brittle database migration system into something that was easy to use, fast, and reliable.

    - System that runs mysql migrations
    - Used to use Liquibase, off the shelf tool for declaring change sets to a db, then you create a job that applies un-run change sets to the db
    - Everyone had to run their own migration jobs, which meant files existed per table, per team, etc - this did not scale as the company grew
    - Significant number of outages from migrations; bad lock states and stuff like that
    - How do we fix scalability, how do we make it more reliable?
    - Wanted to build a system that would allow folks to declare a specific migration, run the migration but not apply it (not swap out the tables)
    - Have a team that owns MySql so other teams don’t have to think about the database as much, don’t have to think about jobs, don’t have to think about running migrations
    - People can still set up change sets the same way, but the underlying implementation of how it applies will change
    - Built a linter to identify if a migration is invalid, or even if it would break stuff
    - Built a system for listening for change sets (Kafka topic) and then validate at runtime that the migration is still valid
    - Then would run that migration alter statement through a system that would identify the “type” of migration - is it an instant metadata change? Or will it lock?
    - The easy ones, you just connect to the db and run the migration
    - For more complex changes, we use Ghost (Github’s change tool) to allow for online migration changes
    - Built a layer called Casper which would orchestrate K8s to run jobs for running the migration against each of the shards
    - Then built something that would allow the UI to show the status of the migration, how far along it is
    - Started the team himself, tech led the team for 5-6 months but just himself, got 2 other people on the team once it was fleshed out to scale the system to the whole company
    - Had faced this problem quite a bit himself, had a good idea of what he wanted, and then went and talked to folks to figure out what they wanted and what pain points they had
    - Went a few layers deeper; why do these problems keep occurring, what could they do more than surface level to stop these problems altogether


Does the candidate execute on their work?
3. Spent a few months building a prototype himself, iteratively improving the experience for developers and using a couple of internal teams to test and roll out the prototype with. Got buy-in from leadership, got 2 more engineers on the team, made a more robust system and rolled out to rest of the company. TL'd for those other engineers. Adapted to lots of changing constraints. Went out and investigated himself to figure out what to solve and how to solve it. Basically, not a 4 because of project selection; it was an internal tool, not a customer facing tool, which presents a different set of problems, but other than that, I really liked his approach.
Does the candidate communicate effectively?
4. Clear communicator, anticipated my questions, described goals at a high level, broke things down at a low level. Paused, thought about things, gave clear and concrete examples. Visual was sparse, but easy to understand. Clearly thoughtful, cares about communication. Treats conflict as an opportunity to learn; basically there was disagreement because he was confronted with a different idea than he'd considered, and he took a step back to try to understand them before diving back in.

- Communication
    - Disagreeing
        - Had a good vision for how to run migrations in a quick way; direct migrations are inherently quicker
        - TL of mysql team disagreed that they should even try to categorize migrations, everything should be run as a ghost migration; nothing should be run direct except for create tables
        - ^ got feedback that that would make sure there was never a locking table
        - Helped him understand that safety and speed needed to be both taken into account
Is the candidate a good leader?
4. Extremely thoughtful. Prioritizes mentorship, helps folks learn a mental model instead of just giving them information. Knows he doesn't want to be a manager but still wants to be a leader. Has some experience TLing small 2-3 person groups. Feedback examples show that he has a strong growth mindset, treats mistakes and wins as opportunities to learn.
Summary
Growth mindset combined with get-stuff-done attitude. Seems like an excellent leader, extremely technical, impact oriented, productive, and values aligned. My only flag - and this is an important and non-trivial one - is that he's primarily been on infrastructure/platform/internal teams. It sounds like he does have an eye for the customer and wants to do product engineering, but it's worth a flag that this isn't something he has a ton of experience with.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Did the candidate demonstrate alignment or misalignment with our values (mutual respect, honesty, mission driven, our work has impact)? How so?
No red flags
Conclusion
Strong 4 for Senior 1. I think Zack is a good cultural fit and would be a great addition to the team.

Off the bat I got a strong impression that Zack is interested in leadership and mentorship. His first comments on the PR were about how if the JD wanted to get more guidance to schedule a 1:1 on his calendar, and they could build out a plan so the JD would have something to write on their performance evaluation. 

Zack caught most of the bugs and smaller issues on the PR, but when asked what he would do if he received this ticket he kind of gave a hand wavy "I'd call for an architecture meeting to scope out the problem and find the best solution" answer.  While it's technically not a wrong answer I would've liked to see him commit to a potential solution that he thinks would be best. He gave a couple responses that seemed to also be hiding behind "well I'm not sure what Semgrep's process is" rather than taking a chance and potentially being wrong.

Zack comes off as a hacker (for better or for worse Matt described him as a young Tom) and I think with a little bit freedom he could bring a lot to the table, although he might be put off by how loose working at Semgrep is compared to the rigidness of Snapchat.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Really enjoyed my conversation with Phaedra. She is currently a senior support engineer with Github. She started her career as a support engineer, moved to account management/sales as she loved talking to customers and transitioned back as she misses working with the technology. Has a CS degree and proficient in coding and wants to be in a role where she can bridge her technical knowledge and skills with her love for being customer facing. She's interested in a TAM role that gives her this ability and is passionate about customer success. She wants to be at a growth company where she can make an impact and grow her career eventually into a leadership role. She's got strong drive and motivation and is a great and crisp communicator which would serve her well in the TAM role. We talked about multiple customer scenarios around handling difficult customers and customers that are needy and she had great responses on how she would handle those situations. Moving forward to next stage, technical screen.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Tyler is very entrepreneurial, likes to build. Low ego, focuses on learning, potentially has relevant connections. 

Experience in 3 buckets
1) First partner hire at rescale, employee 15. Cycled through 2 partnerships ppl before and nothing came of it. Ultimately, it was hey we integrate with a lot of people don't have relationship have a cease adn desist but a massive opp. First thing = ok, what do we want to accomplish with these partners and what is the timeline. Focused on cloud service providres, AWS MSFT and Google. Built strategic relationships, shift from opp/ad-hoc to something programmatic, xfunctional with our org and theirs so there is alignment across product, marketing, and sales. Communication can be the biggest issue bw successful partnerships. Diff goals and KPIs - no one is surprised when the other doesn't do something. Hold one another accountable - resource allocation and investment, if ask eng to participate between have a goal of this will general x pipeline and we will see y ROI. Other wide was managing 50 ISV partnerships diff approach be more programmatic and tiered. Folks can partner, important ot have ecosystem but needed a way to really navigate it in a meaningful way. As this grew, ended up building a team of 7 globally. Essentially, dedicated folks for strategic, junior resources managing pool of partners, and this happened across CSP, ISV, and started to build channel network. A lot of that was global as well given working with direct sales they were new in NA building that, we'll take every opp we can. Spearheaded the partnerships in EMEA and also in APAC. 3.5 years did that. Commission thing. = tricky. Had good system for measuring, QBRs, KPIs and attribution, at some point internal conflict of did that come from partner or sales person, but those are good problems to have. Had 2/3 pipeline from partners. Would call self builder, entrepreneuer. First person in, building business. Ecosystem. Some partnerships roles do not have an impact you can be non-core to BU adn a nice to have, would want to make sure that is NOT the case - an investment, recognize the opp. Exec leadership team.
2) Coverity - pioneer in source code analysis. Finance strategy role, working with CEO/CFO preparing for the IPO. Put that off, repurposed on two things, which was really how do we grow. Vertical strategy, product offerings, and appointed by CEO to 'buy some companies' ultimately acquired by Synopsis. Thought would be done, but after presenting framework for market - stay on adn run with it. Own end to end with M&A, with Coverity it was we could us shares/cash, Synopsis publicly traded so it was cool taking Coverity from source code analysis towards quality and more of a product marketing thing, Tyler led acquisitions that made Coverity what it is today. Interfacing with Gartner on conversations. Towards end, interested in working for a start up where heart it. So, started doing more BD things. Coverity was heavy on tech side so a bit tricky. With one tech acquired IP licensing deal, developed some security testing programs with UL. Deloitte out of spain creating cloud based testing program. From that point, understand the players - tried to buy Checkmarxx twice, knows those folks. Sonar. What attracted to Coverity then = software eating the world, will be in everything. While it may not be obvious on the surface, what gets excited about. Gizmo - everyone watches TV and movies, same mission.
3) Entrepreneur. Unique experience - owning every decision about allocating resources, become so much more mindful and thoughtful. Raised 2.5 million, team of 20, meaningful traction. In terms of leadership - set the culture, functional function leader for folks. Unique experience, and one that will serve going forward. 

Approach the role: there is always that initial earning period. 30-60-90 day plan -frameworks, but formualte that. Come in and learn the sales model, the product, what works what doesn't, where is marketing targeting. Roadmap, PP, challenges. With Coverity it was heavy tech and had to understand where you can partner. understanding product is first and foremost to understand opp. Part of excited =use case development. From a sales perspective, what is the approach what is the cycle where can partners help you win, help grow, who do we see in these deals. Initially learning the pieces and putting those together across the company. PP. Opportunities. In parallel the landscape analysis - obvious players in the ecosystem, lets map players into target apps whether new vertical SI they need to more upstream, flesh out the strategy. Ultimately, phase one is learn. Then take that.

﻿@Kemper Garnham﻿﻿@Chinmay Gaikwad﻿ I had a great call with Chushi! He has experience marketing to developers and working on technical products. Only thing, he has a final interview with another company and may receive an offer. Can I setup a HM call for tomorrow?

NYC, able to go in office.
Current role/why are you looking? 
-layoffs, not affected but re-orged. did a lot of work on the team he was on. wrapped projects. being put on new team doing new things. fantastic experience but core product and strategy moving forward was not confident with. Jira and Cloud. not much innovation. 
-case to move to cloud is not strong. want to work with a product that is in growth stage and new market.

Atlassian; cloud migration and focus on devops.
-create along with CMO to make new narrative for Jira in cloud. put out all collateral. and new features on cloud, create blog, webinar. focus on balance sheet and move from feature to feature comparison to more generational change.
-overhaul tool training narrative. seamless integration. and third party integrations. 
-digital transformation narrative. 

Pathlock; PMM for code vulnerability scanner which was company they acquired. billed as cyber security but not engaging product.
-access to data product. without hindering compliance, able to complete work. 
-take data and anonymize.
-small team and visible work.
-sales kickoff presentation. 

Emplifi; moving to NYC. cool marketing tech but least technical. social media was end user and speaking their language. 
-first break into PMM. probably should've stayed. acquired by another company.

Kloudio; PMM out of college. early stage. pre-seed to series A.
-only marketing hire. setting up hubspot and wordpress.

What is important to you in your next role?
-moving back to smaller company. more autonomy and impact. adding value to customers.
-too many processes that lower velocity
-3+ years and reap benefits of work completed. personal growth as well. 

What kind of content are you most strong at producing?
-Atl; mostly internal facing. talk tracks, objection handling. webinars and blog post for releases.
-Emp; mostly external. customer reports and white papers.
-strong written comms skills. ability to understand space and technical product to technical buyers. internal and external use cases.

Can you tell me about the last time that you had to make a difficult decision? What was it impacting? How did you come up with a solution?
-Not job of CMO to understand technical aspect or user perspective.
-more in line with CTO and strategic goals.
-knows what sets off BS alarm. have convo with PM. hear user feedback. 
-given directive, but immediately knew from conversations with PM and sales feedback that stories sound cool at high level but doesn't resonate. push back on this. 
-articulate the why. Figma project. heard narrative "SDLC doesn't start with code, starts with design." sounds good but learned from sales that it's not a problem. handoff is not a concern. entry of it would derail conversations sales would have. can exist on website but not necessarily be in the directive. notes in confluence page.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: This was a very solid interview. The candidate showed how they balance quantitative and qualitative aspects of positioning and messaging, how they learn about a target audience and their needs, and how to tie these back to higher level company goals (example at Atlassian: getting cloud-based products to 50% of company revenue). The Atlassian PMM team is very large, so there was some limit to how much ownership the candidate would have on a single project. The positioning discussion was good but was a little too product oriented, something that the candidate proactively admitted during the conversation. We discussed Apple vs Android and eBay vs industry-specific shopping apps.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Pros
- Liked that he focused on the problem at the beginning to present the pain / "why this matters", brought industry-statistics which were very compelling.
- Really quite technically detailed with the product; was able to map the technical product requirements, back to product differentiators. 
- I really liked the design of his slides! Used graphics to connect to the broader themes; made it much easier to follow. 
- Downscoped a persona for launch; well reasoned to tailor content given time. 
 - Liked how he tied in his experiences with other vulnerabilities (BleedingHeart, only fixed in 15% of mobile devices 6 months after release)
- Used a blend of qualitative metrics to understand success; worked closely with AEs to determine the success of a launch.

Cons: 
- Wasn't very concrete with metrics/data to evaluate the launch/decide on personas
- A bit on the rambly side with communication, would've preferred them to be more concise for some answers
- Seems to stay at places for ~ a  year, definitely not a no, but something to dig into

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Solid 3.

Pros:
- Really strong, nuanced problem perspective. Started broad with general "AppSec vuln management" and then shared how it's exacerbated for a particular persona "Enterprise at Scale is worse because XYZ"
- Good marketing strategy. I liked his bias for high value content and how he'd prioritize that, as the content is evergreen and reaches out to a broader group. 
- Liked how for metrics, he didn't focus solely on quantitative like other candidates. Mentioned how qualitative metrics also play a role in helping us learn about launch success.

Can be improved:
- Rambled at times, content was good but he could've been much more concise.
- Didn't seem super data driven/analytical, would've liked more understanding of concrete metrics
- Wish he framed the product features in his GTM overview in terms of problems they solved, not just the features

Something to look out for:
- He said he was excited about Semgrep about the product, but he didn't anything about security or seemed super excited.
- Also wonder if what he said was genuine, or if he's just really good at interviewing.

questions.answer: Weak yes - Most of the signs are there, but I wish they had chosen a better project because this really didn't show off a lot of the "senior making great tradeoffs" that we see from many candidates.

questions.question: What stood out about this candidate
questions.answer: Its clear they are being very intentional about finding a better fit with their next job.

questions.question: Raw notes
questions.answer:
Aaron Acosta

# Presentation
- Carbon Lighthouse
    - accurate thermodynamic models for hvac
    - mostly mech eng, small sw eng team
    - had unstructured data that should've been structured
    - scattered across a bunch of technologies
    - the limited structured data had integrity issues
- Storing ts data performantly was not possible
- No single solution for storing results

- Solution store metadata separately from the data itself
    - metadata in psql
    - data in s3 + dynamodb

- All internal tools
- Hours per week trying to find the right data, needed tools to help them work efficiently.

# How did it go
- Estimated that this would take 2-3 months (pretty accurately)
- Deadline of moving data 1-2 months after launch
- Try and measure productivity before/after
- Development milestones vs monitoring milestones
    - launching is not the end
- Start by testing the interface itself
    - store in memory, ok if the data is lost
    - want to know if its useable, works, etc
    - get something in the hands of datascientists -> does this make your work easier
- Add support for different storage in parallel (s3, dynamo)
    - use this milestone to also integrate feedback from phase 1
- Migrating some recent data for validation + performance tests
- Iterate on performance, and migrating all the historical data


- Worked with 2 other people
- One person made the decision to use dynamo rather that other ts db's
- They also were the ones who advocated for fastapi
- They were the main architect

- Unforseen performance problems
- Initial tests showed promise, but then when testing things were much slower than the older system
- Turned out it was multiple passes of serializing + deserialization.
    - GraphQL on top of Avro
    - resulted in the type checking happening multiple times
    - disable in graphql

# Technical details
- Use cases very simple, quick enough io is probably enough
- No complex aggregation, no map reduce, etc
- Casandra is what I'm used to
    - Not as friendly as dynamodb
- Tradeoffs don't seem like that big a deal
    - How hard to run / get started
- Looked at timescale
- Future proofing
    - not structured, for custom schemas
- No sharding needed, ie not mongo

- Data was timed
    - Usually on a couple of values
    - Frequent data points
- Quickly query data by building
    -

Details - yes
