Values seem authentic; many companies had values listed on public website, but they were just things that CEO and PR person wrote up as things they “maybe believe in and sound good on paper” - was very easy to see past them as disingenuous
Have spoken to semgrep folks who make it seem really real, he believes them
Has learned over the past few years that mutual respect and honesty are the most important values; thrives when he can learn from others, when he can trust and can ask questions and know that people trust him, makes him better at his job, makes him more curious and want to learn more
Wants to learn a ton, wants to dive into a new vertical, doesn’t want to feel stuck
Wants to stay growth oriented
Current company uses old tech, doesn’t have an appetite to change, questions are not a big part of the culture; wants to work with people who he’ll learn from
Wants a manager who can be his advocate, who can understand what he wants out of his career and help him get there, wants to know what it takes to get to the next level
Brought up career advancement and promotion a lot

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback and Conflict Resolution
questions.answer:
3. Extremely self aware and cares a lot about giving and receiving feedback. Has been a mentor a lot. He's been pushed into leadership roles earlier than he was ready and he doesn't think he did that well at it, but he learned a lot. Extremely trust oriented, cares a lot about helping people grow, teaching, etc. However, he's a bit uncomfortable giving constructive feedback, and I think he fluctuates a bit between conflict averse and negative conflict - he talked about learning to give feedback to execs and about the company more effectively, but I worry a *bit* that he hasn't fully internalized how to influence softly. Makes me think he's at the upper end of Senior 2, not quite staff.


“I don’t know if I’ve been a successful mentor” - tries to ask a lot
They’ve been candid with him, which makes him feel good
Haven’t been a mentor for long enough to know he’s good at it
Early in his career was the defector manager/mentor of the entire mobile team, felt like he was out of his element, was extremely hard, tried to just be available and try to ask folks a lot of questions
Was mentoring junior engineers, lots of technical mentorship, but lots of soft skills mentorship as well
Constant feedback, being open and honest
Had a junior frontend engineer, wanted to help them learn more about the system and data model so they could be better at their job; had to better understand what they wanted
Had a mentee who swore a lot in meetings with lots of people, was particularly crass, received concerns from people; had report with the person already; caught him 1:1 immediately, set expectations, gave the feedback directly but professionally, said “I don’t think the language is appropriate, and other people have found it offensive, think about what kind of relationships and reputation you want to have”
Early on in his career he was quite emotional “I prefer to think I was passionate”, wore emotions on sleeve, and this wasn’t that productive; I’d call people out in all hands meetings for unfair behavior or rules - whether or not I was right, there are effective and appropriate ways to give feedback

questions.question: Part 4: Growth
questions.answer:
Very growth oriented, wants to work somewhere where he'll be pushed, where he can ask questions and be curious and try new things. Wants to get back to being a tech lead quickly, long term wants to be a manager. I'm a little worried here that he only wants to do things that he's 100% comfortable with; he's going to have to get more comfortable being uncomfortable.


Really enjoys product development
Really enjoyed being a prod eng tech lead, setting the engineering roadmap
Likes working across teams, with customer success, sales, directly with customers
Wants to be a manager - loves teaching, finds it very fun and fulfilling; it’s not simple and there’s a lot to it that he doesn’t know, willing to work up gradually into that kind of role

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Aaron was a great partner in the interview. Before any solutioning - he asked great questions about business goals + philosophy. A concrete example is whether we view Semgrep as the Apple or Android in terms of registry functionality (walled garden or open to all for contribution). This drastically helped shape the direction we took with the product and defined the MVP.

I also appreciated Aaron's thoughtfulness around defining success. He stressed how while metrics are important, they also serve more as high level guides rather than definitive conclusions of progress. If one metric misses, it's important to question whether it is an "acceptable" miss and whether it was due to incorrect metric definition or the feature itself.

Overall, this was the best PM collab I've given (first one to a staff eng)

Raw:
Asked about goals
iOS vs Android app store philosophy
Starting with Semgrep reviewing is a great place to start (2 way door, great thought process)
UI based review system
Open PRs + fork repos (git based workflow)
What do devs get from being a registry
Separate problem into two use cases
Adding rules
Iterating on rules
Add a rule via the UI
ie Semgrep playground
Open a PR against registry (like Github)
Let’s just use github
Syncing playground + github is very simple with their GraphQL API
Contributing to semgrep requires github handle
Where we focus depends user pain points
Adding a rule feels like biggest pain point
Having metrics is important
How many rules are written
Start measuring using existing flow so we have backlog data
How many people have replaced old zip file flow with git based flow (with NPS surveys)
“Add” flow can augment “changing” flow
How do we measure success
10 creating requests / wk-> 50 / wk (50 is less important than increasing the # substantially)
NPS surveys
Ratio of new to old (ideally infinite because old should be 0)
If we don’t hit goal
Question the metric (if NPS is high but we hit 35 instead of 50)
Question the feature
How do you know when to stop (eng driven)
Working with product, important to define functional + non functional requirements (weekly)
Assign milestones to tiers (but be open to moving things in and out)

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Link to coderpad: https://app.coderpad.io/YNH2QA7M

Functionality (6 points - Yes)
+ Button on the login screen changes states from “Login” to “Create Account”
+ Candidate fixes the Create Account component to have the correct form fields
- Candidate creates some kind of state management for form validation
- Candidate successfully creates password validation rules
- Password tooltip opens onFocus and closes onBlur in the password field
+ Newly created user is stored in a User[] array
+ Candidate can log in with their new user
+ The screen displays “Hello [user name]” after logging in with a simple logout button
+ Clicking the logout button destroys the user session and shows the login screen

Code Style (4 points - Strong yes)
+ Readable (variable and function names make sense)
+ Components are broken up into smaller components (if there’s not enough time, at least acknowledge that they should split them up)
+ Follows common React conventions
+ Code follows code style of the previously written components

Development Process (2 points - Strong yes)
+ Navigated the project without too much help or guidance
+ Tested incrementally along the way
- Didn’t require too much interviewer help when writing code 

Culture
I think Aaron's culture values greatly align with ours.

Summary
Overall I had a great time talking with Aaron. Even though he didn't complete the whole assignment (he saved the hardest part for last), he talked through what he would have done next for the validation and I suspect he would have finished given a little more time.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion: Super strong 4. 

Clear communication: 4
Really went above and beyond to ask about how decided to approach resolving the PR and then gave their own thoughts. Also paused throughout each section of his feedback to make sure I was following and gave clear and concrete examples of his ideas when I dug for further explanation. 

Demonstrates expertise: 4
Things they caught: 
- arguments swapped in the backend
- flagged bad PR description
- flagged lack of tests and metrics
- flagged use of new dependency
- Promise<any> is not specific enough
- deprecated querystring package
- remove console.log
- looping through paginated API calls until a list is exhausted is not good long term
- not paginated UI (also asked explicitly what the UI looked like so they have more context as well)
- not assigning variable

Respect and collaboration: 4
They went above and beyond to ask clarifying questions to dig into why I made certain decisions for the PR and to understand beyond the context that was giving in the filed GitHub issue. It always felt like a 2-way conversation rather than being talked at throughout the entire interview. 

Distinguishing between large and small issues: 4
From the get-go, they immediately said that they left a bunch of nits in the PR, and since 

Tradeoffs: 3
Didn't really mention tradeoffs without prompting, but once I asked them what it would take for them to stamp the PR, I think they made reasonable tradeoffs between unblocking a p0 issue for a customer and still making sure we're not letting go of code quality with follow-up work. 

Tests: 4
Talks about lack of tests from the get-go and once I gave them context about the customer potentially being blocked, they were willing to accept lack of tests as long as there is proof (e.g. video recording / manual testing) that the fix was working as intended.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Updated by Brendan from a 2 to a 4 after adjusting the candidate from being aligned to a staff level role to a sr2 level role per the roundtable/below feedback 

Outcome oriented: 2
Frequently asked questions about how/what
Did not ask why, or who made the feature requests (did not seem to immediately consider PMs)
Makes good tradeoffs: 2.5
Besides the usual REST vs GraphQL, tradeoffs were not brought up unless probed
Iterative: 3
Took several steps to downscope
Made assumptions about requirements (didn't ask to confirm requirements)
Cross stack: 2
Pushed to have as much frontend logic in the backend as possible (without providing any reasoning)
A lot of the frontend-to-backend logic was unnecessary and would over-complicate the backend/API


Overall, I do not see this candidate at the staff engineer level. However, at the senior level, I would strongly recommend hiring. The feedback for this candidate is strictly based on evaluation at the staff engineer level, and most of the pitfalls were for driving the product without any guidance. Provided that the candidate had guidance from a staff engineer and a PM, I think the candidate would excel (i.e. senior-level).

questions.question: Raw notes
questions.answer:
Sal Olivares

- Token revocation service for github/gitlab
- Github lets you provide a regex
- when they find a thing, they post to you
- Ensure that paying customer have critical end user data
- Exposing the API key means customer data can be intercepted
- Be able to protect them when their keys are leaked
- Considered lambda
- Internal tooling already supported deploying on EKS 


# How did it go
- Quarter long project
- Design doc
- Make planning estimates
- Complexity x Unknowns
- If we estimated 3 days, but took a week -> pay attention, let manager know etc.
- Break it down so that tickets are able to be finished in a sprint
- Deploying new infra
- Get security involved
- Get infra invovled
- Because it's public facing, sets of extra reviews.
- Design doc is living doc
- Just because its in the plan doesn't mean thats what we build
- Customer feedback via
- hard in this case 
- what would be nice: hearing anything about it
- used other SaaS companies as a model (most revoke so we took the same approach)


- For the purposes of learning, the infra stuff [to break off for a junior]
- expect that most junior folks are familiar with node, can add an endpoint
- want them to learn the infra, since it's complex
- I learnt a lot from this, want people to have the same step up for success

- Blockers
- Gitlab
- One thing to have a dependency on a team internally, yet alone at another company
- Gitlab hadn't fleshed out how their security story would work
- Work with them, try and do what github is already doing.
- Email their PM regularly
- Shipped with just github initially, go back to gitlab later.
- Other example, how do we secure this properly?
- Design + process blockers.

# Technical
- Lambda vs EKS
- Background, we expect a very small number of these requests ( <10 per month )
- Save money by not running infra constantly
- Logic is small, self contained -> much less work that spinning up all this infra.
- No existing tooling at their end
- no docs, tools to spin up stuff.
- not a great devx, no ci, etc

- How is lambda operationally different from k8s
- Where do the logs go?
- Metrics exist, just in different places  
- Running lambda locally is really hard
- serverless framework sort of mirrors the infra
- want tooling that make the local dev experience useful

questions.answer: More like a 3.5. The main reason why I didn't give them a four was because they made a bunch of assumptions for the sake of time, and for candidates who I've given a 4 to in the past, they were able to not make assumptions about the URLs retrieved and use libraries to parse the domain, etc. versus making naive operations to check for valid URLs.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall Sal did really well on the mock review. 

He correctly called out all of the issues on the frontend, and even pointed out some stuff that I didn't know. 

On the backend, he also caught a lot of the issues and suggested implementing cursor pagination rather than offset pagination. When asked about it, he was able to explain that cursor pagination was and why it was better in a way I thought was easy to understand. 

My only real (minimal) concern about Sal are that he doesn't really know Python, so there will definitely be some ramp up time while he learns Python and learns our code base. However, it seems like he has enough practical knowledge to be able to adapt quickly and contribute to the team.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Highly articulate, especially about frontend and API design
Strong focus on iteration and quick release cycle
Brought up a few tradeoffs -- favored options based on speed/engineering effort and reducing complexity
Kept conversation high-level (in terms of architecture) by default, "zoomed in" when needed. The candidate often jumped in to provide context when necessary without prompting

Overall I think the candidate would be a strong hire.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Really enjoyed my conversation with Swati. She's a HUSTLER. In every role she's taken from Accenture to her current role as a technical support engineer at Nirvana, she's always looking to take on new challenges that help her grow and she's not someone who is afraid of being uncomfortable. Very good communicator with a warm personality. She has hands-on experience working on CI/CD platforms and led the Jenkins implementation at Accenture and also has experience with Github actions and Azure pipelines from her other roles. She comes from a software engineering background and has experience working in Python, Java, Go and is very familiar with the developer workflows. She's driven, motivated and looking to join a smaller company where she can experience professional growth. She eventually wants to transition to being a solutions engineer long term which is great  and exciting to hear. She would be a great team player who would demonstrate ownership of her support cases and also be someone who will take on additional projects and initiatives to help us scale the support organization. Moving her to technical screen.

Some info about the CI config individually is a little off. (i.e. secretes, container images).

Didn't know about how to block rules/where we would apply the blocking rules. 

Overall, she knew about the process of onboarding and what to do after scanning, but with little specifics will need a little more expertise on that.

Knows about the ellipses.

SHE USED THE TUTORIAL +

Overall, one of the better rule writing sessions that I have had. Clearly went through the research and work to prepare for this. Had some little syntax yaml issues but overall was clear on her theoretical approach.

Has a strong connection with trying to unblock customers. Also is very willing on looking ahead to, when we solve a customer issue, what should be the next step to avoid that in the future.

Clearly did her research, she clearly went through most portions of the onboarding and UI. Had some hiccups when trying to understand how Semgrep is configured on a scanning basis (i.e. how things blocks (I don't think she investigated within the Rule Board)).

I really loved how she practiced for this and clearly looks like she took the time to prepare. Had some small hiccups with regards to some of the product.

Has interest in becoming an SE. May be worth getting in touch with one of them.

(I didn't ask this question because she answered it with the one above. This will instead be about the high stress situation, other interview question)

Nirvana ingests data from Samsara (e.g GPS data, miles) in order to make accurate insurance quotes. On the day of many customer's renewal, their data ingestion pipeline failed, causing many customers to not be able to have quotes. 

Pros:
- Articulated why this problem was high revenue impacting, and highly stressful.
- Showed a lot of urgency to rally the right stakeholders to solve problem
- Dove into a lot of telemetry herself in order to understand what's wrong, going as far as double-checking data engineer's work when it didn't work the first time
- Prioritized the issues she had to deal with by RICE (reach, impact, confidence, and effort)
- Like earlier example, dogfooded and followed-up with customer to make sure expectations were managed.

Cons:
- Story was also a bit over the place, had to ask follow-ups to hone in on the details
- Didn't really go too deep over how they communicated with the customer during the issue process, only until the end.
- Not sure if they did any technical work to fix the issue, mostly went to engineers after identifying problem

questions.question: Tell me about a time when you took the initiative to improve a process or procedure in your previous role without being prompted by your manager?
questions.answer:
(4/5) I didn't think the 'Measures of Success' were super strong, but everything else was good.

Her company Nirvana is a trucking insurance company, people upload data/documents in order to get insurance quotes.

Pros: 
- A quote from I heard that I liked: "Yeah lots of things I did was not assigned, at the end of the day, it's the customer's experience, focus on making their experience better."
- Used their equivalent of fullstory, saw that a lot of users were running into issues while uploading sheets, the in-product experience was poor
- Took initiative and dug deep into user behavior, noticed key information was being missed, went back to product team and suggested highlighting them in-app.
- Meticulously tracked the bug tickets, and once fixed, dogfooded to make sure it's fixed before going back to customers

Cons:
- Said that it was a painful experience for customers, but I would've loved to hear more on why.
- Rambled a bit, had to ask follow-ups to make sure I was following story correctly.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I enjoyed my conversation with Kenzie and am a yes. What stood out was her knack to act quickly and put feedback into action. Kenzie provided two examples where she received feedback and not only did she take immediate action, she did so with grit. 

In one example, she was asked to level up her technical aptitude. Kenzie took it upon herself to go onto LI Learning to learn the fundamentals of coding so she could better recruit technical candidates. She took 3 different coding 101 courses after which she set up meetings with internal software engineers and asked for them to explain technical terms she'd learned in the training in their own words. Then she did mock calls with them. 

In the other feedback example Kenzie was asked to focus on one org to recruit. Up until then she was picking up open recruiting req's regardless of the org which meant she was recruiting everyone from technical candidates, to fellow recruiters, to finance candidates. Upon receiving the feedback she worked for one org for 8 weeks and ended up filling all of the roles she was given and believes this was due to her sticking with just that org for those 8 weeks. 

I have two concerns, neither of which I think are too concerning. 
1) She got into the weeds on a couple answers and lost me. Had she been more succinct I would have better followed her response. 
2) I wish she'd shown up in something more professional than a flannel. Hey, we're a relaxed company when it comes to dress code, but it's an interview and it felt like she dressed for a 1:1.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Candidate is light on years of experience but overall it was a solid interview. They have a clear sense of where they want to drive their career and the path to get there. The candidate is keen to learn and grow their skills, and showed examples of tenacity and persistence which I think could transfer well to the BDR role.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
3.5. Excellent communication & reflection on culture. 


Culture Add Interview Template


This interview is not about skills or aptitude – it's about culture.
Mutual & bidirectional, with three phases.


Phase 1: Culture (10min)
(can repeat these for more and more companies in history)
* How would you describe the culture of your current/past company - as an ethnographer?
At salesloft: 
Positive : glass half-full
Bias towards action
Inclusive, whole-self (not having to put on a persona)
* What do you admire about its culture?
* What would you change?
Honesty piece - sugarcoat things, mask the shortcomings of decisions we've made. Blend those together even more. Blend the honesty with choosing what we're going to do about it.
* Why is the culture that way?
If I had to guess – it's a leadership thing. They need to be driven from the top-down. The more that's the case the more the folks at the top of the org live and breath them.
Some core values resonate more than others with the teams. 
So for instance the glass-half-full interpretation 
Having the leadership live & breath. Self-evaluation, constant feedback loop. 


Phase 2: Our culture (10min)


Our Work Has Impact
* How do you know you are effective in your role? (Not in the "this is what I put on my performance packet" sense, but in the "I had a hard day at work and I'm laying awake, trying to fall asleep" sense)
Are we helping the revenue team hit their goals? Should see that our work is making their work more efficient and easier.
Stakeholder perspective – hearing a feedback and knowing that they enjoy working with you.
2ndarily, there's a cross collaboration – are you interne
Personally? Small potatoes of that answer – I get a lot of value in seeing that we as  team are marching towards a specific goal.  Seeing progress, things get resolved. Feels good to solve a problem.
– usually don't get to these –
* How would you measure your effectiveness in ${role at r2c}
* How would you quantify that? 
* What would you do that no one else is doing?
* What would your top priority be at a startup?


Mission Driven
* Why a startup and why this startup specifically?
Environment in my experience where you learn the most. Always trying to learn more, gain skillset,s meet amazing people. In a startup environment you create relationships that are different from large, established company. 
Jodie reached out to me. Everyone I've spoken to, it's been an enjoyable experience. Great two-way convo. Lots of questions & great answers from folks. Also think appsec is a great space. 
– usually don't get to these –
* What is your next job – either at r2c or elsewhere?


Honesty 
* Myself and the co-founders want to be held to the highest standards of integrity and hold the team to them too.
* We believe this is the best way to run a business (creates long-term thinking, lifelong relationships) but even if we could make more money by being dishonest we would not–it's a fundamental value.
* We want people who also won't put a price on integrity.
* Tell me about a time you gave or received radically candid feedback
Rewind a couple years – my first two years at salesloft, we did not have a feedback stream in place. 
"We're going to fill this role with someone else. An area of opportunity for you is to fine-tune the nature of your project & proactivity" – initially I was upset. 


Curveballs (usually don't get to these)
* hardest you ever worked and why?
* what are company cultures you admire


What is your leadership philosophy?


Phase 3: (10min)


Questions for me?

questions.answer: {in part 2}

questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: What stood out about this candidate?
questions.answer:
General Rubric: Yes + 
Overall: Do they communicate at the right level of detail (e.g. do they provide examples and details when asked)? Do they refrain from rambling? Do they ask questions? Do they pause for questions or otherwise ensure their content is landing? - Strong Yes. Did a good job with examples and asked clarifying questions to ensure they were answering what I asked
Communication: Are they respectful? - Yes

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback and Conflict Resolution
questions.answer:
Rubric: Strong Yes
Communication: Are they skilled at delivering feedback? - Strong yes. Talked about how to build relationships and approach feedback well. Cares about the people side and how to adjust (and we vs I)
Communication: Do they treat feedback as a gift? Can they give good examples of how feedback has helped them? - Strong yes. Understood and could articulate this feedback in both examples and how they grew as part of giving feedback 
Communication: Do they treat feedback as a gift? Can they give good examples of how feedback has helped them? - Strong yes. Lots of different mentorship examples and chooses to tailor it to their mentee. Could talk through the differences between mentorship for different people. Also talked through how they mentor both technically and socially 

Raw Notes:
Tell me about your experience mentoring others. What do you think makes you a successful mentor?
* Mentoring mostly with interns, onboarding and new grads, early in the career
* Enjoy mentoring and seeing people grow
* Start by building a relationship and getting to know their style of working
* I try to ask or get a sense on their style of work and then do my best to mold to their working style
* Will pair on certain projects or be there to give them advice 
* For mentees with more experience, I would give them space to think about the project first and then give advice or guidance 
* For those without experience, I would be more structured with how to do x, y, and z
* Regularly meet up with them and do code reviews
* Always encourage them to reach out to other people on the team. Find new hires tend to be afraid to approach people so I help to create those relationships (this is my opinion. Why don’t you also get feedback from the team channel) and gives them visibility 
* Chat about projects and them and career wise

Tell me about a time when you had to give a difficult piece of feedback to someone else
* Pairing with a mentee who was really new in their tech career. Had just graduated from bootcamp and they had an internship program for bootcamp grads
* He was a very through person so it would take him longer to look through every solution 
* I was also learning to give more direct feedback
* I knew he was trying really hard but was taking a while and I was encouraging him to ask for help earlier
* Manager had some concerns that the internship will end soon and we need to see some progress faster
* Realized I needed to give him more direct feedback on the project and keep a closer eye on him
* Sit him down and explained that he was taking a bit longer. This is the goal for the project and the why
* Created that more clear goal and how we can achieve those 1 by 1
* Challenging cause he was a kind person but he was a bit sensitive so I wanted to give him the feedback in a way that wouldn’t bring him down but encourage him 

Tell me about a piece of feedback you received that helped you grow?
* Working on an engineering improvement ticket
* Adding some monitoring and it was early on for remote work
* Didn’t communicate really clearly what the goals were and what we wanted to finish by which timeline 
* Didn’t communicate on what needed to be focused on so my manager talked to me about it during performance review
* Could have improved on project management (talking through updating stakeholders etc)
* I may be too focused on dev work and forget to do

questions.question: Raw notes
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: (5 min) Initial Presentation
questions.answer:
SWE at TechsIQ. Start-up, got acquired about a year and a half ago by Relativity. After acquisition, we could use other tools that Relativity has been using. Worked on privacy team.  
 
TechsIQ had two main products - Privilege (clients are law firms, helps with going over documents and figuring out privileged information). Privacy team focused on data breach solution. Businesses may have data breach and need to figure out what got leaked. Solution my team provides helps them ingest documents, figure out PI information, and then has normalization/linking algorithm to figure out this person and that person are the same. 


Business problem: This was for internal/engineering use (and also customer support). Not sending metrics to NewRelic and didn’t really have visibility into what’s going on. I worked on adding application metrics for our product. NewRelic is like DataDog.

We have >10 micro-services. Focus was not to add this to all micro-services. 
 
Another part is to monitor the 3rd-party dependencies. 
 
For our services, focused on java services first (we also have python services). Added some parts to our java service library so if we had additional micro services there in the future, they could get added. 
 
Last part is monitoring service to monitor e.g. the pipeline of a particular customer clicking “run” (could take anywhere from like an hour to a day depending on their data).

questions.question: (10 min) How did the project go?
questions.answer:
Expected it to take a quarter, it took about a quarter plus a week or two weeks to fully wrap up everything. Started with design doc. Proposed some options and tradeoffs. Initially me working on it, later on another engineer joined in. I worked on the pieces to unblock the other engineer. Worked on monitoring service first, then the micro-services monitoring, and finally the dependency metrics collection. 
 
Blockers: playing around with OpenTelemetry SDK, the examples online were a bit simple. Took longer than expected, had to piece together the source code and docs. 
 
Why did you start with the java services? We have more java services than python. Also our team is working on new micro service architecture and all the new ones are in java. All the java services are more important than the python services. 
 
How did you decide what to break off for the other engineer? Intermediate level, could work quite independently but also kind of new to the team. I tried to have her focus on just one area so started working on Redis queue length collector because I already set up an example she could follow. Also because this part doesn’t have other dependencies.

questions.question: (10 min) Technical details
questions.answer: Some of the configs with OpenTelemetry SDK were not obvious. The way it collects it is either accumulation or delta (always report latest value, or report the diff from last time). Accumulation version made NewRelic more complicated, so wanted to change this mode. Naming was different across languages.

questions.question: What stood out about this candidate
questions.answer:
The candidate was very proactive about telling me to interrupt her if I had questions and was great about answering my questions when I did so. I really liked the answer to how they thought about breaking down the work, they talked about downscoping out the python services without me prompting them about what they downscoped, and the scope of the project was very impressive - one quarter, onboarded another engineer who was new to the team. I really liked about how they talked about what piece of the work to break off for an intermediate engineer: 

How did you decide what to break off for the other engineer? Intermediate level, could work quite independently but also kind of new to the team. I tried to have her focus on just one area so started working on Redis queue length collector because I already set up an example she could follow. Also because this part doesn’t have other dependencies.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Clear communication: 4/4
Mentioned clarifying PR description to add reasoning why we picked specific solution
Suggested PR template
Demonstrates expertise: 4/4
Caught all of: arguments swapped in backend, user could still OOM the backend due to lack of backend argument validation, heavy code re-use in frontend, design choice to specify page size in backend questionable
had some questions about consistency of ordering for querying that I would maybe expect a candidate to know (or have looked up) but not deducting because she caught everything
Respect and collaboration: 4/4
Great at making the conversation a back and forth - asking if I had any questions, asking for my reasoning. Because of this I was able to talk about some of the more interesting issues. i.e. instead of saying "we should really paginate this on the frontend" she asked if there was a reason why I didn't implement that, so I got to talk a bit about the frontend filtering predicament
Distinguishing between large and small issues: 3/4
Only not a 4/4 because she didn't bring up the number of issues, but talked about metrics around the number of requests pagination will not require of the backend, since we're loading all of them at once
When starting the interview she said that she would prioritize important fixes off the bat, just brushed over other things since she saw in the ticket that this was high priority
Talked about time tradeoffs with testing as well and how we could do thorough tests given our testing environment constraints
Do they catch and talk about lack of tests: 4/4
I was impressed by the way this candidate adapted their testing strategy around this outage. She accepted that this is a hotfix and that it's a high priority that this get out to the impacted user.
She suggested testing in production by using a feature flag and testing for that one user. Also wondered if we had some kind of way to test with the production db but the local frontend (pretty much describing yarn start:dev)

Overall had a great chat with this candidate. Will note that the interview ran long (scheduled for 30 min, interview went for 45, told the candidate I had time so she asked questions to bring us to around 50 minutes). Follow up: didn't get any read on culture fit

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, I would rate this candidate at a 3. I would recommend hiring at a Senior I backend role, contingent on having mentoring resources. 

Outcome Oriented: 3
  - Very conscious of the user experience
Makes good tradeoffs: 3
  - Tradeoff between user experience and reliability/security

Iterative: 2
  - Candidate would overthink the problems
  - Initially overcomplicated the solutions and got bogged down in the details
  - Candidate was able to recover after I asked some probing questions (e.g. how would we determine optimal thresholds for timeouts, What if we define the "optimal" threshold from the user's perspective, etc)
Cross-stack: 3
  - Admitted that they are not very comfortable with frontend (but this is a backend role) and wanted to avoid the frontend questions
  - Was able to discuss at a high level the interaction between frontend and backend; had an understanding of the necessary infrastructure

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I'm pretty sure they're senior 1, probably at the upper end, and *just* starting to take on senior 2 scope for the very first time. My recommendation would be to hire at senior 1, but not at senior 2, there's too much risk.

Talked through two main projects - she rambled a lot and didn't get as much breadth as I would have liked.

First project was integrating in a new acquisition and basically combining the functionality of the newly acquired product into the existing product. She said this was by far the most complex and ambiguous project she'd ever done, on the scope of 1-2 quarters, and it started a month or two ago. She's breaking it down into small pieces, doing performance testing, de-risking technical approaches, all of the good things you'd want from the tech lead. But it also sounds like it's going rather slowly, and I get the sense that she's still trying to find her footing; she deferred a lot to other senior engineers who are making choices around database technologies and stuff like that.

Second project was from at Segment, she talked about fixing a mission critical vulnerability in the AWS s3 exporter. This is pretty squarely a senior 1 project (if anything, maybe a little less): self contained, well scoped, more a matter of choosing an approach, validating, and executing it.

She's very excited about security and building security tools, I think she is really excited about us. She needs some help on communication, she rambled a *lot*. And she was pretty open and honest that the first project is a stretch project for her, it's the most complex thing she's tried, and she's still working through it. She wants to go somewhere where she can grow a lot technically.

Finally: she emphasized testing and performance testing *a lot*. Much more than other engineers I've interviewed. It might have been b/c of her rambling communication style, but also, the HM should make sure that that's something they want to emphasize on the team, otherwise it might not be a great fit.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Good communicator, good leader, solid decision making framework. Would be a solid senior engineer on the team. The project was 1 quarter in length and not that complex, so didn't get a ton of signal on senior 1 vs senior 2, but strong yes on senior.

questions.question: (5 min) Initial Presentation
questions.answer:
4. Exceptionally well described problem, very clear need, comparison to other existing things we've probably used, and a good overview of the solution. Built a proxy so that they wouldn't share real phone numbers or emails between customers, hosts, and 3rd party campsites:

 
Email and SMS proxies to protect camper info
Started integrating with 3rd party campground providers
Wanted to prevent these integrations from collecting camper information and reaching out to them directly
Wanted to protect camper information, not share camper information itself
Built a proxy system in between campers and the 3rd party; fake email/sms

questions.question: (10 min) Technical details
questions.answer:
3. Solid understanding of the technical problems, made good choices that led to good outcomes. Not a 4 mostly because of lack of signal, there weren't a ton of really hard choices to make, it seemed like a really straightforward project technically.

 
Client phone <-> Twilio
Client email <-> Mailgun
Webook controllers for each, inbound and outbound
Twilio provides a proxy service off the shelf, the easiest thing was just to use their service
Needed to integrate with in-house messaging system, wanted to be able to control what was happening, plus their service was in beta at the time
In the beginning, cost wasn’t front of mind, but toward the end it became important; Twilio cost almost $100k/month, dedicating a phone number, and naively it would cost 400-500k/month, so had to make things more complicated to share numbers so that the costs wouldn’t explode
Most of data is in Postgres, made it a nice choice if we could just continue in postgres
Thought about using Redis because access is much quicker b/c in memory; but if redis goes down you lose all of the numbers (that had happened before)

questions.question: (10 min) How did the project go?
questions.answer:
4. Very straightforward and clear project management. Worked with management/pm to get it prioritized, wrote an RFC and got feedback, then broke the project into chunks and streams and helped more junior folks get it done. Tested it a bunch, rolled it out well, adapted to some obstacles as they came. Had a very clear process for getting it done, shipped it on time and by eoq.

 
Started working with management and PM to figure out whether or not to actually do it
Wrote an RFC, determine whether it was possible, how expensive it would be, could they save money?
Presented RFC to whole eng team, got feedback from managers and staff engineers
6-8 weeks of dev work with a couple of other junior engineers
Tested it from multiple devices, tested campers and hosts
Launched to beta, had caught almost everything, so beta rollout was really smooth
Broke down the work into two streams, a third stream if you count the foundation (data migration and some architecture documents) - email and sms sides were easy to be split off

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
One of the best interviews I've ever done. Almost everything he said seemed like a perfect fit culturally for Semgrep and also specifically for my team (with the exception of loving Ruby, which we don't use, but I checked with him and he said that is fine and he is happy using python too). 
- Loves mentorship and eager to do more of it
- Values collaboration, communication, etc.
- Motivated by security mission, wants to work on a product he cares about
- Really wants to work across the stack, not get stuck in one or the other

questions.question: Part 1: Why Semgrep and values alignment
questions.answer:
In Miami on vacation, based out of Virginia usually, used to live in SF but travels a lot now with remote work. 


From hip-camp as senior engineer, promoted and then laid off. Leading projects around Ruby on Rails and React in the frontend. Built an SMS proxy so people aren’t texting from their own numbers. Built that from scratch. 


Moving old javascript legacy checkout system over to react and graphQL. 


Before that, at Checker, API-heavy company, so a little bit of frontend but not much there. Predicting whether background checks would pass, working with ML engineers. 


Security is something that’s sometimes in back of head and not at forefront but it should be. Hipcamp checkout flow thinking hard about not leaking information, utmost importance. 


Work style I’ve heard so far. What if new language and nobody knows about it? Excited to learn.


I feel out roles based on human connection. Feel able to connect with the team and work well with the team. Hipcamp felt really connected with the team, but tech stack wasn’t exactly what I was looking for. 


Specific behaviors: eager for collaboration and being willing to jump on a call. Frequent communication, lots of RFC’s, dedicated time to talk. Makes remote easier having time on calendar to communicate.

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback Mentorship and Sponsorship
questions.answer:
Mentorship: one of my favorite topics, one of my favorite things to do. Best way to learn is by teaching. The longer my career goes the more I see that’s true. At checker, was mentoring someone else for the first time, it really leveled me up. Had to communicate what is going well and not. 


Working with ruby for a long time, able to share where we can improve, people started asking me questions. My manager asked me “you have all this ruby knowledge, how can we scale that? Why don’t you work on a tutorial or guide?” Got to write like a 16 chapter guide, led a weekly class. 


How would you feel about working somewhere that doesn’t have ruby? Ruby and I will always be together, but in 9-5 sometimes to get away. Use python as well, using it on a project I’m working on for NMT. Picked up python and flask, built quick API. 


Feedback to help someone grow? Yeah, tough conversation with one of my PM’s. Didn’t like direction project was heading. Refactor checkout was long, being careful, planned it for short-medium time. Took much longer, so PM was pushing for full switch over. 


I’m a more iterative person, ship quick, get feedback, iterate. PM didn’t want to release something where people were in split experience between old and new. I didn’t think we could get it out on time without doing that. I was pushing for it as a necessary evil. Communication and saying “why” was really important.

questions.question: Part 3: Growth
questions.answer:
More mentoring, room for me to not just learn but also teach. Management or director level eventually. Communication doesn’t come easy, so want to continue to foster that and get better at it. Not somewhere my voice is heard and I won’t be stuck in one part of the stack.


Working in a company and product I believe in.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, I'd want to work with Daniel. they demonstrated a strong product sense, and ability to empathize with the user, define metrics, and provide clear estimates.

Pros: Solid product sense, asked pretty detailed questions about the product.
- "Generally in favor of ripping things out, code isn't free. Can we take a week now to roll it back, will it be >40 hours in support later? At hipcamp we added a lot of features that didn't pan out, something that affect 1% of users, resulted in  20% of the support tickets."

- Proposed solid metrics: "See how many times someone started to publish but didn't finish, see how many rules were written, how many [findings] it flagged. If our public rules find more/less than Semgrep rules, we should investigate"

- Clarified team size & estimation system (points/days) before providing an estimate. Clearly walked through what technical and interpersonal constraints can increase/decrease an estimate (tech debt, vacations, PR reviews, how a migration is setup within a company)

Cons: A bit hard to follow on communication (introduction was a hard to follow & some parts of the MVP description) but nothing too much of a concern.


Question:  Curious how others felt on the technical portion - he didn't get into too much detail about how he'd technically solve the problem, he talked high-level about which endpoints & components, but no more detail. Fine for my interview, but wanted to checkin.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion: A 3 for senior, but flagging that I didn't get solid signal on the collaboration/mentorship front. Details in the section below, but it was mostly due to how it felt like a one-way conversation where they told me what to fix/improve on versus them also understanding why I implemented things a certain way. I'd be curious what others thought of them in terms of mentorship as well since the signal on collaboration / mentorship was a bit low. 

Clear communication: 3

Demonstrates expertise: 3
They immediately suggested from the get go that there was probably an easier quick fix for this, which was to surface X amount of findings that was large enough because the user probably wouldn't scroll through to the end of 50k findings anyway, which I thought was a good callout in terms of a quick ix. When I called out that what would happen if you had to access a particular finding that wasn't returned in the first X number of findings, they said that doing this would be just a quick fix to unblock the customer, and we should push an actual follow-up with a more maintainable solution within the next few days. Other things they caught included: 
- duplicated code on the frontend
- not paginated UI
- Promise<any> is not specific enough
- console.log
- lack of tests and metrics
- argument for limit is wrong
- postgres doesn't guarantee order of objects returned from the database

Respect and collaboration: 2.5
The candidate was respectful, but rather than feeling like it was a 2 way conversation where they tried to understand why the PR was implemented a certain way or understand why I as the supposed person who implemented the PR implemented it in this particular way, it felt more like I was being talked at while they explained their PR review feedback. However, when I did stop them to ask clarifying questions, they did a good job of answering and explaining, but they didn't really pause and check in if I was following at all, which is why they weren't quite a 3 in this category. 

Distinguishing between large and small issues: 3
They called out the high level issues that needed to be fix, which were: parse-link-header using a vulnerable version, considering a quicker fix to surface only X amount of findings and how there are no tests. After making sure they covered why they considered these 3 issues the biggest issues, they then proceeded to the nits/questions they left in the PR. 

Tradeoffs: 3
I got pretty good signal in belief in shipping fast when they suggested a "quicker fix", and when I asked them specifically what it would take for them to stamp the PR, they just flagged the security issue along with the swapped arguments bug that must be fixed before they would let the PR through. 

Lack of tests: 3.5
They immediately flagged lack of tests from the get go and does offer flexibility in not having tests if this was a P0. However, they only put it in the context of the outage only when I prompted them about this being a P0 issue blocking a customer.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Graded at Staff eng level (based on the meeting invite)

1. Outcome-oriented: 2
    This was difficult to get a signal on without probing (which in itself is not exactly a good thing). Most of the candidate's questions were focused on Semgrep rule syntax, not on the architecture of the playground. I had to nudge the candidate to get back to the architecture a few times. The candidate did not drive the discussion towards an overall architecture/design, but rather on technology choices.

2. Makes good tradeoffs: 1
    While the candidate did bring up alternatives, they didn't once mention tradeoffs without my probing. The candidate drilled down on a few technologies (graphql), offering a few benefits, but did not provide an cons without probing. Similarly, they did not bring up any alternative technologies or approaches without probing.


3. Iterative: 2
    The candidate gave an adequate task breakdown. However, the candidate assumed that they would be implementing it independently (didn't ask about eng resources). This led the candidate to plan/estimate the project as if all tasks were being implemented sequentially.
   
4. Cross-stack: 3
    Candidate was able to provide an e2e architecture. They seemed especially confident discussing the frontend.

Overall, I would not recommend hiring this candidate at the staff level. I could see this candidate being successful at a senior 1 level (possibly senior 2).

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I really enjoyed my conversation with Brian and believe he would be a great candidate who brings a combination of strengths that would be valuable to where we are and what we are looking for. Comes across as a strong people leader who is passionate about customer support , helping customers get the value they are looking for and helping support reps see career paths for personal and professional growth. Brians risen from the lowest ranks of being a support agent to front line manager to heading global technical support at Amplitude. He joined Amplitude when there were 3 support agents and scaled and grew the team to 20+ agents with having multiple managers and program managers that he was leading. Grew support coverage from 16X5 to 24X5 and launched premium support offering of 24X7 for critical issues. He also had ownership of building/maintaining the help docs, and developer docs and building out the community at Amplitude which helped them lower 70% of the community ticket volume without impacting lead conversion. He also has some experience managing TAM's in his previous role before Amplitude. Has experience with all the standard support ticketing platforms and comes across as someone very analytical and data driven in his overall methodology and thought process. Brian's a very strong career champion for his team and had the lowest attrition rate and highest engagement scores at Amplitude and walked through how he's personalized career growth plans for support agents based on their strengths, what they are passionate about and what brings them energy at work. Brians gone through the scaling challenges of going from a startup to post IPO journey, has helped lower headcount needs for support hiring based on gaps he's identified and helping deflect/lower case volume and is passionate about building and scaling a world class support organization.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Very strong candidate. Good at individual support level, leading people, and the data analytics to run a large department. I thoroughly enjoyed my conversation.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I really liked Brian's 100 day plan and his overall thought process in approaching and building out the plan. Has very strong communication skills and it was very easy to follow and understand the different aspects of the plan he put together. He had a clear plan around getting to know the team, the product, the extended teams. Also acknowledged the need for driving quick wins in the first 60 days to build trust and momentum with the team. He also had a good mix of understanding insights and what's working well and what can be improved from internal and external stakeholders and placed importance of meeting customers regularly which was good to hear. Passionate about leading people and contributing to the community which came through in multiple questions around how he approached career conversations and team recognition. I am a strong YES for Brian and moving him forward.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Brian had the most organized and comprehensive model around post-sales engagement I've seen yet. The preso content was well organized and his presentation was solid.  He had good discovery early on and related some of his points to our answers.

I also liked his examples of incorporating in product deflection and product based experience metrics (like chart shares in amplitude) for quantifying impact and customer health.  Bonus points for holiday team culture.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
One of the best chronological walk throughs I've done since Semgrep. Thoughtful reflection on Brian's change from a career in accounting/finance to teaching himself how to code and moving to New York on a whim. He wanted to move into support, took the leap at Foursquare, crashed on his cousin's couch. Interpreted this to be someone who's willing to take risks, work hard, and make things happen.

Taught himself to code for Optimizely job, initially didnt get it, but stayed in touch and kept emailing the team to share his progress. Lots of grit. Perserved and committed to make it happen. Once there, revamped onboarding, known as the person who asked questions, wanted to make things easier for others.

Great tenure at places, humble, hard working. We'd be lucky to have Brian join us.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
﻿@Adam Berman﻿ Had a great convo with Misha. I didn't dig into why they would leave Sentry after an 11 months stint in a similar role so that may become a flag later. They met Clint a while back and has been following Semgrep, so is opportunistically interested. They also have an onsite coming up elsewhere which is why something doesn't add up. 

Overall, their experience is in line with what we would like them to do. They have build programs and teams around security. Is hands on and possess good leadership skills and management. I will move forward buy may want to dig into current role.

Where are you located? 
-based in SF but may be remote


Current role/why are you looking? 
-opportunistically interested. 
EM at sentry. head of detection and response. building from ground up. 5 people on the team. challenge with team, as EM straddling work as lead and EM. don't leave hands-off portion.
-know how to scale a security program at Cloudflare. joined right after IPO.
-at Sentry; had a couple eng. able to hire for team. skills to optimize for. 
-at Equinix; first role and small team. able to work on firewalls, configurations. KPI and OKRs for CIO. new grad and lot of opportunities.
-at SN; federal efforts. large scale infra and attacks. P0 responder. and contact at gov. 


MGR; able to manage at Cloudflare first. like to be part of their success. 


What is important to you in your next role?
-similar to Sentry. straddling lead and people manager. Sentry is late stage and like the startup environment to add structure and process. enabling growth.
-move towards being more of a people manager.


Can you walk me through the area of security engineering you focus on? (Looking for more AppSec focus vs. InfoSec. Need direct coding experience; writing scripts, building arrays, strategy. At least 30-50% hands-on.)
-at Sentry; detection and response is what hired to do. vulnerability response is also something she's taking on. navigating through incidents. 
-has been in ops roles at SN. familiar with measuring impact of teams. 
-developer relations and end point when she first started. 
-launched bug bounty program recently. how to evaluate is what she is tasked with. how to address these vulnerabilities. 


Have you built a program from scratch or taken over an existing program?
-both. taken existing and find where to go in a year. work backwards and describe to leadership.
-more of a luxury with new programs. there are existing scale issues that it would need to adapt. 
-building DNR from scratch at Cloudflare. identify bad things before they happen. SMB to enterprise customers. focused on building detections for environments. 
-challenge was priorities of business, ie performance of services, was up there with security. had to write detection that wouldn't interrupt SRE. memory and CPU usage. trade offs between team and business. heavily scrutinized. psychological safety at company culture level. 
-1st year was the same team. more operational load so hire 4 detection engineers. kind of like SOC. spin up team and one of hm for it. 


What is your management style? How have you been able to get the most out of your team members? What is your greatest success story?
-hands off manager. trust team to get tasks and projects completed. collaborative and get people talking to each other.
-need a time for security eng to talk to each other. opportunity to think and help each other. started this last quarter and continuing.
-at core, understand people and what gives them satisfaction. able to track work and metrics. psychologically, ways to stay motivated. 
-understand how they work and how the like to work. collaborative nature and friendly adds. personable and able to describe how they feel to make impact greater.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Misha is a terrific candidate. Growth oriented, hands-on technical, cares a ton about people, wants to see her impact on the business, cares about the company culture a lot. Only thing that doesn't make her a 5/4 is that she's on the low end of management experience.

questions.question: Management and Leadership - Notes
questions.answer:
Weak 3. Not a ton of experience as a people manager - manages 1 person, and only has for about 7 months. Has been a tech lead a lot before that. Has known she wants to be a manager for a long time, and has done a *lot* of prep, has good mentors, has thought a lot about managing. Dove head first into hiring, cares a ton about DEI, sees herself as responsible to the business, all of those are good characteristics. Just a little weak on the experience side.

IC to manager:
Started as security, then senior security engineer at cloudflare
Was a “lead” at sentry, had an open jd, so immediately became a manager as they hired someone
As a manager, you’re responsible for so much more than just your output
For the 6-7 months they’ve been there, it’s been amazing to see them grow and become successful
Think a bit more about long term relationships outside of the scope of a single project; how will you work together with other teams
People’s experiences at the company really are shaped by you role as a manager
Lots of good mentors (head of engineering at cloud flare), learned a lot from good mentors
Tried to read a lot of books, podcasts, etc
Hiring
First time working with the recruiting team
Learned where their blind spots might be - they have a JD, but she really knows what exactly she’s looking for
Had linkedin recruiter, reached out to candidates herself
1 year vision for the team, what the team will need to achieve that vision
Work backwards to what skills you need on the team
Do we need this now? Do we have the budget? Or do we have the time to train someone, invest in someone’s growth
Trying to be realistic about the hc we have now, what do we _really_ need to do now, what are we willing to give up
DEI
Cares about this a lot, not a lot of people in security look like her
Had the privilege of working on a diverse team at cloud flare
People are different, they open up differently, and making space for people is important - if you don’t make room for people, not everyone will share
Necessary for psychological safety
Being able to oppose a leader’s position - would want to be able to see the interactions of the team, make sure that ideas can be debated safely
Coaching/growing a direct report
Her direct report, this is his second role
First job in-house, so had to think more long term
Long term consequences were something he had not had to think through
When they would talk through tradeoffs, made him explain his decision making process, asked questions about maintenance
Teaching them the framework, leading people to explain their thinking helps them have the lightbulb moment
Not just looking to give them a strategy, but wants to help people figure it out for themselves
Had a bunch of security operations engineers that they mentored, helped them build strategies around how to tackle different kinds of problems
Feedback
At cloud flare, was told that she wasn’t giving enough praise or recognition to others
Was eye opening, wish she’d gotten that feedback earlier
Constantly firefighting in her roles, always thinking about what’s next, pace is very fast
Not always thinking about how to celebrate wins, wouldn’t naturally think about highlighting things they’ve done or shouting people out for their help in their week-to-week
After that, made sure to encourage teammates by calling them out, trying to highlight things that others wouldn’t normally see
Giving positive reenforcement really makes a difference
People know you’re paying attention, they know you have their back, creates a more positive and helping culture on the team

questions.question: Values - Notes
questions.answer:
Super strongly cares about values and culture. Loved them at Cloudflare, is leaving Sentry because things are very top down, lack of trust and psychological safety, makes it so folks don't want to own things and be on the hook for them if they fail. Cloudflare the culture was strong, lots of safety to experiment, try things, learn. Has tried to evolve the security culture so that people are not afraid to fail.

Why Semgrep:
Culturally, Sentry is very different from impressions
In next role, want to pay a lot more attention to the culture
Paying attention to Semgrep since 2019, met Clint
Good product, people like using the product
Committed to continuing the journey, exciting to see growth
Role is in line with next opportunity, new she wanted to be a manager, loves being a manager, looking to really grow as a manager
Really likes working at a security company
Next role:
Strong sense of ownership
People raise their hand, are not afraid to own things
At a startup, teams fall apart, new teams form, responsibilities form
Do people want accountability
Want people to be able to fail safely
At cloud flare, went through a lot of experimentation, a lot of growth
People were open minded, not tied to a single solution
If things aren’t working, can pivot, try something new, and those things could fail and you’d try again
Current company, people don’t want to own things, they’re afraid to fail, very top down, not a lot of innovation
When working in security, ownership is important and people willing to chip in and share and help is important
Past teams:
Lots of psychological safety
Lots of trust among teammates
People give each other feedback, they improve, they work on things, they push each other to grow, morale is higher
Positive signs of people feeling more comfortable, they don’t shy away from the hard problems

questions.question: Growth - Notes
questions.answer:
Had a very clear set of things she's looking to grow: wants to grow as a manager, learn how to better impact the business, take on more cross-org leadership challenges, help grow a team's impact, etc. And is specifically targeting small security focused startups b/c she thinks that's the best place to get some of that experience and grow given her background and skills. Extremely growth oriented.


Want to help grow a security team, build a strong security culture, ideally at a security company
Wants to go somewhere they can stay for 5 years, grow as a manager
Loves what she does
Opportunity to build something from the ground up is really interesting
Likes balancing business needs with the team goals
Wants to be a leader at the company, at a smaller company

questions.question: Tackling Complex Problems - Notes
questions.answer:
Very very deep technical knowledge. Was the lead engineer for the role, then started hiring people. Very very thorough understanding of the design of the systems in place. Was able to quickly articulate why the problem was important, what they did to solve it, why the solution was good, and why they might change things in the future.


At Sentry, had Panther as SIEM, and that was it
Were getting about 100 alerts/day, and just her responding to it
Needed some post-alert pipeline for processing alerts and deciding what to do about them
Hired someone who was a security engineer and wanted to own a real system
Built an alert ingestion pipeline
Built in GCP, relies on panther, and outputs to JIRA, but this sits in the middle
Deduplication, suppression, and routing
Helped him figure out what the goals are, coached him through writing a PRD
Main goal was to write a framework where they could extend their post-alert enrichment to deduplicate, make it something other engineers can contribute to
Thought about Tines, Fork, rolling something themselves, but used GCP (already had GCP)
Didn’t need a two-way integration with panther, just needed to scale with # of alerts
Didn’t want to be too specific with cases that these services could solve, so that if they moved off of Panther it would still work
Pipeline is built, alerts are down to 30/day, big improvement but still high
Enrichments from other tools like Permisso
Ties session IDs together from other tools, so lots more context
Made design decisions so that things restarting or going down aren’t impactful to the other components
In a position so that hiring someone new, they can contribute to it quickly
Not too much bugs or maintenance costs
Grafana instead of GCP dashboards - 90% of monthly costs are Grafana, GCP is quite cheap

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Applicant submitted solution in a timely manner - received August 18th, reviewed by Kurt Boberg August 22nd.

Script submitted correctly performs a github search, collects repositories to scan, scans, and collates/prints the results.  Script will use OS environment variables to perform authenticated scans if available.

Has an uncovered fault case reading a metadata field that may not be present (vulnerability_class) - this would require extensive knowledge of Semgrep's output format to anticipate and correct.

Applicant identified some text processing edge cases in script comments, also pointed out unsafe behavior of shell_execute and lack of user input type checks.

requirements file included with submission has more dependencies than necessary to run the script, reviewer ended up constructing a minimal Poetry lockfile with only the necessary dependencies.

Input arguments required to reproduce the screenshot output *not* included in submission.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Pros:
- highly professional
- strong interpersonal skills
- demonstrated cross-functional interaction skills
- strong ability to coach to craft SMART goals
- good ability to help me feel supported, draw out relevant information needed to coach
- great questions for me about the team / company etc
- great enthusiasm for the role and the company; enthusiasm was about appropriate things
- said she had a lot of experience in detection and response and crafting information that helps engineers take action; I expect this experience would be very helpful in trying to craft an actionable, dev-friendly supply chain product

Cons:
- needed prompting to introduce the idea of outside learning materials as part of development / meeting goals

questions.question: Does the candidate help the interviewer be creative in setting their goals?
questions.answer: Our conversation was pretty tactical and specific.  We didn't get into more creative aspects of goal setting.

questions.question: Does this person enable growth through coaching?
questions.answer: Yes, I felt very supported and I felt she was actively trying to help me identify a path the enables growth while also ensuring team goals are being met.

questions.question: Does the candidate build trust and psychological safety?
questions.answer: Yes, the candidate made sure I felt supported throughout, that I had what I needed to get going, that I felt heard (e.g. asked for my ideas).

questions.question: Does the candidate employ a variety of management/growth tactics?
questions.answer:
Yes.

[I'll use this space to address the second half of the interview, which is about unblocking yourself]

- the candidate was very supportive, thanked me for bringing my concerns, non-critical
- asked appropriate questions to draw out enough information to help me get unstuck
- asked for my ideas about what is most important
- effectively drew out who could be helpful (e.g. working with the PM since the tech lead is out sick).  demonstrated ability to help me with the cross-functional / interpersonal side of getting unblocked.
- great focus on the customers and what the customers are asking for
- near the end of this section, made sure to ask me -- 'what do you think is the most challenging thing for me in getting started on this project'.  this was a useful / impactful thing to ask at this point -- making sure to address head on the biggest personal blocker I have.

- one area I wasn't hearing from her in either  half of the interview was helping me find external learning resources (the more structured side of learning, in addition to the finding work opportunities that help with the learning).  So I brought this up 'you know, I feel like I would benefit from some broader understanding of cross-site scripting'.  Once I brought this up she was very encouraging, described appropriate resources that would help, with a good focus on real-world vulnerabilities and how they impacted companies in the real world.

questions.question: Does the candidate help the interviewer set SMART goals?
questions.answer:
Yes, the candidate worked with me to refine the vague (get better at finding vulns) to make sure the goal was
- specific (refined down to get better at cross-site scripting)
- measurable (we agreed on 5 cross-site scripting rules per week)
- attainable (made sure this was reasonable along with other work I might have; set check-in plans so she could help mentor if needed)
- realistic (talked through whether this concrete goal woudl help achieve the overall goal; talked through hwo to balance this with other team priorities)
- time-bound (she suggested 2 weeks, but also since I role-played a new analyst, she made sure to make sure there were short-term checkins).

questions.question: Does the candidate help the interviewer hold themselves accountable for their goal?
questions.answer: Yes, we talked about how we would measure / how would we know the concrete goal was being met.  She introduced the idea of milestones and tried to align this specific goal with some mid-point milestones.

questions.question: Does the candidate give feedback clearly and appropriately?
questions.answer: Yes, the candidate was very positive and encouraging but also willing to offer direct feedback.  'This initial goal is pretty broad, help me understand more specifically how you're trying to improve in this area'.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall the presentation covered most of the key sections we'd like. 
Pros
- Talked through the decision process to build vs buy, and how they evaluated different vendors
- Designed a high level plan with explicit out of scope areas to ensure they hit the MVP on the head
- Discussed and solicited feedback from stakeholders
- Communication wise, presentation was well structured, key points were emphasized, and questions were answered in a succinct and effective manor.

Cons
- Didn't get a solid success criteria - but when prompted, we received a really solid answer why they chose the feature set, how it would be used, and what it would enable them to do
-

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
For section 1, candidate was very interested in business logic/behavior, spent most of the time asking about capabilities/design/behavior before diving into technology stack.

Per the rubric:
Wanting to understand what functionality the app can do
 Candidate asked a lot of questions about app functionality, separation of concerns, object types, allowed user behavior, etc.
Asking about different levels of privilege - are there types of privileged users? 
First question candidate asked was "how many user types are there"
How does authentication occur?
Third stand-alone question after drilling down into admin behavior.
Dynamic testing: Trying to map all the sources / methods of supplying user input to the app 
Candidate asked about testing around the 9 minute mark, generally in the SDLC context - do dynamic/static test cases exist?
Is there admin functionality?
Second stand-alone question candidate asked.
Source code review: 
What languages and tech stack are used?
Candidate got around to this around the 9 minute mark.
Understanding routes, do they use exec() or related functions, unparameterized SQL queries? What other systems does the main app talk to and how?
Went over time, did not cover - candidate mentioned technology stack concerns later when discussing vulnerabilities.

For section 2, candidate chose IDOR (interested & recent memory of working on/solving IDOR bugs)

Candidate summary of IDOR was simple, to the point, and correct - possession of identifier/enumeration + lack of access controls leads to this bug.

Candidate's history is very detection & response heavy - responses were weighted towards identifying and capturing behavior.

Covered CSRF, SQLi, XSS in some detail - candidate is obviously knowledgeable about OWASP classifications + general antipatterns that lead to them, along with historical frequent fliers (PHP & SQLi)

Interested in history & makeup of team, very visibility focused thinking. Super functionality biased when thinking about security - code is an afterthought, not the main focus. I would argue this is a benefit - code is a byproduct of tasks, not a generator.


Raw notes:

How many user types are there?


1 for production app
admins use back office with its own db connection
AD creds work, need to be on corp VLAN


how do admin entitlements work?


AD group


Authentication for production app?


social SSO
user/pass, no MFA


do we process payments?


no, users don’t directly pay us money


does it have integrations?


no other than social SSO


What data do we collect?


everything that we’re legally allowed to that users give us


Where is data stored?


AWS RDS - basic cloud deployment


What languages do we use to build it? Tech stacks? Hosted?


AWS


Admin - LAMP on EC2 classic


public page is Next.js, GraphQL talking to Node microservices


What kind of testing have we done?


automated/manual/static?


nope, none


User inputs - how do we handle image uploading? Posts/Comments?






Favorite vuln - IDOR


had a few at previous jobs


access to an object because of missing access controls + identifiers exposed to user


users could download source of any worker app


impact - read access


remediation focused discussion


API requests for issues over-expose information when sharing


how would you tackle this at scale?


capture a request - find identifiers
check to see if you can modify parameters and get information back
get a list of API endpoints you can alter
directory traversal




explain vulns to a 1 year tenure engineer


OWASP puts out a top 10, standardized way to audit apps


changes over time, released every few years


Frequent things we see
 
 broken access control


IDOR
CSRF


no one approach - layered approach
CSRF
- identity is inherited by the request, lots of context saved by browser, site is tricked into thinking request is coming from a legitimate user
- not stealing data, performing actions as you
- e.g. deleting user
- watering-hole attacks
preventing:
csrf tokens - synchronizer pattern


Injection


os command injection
sql injection
- user input used to make a request to DB without sanitizing
- prepared statements (parameterized queries), no dynamic SQL queries
- input validation/escaping
cross-site scripting
- stored/reflected
- can be more dangerous based on exposure
- not validating input
- exploits trust that user has in the browser




CORS
feature not a bug
CORS preflight
onus is on the client
