All raw nots: 

Very clear outline of the goals for the program 

Setup the SOAR program for Sentry, navigating a small team & limited budget. Seems strong at doing a lot with a little.

Solid market analysis; existing commercial solutions were very expensive

Defined an MVP!! <3
- Built a process in  house, since existing commercial solutions were too expensive ($40 / month vs. $250K a year
- Got feedback from the team to figure out 
- Designed the architecture 

Essentially sounds like she designed a way to take the scan alerts; store and procss them internally, and pipe them to Jira for devs. Seems like highly relevant experience. 

Say you want to know when an admin was created; in any log source (standard set of rules), we have to use the alert ID to pull the event. Have to query every log source  table to, takes a long time. 
- Need to have the events info in the ticket for developers; & didn't effect their cost, so just accepted that inefficiency 
- Sounds like she had a good handle on the priorities for her org; low dev friction, and low cost

Clear articulation of her impact 
- broke down the team size
(reviewed prs; helpeprovide feedback, write rfcs & design tradeoffs, coaching, looked for cross-functional adoption, try to open-source this, could add more context for alerts)


Nits: Neither of the challenges were particularly difficult;  just accepted unoptimized queries for now. Mentioned there were other long-term solutions, but not paying for the cost themselves so didn't prioritize fixing.

Question; you talked about meeting with stakeholders to identify what was needed from develoepers? Was
Predifctable & safe, don't have to send any data to 3rd parties


shield from operational work; 2 quarters of work. first quarter onboarding engineers (1 of 2 eng), work on this project. short-term shielded from that

success criteria?
- had a definition of a v1. process panther results, push to jira. broke it down into enrichment & suppression

how did you know successful?
do everything in one pace -> improved response time! conversations with users, surveys etc. Use SRE to guinea pig; to help see which components to make it easier for external teams to interact with, don't have to manage it themselves.

high functioning team; have to keep it manageable level
tech debt -> 
 - keep track in backlog, managing; repaying debt & innovacting
low larson, good framework to consider where you're team is at

use 

challenges; lessons learned, would've like to try a paid product, or do siem, didn't see us suddenly scaling up, for now it's OK. very different from cloudflare, had to build this in house

questions.answer:
Name: Stanley Chan
Email: stahn.chan@gmail.com or schan@cloudflare.com
Phone: +1 (646) 251-5727

Establish relationship history
How long have you known them?
at Cloudflare since 2019, worked with her at Cloudflare starting in 2020
What was the nature of your interactions?
she was member of detection response team, title was detection response engineer. he was on infrastructure security team, title was security engineer. logs and endpoint tools for detection response team. her team would have reqs and he would build solutions. 
What did your day-to-day work with them look like?
didn't work with her day to day. mainly interacted with her for project coordination and planning. his team stood up new tool to be consumed. her team would be the consumer and request ways to improve. Misha was great at making detection and feedback. also some risk assessments. cross team assessments to profile potential weaknesses. she always takes action and improves. 
during incidents of downtime. her team was first responder and coordinate messaging. she took point and sync with other teams on gathering information. 
Understand the candidates communication
Does the candidate make their peers and those around them better? How?
she is very empathetic. listens and communicates. build consensus very well. listens to concerns and finds common ground to take action on. at cloudflare, some vendor calls and her team was ultimate consumer so would provide feedback and ways to improve. 
Have they made you better?
would call Misha a friend. a quality of delivering has improved for both of them. 
Have you ever had trouble communicating with them, and if so, how?
not particularly. for deliverables, one team is relying on another. deadlines shift and having transparency on why makes things better. clear communication is her strength. 
Assess strengths and weaknesses
What is something they can always be trusted to do / deliver on?
if you need a project done, she is there to do it and do whatever is asked of her. 
Understand growth path / trajectory
How has the candidate grown and changed technically / professionally since you’ve known them?
knew Misha as IC. haven't worked with her as manager. have chatted and talked about experience. very protective of her team. a good manager shields you from bs. does that as much as she can and sets them up for success. 
as IC, don't have to do that but keeps it in mind to help make her team better and execute
Over the next year, where do you think they should focus to continue their growth?
needs to keep doing what she's doing. some managerial aspects like being a good mentor, taking ideas. these skills need to be honed. Being more unflappable and a reliable foundation that her team can rely on. 
As the candidate’s future manager, is there anything we can do to set them up for success at Semgrep?
to take to next level, need healthy feedback and amazing mentorship. make her more comfortable on a new company and team. integrate her to the culture. 
the role is a pseudo SR and Analyzing vulnerabilities, enable her to be part of the community at large. 
Rating 1-10, where 10 is “best person you’ve ever worked with” and 1 is the worst, how would you rate them?
10, would work with her again. delivers on her work.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Establish relationship history
How long have you known them?
2-3 years. at Cloudflare. Manager of security engineer team.
What was the nature of your interactions?
day to day management. operations and roadmapping. projects. 1:1 with her. she was production side of detection response. how to be more proactive. track hunting. working with infra to check modeling. translate to detection opportunities.
What did your day-to-day work with them look like?
working on project. collaboration with infra and detection team. assess and what are things they can harden. monitor proactively. interacting between teams. creating rules. reviewing detection rules.
Understand the candidates communication
Does the candidate make their peers and those around them better? How?
definitely. likes to look at big picture. how does it impact. look more than just the solution but any benefits that help the team. evangelize. detection is behind scene but understood how to evangelize. 
Have they made you better?
really enjoyed having her on her team. if there was anything she didn't understand. didn't hesitate to ask. helped her as her manager to communicate and influence. 
career path she mentioned was that she wanted to be a people manager. nearing end, want to do more. did mentoring of new hires. bringing people up to speed. share bigger picture. if given initiative, she would go brainstorm from high level and present back to her. 
Have you ever had trouble communicating with them, and if so, how?
no. she has been very receptive. even during performance reviews. after, would check in often to make sure she is meeting the milestones and improvements that were discussed. receptive of feedback from manager and peer. 
Assess strengths and weaknesses
What is something they can always be trusted to do / deliver on?
if given task, can trust that she can deliver on time. break down well and understand objectives. final results. 
5 eng on team. 
she listens and understands to peers. what they are struggling on. create a path forward for any issues. 
Understand growth path / trajectory
How has the candidate grown and changed technically / professionally since you’ve known them?
joined as security eng. new space in detection response. did well to understand. always involved with rubric planning and vision. contribute and be more involved with annual planning. technical capability but also business needs. 
Over the next year, where do you think they should focus to continue their growth?
getting more experience with manager part. exposed to more people manager staff.
As the candidate’s future manager, is there anything we can do to set them up for success at Semgrep?
in terms of security knowledge, she has that. for product security, need to get more familiar with that piece. not too deep in production vs monitoring. expose more product security. plus manager exposure; training. 
Working at a smaller startup? 
Cloudflare was small when she started and she wouldn't have any issues in startup culture.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Let’s focus a project you worked on recently — please share an overview of the project and its process to start.


Katie gave an overview of the process at QwietAI to produce documentation. Process includes:
Kickoff meeting with Engineering
Working with the Engineering Manager, who is SME / reviewer for the doc
She tests the feature when a demo becomes available - not before, because too many things can change between scope and demo.
She produces a draft which is sent to QA for correctness.
She is technically a part of the Product team.
Docs almost always originate from her.
Maintenance of documentation tends to happen in Summer and Winter as Spring and Fall are product launches.
Her answer is similar to, but not exactly, the process we follow at Semgrep.
Her technical writing specifics are as follows:
Follows Microsoft Style guide for TW, Chicago Manual for non-TW (i.e. blogs).
Much more loose than ours, I would say.
Directional language for UI elements is okay in her style guide.
We discussed other types of media in docs: gifs, video, screenshots, and how we'd handle each. The following are her answers:
Gifs: not in docs, because of lack of playback and scrubbing (user control)
Screenshots: used sparingly due to maintenance issues (screenshots become "wrong" quickly), but they are necessary. Should be full-screen and consistently-sized if possible.
Video: takes a lot of resources to produce, can be used in docs, but depends on how out of date it is
Discussion of taxonomy (the docs sidebar) - necessary to redo sometimes.
VSCode with all the plugins (excess whitespace, double spaces, spellcheck, etc.)
Docusaurus, GitHub PRs, Jenkins
Uses Jira for tasking, and sounds like tasks are arranged in a Kanban view.
We discussed bugs in Docusaurus. She is aware of some pitfalls of the tool.

What were the deliverables? 


External-facing task-oriented product documentation (guides). I heard very little about any other type of documentation (references, explanations, etc.)
For context, at Semgrep, we also produce referential and conceptual explanations. Additional to product documentation (steps in Semgrep Cloud Platform, etc.) we also write about the Semgrep syntax (rule-writing.) Rule-writing is not a process that strictly follows "steps" and so the way we approach the documentation on this has to be different. 


What resources were available to you?


Engineering manager, developer for tooling in Docusaurus, QA team.
Review time from SMEs to her work is very quick - 1 day.
Prefers to refer to working demo rather than scope docs.


How much time did it take to complete?

I (Sara) did not get to ask this question.  On the subject of "completion" we discussed that docs are never "done" because of the nature of the product.

How did you receive and give feedback about the documentation during the project?


GitHub PR comments
Wrote tickets when UI/UX steps were getting too long for user in case the Product team needs to revisit

What was the result of the project? (feedback from users or stakeholders after completion)


Her answer here was a little vague (granted this is a very difficult question in documentation because we can't extrapolate meaning from a session duration of a user in a document.) This boils down to "hits". I feel like this could have been answered more concretely in many ways, such as internal adoption (knowing that her docs are used by Support, for example), or mentioning docs referenced inside the product itself.

Some observations
Has significant product, process, and documentation experience that is relevant to Semgrep, except for some gaps.
Focus is more on specific documentation production rather than holistic approach to "docs" ecosystem internally and externally, such as analytics in docs, how docs could support CSEs, be embedded in product, etc.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion: Yes. I think they would be a good addition to the team

Pros:
Works with engineers and product managers within their processes
Shown ability to manage multiple projects at once 
Deals with conflict by focusing on users are the judge and impact value 
Provides feedback to the product and its usability but recognizes when that can be really difficult to implement 

Cons:
Definitely a bit process driven but I think they are able to still be flexible 

Raw Notes:
Let’s focus a project you worked on recently with a software or product team involving a new software product or feature. Can you describe the project briefly and then talk through how you collaborated with software or product team members on it? 
* Work in tandem 
* Send to JIRA feature (create a vulnerability and push that to JIRA)
* Start with the kickoff meeting and read the documentation from product/eng (what is the problem, timeline, MVP, extras) -> start with that ahead of time
* Then I can plan features based

How do you take what can be very technical information from these teams and make them accessible to others? Please share a specific example. 
* What engineer say they ship may not be 100% what they ship 
* I use context to how to guide the users
* I write from scratch with the user in mind
* How do you digest the technical information for the user?
  * “yes it’s technical but I try to explain it simply” 
* If your documentation is too hard, then you either need to change your product or you need to improve your documentation

At what stage did you get involved in the project? What was the state of the product then? 
* Kickoff ideally
* How do you handle if you can’t get into kickoff? -> Can I go into a different process. Is there is a process change to understand what is missing from the documentation?

How did it evolve during the time you worked on it? How did you work with others to incorporate those changes on your side? 
* Kickoff to eng is long. How does that evolve?
* I sit in eng sprint features and see them move along
* Only technical writer right now so features come out more in fall and spring 
* Number of features you are supporting drives 

Describe the process of creating content, review/editing, and final publication. 
* Writing process begins when there is something to use
* Don’t want to write until there is an end user cause it changes 
* What happens if engineering ships ahead of you? 
  * Doesn’t happen cause I stay on top of it and PM can see when things are getting ready to ship 
* Review with engineers
  * All through PR comments 
  * No tension there and people will work through the goal
  * Understand the differences and then we align on the goal of the customer 

How did you receive and give feedback during the project? 
* Review (eng manager, and PM are informed) Engineer reads closer, QA team checks for accuracy 
* How do you give that product feedback? 
  * Give it to the product manager (not the engineer) 
  * Regularly cadence with products to bring up the issues 
  * Sometimes you can’t update it cause it is hard (i.e. database migration) 
  * Example: SSO/SAML is currently done via API calls and that’s hard. I think they need to either create a UI or use a id provider 
    * Wanted it to be hard intentionally and they aren’t sure how 

What was the result of the project? (feedback from users or stakeholders after completion) 
* JIRA shipped and people are using it 
* Docs helped with onboarding
* Feature is hidden so you need the docs to be able to use it

Would they want to add one at a time or all at once
What happens once they want to use it? Answer: assume they can add it to their organization

Do we track any anonymous user data


Scope:

Didn’t ask how many engineers though (maybe assumed 1?)

How would you get qualitative feedback?

Do you have access to user code?
Optimize for case without it

When would customers go access this? Current workflow pretty reactive
Would be nice to be non-reactive, one place that is source of truth and has all info they need

Start with user journey


User looking to identify new rules relevant to them
Filter by “new” or “newly released”

Add to their org

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
﻿@Sachi Shah﻿ Great convo with Jack. Prior work as CEO/co-founder of Blue Canvas and their version control product for Salesforce was impressive that they could balance product fit, customer requests, and go to market. They have been in devtools space for 10 years but not necessarily for software engineers. I would move forward to HM screen. 
 
Current role/why are you looking?
-PM/builder in devtools space for 10 years. Blue Canvas; DevOps for Salesforce product. helped create an API for static analysis. Launched successfully before getting acquired.
-Sauce; 5 employees when started and 200 when left. Got some exposure to company scale. 
 
What is important to you in your next role?
-a good work environment and culture comes from exceptional colleagues. people that are passionate. 
-want to go back into learning mode after being a CEO
-stay in devtools space
-wearing a lot of hats as CEO. PM work is cross functional and want to own a particular area. 
 
Tell me about a project/role where you had ramp up on something new very quickly and/or stretch yourself. How did you go about doing so?
-BC; knew version control. but knew nothing about Salesforce platform/developer platform. companies employ dozens of devs to run it. low code platform.
-5 or so admins to leverage Salesforce db. GUI. 
-ramp up and learning about the ecosystem. 
-talking to users, cold outreach. talked with eventbrite and atlassian. 
 
Have you worked with Marketing, Design? In what way have you worked with these teams. Price and positioning, enablement. 
-at BC; CEO, sales, marketing and product. first had to ramp up SEO himself. helped close all of the business. understand what is necessary to close.
-at Sauce; partner with CI/CD partners. did partnership with MSFT.
-BC; one example is that companies requested static analysis for their service. knew it was important but there were diversions. how do you manage the trade off? worked with engineering and develop API and use webhooks to use a scanning tool of their choice. released in a couple of weeks.
-API became a core part of their demo. spent time on documenting it. also wrote blog posts. 


Team at BC?
-7 employees, 1 mil in ARR. 
 
We typically look for prior experience working on products that are used by developers OR prior experience as a developer to help us understand your technical knowledge. Can you walk me through some technical challenges you had to work through?
-biggest challenge; their product is version control but admins had weird way to update. SF is low code. admin making changes but generate massive XML files. 
-Their product would capture snap shot changes in environment and commit it on there behalf. Built trust with customers that what was in their git reflected correctly. solving a branching problem. 
 
*Outcome-driven — focus on metrics they moved, impact on customers and the business
-bootstrapped company. data was not mature.
-time to deploy; 3 core product values and 1 was that it should take seconds vs minutes. 
-time to deploy after sign up. 


How do you prioritize?
-was going to start with command line tool originally. but admins wouldn't use this. 
-make sure GUI profile was most important. meet users where they are at. 
-were on qualitative side for prioritizing. watching over their shoulders. 
-at Cisco, co-founder watched how they use the product. get in front of users. 
-RICE style at Sauce prior.

questions.answer:
tl;dr - for founder interview, explore why/how they'd be ok working within a company vs. starting their own (founder -> employee hard transition imho). Seems values/philosophy aligned in dev process: talk to users, iterate, do it small and cheap, pushed to sell the MVP early. questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall solid 3!

Was the only candidate that touched on big picture about the point of the whole exercise -- which was to need find, come up with a proposal, and get sign-off from stakeholders in order to help Purple Chat close this deal. 

He quite thorough with the questions he asked, making sure to ask for the "why" and motivation behind prioritization, features, and personas. I liked that he was collaborative in opening up a Google spreadsheet, walking through how he'd prioritize, and some potential downscopes in case circumstances changed.

A few things to make note of: I wanted to see him drive the proposal more. A few times he'd come up with ideas and ask Raghav and I how felt first, but I almost wished he make his recommendation and back it up before going there. I would also be curious to learn why he left his startup as a founder in order to join another as mid/senior level PM.

What do you know about Semgrep?
spoke with Milan and Vivek. reached out to them after applying. Still not sure if she knows what our product does but the effort seems like it's there.


Why are you interested in an Internship at Semgrep? 
-some criteria; want to work at a technology focused company. engineering at forefront.
-scope of impact and opportunity to grow. empowering engineers through our product.

Can you walk me through your experience? (Is this person who they say they are)

questions.question: Summary
questions.answer:
Values Alignment: Yes + 
Rubric: 
Do the candidate's interests and values align with Semgrep? - Strong Yes. Very high on users are the judge and do it fast. Cares about collaboration
Did the candidate engage with our values and mission? (high impact, mission first, low ego, mutual respect, honesty) - Strong Yes. 
Did the candidate demonstrate behaviors that align with our values when talking about feedback, conflict, and overcoming challenges? - Yes. Definitely humble and curious. Learning to focus on the outcome 
Raw Notes:
Tell me about what made you look at Semgrep?
Junior at Northwestern 
Explored data science, computer science, and communication 
Internship at active campaign -> SSO (100-150 users using the API)
Implemented an audit log for the API usage
Has joined two student startups at school (FE eng) -> mobile app for community events
Just joined another startup as a PM -> thinking about product design -> found out through user testing that it wasn’t useful and continuing to try 
 Good values alignment 
“I really like talking” -> a bit verbose 
What’s important to you about where you work? What are you looking to get out of your internship?
Internship criteria:
Industry: tech -> wants fast paced environment for both technical and social 
Scope of Impact -> looked into cybersecurity from the product 
Cultural -> Talked to Milan and Vivek before applying and people are supportive and answering my questions -> cares about collaboration 
Cares about personal impact not just money

Technical Project Raw Notes:
Internship project
Goal: Implement open id connect for this API
Challenge was dealing with the uncertainty vs school projects

Technical Execution: Yes + 
Rubric: 
Does the candidate execute on their work? - Yes 
Does the candidate break down complex problems into achievable tasks? - Strong Yes with estimations
Does the candidate own their work? Their mistakes? - Strong Yes. Ownership also over the compromises and the end goal
When faced with changes in plan or unforeseen obstacles, does the candidate adjust their approach to make progress? - Yes, but it sounds like it can take time for them to get there so this is a learning opportunity for them
Raw Notes:
What was most challenging about this project? How did you deal with the challenges?
Dealing with the unstructured goal
Broke down the goal into manageable parts 
Did research and worked with the manager and thought about it before the internship started 
Understood needed Okta and Go and OAuth flow
Did some udemy courses and oauth flow and then during the internship created a diagram for an access control flow and presented it to my mentor and other senior members of the team
Iterated on the design doc for a bit
Got it working 
How did you make sure, as the project progressed, that you were on track?
Broke down the diagram into milestones with some estimations 
“My own sprint planning”
Then as I was presenting the diagram, asked for feedback on the estimation
Last week was behind: behind on the access token middleware for the API 
Tested and pushed 5/15 endpoints or Do the audit logging project
Manager recommended to implement all the endpoints so it was easier to pass it off
I wanted to be more through (the audit log was the end goal) and we compromised on doing all the endpoints by x day then do the audit log
Audit log ended up in staging (just not production)
Did some extra hours 
What is the biggest lesson you learned from this project and how have you applied it? 
Project ownership 
If I had estimated with more understanding, I might push myself more at the start to get the timeline going to give more time at the end 
During code review with senior eng, left a comment “why is this line necessary and what does it do?” -> first time I got opposition vs in school when people say that it’s error vs opening a conversation 
Was able to explain it rather than see it as a challenge as why I was wrong

Technical Communication: Yes - 
Rubric:
Does the candidate communicate effectively? - Yes but definitely verbose and will need some mentorship around how to handle this
Does the candidate ask confirming questions to understand their audience's knowledge? - No
Does the candidate respond to and fully answer questions? - Strong Yes
Does the candidate refrain from interrupting or rambling? - Strong Yes
Does the candidate resolve conflict in a respectful and productive manner? - Yes. Her example was how she learned how to do so
Does the candidate respond well to feedback? Are they comfortable giving feedback? - Yes. But the feedback she got is something I also noticed so I think there is some growth still there for her
Raw Notes:
Tell me about a time (maybe on this project or another) where you disagreed with another engineer. How did you resolve this disagreement? What would you do differently if you had this disagreement in the future?
In startup and doing product prototyping
People were really emotionally invested in the prototype even though it’s important for to validate it (as the PM)
During the final figma mock, did some user testing and the feedback was “this was so cute. I would use it for 2 days and not after that”
As PM, the success is not just user adoption but also user retention 
But we spend so much type on it (started in June), but I knew it wasn’t the right thing
So I said “it was bad”, I was immature in that I didn’t present it professional 
People didn’t respond well to that cause people were emotionally invested in 
After 2 weeks, we have decided to reiterate but if I did this, I would approach it differently
Revisiting the notes and then quote them and use data -> focus on the data over the emotional piece 
Tell me about a time that you changed your behavior based on feedback from a peer, your manager, or even someone you were mentoring. What did you learn?
Summer internship experience 
“Ties back to my weakness”
When I get a new problem, I just want to solve it immediately which helps me get stuff done but it can come off as too anxious or eager 
Manager gave me feedback during a 1:1 about this “how are you feeling? Are you okay? Is it too much for you?”
Realizing that I was a sophomore not a junior so the scope was a bit big 
Realized I didn’t know that much so did some work around the anxiety 
Working on setting back and taking a breathe so I can actively practice it and seek out other people’s help early (rather than figure things out myself)
Learned to time box and then ask more questions

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: A low 3. They got through all of part 1 and 2 on their own, and with some hinting from me on part 3, made it through most of the test cases (1 failing at the end). What most likely prevented them from fully coming to the correct solution was because they made assumptions about the cases they had to handle and initially didn't take the time to take a step back and understand the problem. However, once I nudged them to pause and look at the test cases, they eventually did code out a 90% working solution. Although they didn't get through all of the test cases correctly, if they were given 5 more minutes in the interview, I think they would've completed the problem. Overall, I generally appreciated how responsive they were to feedback and how they articulated their thought process throughout the entire interview.

questions.question: Part 1 (2 points)
questions.answer: 2 points. No hints required and completed the question on their own.

questions.question: Part 2 (6 points)
questions.answer: 6 points. Cruised through this problem with no hints as well.

questions.question: Part 4 (7 bonus points)
questions.answer: N/A - did not get to this point

questions.question: Part 3 (7 points)
questions.answer:
-0.5 for half hinting that self.findings should be a dictionary mapping from match_based_id/syntactic_id to finding. She got most of the way on her own, but was trying to do some nested dictionaries, so I had to point her to the right direction. 
-1 for hint that match_based_id is not always present so it is not always available as a key
-1 for hint on when to add a new match_based_id / syntactic_id mapping

They spent a lot of time thinking through the solution and wrote out a bunch of pseudocode to explain their thought process, which ultimately might have slowed them down quite a bit. While planning out the solution is great, if they had started coding a bit earlier and worked through the test cases, that would've allowed them to fully understand the problem comprehensively. At the end, they mostly got a working

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, Aimen was great to chat with and explained her solution very well. When there were problems, she was able to identify those areas and correct those mistakes through testing and working through the logical structure iteratively. 

One concern I had was with her code iteself. Understandably it was an interview so I did not expect the most product-ready code, but some design decisions she made were correct for the scope of the question, but didn't leave much room to expand to topics like circular dependencies or performance improvements. 

Aimen did complete the question and covered the transitive dependency case with no errors, and her design was easy to follow and she was very vocal about the issues she was facing and how she intended to solve them- and so I feel we should continue to consider her for an internship, but I don't believe she is competitive enough to be a strong yes.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, I was impressed and this was by far the best performance I have seen on this interview. She required some guiding questions on part 3, so it wasn't a flawless performance, but I didn't explicitly give her any instructions and when I did give her guiding questions she answered them well and quickly realized the issues, in general.

She was also a good communicator. From the beginning, it was clear that she was trying to understand the problem and its nuance, and she talked through her thinking well. When we were talking about possible solutions, she explained her thinking well and we were able to have good conversations about possible options.

I didn't get a sense for her ability to debug, since for the most part her solutions worked on the first try, or, in the case of the bonus test, she figured out the issue very quickly.

She didn't seem to have tons of familiarity with Python (her chosen language)---she had some issues with self, etc---but I don't think this is a negative.

questions.question: Bonus
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: Part 1 (2 points)
questions.answer: 2 points. Got through this quickly and with a clean solution in about 5 minutes. She asked very good questions at the beginning and it was clear she was trying to understand the problem.

questions.question: Part 2 (6 points)
questions.answer: 6 points. Came to a clean solution without prompting within the first 15 mins of the interview.

questions.question: Part 4 (7 bonus points)
questions.answer:
+1 bonus

I asked her what improvements she would make to the test cases and she didn't immediately come up with any.

She had a bug in her implementation of part 3 that the tests didn't catch, so I gave her an example case set of 3 insertions that I said might be interesting. She wrote up the test case for that situation and the expected result, and realized that her implementation has a bug. I guided her a bit by just interpreting the test failure to tell her that her implementation was creating instead of updating and she figured out and fixed the issue quickly.

questions.question: Part 3 (7 points)
questions.answer:
5 points. Got to a working solution but it took some leading questions to get there.


She initially wanted to do a dictionary mapping syntactic ID to findings, and my leading question about how match_based_id findings would be handled didn't make her realize the issue.
But as son as she started coding she realized and asked a clarifying question that made her realize that something was wrong.
Wanted to do a tuple as a key, but I had her talk through how we would do the lookups and a bit about how python dictionaries work and she realized that that wouldn't work either.
Almost landed on a single dictionary with both syntactic and match based as keys but I asked her how the details would work and she gave up
After some discussion, she seemed a bit stuck, and I gave the hint that our fundamental problem was that we need to efficiently do lookups of both syntactic and match_based IDs, and then she came up with the two-dictionary solution.

Once she had the two-dictionary solution, her implementation of it was quick and it passed all the tests on the first try.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: 3.5 - Strong spike in feedback and communication. Project wasn't great for understanding how they break down projects (it was a hackathon). Would like to see how they did technically but I think they take feedback well and that's huge for an intern

When faced with changes in plan or unforeseen obstacles, does the candidate adjust their approach to make progress? - Yes. They focused on the highest impact for the time they had and then already focused on next steps. 
Does the candidate communicate effectively? Yes + 
Does the candidate ask confirming questions to understand their audience's knowledge? - Yes. Very easy to understand
Does the candidate respond to and fully answer questions? - Yes
Does the candidate refrain from interrupting or rambling? - Yes 
Does the candidate resolve conflict in a respectful and productive manner? - Yes but aires on the side of decision by committee. Not sure how they will handle when the committee is split but they had good thoughts on what they would do (i.e. be respectful and approachable)
Does the candidate respond well to feedback? Are they comfortable giving feedback? - Strong yes. Very reflective candidate who cares about giving and getting feedback. Cares about respectful approaches and focuses on the why and understanding that humans are humans
Summary
Based on the above questions, how did you arrive at your decision? Yes 
What next steps do we need to take? - If they are technically strong, I'd hire them. They are excited to take feedback and learn and that is a huge plus for me as an intern

Raw Notes:
Values 
* Tell me about what made you look at Semgrep?
  * Met with Emma at Grace Hopper
  * “Like what you do”
  * Enjoyed the coding interview and it shows me that we “set ourselves apart”
  * Liked the culture from Emma
* What’s important to you about where you work? What are you looking to get out of your internship?
  * Relentlessly improve value 
  * I like to get feedback and improve myself and the work I’m doing 
  * Want to work towards a goal constantly 
  * Create an impact and grow as a person
  * Environment where you are challenged and work harder every day 
  * Be the best version of yourself 
  * “Grow as a person” -> technical aspect (learn something new, but over multiple technologies on a technical basis)
  * Learn something new every day even on the things you already know
  * That’s a mindset 
  * Interaction with other people -> get good feedback
  * “Everybody has something to teach you in life”
  * Taking on multiple roles and juggling things 


Technical communication (15 mins)
* Now please walk me through a technical project you’ve worked on with someone else
  * Equal wheels -> hackathon over the summer project
  * Worked with one other to build the web application in 36 hours
  * Ideal was to reliable safe, reliable transit for marginal groups (currently dependent on other people around me for transportation)
  * FE -> react
  * BE -> Firebase 
  * Auth -> auth0 (hackathon category)
  * I was leading the team so my strategy was divide and conquer 
  * Teams cover each other 
* What was most challenging about this project? How did you deal with the challenges?
  * Completing the entire thing in 36 hours 
  * Choosing which features to implement and which to downscope 
  * “We did a pretty good job on that”
  * Talked through the features on downscoping
  * Is it more important to have auth vs a history of rides 
  * Group decision on downscoping 
  * Making decisions as a leader is giving everyone’s opinions 
  * “I think we should do x, what do you think?” (Make it a 2 way street)
* How did you make sure, as the project progressed, that you were on track? How did you coordinate work with others?
  * Communication 
  * This was a new peer that I met on discord for hackathons 
  * Used discord to keep in touch 
  * I’ll let you know when I push the code and if you want to adjust anything 
  * Check in every 2 hours 
  * Slept schedules were opposite 
* What is the biggest lesson you learned from this project and how have you applied it? 
  * Time-management 
  * During a crunch time with a huge application 
  * Breaking down tasks that helped a lot
  * Started using it during pressure situations (like during Grace Hopper)
  * Allocated time to make sure nothing was compromised 
  * Everything has to be in the calendar 
  * Team work is a 2 way street was re-enforced 
* What was the success?
  * 2 awards at the hackathon (2nd overall) and best use of auth0 and that was the first time we used it
  * Project is deployed and still online 
  * Next steps: Make it more safer and college specific (allow only the college .edu students to access it) so that your community is the only one who can help out 

⠀Interpersonal communication (15 mins)
* Tell me about a time (maybe on this project or another) where you disagreed with another engineer. How did you resolve this disagreement? What would you do differently if you had this disagreement in the future?
  * Couldn’t say I have had a project one but have during the teams 
  * Part of women in CS group and we were hosting a hackathon for our participants 
  * It was a beginner hackathon 
  * Hosting a workshop on the basics but someone else said lets only focus on react (rather than html etc)
  * Talked through it and listed on the pros/cons on both 
  * Took input from others on the pros and cons
  * After all that there was still a disagreement with all that data
  * Finally suggested we poll for the participants on what they want to learn 
  * Participants wanted to do the html/css one and that’s what is what they needed
  * If I couldn’t get the participants feedback, I’d respectfully lay down what I think and then do a majority vote and then if that was fully balanced we would have to go 
  * Lean towards the guidelines of the project 
  * Assumptions people learn html and css is learned in high school but I did bring up that not everyone 
* Tell me about a time that you changed your behavior based on feedback from a peer, your manager, or even someone you were mentoring. What did you learn?
  * Currently a stem guide for one of the courses here (TA, but also works to gain resources for students) 
  * Responsibilities include resume, job search, which organizations to join, etc 
  * Told that I speak really fast when I’m trying to teach someone 
  * I enter a zone that sounds like I am trying to be very assertive and bring them down during peer tutoring etc
  * I went too fast and they were scared to ask me questions
  * Now I am more mindful of that and check in more for understanding 
  * Make it more approachable, but I still do talk too fast and zoom will let me know 
  * Still working on it
* Tell me about a time when giving feedback didn’t go well for you. What would you do differently? 
  * In high school, was leading a team for a pitch competition 
  * One of my team members was tasked with making the presentation of the finances 
  * It was delivered “very poorly” and the slides didn’t look like the numbers were adding 
  * It was unclear where the numbers were coming from 
  * I think she was going through stuff that time and I found that out later and “it’s understandable”
  * We have a time crunch and can we double check them and align the numbers
  * “You don’t trust me. I double checked it” and so I said “fine I’ll do it” since we had to do it over time 
  * If I would do it differently, I’d try to remind them that we are working on a team and you have to listen to me and be respectful about it 
  * We talked afterwards and that’s how I found out things were going on 
  * Be open and communicate about it “Life happens”
Asked for feedback at the end to work on myself every day

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
This was a very good performance in the interview, in my opinion. She got through the implementation with just a couple of quickly resolved bugs, and her code was straightforward.

My only concern is that she was not super communicative with her thoughts. When we were actively discussing, she was able to explain her ideas clearly, but there were times when she was just thinking and I was wondering what she was thinking about. But I don't think this is a flag really, since she was still able to communicate her ideas in discussions and we had productive conversations, which I think was a good sign. She seemed to take my feedback and suggestions well.

We also had productive conversation at the end of the interview about what she was looking for; it seemed like she was interested in a startup but didn't get any particular sense of passion for this job over another.

questions.question: Part 2 (6 points)
questions.answer:
6 points. Got through in just a few minutes without any trouble.

At the end of part 2, asked her if she has any concerns and she said efficiency, noted that this was O(n).

questions.question: Part 3 (7 points)
questions.answer: 6 points. She got through this fairly smoothly. It took some initial discussion and coding to land on a solution that would work, but she did this mostly independently, I just asked a couple of questions asking her to think through some cases or to explain why she was switching gears. She had one bug due to the way that she was checking if findings were equal across the two dictionaries, but she figured it out without much trouble. For this one she mostly relied on thinking through the question rather than printing, but she figured it out quickly so that seems OK.

questions.question: Bonus
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: Part 1 (2 points)
questions.answer: 2 points. Got through without much trouble, but did stumble a bit because she didn't fully understand the requirements. But she relied on a print statement to debug and figured it out quickly without help, which was a good sign.

questions.question: Part 4 (7 bonus points)
questions.answer: We got to this, but she didn't immediately think of any test cases. No bonus points.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
This candidate had great answers about giving feedback, talking about wanting to understand what motivates other people and giving them the benefit of the doubt if they are not doing what you need them to do that maybe something bigger is going on that is worth getting curious about.

The values alignment was not stand-out but no red flags either (strongly aligned on impact and collaboration, but our mission didn't really come up and their answer about receiving feedback wasn't very pro-active, as they talked about receiving feedback in a setting where everyone has to get feedback (section leading preparation).

questions.question: Do the candidate's interests and values align with Semgrep?
questions.answer:
Weak yes - interested in a high-impact and collaborative environment and technical growth. Based on their answer, they partly just want to try a start-up because it's something they haven't done before as opposed to really understanding the differences and preferring start-up. 


---- 
 
SavEEtha. BS/MS Stanford. Experience across SWE and ML. Worked at IBM research on large language models. Past 2 summers at Meta. Full-stack debugging tool. 
 
At Stanford involved in AI and Healthcare. CS education, SL intro to data structures and algorithms course. 
 
Why Semgrep? Met Milan at GHC. Really cool learning more about the company. Semgrep seemed like a cool place with opportunities for both ML and FS engineering. Excited by start-up/entrepreneurial setting. Haven’t had chance to try that. Product-focused, used by people. User impact and consumer impact is important to me. 
 
Important to you? Technical growth opportunities, working on something with tangible impact. Could be boosting efficiency internally for engineers, etc. Sense that I’m contributing to something more. Collaborative setting, somewhere that I can easily ask for support, bounce around ideas, good mentorship. 
 
As a woman in engineering, I want support on diversity in engineering, getting to talk to people who’ve had successful careers and learn from them.

questions.question: Which technical project did the candidate describe? (Not graded)
questions.answer:
Debugging tool at meta for auto-responses when you message a business.


Project at Meta. Message and inbox team. If you’re a customer sending a message to a business page you get some kind of automated reply back. Built debugging tool to better visualize flow of logic and debug any issues. 
 
Was given codebase. It was very intricate/complex. Had a few engines (trigger engine, condition engine, ranking engine, action engine). Nested logic, I had to understand what was happening and make a proposal. Meeting with other engineers - do you think this will be useful? If not what other suggestions do you have? Independently wrote read/write in Hack/PHP which was new language for me. Designed UI for debugging tool and React/GraphQL to fetch. 
 
Example: Send a message to the page like “what time will you be open” and page sends back 2 messages back. Or repeatedly message page and stop getting responses back. Or answer that doesn’t actually make sense for your question. 
 
I was main person working on project. Had mentor who I could ask questions of. I was the only one writing the code and working on it end-to-end. Main form of collaboration was in PR’s. 2-3 “peer engineers”. Depending on what part of code I was editing, different people were more familiar with the code. 
 
Mentor and I had a document with milestones and deliverables per week. They had document ready when I started (manager comes up with detailed document and submits it for approval and assigns an intern). IBM I had more ownership and agency on what I did and how. Project was more experimental, so it made sense that it was more open-ended. A mix would be my preference. 
 
Lesson learned: when working with engineers and having meetings with them, important to have solid idea of what you’re going to talk about. Clear proposals. Also being given a code base is a lot like research, lots to figure out. Very rewarding to work through it and come up with a solution on your own. 
 
Biggest challenge: getting conceptual understanding of the code base and architecture.

questions.question: Does the candidate execute on their work?
questions.answer: I actually forgot to ask about whether this shipped/is being used by others because it isn't in the template. But they had the work broken into weekly milestones by their mentor. I would want to ask follow-up questions here to get a better sense of how it went, whether it is being used, etc. The scope of the project was impressive (implementing a debugging tool with a UI for other engineers).

questions.question: Summary
questions.answer:
This candidate had great answers about giving feedback, talked about wanting to understand what motivates other people and giving them the benefit of the doubt if they are not doing what you need them to do that maybe something bigger is going on that is worth getting curious about.

The values alignment was not stand-out but no red flags either (strongly aligned on impact and collaboration, but our mission didn't really come up and their answer about receiving feedback wasn't very pro-active, as they talked about receiving feedback in a setting where everyone has to get feedback (section leading preparation).

Not a red flag, but also seemed like a low-stakes disagreement and was not a particularly insightful answer.)
Receiving feedback - 3
Giving feedback - 4

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I enjoyed my discussion with Jason. His experience in the role showed that he knows which traits to look for when hiring BSRs, which leads are best followed up on by BDRs and the power of staying the course with outbound strategies. He seems like someone I can partner with. I am a yes. 

What do you look for in potential SDR hires? What are the traits of a killer SDR?
1) One who unblocks themselves
Its a job where you have to move quickly and live in grey
Display critical thinking
2) Someone who's not satisfied getting to 100%
An SDR who runs through the finish line versus to the finish line
Self motivated
Tests this by asking about goal setting questions
3) Are they deliberate in their choice to be an SDR?
Its a tough job
Jason weeds out folks who want a job at a start up and don't care what the job is
Tests this out by asking, "Why sales" "Why SDR?", "Why and BDR here?

Can you share how you tripled your team’s monthly output from 50 to 150 sales meetings at Monte Carlo?
Hiring had a lot to do with it - grew the team
More SDRs because accounts sat in "pending"

Grew quite a bit on inbound side
Webinar leads
Content leads
Learned how to convert lower intent leads with A/B testing
Responds well to "we have knowledge, you seek knowledge, lets chat"


Can you share more about your Outbound workflow and strategy at Branch?:
Branch's messaging was good - the challenge wasn't the messaging
The problem was marketing would give a great sequence and SDRs were not executing against it
Aligned SDRs to executing the sequence to the very end

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Rubric: We should hire Lisa. See the quotes
1 Identification of market opportunity and value to the business - Strong Yes through data and being scrappy to get it
2 Identification and validation of problem including target user + pains - Strong Yes. Even without resources and recognizing proof of value
3 Effective communicator - Strong Yes clear they understood our needs and communication well
4 Collaboration with Eng and stakeholders - Strong Yes!!
5 Collaboration with Design to iterate and land on a solution + get feedback from users - Yes. Didn't get signal on design but yes on the rest
6 Ability to move fast and iterate - Yes. Clear they have a funnel on it
7 Collaboration with PMM on release process - no Signal
8 Hyper-focus on users and solving for their needs - Strong Yes
9 Ability to say no and make tough decisions (e.g. cutting scope) - Strong yes
10 Scrappiness (e.g. can they make decisions in the absence of a ton of user input, did they learn anything new or stretch themselves during this work) - Strong Yes
11 Data and metrics-driven (whether qualitative or quantitative) - Strong Yes
12 Are they set up for success with our current EPD culture? - Strong Yes

Raw Notes:
* Staff PM for customer experience and growth teams (25 engineers across 3 different teams)
* Wants interactive and ask questions as we go
* Really good at remote presenting 
* Seller Portal Ramp-up Plan (Q1 ’23)
* Goal: Increase number of new users activated by 3x
* Team was focused on driving new signups and then product team was focused on activation through the journey 
* What’s going well -> high product retention rate
* What’s not going well -> NPS scores were low (something about product eligibility), low activation rate, high manual interactions with sales and support team
* Must Have Features:
  * Get a report for each activation step
    * Experiment in each phase
    * Saw a lot of leads from marketing team but not gaining the results from it (looked at the leads on by one -> 95% were wish so it’s mostly just small side hustles, saw customers weren’t qualified (most likely bots))
    * Goal: Clear activation rate
  * Enhance onboarding (activation checklist, product tour, redesigning the onboarding flow -> make the barrier to entry lower)
    * Users aren’t familiar with the industry and they aren’t ready to get to the ah-ha moment cause they don’t know the industry (good user persona)
    * Goal: Drive customers through the journey 
  * New user feedback
    * Gather feedback quickly and then on quarterly basis adjust the roadmaps (rather than the NPS score)
  * Scaling infrastructure as a technical issue to consider (*)
* “We were conscious about eng resources” so I set up the tour and checklist myself and then did A/B testing on coverage rate 
* If any growth pm tells you they will move a metric, they are probably bluffing until users react to it
* So did experiments before getting eng resources (increased conversation from 25-30%) and then build it out with engineering team
* Success Metrics:
  * Activation (15% increase -> $40M revenue, Reduced time because of support team waiting)
  * Manual Interactions (onboarding specialists went to lead generation)
  * Product Technical Stability 
    * Included the scaling of the growth initiatives 
    * Tech debt happens. So we need things to be stable
  * NPS 
* Launch Risks:
  * Product Features (will things improve stuff)
    * Gathering leading indicators to be confident moving metrics 
    * Is this a short term solution or long term solution for engineers
  * Product Scalability 
    * Can we handle scalability (work with sister teams to meet this)
  * New Leads (“do we have the right things on other teams’ roadmaps”)
    * What are workarounds when they don’t have capacity
* Chris Question: What was your team like? Interacting with other teams?
  * Started at head of growth not growth PM 
  * Very clear it was more PM related role
  * Started with activation funnel 
  * Had to get resources to build up the funnel (but all the other engineers were busy so I hired one engineer)
  * Then from features roadmap 
  * Set up experiments to focus and company should prioritize 
  * Then it was easier to give the value proposition to get additional headcount + 3 engineers to build 
  * Then had a team of 4 engineers 
  * Grew to support other onboarding 
  * Product teams to support integrations etc (self serve team)
  * Sync a lot with support and sales
  * Two customer feedback sessions a day 
  * “Funnel to other teams as well”
* What was the plan for maintaining the experiment? 
  * “Growth is not on my mind. It’s on everyone’s mind”
  * “Every team is focused on revenue and metrics”
  * Everyone has the same metric and drive it
  * Makes it easier on my end 
  * I share all the inputs daily or twice a week so I’m not the only one seeing the problems -> building context across teams (“clear dependencies teams”)
  * No initiatives are surprising but the product team is allocating resources to me to help 
* Chris question: Jump 5 years. What’s the ideal customer experience? If I could do x? 
  * Lots of kinks around activation and retention has been ironed out now 
  * Nothing is hard blocking customers from experiencing stuff
  * Margin of impact is small now
  * So now with growth is to grow customers and provide different capacities 
    * Now I’m mostly coordinating cross functional initiatives 
    * Changing pricing and requires cross functional
      * Product focused and marketing focused
* How teams grow the customer personas can change? How do you consumer that
  * Our providers are the same but the businesses are large
  * Customers are still similar 
  * Priority of focus on specific segments has changed (originally focused on home products, now we focused on apparel)
  * How do we target this customer over other customers? (You need to focus on different things for apparels)
  * Define the target segment with other teams at the beginning of the year
  * “Every PM on our team is capable and smart…we just need to provide a guideline from the growth side so they can go and figure it out”
  * “My role is not to tell people what to do. My goal is to communicate and help define the problem they need to solve for and provide the information” (******)
  * “I’m not going to micromanage the PMs. I’m giving them context so they can deliver on their own metrics”
* Chris: How do you learn about customers as you join a company?
  * Manual and iterative approach 
  * Read the industry reports (get high level context)
  * “You are the lowest person with customer insights. So talk to support teams and learn from them”
  * Ensure you are talking to customers frequently. 50% talking to the user themselves 
  * How can you utilize what you already have? (OSS vs Paid)
  * Watch users move around
  * “Sometimes you just have to look into the database and figure it out”
* Teams can be strapped. How have you handled conflict on roadmap?
  * “It’s not pleasant and can depend on the personalities”
  * Start by understanding what the team is caring about and if that aligns
  * Start by asking for context and ramp up on it (what can I learn from)
  * So then when I ask questions they are more in depth 
  * Every team is busy but they should be be able to spare 30 minutes so understand if we can just do touch bases on their end
  * Influencing roadmap: important for the company and team tracks together (“we stay away from tech debt cause that also affects revenue”) 
  * Keep the scope minimal or just show impact is high 
  * Those teams know their systems and code base the best. “I don’t design in isolation. This is something they are doing to drive metrics for my team”
* Fear that people hate you going in

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Great candidate who has developed a framework for thinking about products, is very professional and thoughtful.





======================== 
Raw notes

 
Interested in software development teacher 
 
TA a course that teaches ocaml 
 
Passion doing little mobile apps, implementing a shell 
Ultimate goal for software engineering engineer, 
 
intuitive, intentional and robust software that solves real problems 
why? 
* Intuitive, always wanted to create something that is straightforward, self documenting 
* Intentional, whenever I develop things, I should keep the users in mind. One of her biggest inspiration is suzane care, first set of logo in use in the mac. She put a lots of thoughts. Chose a smiley face over a realistic face so everyone can relate to it. But also intentional for developers 
* Robust, because people can rely 
 
Love with proving things 
 
Project that embodies these three qualities? 
Research project that works on property based testing. You have to define properties for yoru test, have a a generator for inputs to test these properties. 
Intuitive: kind of realized of unintuitive it was to use that system. It involved reading source code, the python base, the haskell base. She volunteered to make changes to the infrastructure and make it easier to use. Made it support multifile parsing. You had to copy everything in a file first. Now it's more natural as it finds everything it needs. 
Better integration between the python/haskell side. Relates as well to the intentional part. At the beginning it was developped without a clear idea of which side would do. It made it really bulky. Tweaked things around so now a side won't repeat the other side and seamlessly integrate. 
Robust: tried to test the workload added clearly. Tried to write other generators. Also wrote a type oriented generator, which basically is directed by the type. So if it's a string, it will generate a string. Also always try to wonky examples. 
 
 
 
What do you hope to get out of an internship? 
 
Hope to see the application of functional programming in a real context. So far had only done it in a teaching context but not in a real context. 
Having more industry experience. 
Collaborative setting 
 
 
projects with disagreement 
project with collaboration/communication issues? 
 
 
Part of TA, teaching. 
Usually conflict avoider, but if there is something she truly stands behind, she would not net it go 
One time, they were teaching the concept of fold. 
Her co-ta wanted to teach it in a way that breaks down the whole recursion. 
She didn't think it was the right way because students hadn't been introduced to all these concepts. 
So she insisted as describing fold as the library website was doing. Like inserting a + between each elements. She does agree the recursion is fundamental. 
In the end she explained her thought process and ended up teaching her way before introducing recursion and then explaining it in full. 
She feels it helped her students learn better. It was a good disagreement 
 
What if it happened again? 
She would not make it feel like a disagreement. More like the two of us, together, figuring out a problem. Instead of me being the advocate for one way and him for another way and having that weird tension. 
Worked more as a team against a problem. 
 
 
 
======= 
 
 
 
# An anagram is defined as a word, phrase, or name formed by rearranging the letters of another, such as cinema, formed from iceman. 
 
# Here are some examples of anagrams: 
 
# - EVIL - LIVE - VILE are all anagrams 
# - aab - aba are anagrams 
 
# However ("aab", "abb") are not anagrams. 
 
# Questions: 
 
# - Could you write a function or method which takes 2 strings as input and return a boolean to state if they are anagram of each other? True meaning they are anagram and false meaning they are NOT anagrams. 
 
 
 
def isAnagram(s1, s2) -> bool: 
    s1_dict = {} 
 
    for c in s1: 
        if c in s1_dict: 
            s1_dict[c] += 1 
        else: 
            s1_dict[c] = 1 
    
    for c in s2: 
        if c in s1_dict: 
            s1_dict[c] -= 1 
        else: 
            return False 
        
        if s1_dict[c] == 0: 
            del s1_dict[c] 
 
    return len(s1_dict) == 0 
 
print(isAnagram("aab", "abb")) 
print(isAnagram("EVILS", "LIVE"))

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Katrina got 90% of the way to a correct type solver solution.

Her issue was that she came up with constraint generation, where she essentially globally accumulates constraints on each variable, but forgot (or ran out of time) to verify that all her constraints were cohesive. She added a manual step in the toInt case where she checks constraints, but it actually only needs to happen at the end, and it's also still not necessarily correct if she later accumulates another constraint.

That being said, I am impressed by the ability of someone who is not aware of the algorithm, to essentially invent unification via constraint generation + solving.

I am not giving a strong yes, but I believe this candidate is halfway to a strong yes. My concerns are that her code was messy in quite a few places (using `=` to case instead of pattern matching, shadowing her constructor names, using a list instead of a map), and she also took a little bit to get going. Her representation had a fair few issues before it got to the right place, such as staritng `Var` with no args, then `Var` with a single arg, adding `Ill` as a variant to the type instead of making it an option or something, and conflating the `Var` case with the int or string that it stored.

Half of these are maturity things, the candidates surprised me with their well-versedness in OCaml in some places (such as familiarity with `ref` syntax, and being able to correctly write an instance of `List.fold_right` with no documentation), and also made novice mistakes that made me quizzical, at times. These I am not so worried about. But the difficulty in crafting the correct abstraction is definitely non ideal.

That being said, the candidate performed admirably at iterating on their solution. Many times, they would realize there was an error in their abstraction, and iterate upon it by realizing what it actually should have been, and changing it to be correct. She was able to relatively quickly propose solutions to problems she ran into.

My concern is not a whole lot of her time was spent on validating her solution. She would have an idea, and it would solve the problem she was dealing with, but she wouldn't actually run through the examples. This came up when it came to her solution being partial, because she wasn't able to have tiem to think through the actual part where she needs to discharge the constraints.

My rating is a 3.5.

"There are 5 kinds of expressions, 3 of which are values"

Asks something about variables

Gives the canonical OCaml type.

toInt of int * string though...

var maps string to expression, not type...

Had to clarify that the varibale maps to type, not expression

Two variants for varint and varstring, with the actual bound exp...

Asks if she should return the type in a string, I said to define a representation for the type
shadows her Int and String constructors

uses begin, lol

is confused about how to define the context, possibly because its embedded into the AST

I ask her to write an example, and she realizes it wouldn't work, she doesn't know the specific integer, only the tyep

Var of exp_type, which is fine but will be wierd in part 2

HAve to call recursively to see the type of th esubexprs, and have to check if theyre a valid pair.
Both int and both str are valid fro the plus operation
"if t1.= t2 then return one of their types", else failwtih
(actuallly adds an ill typed variant to her exp_type)

Upon giving her more examples, she realizes toInt takes in an exp.
Identifies toInt must take a string

Finished at 1:18, feels like its good. I asked how she would be clear that it's good.

Explains her thought proces, of course she starts with the simple cases and they're obvious, but realizes she needs to recursively check the subexprs.

questions.question: Part 2 - Type Inference: Notes
questions.answer:
"First of all, I have to change the way I pass in the context. Rn we assume each var has a type, but now we can't"
"Could make it an option, or maybe a better way would be to pass in a list of tuples which maps variables to types"

"For checking if its possible, let's say we're in Plus, 3 + a, where a is not bound. Then, we could assume that it works first, and
assume that it typechecks and returns int."

Asks if I think it would work, to return the types up and see if it's cohesive.

3 + a, don't know if a is an int, but know 3 is, so if one is a variable
because of var, will check context, if doesn't exist then return the type for 3

Identifies a + toInt(a) as the interesting case
Based on toInt, assume its a string. Then, when I go to the previous recursive frame, realize that this ends up being an int, but a was decided to be a string. Maybe modify the context somehow along the way.

Having a mutable context? Do you think that would work?

Thinking if she should make it mutable or not, because if its mutable then she can add values as she traverses.

got the parens wrong for the type, * binds less tightly

commented out her varcase, but realizes she still needs it

realizes she needs to iterate the list to find the mapping for the var

added a Var case to her types

remebers the type for List.fold_right!!

I pointed out the parens in the type are wrong

"new case, fi eitehr of them are var"

tries to do case where t1 is var, thinks she should return just the type of t2, and add the binding 
assume t1 has same type as t2, return t2's type
realizes she doesn't have the reference to the string

I ask her what she could do, she comes up with storing the string in the var type, but realizes can't do comparison

Decides to propagate the var up in the double var case. Why not.

realizes that the rest are ill cause she already checked the equals case

What if I change my context to allow a binding of a to the Var type?
I ask her how she would validate that.
She states hte problem is if there atw ovariables, both of which she doesnt knwo the type of, she can't guess their binding as the result,
she has to return Var. But having the string in the Var lets her pass up only one string. Now she has multiple variables.

If add to string, of course it binds to string. But if bound to Var, ahve acess to all the variables, no amtter what recursive frame.

In Var Var case, can't pass anything up or change the bindings.Realizes she needs to still know the name of the variable at the time she wants to make a binding. But before, had to pick one, could only have one to pass up.

Realizes she can have a list of names. Var of string list. But she feels like that will make the impl a lot more complicated.

Every time she wants to check, she has to consider the multiple cas,e and find the binding for each.
asks if it is possible to use some tree to do it
realizes the expressio nis itself a tree

I recommend we jhust get going with a solution, she decides to try the stiring list method

If the left is var, add the binding for all the vars into the context
create a new list with all the new bidnings, to add to context

I realize, she's actually doing just constraint generation here... which is pretty cool

toInt, if it's string then of course it's fine, could be int, but realizes it could be Var too
is using ifs to pattern match, basically...

have to check if all the vairables are of type string

unfortunately is doing the solving hand in hand with the generation here, is gonna check the context at the toInt case

We were running out of time so I asked her to summarize what she should do to me verbally
For the toInt case, she said she would check the context for whether or not all the bindings in Var were of type stirng. Then, seh would add a binding to String to each, and then return Int.

I then asked her how she would return just a bool for the function. She said on Int an String, return true, but or the Var case, she gave an uncertain true. Ill case definitely false.

This is almost the way there. You have to check for conflicting constraints. But she's almost exactly there.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways

questions.question: Do the candidate's interests and values align with Semgrep?
questions.answer: Yes, really interested in functional programming, cares about making an impact. Writing good software is important to them

questions.question: Summary
questions.answer:
Raw notes:
 
Art, EMT, software engineering. Really interested in functional programming, taught functional programming class, likes first class functions. Ultimate goal is to create intentional, robust software solutions to do something real world. 
 
> How did you find out about Semgrep? 
Grace Hopper! Didn’t realize there was real world application of functional programming. Through talking to hiring managers, team, have learned more about what semgrep does 
 
> What have you heard from hiring managers that is interesting to you? 
Remember hearing about the product where you can find vulnerabilities. Interesting that you search your dependencies in addition to your own code. Also find secrets product cool. Generally value nice developer tools so that they can make good products for other people 
 
Want to work in a collaborative environment. Most previous work has been independent. Really nice that you work together towards the same goal. Eng focused is a plus 
 
> Anything else you would want culture-wise? 
Would want to know more about interns in particular, how they play a part in the big picture 
 
> What kinds of projects do you prefer? 
Like a mix of big projects and small infrastructural/documentation things to do on the side. For research e.g. have a big goal and like writing a lot of code and having a big project but also sometimes I’m stuck and need something else to work on 
 
> Do you rewrite or alter existing code? 
Prefer to work with existing code, rewrite if needed 
 
> Were there any projects that were challenging that you want to talk about? 
Property-based testing. Liked it because functional programming and it was pretty different from other projects. Assigned to work on an evaluation platform for property-based testing. 
 
PBT is a technique where you specify the properties your program should fulfill and you generate programs that check if your program has properties you stated. 
 
For example, if you have a function that sorts a list, a property should be that the output list should have the same length as the input list. Another is that output list should have its elements sorted in increasing order. Specifically was trying to test property based testing on parsing and pretty-printing. Interested in round-trip properties 
 
[Stopped herself to say “let me explain this better”] The platform works by injecting bugs 
 
Challenge: when I tried to write a more type-directed generator and didn’t have that many constraints (<gave example>) realized that even if I didn’t give that many bugs it still failed the roundtrip. This is because it generated ill-typed code from the start. Did a little work on improving the infrastructure on how to include preconditions 
 
Wrote a blog post 
 
Did you do this alone or with other people? Brainstormed with other people 
 
> Biggest takeaway? 
Had to keep a log on everything. Didn’t keep a log initially, would forget what she had tried and the result. Takeaway 2 was to think about the details 
 
> Time when you disagreed? 
In recitations, teach with a co-TA. We had a disagreement about how we should teach `fold`. Students haven’t learned the recursive frames, etc. co-TA thought it would be nice to destruct `fold` in a recursive frame. Write it out and show students how it pops up. I thought because they haven’t learned this idea, they’ll just be even more confused. I suggested to just write out fold as you see it in the OCaml library, by example. co-TA really believed in going into the fundamentals. Ended up settling on first teaching it my way, then bring it back when you get the new concept 
 
Main takeaway: next time if there’s a similar argument, not see it as me against other person but see it as us as a team against the problem. Both have the same goal 
 
> A time you had to give feedback 
(Was a little confused by this question, asked for clarification. Austin gave the example of a student in OH) 
 
Not confrontational, would definitely not say anything like that”you did not study”. Generally would start off giving student the definition, resources. 
 
> Another example: 
Worked with friend on an HCI project. Noticed there was duplicate data. Explained to her why I preferred it doesn’t have that much duplicate. Gave feedback in a mild way. Because we were friends, it was casual, she just made the change 
 
There was a lot of data we got from Twitter, we were trying to join them 
 
> Feedback and it went wrong 
Pretty rare that people don’t take my feedback well. I generally try to not cause conflicts. Remember there was one time in freshman physics class. I was really certain my way of doing it was correct. Teammate just refused to listen. Was not fun but I tried my best to write out all the equations I know. Explained enough for him to doubt his point of view 
 
> Times you’ve received feedback 
Good feedback - time I wrote the blog post, got helpful feedback. Wrote too much in some parts, in some parts wrote too little. Got a lot of comments before final revision. In the beginning, took it a little personally 
 
> Questions? 
Difference between expectation and reality? 
Favorite part of working there?

For example, if you have a function that sorts a list, a property should be that the output list should have the same length as the input list. Another is that output list should have its elements sorted in increasing order. Specifically was trying to test property based testing on parsing and pretty-printing. questions.question: Tell me about a time when you took the initiative to improve a process or procedure in your previous role without being prompted by your manager?
questions.answer:
At Capsule, was one of two App support engineers
His impact was adding support best practices that weren’t in place
Problem 
High ticket abandonment rate
No tracking, dump the tickets, no tagging or anything to gain deeper insight to common issues
Tickets not being fixed —> People couldn’t get put orders in for online pharmacy, switch to CVS, abandon their carts, not reorder their medication
Made sure tags and categories were set properly to move into Zendesk, created better experience 
Working with cross functional teams to set up ticketing
Created a lot of reporting for them, templated reports
Get an understanding of ticket patterns, MFA, login issues, and then tag it to things + checklist
Removed by 60%, measured by # of MoM total volume decrease in login bug tags

Colleague OOO, figure out terraform thing, get help to walk through terraform integration
