Do as much due diligence as possible for engineering
Impact was drive $30k ARR deal close

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Overall, I am a yes on Jonathan. Comes with a lot of customer support experience and has experience working with a developer audience from his latest role at Slueth even though it was very short. Through his examples, he showed the ability to outline his process of diving deep into an issue and how we went about narrowing down the root cause. Also displays an continuous improvement mindset where he had to learn new skills and tools to solve customer cases. He also seems someone who would work well cross-functionally with engineering and product and gave good examples where he aligned his efforts to the objectives of those teams to help deflect support case volume and helped engineering get visibility into the right issues that helped drastically lower the overall ticket volume for a product. He's had experience with the need to be scrappy working in smaller support teams and helping them scale by being data driven and using insights from support cases and documentation to deflect support case volume which is a huge challenge we are currently facing. The only reason I am not a 4 is he went on a tangent a couple of times and did not really answer the question that was being asked. With a slight nudge, he was able to get back on track. If we are confident in his technical abilities, he would make a good support candidate.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to dive deep into a complex problem to understand its root cause?
questions.answer: Sleuth. Common issue is failing to pull information from a 3rd party integration. One customer using GHA to get those deploys to show in Slueth. First step search for it and could not find it. Backfilled those deploys which also failed. Went to Datadog and reviewed the workflows and everything ran fine there. One Final step, leveraged the third party API to pull JSON info to search through all the deploys. Reviewed every line of the missing deploy. Noticed the status was not one that was commonly used. Could not recall what the status was. Reached out to engineering and created a linear ticket. Got clarification whether this was supported status. Engineering replied it was not and they won’t have time to support it in the near future. Presented to customer. Changed the status of the deploys of the system and they were able to see it in the system. What stood out? 3-4 different status that were common. Status was out of the ordinary and escalated to engineering. He also checked status of deploys in his test environment. No enhancements to improve troubleshooting the process. Only 2 support engineers, not documented. Helped improving documentation for integration to show supported statuses.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to learn a new skill or tool to solve a customer problem? OR Tell me about a time you found you needed a deeper level of subject matter expertise to do your job well.
questions.answer:
Capsule. App support engineer. New team with only 2 people on that. Engineering was doing support before then. Make changes to db, fix things. Worked with engineering to transfer processes from eng to support. Often times inacuuracies in the DB. Name/order that were not displaying info correctly. Engineerins would Jumpbox to SSH into the server and use scripts to update the data. Identified good candidates for transitioning to support team. Created tutorials, recorded it and wrote documentation for that. Help the engineering team write the runbooks. Prior to these processes Engineering team helped with 70% of the tickets in the queue. Thirty percent only after one year. This actually did not answer my original question so I asked the candidate about a specific example/skill. 
Learn to use MySQL. This is what is showing vs expected. Variables we had to fix. Plug into a python script. Jumpbox into production db, write python scripts. Beforehand had exposure to SQL. Linkedin learning class on mastering SQL. Additionally we had lot of examples that engineering provided. Manipulate existing queries. Lot of documentation online and chatgpt.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to communicate effectively with other teams to resolve a complex problem or achieve a common goal?
questions.answer: Capsule. Worked closely with the engineering and Customer ops team. Customer ops interacting with patients. Main objective from CTO was make number of tickets in the queue reach zero. Each team had diff objectives. With Engineering team, helped prioritze what tickets/bugs to fix.  Ticket volume had increased after they released a new product. Met with them biweekly to do sprint planning and outlining priorities. With the Customer ops team , identified areas where we could deflect tickets through training and reflected in zendesk through tagging. We would report all of that and present that on a weekly basis which issues came the most and which can be fixed on patient logging issues. Would take on those cases, get on calls with them and recorded different reasons they had different logging issues. Categorize everything and created detailed documentation for customer ops team and how to resolve the issue. That decreased the tickets for logging by 60% in a 2 month period before documentation was released. Biweekly meeting with all engineering leads. Children tickets to parent tickets in zendesk. Attach tickets from customer to parent tickets and track the number of times the issue occurred over one month. Seeing volume helped engineering understand and prioritize bugs. 1600-1800 tickets a month. By the end of the year, the ticket volume was 600-700.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Overall, I am a 3 on Michael as a support engineer. Feedback recapped above for each of the three questions. My only concerns are his ability to effectively work across teams and his short tenure at multiple companies looking at his linkedin profile.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to communicate effectively with other teams to resolve a complex problem or achieve a common goal?
questions.answer:
Particular project with developers. Build the CI/CD pipeline using CircleCI. One of the users of the product was experiencing an issue where the application was failing to scale properly. Had to work with the developers and the infrastructure team and the customers on the RCA. Asked questions to narrow down the root cause. Met with customers to understand the business impact, urgency and nature of the problem. Review the deployment process to ensure the application was being deployed correctly. Unhandled exception in the codebase that was causing the issue and causing lot of log files to spam the process. Created unit tests and integration tests to ensure everything was working. Pushed the fix to the production. Application was able to scale properly. Took 1 hour and 30 mins.

tl:dr - Not a great example. Did not highlight on how well he works cross-functionally and how he can navigate and influence competing timelines and priorities.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to dive deep into a complex problem to understand its root cause?
questions.answer:
Time at Microsoft. Ticket trying to deploy an App into Azure cloud service. Normal steps were all followed and the app was not getting deployed. Error was really vague on why it’s failing. Made use of the available troubleshooting and debugging tools. Checked logs and monitoring dashboard. This went on for 48 hours. What VM are they making use of. They were using an old version of the VM. Was able to reproduce it on my end. Attention to detail was key to resolving the problem. Discussed with the team. The VM was not compatible with the application. That information is not something exposed in logs. Upgrading VM helped resolve the issue. Feedback to the Microsoft product team to improve error messaging to help troubleshoot similar issues.

tl:dr - Showed good thought process of dissecting a complex problem , good customer communication and empathy, and showed initiative to help improve the product by providing feedback to the product team.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to learn a new skill or tool to solve a customer problem? OR Tell me about a time you found you needed a deeper level of subject matter expertise to do your job well.
questions.answer:
Opay is a payment gateway company where they create API integrations for different vendors and also have a SDK kit. Very familiar with Python and JS but not with Angular. Integrate with Angular application. Angular is built on JS. Come up with a script to convert what we had in JS to Angular. Learnt the basics of Angular and JS and come up with a script. Product team was swamped with tight deadlines and not necessarily my job. Shared the scripts for Angular with the customer. Script converted the javascript SDK into angular. Got blessing from the product team. Took a youtube crash course on Angular. Viewed small basic projects to understand the concepts. Refresher course on javascript. Took me about a week to learn and write the script.

tl:dr - Great initiative to learn a new language in a week's time and find a way to unblock the customer amidst competing product timelines.

Doing reference checks and reviewing their SourceGraph OSS contributions will reduce my concerns. For Opay they shared that they grew a team from themselves to 6, and built out support processes. When asked about how long this took, they shared it was over a 2 month tenure. This is a brief time period for the stated transformation/work they led.

Broadly, the candidate doesn't feel they're growing at their current employer and that rough waters are ahead, they want to get out before they do layoffs and immerse themselves in technical product with technical users. They don't find engineering itself fulfilling (or dev ops), but do find debugging and using their soft skills for support very fulfilling. Their strongest reason to hire from their self-description is a growth mindset + stronger-than-average technical background for a TAM.

I continue to worry they have not had exposure to exceptional support teams or processes, which is mitigable but concerning for an anchor EMEA rep.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Eric shared an example where he built out a AWS sandbox environment through scripting and automations for the Datadog support team who were struggling with getting environments setup in AWS for customer replications. Eric in talking to a few support engineers identified that it was taking support engineers a lot of time (1-2 days) to have everything configured correctly whenever they had to do customer replications in AWS. Lot of these engineers were not extremely proficient with AWS and were leveraging a combination of internal and external facing docs from AWS to get the environments setup the right way. Eric created a sandbox environment where support engineers would just need to pass certain parameters (size of the EC2 instance, AMI, name etc) to a script which would then go ahead and create the entire AWS environment for them. It would also help setup the right roles, EBS volumes for storage, logging etc and would have that entire environment setup completed in 15 mins or less. This was a huge time saver for the entire support team and this is now being used by over 400+ Datadog support engineers after Eric demo'd the solution to them as part of the support standups that happen. Eric demonstrated initiative when seeing a problem/opportunity even though it was not his primary responsibility to fix it. He also comes across as someone that's technically sound the way he walked through the script he built and the reasoning behind his decisions. 

Eric also has an interesting career trajectory where he's transitioned from being a AE to a SE role and seems to have found his true calling. When asked about his long term career goals, he mentioned he was a "SE for Life". He wouldn't mind having the Enteprise SE role as the title until he retires and feels that it's the role for him. He's been a top performer with the Datadog Commercials team where his deals contributed to 22% of the revenue for the segment in a segment of 30 SE's. He got moved directly to Enterprise and looks like he's had success closing and working with big accounts there as well. Given his experience and background working as a AE, he understands the sales motion and what it means to truly partner with sales which would be a huge advantage for him. The only reason I am not a 4 is his response to why Semgrep. He mentioned Semgrep being at a company size which was not too large and not too small and he doesn't want to be in companies that are too small as he would have to do multiple things beyond being a SE. I didn't hear excitement for the product which gave me a little concern but I am hoping that will be datapoints that get uncovered in the other interviews.

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
tl;dr - easy conversation, did not have to probe too much. Has goals + a drive to achieve them. Wants system where performance  leads to new opportunities (advancement, $) loves uncapped commission aspect. Driven, but also team-oriented. US counterparts, satellite experience. 

This is an exciting opportunity to be the first London based, and first international, SDR on the team. Our idea is that this person will build out the function, our customer base, and partner closely with our to be hired local AE and as we build out the team take a leadership position. Basically, you’re going to grow out sales in EMEA as our founding SDR.

Housekeeping
Do you now, or will you in the future, require sponsorship to work in the UK?: no
Timeline:  notice period is a month, ideal start date would be April 15th, could do earlier
Why are you on the market?: despite being a top performer, there is no way to accelerate and get early promotions. They are on more of a class system, and there are 4 levels, TC wants more of a merit-based system. Goal: purchase property for family, drive. 

Career History
Uni graduate, looking for part time job while studying. Essentially fell into sales, found a job at EE, a mobile network provider. Overachieving every month, stores top performer. Surprising to TC saw self as introvert, reserved, quiet did not see self as people person. Have a skill where people find it easy to talk to TC, can find common ground quickly. Relationships are important, listening - maybe does not speak as much as traditional sales profile. Wants to listen carefully and thoughtfully to find pains, common ground, try to figure out solution. Relatable, understandable.

Sales and rentals after Uni for a startup property company, that really tested TC. Sole salesperson, that experience led to TC to learn and grow quickly. Biggest strength = cold calling, developed there. Handle objections well, combat quickly. Not nervous on the phone anymore. LISN, emails, etc. as well.

Career goals?
Career goals = transition from SDR to AE role. Full sales cycle, has done that in the property role. Full, closing sales role SaaS. Climb the ladder, gain valuable experience. Leadership? Has done that in a previous role, even done that at Cvent. Videos of process of how successful, sharing tips with fellow SDRs whether in US or Europe. Team-oriented, but also very goal oriented.

KPIs/Goals/Attainment
Cvent on enterprise team, the way they are targeted they have their quarterly goal which is 18, have to have 6 qualified demos a month disco call -> good fit -> demo, AE will qualify as qualified demo yes or no and needs 6 of those monthly, also get targeted on the mvv generated, but is aligned with account executive. If they hit their quarterly quota, get a kicker. Aligned to 1 main AE who targets greenfield and that is the focus, and aligned to one other AE. On average, 2-3 per SDR.

Questions:
- Process
- What opp for growth looks like

questions.answer:
I thought that he had a great grasp on the types (and depth) of questions to be asking to uncover pain in a prospect. He came into the call very well-prepared -- obviously did his homework and was really only stumped on the pricing question (to which he deferred to an AE and used it to help set the next meeting). He uncovered other shareholders in the decision-making process and wanted to loop them in. Additionally, it feels like he's got a sales mindset and asked about progression to an AE. I give him a 4.

RAW NOTES BELOW:

Authority identified?
Asked how it is being a security lead at netflix.
Yes, through the need questions.
Asked if he’s the decision maker or if there’s someone else to loop in.
Asked for the manager's name and said that it might be great to share some collateral, get onto the next call, etc. Mentioned that the timeframe being tight and that this could help.


Need identified?
Looking for the need - Why are we here today? What are you looking to achieve? Very straightforward.
Asked for further information on “gaps and holes” in security.
“What are your current guardrails like and what does your ideal solution look like?”
Asked about the number of sec engineers to devs and how the relationship is working.
Are you using anything currently? Anything that has or hasn’t worked?
What’s your top priority?


Timeline addressed?
Yes
When are you looking to implement this? You’ve looked into competitors, so I assume it’s something that you want to handle soon.


General notes:
Personable – asked about Netflix recommendations – building rapport
Quick overview of Semgrep - Did his homework. Nice explanation of customizability and integrations through ci/cd and apis. Very transparent! Built value. Differentiation, customers that we’ve had, etc.
Any insight into price? Have an AE build a quote.
Booked the next call while on the call.
Is there anyone else that you might like on the call tomorrow? Engineers/end users.
Closing question about further questions.
He thanked Jason for time. Looking forward to the next call.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Team development & culture - Look to understand the candidate philosophy in building, leading, and coaching a high-performing team. This will be a 45 min workshop as the candidate explains how they’ve evolved their team.

Rubric: Pick a successful team that you’ve managed at least for a full year.
Explain quarter by quarter:
Attainment : %  and why (did they consistently meet / exceed forecast?)
New hires: how long did they stay, how did they do in their first 6 and 12 months?
Departures: why and what was the process? (did they tolerate low performance or did they make the right moves? Where they too fast? Too slow? What would they do differently?)
Promotions: Did you promote anyone? What was your role in their growth? 
I am trying to extract the signal whether they’ve inherited a good team and nibbled at the edges or did they build their own team and if so, did they do it well.

----
DATABRICKS
- startup division
- leaning onto SE was a flag

Okta was 7.5 years (employee 250ish) - first 4 years as IC, moved to DC to open east coast, and move SF
Databricks - brought into from a sales leader, sales leader left, 3 people who ran in 3 months - person that brought in is Aurora Solar - would not join

first thing
- hear what those theories are, dig-in, data driven approach, use as much data as possible. Problem statement is: not hitting target and pipeline issues,
- what is pipeline coverage, when we have pipeline, close rates aren't there, deal qualification, methodology, running after the right deal, identify strenghts and gaps in deals, where are the deals coming from (inbound, outbound) 
- databricks, joined at a unique inflection point for sgements, inbound leads were strong, but looking at numbers, we saw that we couldn't rely on inbound, educated on outbound motion and getting them to hold them accountable on building momentum. 
- campaigns for prospecting:
- databricks - brought over a program from Okta - pipegen counsel. 4 startup teams but each team taking their own approach and not a lot of collaboration. took 1-2 lead AEs that would meet and brainstorm ideas then narrowed in to 1-2 startegyies and teams could take that into practice. established collab but also establish sample size of what's working and what's not. 
- okta - best program seen in terms of marketing, SDRs, and AEs aligned. Commercial 1000 program - every AE identified 10-15 accounts with 1000 accounts, all hand on deck approach - marketing focused on accounts, SDR SPIFFs, AEs put emphasis on pipeline strategies, how we were doing breaking into it. 2-3x rate when they were designated. It helps streamline the approach so we weren't doing the same thing over and over again. 

How do you look for real pipeline
- standardaize - medpicc
- team wide deal review - 1 to many vs 1:1, poking on gaps, and learning 
- direct and talking through the fluff - forecasting and predicting the business
- confidenct and stand behind pipeline


- tapped to come to databrinks
- long stance at Okta
- standardize
- want more details, no data share, not impressed by his approaches/textbook'
- strong opinion - lacks
- balance out the leadership team - but will they respect him?
Soft yes

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Summary:
Kenzie is a solid IC1 candidate who exhibits IC2 attributes. I support a "yes" rating at IC1 or IC2 with the expectation of rapid development.


-----

Details:
Question 1
Can you give me an example of a process or procedure that you improved in your current or past roles? 
What was the issue with the way things were done previously? 
How did it affect your team, organization, or internal customers?
Feedback
Kenzie used a tool (GoodTime) in her last role and found team members were using it in a very manual manner, which was both inefficient and cumbersome. She did some self-initiated discovery and learned to use the more advanced features which increased her own productivity by an estimated 2x. Kenzie's manager noticed significantly more output when compared to peers and asked her document and disperse the knowledge within her team. 
While Kenzie was able to clearly identify an issue with her workflow and initiate taking corrective action, she did not proactively present the issue for broader implementation. 
Kenzie's actions and outcomes align solidly with IC1 approaching IC2. The issues encountered were scoped to a particular application and limited to her role but she has awareness of the impact directly to her own team and customers teams. She is also able to take general instructions and iterate/improve on processes.


Question 2
If you have an idea for a project or to improve a process, how do you bring awareness to it and sell your idea? 
Then how do you effectively present a solution and communicate it out? 
Have you ever gone through a formal process to define a project or process change?
Feedback
Kenzie immediate answer was: "Just do it". Her philosophy is a very clear sense of action over dwelling which is a good fit for a small and agile team whose philosophy is a close match. However, while taking action, failing fast, then refining can be a good approach, the more thoughtful and considered approach also has its place, especially when constrained with time or cost.
Kenzie does not have experience in a more formalized initiative or project execution that details a problem statement, solution thesis, risks, costs, and comms/execution plan. That being said, she is familiar with the aforementioned concepts and was able to acknowledge the benefits of each. More importantly, she was enthusiastic to learn and implement a more formal approach to more complex issues.
With respect to initiative and project execution, Kenzie strongly exhibits IC1 characteristics while aspiring to characteristics of IC2. Her willingness and enthusiasm to wrap changes around some process balanced with a "just do it" attitude is a combination that seems to align with the company.

Question 3
How would you proceed if an initiative was unpopular but it’s still the right thing to do?
How do you make a case for your initiative or proposal?
How do you deal with an unresponsive or even an unwilling customer or vendor?
Feedback
The focus of this question was to elaborate on conflict resolution and handling blockers to an initiative. Kenzie's approach was very qualitative. While she would compare past and present performance has been her primary benchmark to prove some level of success, she found it difficult to describe how she could be more convincing in a quantitive manner. 
A standout part of her answer, though, was her emphasis on being personable, empathetic, and addressing issues through direct conversation.
Kenzie did not touch on more industry-standard concepts like gathering empirical evidence and escalation. However, her natural emphasis on empathy provides a strong basis to build expertise around conflict resolution. This aligns with an IC1 with a strong affinity to IC2.


Question 4 (Skipped)
What are some ways you’d measure whether or not an initiative is worthwhile? 
How do you keep a pulse on success after rolling out?'
Feedback
 Skipped due to time constraints.
Some aspects of this question were covered in the previous question so this question would not add any additional signals.

Question 5
In a role that you don’t have extensive experience in, what steps do you take to improve your own skills and excel?
Give me an example of going above and beyond expectations.
Feedback
The main theme from Kenzie, similar to question 2, was "just do it" and "learn by doing".
To introduce a new direction, I prompted Kenzie with the concept of execution, regression testing, and iteration/refinement to which she was very receptive. She was able to apply the concept back to question 1 and describe how this approach is important to continuous improvement.This demonstrates Kenzie's receptive attitude which doesn't necessarily signal a level per se, but strongly suggests she is open to critical feedback and can map concepts to practical applications.

Main questions



Describe a situation when you were able to help out an employee or team member. What was the situation and what was the outcome? (KM)
VErkata- trained the next person that came in, daunting, helped me feel like I know what I'm doing. She's doing great, we're still connected. She didn't know anything about the recruiting space and now she's the senior coordinator on the team.
- what was your approach? I'm very hands on - trial by fire, had to learn her style, she was an observer, get a pulse check in the first day or 2, didn't want to overwhelm her. Figured out what's best for her. Everyone needs a different approach. I interact differently with different people, knowing what people need. 
Collaborating with others can be challenging at times. Tell me about a challenging project that you worked on in a team setting. How did you work with others to overcome the challenge? (RR)
I like to take charge- owning it and making sure others are involved
Everyone’s heard but the 
Huge recruiting project- intake of every engineering of who can interview
People had different thoughts
Ended up speaking to every employee
A project that not everyone thought we had to do but it was going to really help streamline the interview process
Describe a time when a decision you made wasn’t well received. What was the decision and how did you handle the pushback? What would you have done differently if you could go back in time? (KM)
Candidate that didn't quite match the criteria/ what the HM thought was the perfect fit. I thought this person was amazing and would be able to do the job. I wanted to move them forward. My manager told me no. I felt the pedigree was more of a priority even though I knew they could do it. If I could go back I would have gone back to the hiring manager and explain the reasons why. I would have advocated for them more. I was very junior at the time, I was a recruiting specialist - I didn't feel like I had the pull to do that. 
Tell me about a time when you were unable to help peers/team members solve a problem. (RR) // When is a time that you had to work with someone difficult?
Everyone knew working with this hiring manager was tough
How i approached it was I tried to not pay attention it
Got close to the team that reported to him
Ask the team members about the interviews
Forming a bond with the team 
Keep a positive demeanor  
I didn’t let it affect how i worked

I told her it was noon
Always deal with the physical stuff first

Delivery person- real quick
Help employee

Probably the email I was working on

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Kenzie exhibited a lot of the qualities we are looking for in this role. She is wanting to have her "hands in things" and make more of a direct impact on the company. questions.question: Key Take-Aways

Conclusion: Kenzie would be a good fit for the office manager role. I'm a yes!

Pros
She has experience with customer service - her current role as a BDR at Semgrep has taught her how to interact and talk to customers. 
Her role as a recruiting coordinator (which can be highly administrative) has given her the understanding of the administrative bandwidth the office manager role would require
Kenzie has the right mindset and attitude for the role. There's going to be a lot of asks from all different groups and she's knows how to take feedback professionally and it's not personal (mature). Understands there's a lot of opinions.
Would like to work more in a team setting 

Cons
My only reservation is the facilities part of the work. The work that is involved in managing the SF office can feel very entry level.

Process:

Started as a social media specialist - responsible for discord, twitter, LI, for WHP and Bright. After a few months, started to take on a few more responsibilities - email marketing campaigns, within the last year promoted to a marketing manger position and now TC does quite a few different things. Also helps with all events, have a pretty strong webinar program they've been building out for about 1.5 years now. Run 1 webinar a month, 200 registrants, and about 40% of them attend. Organizes for webinars, campaigns for visibility, paid advertisements. Beyond webinars helps with virtual events. Now that conferences are back in full string, only join a few virtual events over the course of the year and helps with shaping those. Has taken over all of in person events as well. With those, responsible for getting the contracts going, negotiating that is needed, activities for the booth, working with VP of sales to decide who is going to attend, schedule to ensure everything goes smoothly. Work closely with SDR team as well to follow up on leads from events to see the outcome and engagement.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
• Very experienced in the marketing functions that we hope the community manager will help lead, such as webinars, and in-person events, and has done a lot of content creation, email marketing, social marketing
  • Mentioned that she's lead 5 webinars in a 5 month process recently (30-40% conversion rate of sign ups), and has been following up with email newsletters to generate leads, drive engagement
  • Has developed processes (e.g., 1 month before webinar she launches a landing page, 2 weeks before is when social media starts), but understands that each event needs individual attention to ensure that things aren't stale
  • Familiar with and uses project management tools (Monday, Asana), Google Docs
  • Collaborates with others (e.g., her boss, team leads) to help prioritize tasks to determine what's critical (everything is important)
  • Challenges faced:
    • Wants to be able to do everything herself, so it can be challenging to ask for help
      • Has learned that asking for help is not defeat, it's just that not everyone can do everything
    • Prefers perfect and late over done and good to maximize customer impact
    • Doesn't come from a technical background, so it can be hard to write super impactful content without first learning about the technology
    • When there's too much to be done, she reaches out to her managers to help prioritize. If there's still too much, she asks for help and hands off tasks
    • Learned a lot starting out via trial and error; that happens less now that she has more experience
  • Very proud of her 2024 Guide to AppSec Testing Tools paper: lots of research in an unfamiliar area, created action items for readers based on knowledge she learned to help users pick AppSec tools; used this to drive engagement, generate leads

Summary: experienced marketing professional with solid communication skills; knows how to get things done regarding events and content creation, and knows how to handle challenges. Has grown and continues to grow in her understanding of technology.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways

Pros- 
She's well rounded in setting up webinars, events, and social 
Very much like a marketing manager and has good experience running a lot of different things end to end. 
I liked her answers around 'what does community look like for you". Answer- community is about bringing together like-minded people to educate and not in a selling context
She had a good response to my question around how do you uphold the code of conduct/resolve problems with problematic individuals

Cons- 
Not as much experience in setting the overall tone/strategy for a community (see notes below)

Other notes 
Amanda seems more like a marketing manager verses what I would imagine a community manager would be. Based on what Tanya shared though this is the kind of person that we're hiring for so this should be fine. Although if our expectation is that this person will own all community efforts and be the face of the community/company like other companies then I don't know if Amanda is the right fit (I don't believe this is the purpose of this role but want to confirm with the wider group). 
Some companies classify Community Managers as being the face of the company for the community + being technical. This may affect what kind of skills/applicants we get in the recruitment pool

I am torn, I know that Tanya has worked with Amanda and thinks highly of her. soft no. There are no glaring red flags, but some pink ones as summarized below.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
tl;dr - do they have a community plan? Will they work with urgency (reserved, yes, may be confused with lack of urgency. Is it being confused by me?). Community centric, yes, but will they work strategically to make the community strong for the business? They shared one of their values is making an accessible product (meaning providing our OSS, something that is free), do they fully understand the implications of being Series C growth?

Overall though, candidate came across as growth-minded, hard working, and diligent. Good qualities and aligned philosophically. I'd like to explore the intro questions with Tanya BEFORE an offer to make sure the candidate is set up for success, and won't experience philosophical misalignment pain.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I enjoyed my conversation with Adam. He is driven and passionate. He is eager to advance. He and I started with his 'funding lens' philosophy (how to allocate capital among eng). Then, he talked about the advise from his HackerOne friend and book. He also gave me examples of his 'blocks' concept for planning. He understood the needs to seamlessly connect strategic planning and operations. He and I touched on the budget exercise (he did it at Meraki and here last year for $1.7M commitment). I also remembered well of his customer-driven problem solving attitude (a good product is to serve the untapped area and solve the pain points from the customers. The first prototype can be simple and raw. However, the first prototype with constant iterations will turn to a feasible product). We also talked about what signals we needed to look for in the next gen product investment. We had a good chat.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall a yes. Spike in culture setting and how they approach the role. Not sure about how they will advocate for engineering across departments and how they will work outward. 

Rubric:
Engineering Wide Culture: Strong Yes -> Leaned heavily on the tactic of setting vision and metrics and letting teams and people own the outcome. Showed clear experience in building culture and handling challenges around culture building. 
Engineering Leadership Culture: Yes -> Has experience being coaching rather than telling. Realized he was talking a bit abstractly and eventually got to an example with Brian's pushing. Worry alignment driving answer took a while to get to the heart of what I need vs what they need (i.e. planning) 
Strategy/Prioritization: Yes. -> Strategy was very project specific. Talked about how to get QC back on track while also keeping the team engaged. Wasn't clear how he sets better goals to ensure the team was bought in. Wants to approach first 90 days by listening, learning, and then saying no correctly with the needs of the business. Appreciated bringing up that not everyone will be okay with it but still needs to drive alignment to the end goal
Feedback: Yes. -> Doesn't shy away from tough conversations with their reports. When feedback doesn't land, focuses on why and how to make it land. Bit curious why he said that he keeps getting the same feedback from reports and how he grows authentically while knowing that feedback. You never have enough data so worry he falls back on data fast 
Executive Presence: -> No. I think this is just lack of experience but I wanted to hear more about driving alignment with Isaac, product, and other orgs. Wasn't clear how they advocated for engineering in the story but rather how they slow burn and handle change management 


Raw Notes:
Culture
* [Annika] Engineering Wide Culture: 
  * Give us an example of great team culture and how did you foster that?
    * Psychologically safety -> room to fail and learn 
    * Slack to fail
    * Concrete expectations on the goal but getting out of the way on tactics 
    * Supply Chain: My job was to paint a vision and pull people towards alignment 
    * See how their work is impactful and building trust that people “have the freedom to go build that”
    * See that shift on code SR teams -> working with Claudio there and went from everything (team wasn’t control) and then working to figure out what the mission and success metrics
    * Seen the team be more excited cause they are defining the how (Romain changing how we measure false positives) -> team knows they can measure 
  * Tell us a challenge you've faced in fostering an engaging and inclusive culture and what did you do to change that
    * At Meraki -> hired someone (senior level) and he had a “very different way to engage with the team”
    * Early flags -> he wanted to be handed the thing to do. Load across the team wasn’t distributive cause he was really passive in taking things
    * Started making comments that made things uncomfortable (all about himself) -> never crossed a line but got pretty close
    * First sets of conversations didn’t resonate with him strongly
    * Clear that the person who make other people feel uncomfortable 
    * Had to make it clear this wasn’t acceptable behavior 
    * During covid had virtual dance period to make people be a “silly authentic version of yourself” and not have people judge you
    * Ended up this person wasn’t a fit. Ended up exiting the person (language changed and the passivity did not)
    * How did you work on the team during this time -> Re-enforce the outcome of the team and what you need. “This person may need help on this project” 
    * Helps ensure the team is still bought into the outcome and culture (wasn’t breaking down the work, it’s important to break down the work and track your work, “Here is what is important to the team”)
* [Brian] Engineering Leadership Culture
  * Give us an example of a time you had to coach and advise someone when their field of work isn’t something you are familiar with. How have you approached this? 
    * Started as a cloud engineer, ended up managing ML, firmware
    * SRs, PA engineers here
    * “You can’t rely on your technical expertise” -> go to mentoring more of the time
    * “I feel like my role is setting clear, direct outcomes and be really curious. I’m never going to get to the level of technical expertise but I can be really curious” There are usually people who are excited to dig in 
    * Build trust and help craft direction, vision, with the team and the leaders of the teams. Hold people accountable to meeting those goals
    * Spending a lot of time in coaching and help people unlock themselves
    * Example of questions?
      * “Help me understand this piece that you glossed over” 
      * Take the 5 why’s. “Why is this part is important? Help me understand what the goal is”
      * Label my assumptions
      * Caught himself talking abstractly: Assumption for 10x rule writing is fast and so we should have 10x coverage. “We don’t have 10x coverage. Why is that?” 
        * Assumption came from 3 or 4 levels of indirection 
        * Hardest part of writing rules isn’t writing rules. It’s the research for what the vulnerability 
        * This feels like the wrong expectation the company has. What’s the actual goal? -> “I hope I get to be proven wrong” 
        * Maybe there are low hanging fruit to make it faster to rule -> maybe 10x isn’t going to happen but we can meet in the middle
  * Give us an example of how you’ve aligned different teams with different goals
    * Period of misalignment -> let’s be really explicit and raise it to the surface instead of pushing it away 
    * Have the difficult conversation 
    * Planning process for Semgrep should be in the last 9 months
      * Introduction of the big rocks ARR spreadsheet
      * Idea at the core: more visibility into what is going on, hold ourselves accountable to those results
      * Tactic was demotivating cause we took a team’s plan out of their hands
      * Misalignment with peers and through the chain
      * Unclear why this was happening 
      * Felt like my own autonomy was removed 
      * Shared the feedback over and over again 
      * “We can get what we want”
      * When groups are in conflict it usually means underlying meet is not being met. Let’s understand what those needs are “what our teams need is to decide how to achieve” what leadership needs is what is going to happen and hold people accountable to them
      * Feedback come earlier. Give them the knowledge. So by the time we have roadmaps 

⠀Strategy / Prioritization
* [Brian] Give us an example of how you’ve put together a strategy. How did you know it was the right bet? What did you say no to? 
  * “I have known it was the right bet but I’ve been confident enough to green light the bet”
  * Query Console -> I turned my mind around over time
  * A year ago: writing rules -> bug bounty -> then codify best practice but we need improvements in connecting code to that workflow
  * Felt like the plan was going off the rails -> team had to build social capital to get the buy in to do the work. Mission go really wild
  * Want to be a place for ideas but if you open up the constraints too wide, you can end being demotivated (cause of resources)
  * Hack weeks are another example (90% don’t make it anyway)
  * Query console -> whole new market. Code search product -> huge strain on eng, support, sales (company doesn’t have the bandwidth to open this up)
  * Put in the right set of constraints (“build this thing is too narrow”)
  * Recipe for success is being clear and setting clear goals 
  * “Has to drive adoption for the code product. Has to be within the code product -> same audience. Used by the same audience of people” -> driven the cost of the feature down. Customers are excited and now seeing the results “I was really worried that would demotivate the team” but so far seems like the team is good with those constraints 
* [Annika] What is your plan for approaching the first 90 days? How will you decide what is the most important use of your time?
  * “I am familiar with my part of the org but not others. So I need to dig and build context there. Talking to ICs, managers, directors”
  * Even for the people I was regulating working with, our 1:1s may have been constrained by other role and what they want to talk about
  * Understand why we live in this world. What are the problems that are top in mind. What needs to continue. What are people not paying attention to
  * Drew and I would pay different set of things but I want to make sure I expand from my first set of mindset
  * How do we scale to next set of ARR? We can’t do the same things we are doing now 
  * A lot of this job is what do we need to change. Doesn’t mean it is my job to go in and change it but facilitate the conversation what the problems are 
  * How do you balance the feedback across the org? 
    * There are 50 people. Spend a lot of time building context and over time rely on directors, staff, principle engineers but with occasional skip levels. 
    * Pulse surveys are good
    * Staying in touch with different practice groups (support, fe) 
    * Rely on resource groups (erg-women, erg-parents)
    * Job is not to make everyone happy all the time. Prioritize the business and its goal through engaging people and giving them avenues for success and goals 
    * We want to build a long term business
  * Each directors will have priorities that are really important to them. We can’t do everything. How do you determine the top 3 priorities we focus on as an org?
    * Get feedback from strategy of the business as well
    * Engineering can’t just decide we are changing PLG if sales and marketing aren’t aligned (row in the same direction as the rest of the business)
    * We have to get ahead of the 12-18 months 
    * Philosophically -> our goal isn’t to make resources distributed evenly. We should successfully complete the top 3 over getting the top 10 done
    * Some spread needed, but we need to consolidate our resources more 
    * It’s easy to agree on the top 3 and harder on the bottom 3. What are the things we are okay with deresourcing?

⠀Feedback
* [Annika] Constructive feedback:
  * Tell me a time a direct report gave you constructive feedback and how did you apply that?
    * “I am good at the coaching and nudging but I need to be more direct and concrete” 
    * I would rather be on the coaching side than be a dictator 
    * I need to move to holding people more accountable 
    * “Even being direct on their own performance where they know they aren’t meeting the bar would help them quite a bit”
    * This person is extremely effective in certain areas but struggling to find the right path 
    * Conflict around what is data here, ML and AI here
    * Strategy and founders are misaligned with their goals
    * Giving feedback and nudging them. Being direct on how they should operate here cause there is no clean answer
    * They struggle to work with other folks who work on the AI. As long as that is a struggle for them, it will hold them back
    * “I wish we had had that conversation earlier” 
    * Faster I can get into the old bits
    * Knowing when you have the right amount of data to make the decision is really hard -> underperforming -> “with the right set of feedback they can turn it around” 
    * Throughout the whole cycle, that person is really struggling. They are looking for better guidance “be clearer faster what you exactly what you need to do”
  * Tell me a time that you gave constructive feedback to a report that wasn’t received well and how did you approach ensuring the feedback was understood
    * Romain -> staff eng. Has been principal elsewhere
    * Sees himself as principal. Got hired with the assumption that he would be prompted within 6 months
    * HM wrote this up with a pretty vague need
    * “I don’t know the context. I don’t know what this doc means. I want to work with you but I need you to work with this team and this mission”
    * “What am I not doing to be principal?”
      * Talked about it. Feedback was that he was having trouble working outside his team
      * When he works outside his team, there is a lot of friction 
      * Instead there was friction and directors had to get involved rather than staff working to reduce it
      * Wasn’t stepping out of his land on the code product (sales eng, cloud) -> thinking through the outcome
    * Romain was pretty demotivated by the feedback. He wanted to leave “I’m very upset” and he left
    * I checked with the other directors to pressure test the feedback 
    * Talked with other people to understand what how to build trust
    * He was worried about getting boxed into a role where he had to stop doing the work that was impactful to “play a principle engineer”
    * Doing this remote would be really hard (timezones)
    * “I’m not asking you to give up this work. I’m asking you to be an advisor and spend more time influencing people. It’s just not done when you finish it” -> constructive here and he’s more motivated now. What does it look like to put the finishing touches

⠀Executive presence
* [Both] What’s an example of a time where you successfully advocated for the needs of Engineering? How did you convince other leaders to prioritize your team’s needs over their own?
  * Supply chain: Isaac wanted supply chain to be a loss leader. With 3 people
  * Here is what it would take to build that. We will launch a year later if we do this
  * “Here is our choice and constraints” 
  * Everyone here wants the same long time goal. We want semgrep to be successful and be impactful. Build a company we are proud of
  * There are a lot of different ideas on how to get there
  * All the work that goes into making the change successfully (something about replanting bonsai trees)
  * Lots of socializing an idea before you propose the idea -> catching people off guard if you just go into the idea
  * Combining PA and SR engineers (year ago was really scary) so let’s start with what is the fear so we focused on getting these people working together (we are doing everything teams would do but call it a team)
  * Downside is that takes a while

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I'm a soft 3 and on the fence - I think he can do the job that's needed right now,  he can likely learn and grow within the role, but for the long run he'll need support.

I think Adam would instill a culture of collaboration and curiosity to get alignment on his leadership team. There will be areas of obvious need for attention and direction - those areas I think he'd do well in and be able to create a strategy and plan around. But eventually we will get in areas that are less obvious and more obscure, which will call for making the right bet in the face of higher levels of uncertainty - and in those situations I didn't come away with the most confidence. While I think conceptually he provided some good ideas/examples, it took him some time to get into details that were a little lacking at times. Couple that with his admission that directness is a piece of constructive feedback he's had trouble actioning, I do have my concerns. Directness is a really core part of leadership at this level because there are things we will be saying no to and there is negotiation within Semgrep that will be needed to determine bandwidth and resourcing. 



Engineering culture: Supply Chain was pulled in many directions. Worked with Claudio on setting a mission that they are aligned to. Created energy and engagement towards that mission by giving more focus and direction
Challenge in fostering culture: Meraki - there was someone who used inappropriate language and made other people feel uncomfortable. Gave direct feedback to this person and eventually let them go due to performance issues
Coaching and advising in unfamiliar field: SR team - original assumption that we are 10x easier than competitors and we should have 10x coverage. Got to dig into that and realized, we're not actually 10x easier; so they had to change their goals and assumptions. Got to this point by being curious and asking questions.
Align people with different priorities: Planning process at Semgrep; shared feedback "over and over" and "being unwilling to quit"; leveraged non-violent communication - groups are in conflict when there are underlying needs that are not being met; the underlying needs are that individual teams need to decide their mission and Semgrep leadership needs to know the plans for the quarter
How do you know you're making the right bet? (strategy): thought it was missing on taking us into the process of defining the strategy. Conceptually it made sense that a strategy needs to allow teams to be autonomous but within a defined area. 
Prioritization across teams: Need to be curious about what other departments are working on and Semgrep's strategy. Then align the Engineering strategy to it


Constructive feedback: This one was the biggest flag for me. I appreciated Adam's honesty in sharing that he's constantly getting feedback that he needs to be more direct with others especially when it comes to individual performance
There was also feedback in which a staff engineer at first wasn't very receptive to the feedback that was provided but they later were able to clear it up and get on the same page (which I don't think is necessarily a bad thing)

+++ excellent attitude, i can see the drive/motivation. That + Joe's reference/back-channel is enough for me to give him a 'strong yes.'

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Pros: 
- Covered BANT pretty seamlessly
- Managed the call well, carried strong energy to execute
- He's a sales guy through and through.  I trust his drive.
- Left a small amount of time for slides by designs and loosened up a bit with his suit analogy.

Cons:
- He didn't close us.  Aasim offered that he would be giving him a good score, but no hard "are you recommending me, giving me a passing score?  If not, what would you need from me to do so."
- He didn't ask us what he could've done better.
- Too many words in his questions, rarely asked open ended questions (he asked which bucket we fell into typically)

Conclusion:  All the cons I've listed can be easily remedied through coaching/training.  He seems very eager and comes from a solid sales org with a strong reference in Joe Brown..

questions.question: Key Take-Aways

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Seems pretty confident in his experience technically. I was honest with him that he's likely more of a MM SE and he was ok with it. 

General:
-Has been at Datadog for almost 1.5yrs.
-They have a very strict in-office policy, 3 days per week.
-They may relocate to Washington DC because his gf graduates from law school and thats where she thinks she'll work. Which is the reason they're looking.
-He was a software engineer for 3yrs before getting into pre-sales.
-He got promoted 9 months into the role.
-He primarily works in the commercial segment. 1000 employees and under.
-There seems to be a weird commission threshold for SE's at Segment. 
-Pretty familiar with CI/CD; did build and release pipelines with gitHub actions. 

What they're looking for:
-Something remote. 
-Level and comp, feels he's under appreciated in his current role based on his SWE background.
-Enjoy's pre-sales, sounds like he wants to stay in it.
-Wants to eventually move into 

Interview Status:
-Actively interviewing. 
-Just started his search.
-Ready to start with a 2 week notice.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Really loved Stuart and he has all the makings of a great Solutions engineer. Started of his career in support, moved to software engineering and moved to sales engineering where he's found his calling. Loves working with and talking to customers and being in a technical role. Got promoted couple of times in support and got promoted in Datadog last year and might be getting promoted again this year. Datadog has strict in-office requirements and he might be moving out of Boston to Washington DC or somewhere else on the east coast which is why he's looking for a remote SE role opportunity with a technical product where he has the opportunity to grow and make an impact. Very hands-on and not afraid to roll-up his sleeves. Gave a great example of a pre-sales deal where they ran into a support issue that was a blocker. He worked over the weekend to reproduce the issue, dig through all the logs and found the issue before the support team could diagnose it and was able to unblock the customer. Also extremely self-aware and spoke about deals where he could have done a better job taking a step back and qualifying better instead of trying to solution everything the customer was asking for. Great communication skills and really easy to talk to. Also based out of east coast which is what we are looking for. 

Given the rest of the candidates in the pipe, can we move him to a tech screen + onsite for Monday of next week to help accelerate him through the interview process and make a decision by early next week.

strong 'yes' - a lovely interview.

Reads code fluently: 2
Able to step through code and knows what each line does in isolation but unable to articulate what each for loop is doing

Can read and write SQL: 3
Able to talk at a high level on what this is doing and what it would be used for. Can list limitations of using IP address for measuring activity.
Used it at flexport but I get the sense that historically they have not focused on quantitative measurement to make product decisions

Asks valuable questions: 4

Demonstrates the ability to ramp up in our space: 4
Many clear parallels with their work at Panther (rule authorship, false positives, prioritization, security team needs). Able to ramp up in many different new spaces. Able to speak sophisticatedly about our users and space.

Demonstrates the ability to speak with users (whether SWE or AppSec) and understand their pains: 4
At Panther watches lots of gong recordings and they generally use qualitative data on decision making.

Likes working with customer focused engineering teams and collaborating with Eng early on solutions

The way they make decisions and measure success in some ways very much aligns with what semgrep does (deal focused, qualitative) so if I had to find a reason to dissent it would be that if we wanted to move towards a more quantitative data driven product led roadmapping, candidate would be in the wrong direction.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways

- Favorite culture was at Flexport (1st year) , lots of growth, great smart people, learning a lot

Not worried about chasing tech trends (arguably ahead of trends - working on data science in early 2013/2014, building out bootcamp); ocean freight shipping at Flexport - not super glamorous but really important

Loves Series B stage companies; analysis & security space - sees parallels between us and Panther and feels like there's unfinished business from her time at Panther that she wants to see through at her next company

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Summary
Major pros: strong in design/user research/digging at problem. Effective communicator. Really good roadmapping to iterate toward a solution while delivering short term value.
Cons/questions: didn't get as much signal on scrappiness and details of collaboration with engineering


3: Identification of market opportunity and value to the business. Need to cross chasm to pragmatists was ID'ed by investors, but need to address security analysts was specifically ID'ed by candidate.
4: Identification and validation of problem including target user + pains. Clear ID'ing of problem at high level of granularity
4: Effective communicator. Easy to follow, didn't assume familiarity with domain.
3: Collaboration with Eng to scope solution appropriately. Effectively downscoped big vision, though would like to hear more about the nitty gritty/how technical this candidate can get.
3: Collaboration with Design to iterate and land on a solution + get feedback from users. Didn't get into details of iterations, but talked about moving filters into analyst workflow.
4: Ability to move fast and iterate. Oversaw good number of iterations on same problem within a year.
2: Collaboration with PMM on release process. Talked about release process but their role in it was a bit unclear.
4: Hyper-focus on users and solving for their needs
4: Ability to say no and make tough decisions (e.g. cutting scope)
2: Scrappiness (e.g. can they make decisions in the absence of a ton of user input, did they learn anything new or stretch themselves during this work). Don't feel like we touched on this
4: Data and metrics-driven (whether qualitative or quantitative)

Raw notes
scans while data streams in to be faster
crossing chasm from security engineers to security analysts (there are 10x more analysts than engineers)
when joined, vp of product, 2 pms, 2 designers
worked on personas while I was there
had done lots of user research over prior few years
led 1 year design vision project where we talked about what analysts and engineers would do together in the tool, crystallizing personas was part of that
most siems have sql-derived language
panther detections are python files in open source repo
would tell customers to fork repo, we'd continuously update, taking hours to fix merge conflicts. security engineers don't see themselves as production engineers, so can be slower at this, get frustrated
had data in productboard, usability calls, support input, lots to start with
looked into editing because editing was root cause of merge conflicts
set up interviews with closed-lost accounts where detection editing had come up
lots of sticky noting and affinity mapping in figjam
mapped out workflow in detection editing with pain points
can do things on webapp or on CLI for almost anything, so have to design for both versions and what would happen if people were using both
vision was merge requests through web ui, would take 6 mo though
saw many conflicts weren't complex logic edits, simple edits to exclude certain things from checks
so filtering before running detection; have customer code run before panther's detection
prioritized value vs impact (RICE-ish)
6-7 engineers + detection writers
filters for any keys/values applicable for the type of logs you're running on
later supported nested logic, more conditionals, etc
added more labelling in UI after usability testing
designer advocated for pre-populating things because analysts are less familiar with the data
e.g., only show operable values, bumpers
experiment to see if we could have analysts engage with detections as code, they did, but saw this screen as territory of security engineers
so, integrated into workflow--when reviewing an alert, in a drawer. Collaboration between Detections and Alerting teams.
"add as unit test" = use this log to test detections in the future
added filters to YAML file as well
success metrics
qualitative, deals, adoption, more users editing 
reflections
downscoping, lack of overlap between personas
what was collaboration with design like?
had weekly product and design meeting, no weekly product and design separate meetings
PM was responsible for "what is workflow going to be" and designer was responsible for interaction design
for filters, PM had to make sure usability research happened. Designers would run usability research. PMs would do general ux research.

Qs
personas: how confident research was representative?
tradeoffs to storing detections in Github?
design debt?

Katie's questions for us
How much is roadmap influenced by individual enterprise accounts?
Luke: Figma, lighthouse. Yahoo, bigger, less mature.
Do we think they're representative?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:

Did a good job at getting to the “why” - “why do you install these apps?”
Pretty direct and got to the point relatively quickly by asking the user directly. 
Iterated and proposed ideas live on the call, “If we did this how would that be?”

Great communication 
Was able to identify which features were absolutely essential versus what could be downscoped. 
Asked follow good up questions even while she was consolidating her notes which made me feel like she is collaborative.
Post need-finding: great questions about Semgrep and answers about what she's looking for next.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Overall I thought TC’s presentation was rather long — I counted ~43 mins (and we did not interrupt with many questions) — and at times I looked for signposts to see where we were in the presentaiton progression. The presentation was a bit too in-depth in certain parts (especially execution) and rather light in others (especially launch and post-launch iteration). We spent a lot of time in the deep details of the UI, too much IMO, and comparitively little on the launch and post-launch aspects (1 slide on launch, 1 slide on 3 metrics to measure success). I appreciated the metrics to measure success. On the launch side, I would have loved to have seen a more nuanced discussion that tied the scope and approach of the launch back to the product strategy of reaching a new target persona. There wasn’t much detph to the launch discussion other than ~there was a plan, there was messaging, and we had a tier 2 launch.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
tl;dr - candidate was well prepared and spoke thoroughly about a UX/UI set of improvements that solved similar problems to what we face here. They discussed the development of concept cars and personas, and the downscoping that followed to actually ship things quickly for the business. Main concerns coming out of this are: can they do it fast? Will they be overly process and (potentially) design oriented, and/or will they validate quickly and ship product that gets better every week. Negatively phrased: will their work have a Google bent to it?

In their presentation they spoke most about design, and only lightly touched on marketing. Of our candidates so far, they are strongest on design, weakest on marketing, looking at the presentation alone. Culturally they were aligned with our epd triad, saying many of the "right" things: "value vs. effort", the pm/em/design triad, "epd".

Why come to Semgrep to do work similar in flavor to Panther? Why do they need us? It seems they've been there done that before.

---

started a company - machine learning + coaching to get people better jobs

led product team from 6 to 18M in revenue

spoke credibly about all this

solved very similar problems to us: upstream merge of rules, handling false positives, 
Design leaning, thorough work.

found sub problems to solve: smaller changes
In-depth presentation, much more detailed than most candidates.
highly relevant and tied to our space (because it's Panther)
Slides were polished, with clear design + PM + sales focus, less PMM.


Culturally
EM, PM, Designer triad mentioned

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
tl;dr - Lance seems smart and driven, also seems adaptable. Competitive ex college athlete. Knows exactly where TC wants to land company wise, sweet spot Series B/C. We were short on time due to a technical issue with our line - so I wasn't able to dig into performance. Seems like someone who could hit the ground running and flex into whatever was needed of him. 

Flagging that Lance would be an end of May start, so not the immediate hire. 

Office: in office is great, go in 3-4 days now 
Timeline: ideally based on contractual obligation, best scenario would be an end of May (5/28)

Questions:
Team performance 
Logistics, processes 
Growth

Overview: growing up wanted to be a pro tennis player, played college tennis. Learning how to compete, work with a team, general discipline, work ethic. Not good enough to play professionally. So now, what do I do?! Brought him to 1st Republic. Incredible experience, credit and equity. Worked with Blackstone, gen atlantic. 3 years wanted a new challenge and smaller company with larger impact. OfficeHours = small seed stage company, employee 9. Second growth hire. Pretty much, a growth hire was a BDR and AE a CSM/AM all in one. Such a small company. A marketplace similar to AirBnB for consultations, go and learn more about Semgrep and talk to someone who works at Semgrep or a customer of Semgrep. Knew we were r2c. Covered the venture capital space, sourced their customers so to speak. SDR/BDR heavy role tied in with some AM responsibilities. Early 2022, expand to startup market. CEO appointed Lance to that. The money coming in not the best bc TCV is small given 2022 was train wreck for companies re: layoffs, budget constraints. Had to abandon ship on product, that was Lance's role and half the company.  Want to understand how do they problem solve. Bad = look at problem as problem. Wants to get back in the saddle, do something cool.  Coterie B2C Fintech. On contract with them, year that ends towards the end of May. Option to extend and go full time, but would have to let them know by mid May. Wants more pure play sales, when joined Coterie in the midst of product shift. 8 months into it, pivoting again. Ambitions of being sales/GTM paused. 

Why sales/SDR? (looking for a clear reason why they want to be in sales, have done research. Purpose) Looking at career, last 2 employers under 20 First Repbulic large. Cog in system, processes. Office Hours and Coterie, below 20 people a lot of blank space to work with but needs nibbles of traction before you can launch something. From a GTM standpoint, they have a product but looking for a true market and that product market fit. Small enough to pave path. 

Challenging problem: could be recency bias, significant = getting let go by office hours. If you told TC would have been let go first graduated would say no chance. Put in so much effort, cared so much, for that to happen even though it didn't' reflect TC it was devastating. What could I have done better? How could I have reversed this? Came to terms controlled what controlled, put best foot forward and have to keep head up. Feel that personally, have rebounded from it and learned about perseverance and need to continue to make opp happen.

Considerations

Wants to make a decision on company by May 1

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall:
-Strong athletic background, wanted to be a pro tennis player but moved into finance after college. Has a diverse background in sales and managing customer relationships. 
-Lots of startup experience, was the 9th employee at Officehours. Definitely knows what it is like to get scrappy and pivot in the day-to-day!
-I sensed a heavy focus on product marketing/the complexity of Semgrep. Heard of Semgrep when it was r2c and was interested in "how cool the product was."
-Creativity and curiosity were big values to him. This was great to hear. 
-Gets energy from other people, loves managing relationships, whether inside or outside of work. 

Pros:
-Understood the importance of actionable feedback and accountability. 
-Understands that when it comes to asking for help, it is best to put ego aside. There is no silly question. 
-Loves healthy competition, cited his work ethic and desire to be ontop as his super power. Cited his background in tennis as both an individual but also a team sport. 
-Asked great questions around the day-to-day of life at Semgrep and the ramp period as an inbound SDR. 

Cons:
-Took a bit of prompting when asked about a specific piece of feedback that helped him in his career. I guided him towards providing a specific instance when he was given feedback and able to change his approach because of it. 
-Did not get super specific in terms of his sales experience, he mostly discussed his intrinsic qualities as a person. I should have dug a bit deeper on specifics and prompted him a bit more.

I think Derian is a bit more junior than some of the other candidates that we've met with, but he was very easy to speak with and I think has the foundational knowledge and interest to excel the role.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, a 3. They started off really well and got to fetching the first page and its details very quickly. They struggled a bit more with handling relative links and the crawling and needed some hinting to get to a working solution, but they picked things up very fast and it seemed like with a bit of explanation and guiding, they could take something and run with it.

Coding styles: 3
Development process: 3
Culture: 3
