questions.answer: Asked a lot of clarifying questions throughout, especially with initial input (e.g. what they could assume about the links, valid domains, etc.)

questions.question: Code Style: notes
questions.answer: + 0.5 readable, since towards the end when they were debugging some of the bugs, the code started to get a bit messy, but not a dealbreaker

questions.question: Functionality: notes
questions.answer:
+0.5 relative links are handled
Needed quite a bit of heavy hinting to understand what to compare and how to parse the domain from the URL and why their original implementation didn't work. 

+0.5 outputs json correctly
Needed hinting with how to store a list of dictionaries. Also because we were running out of time, I told them that they could pass in the results through the recursion to help with outputting the correct result. 

+0.5 Crawling respects max depth
I think they were very close though we ran out of time at the end. If they had handled skipping seen URLs , they probably would've had an easier time debugging this.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Derian was definitely not very familiar with traversing GitHub and reading large codebases (e.g., I had to walk him through using the GitHub search bar). 

Derian had a reasonable understanding of the fix PR, and was able to identify the likely concern.  What Derian recognized, is that the PR referenced in the advisory shows code that is not vulnerable.  Derian took it upon himself to google the library used in that section of the code, which led to this discovery.  Because of this, I had to direct Derian to the vulnerable code snippet in a different reference (huntr).  
 
Derian was not familiar with internal/private function syntax in Python (pre-pended '_'), but after explaining it, he came to the conclusion that we'll want to figure out what functions within the paddle package may be using it.  Tracing back the function required quite a bit of handholding, but he was able to re-perform steps to eventually find the primary function (that we would write the rule for).

Derian was familiar with ellipsis and metavariables but needed some help in finding a good code snippet to test his rule on.  His initial rule was very simple and didn't consider the import statement nor did it use a fully qualified function call, but after asking him to reduce FPs and explaining some strategies, he seemed to grasp the ideas well.  

Overall, I think Derian has a good enough foundation to quickly learn the typical rule writing practices.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall soft yes on Derian. There were some times where I had to dig a bit to get him to talk about things, but he did express some vulnerability later in the interview. His answers to why Semgrep/what is appealing about security research were kind of weak, but not so much so that I'd give him a no considering this would be his first full time job

There are lots of jobs out there, why Semgrep?
The idea of doing security research is cool to me, I hadn't heard of Semgrep before the interview process, but I got to use the tool as part of one of the earlier interviews. 

What about security research is appealing to you?
There's a lot of areas within security. I wasn't really interested in being a SOC analyst or IT security person, I want to help build something new and learn new things. 

Is there an interesting project you've worked on where you had to build something new?
I had a class where we had to work with hardware using assembly. There are no protections for memory issues, and so we had to build in simple protections. 

How'd your project do? It was a lab and I got 100 on it, so it went well. 

Was there anything surprising or unexpected that happened over the course of the project? When coding at that low of a level, there's a lot of tedious errors that come up one by one. You expect that this is going to happen, so it wasn't really unexpected.

What are some things about your internship managers that you've liked or not liked?
My two internships were very different. At Rockstar I had an intern partner, and our manager said we were going to work on every task together, we would excel or fail at as a team. Our manager was always there to help us, and I liked that we learned a lot about working as a team. The internship did feel like homework since we'd get a task, we'd complete the task, and then get a new task.

At my second internship, it was the polar opposite. I could just pick tasks off of the team's backlog and work on what was interesting. I was much more on my own, and when I needed help it was more effort.

What was something you worked on in your second internship?
The main project was working on linux kernel modules. We were working on a hardened linux kernel. I was writing simple kernel modules to demonstrate vulnerabilities. 

What did a normal week look like at this internship?
It was pretty wild. I was writing two modules that demonstrated flaws in the kernel space. Another task I worked on was remotely trying to figure out if a system was running a normal kernel or this hardened kernel. Once I finished one thing, I would move on to a completely different task, so it was kind of all over the place.

Assuming you had someone to help you, did you prefer the style of working the second company or at Rockstar? 
I liked being able to pick my own tasks, but it would've been nice to spend more time in a given area instead of jumping around.

When you were working with your intern partner at Rockstar, can you tell me about a disagreement?
We didn't have very similar working styles, and if one of us couldn't convince the other person we'd apply our own methods and compare the results.

Are you able to tell me about a time when your partner was right and you were wrong?
A lot of what we were doing was writing scripts to automate reverse engineering using IDA. She suggested something that didn't make sense to me at first, and once she took the time to explain it to me it made sense and we explored her idea.

Can you tell me about a piece of feedback you've gotten that has helped you grow in your career?
There was a paper where a group had to write about a vulnerability. I got feedback that my section wasn't related to the surrounding sections written by other people. This made me step back, and spend more time planning. It was also a little bit embarrassing since I wasn't on the same page as everyone else.

I still sometimes jump into tasks without enough up front planning, but this is something I'm working on and I think back to the time from school since it is something I want to avoid.

What's one skill you want to develop in your next role?
I'd like to develop some leadership skills. I haven't had opportunities where there's been a small group working together where I've led the group. For tech skills, I'd like to build up anything I can. I'd also like to get better at asking questions, since in my previous internship I felt like this was something that held me back.

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
tl;dr - I love the marriage of technical + business. Did research on Semgrep, loved the JD called out technical background as a plus (CS degree). Showed some grit too - in Melo Park and ready to start immediately. 

Source: 
Office: no issues there 
Timeline: graduation 5/24, timeline wise free until then. In Menlo Park right now, immediately available. 

Questions:
- Strengths for SDRs?
- How performance is measured
- Company culture 

Overview:
- Background is in tech, or technical at least. Just finished BA in CS. Have done some technical internships, one with front end web dev and one at McKesson platform eng internship. As far as what looking for: something that meshes technical background with business, what TC is interested in. Really feel like - relationship building, working with customers, working with people - not being in front of computer coding endlessly. But would like to bring technical background with me. Better understand customer paint points, more deeply from a technical nature. Saw looking for CS background, matches what TC wants as well. 

What is the hardest problem you've faced? (frame however, academic, athletic, etc. Want to understand how do they problem solve. Bad = look at problem as problem. Good = solution oriented and wants to overcome challenge) -  probably, it was during the solutions architecture internship. The hardest problem = doing something called multi commodity flow tech concept that is a bit convoluted and vague. That is the most technically difficult project - people presenting to on NVIDIA's team, a lot of them have PhDs, technical problem for technical audience - that was challenging from a technical standpoint. Reading research papers, trying to get up to speed on what is happening and the problem. What made it hard was a new area, very technical - wasn't coding or anything similar. The challenge was not giving up on that jargon, and the papers upon papers TC had to read to get up to speed. For TC, I can do it attitude and led TC to overcome, successfully read and present findings and ultimately come to a good conclusion with then. Applicable to sales - there are some things, looking through Semgrep academy and things get quite technical, 

Why sales/SDR? (looking for a clear reason why they want to be in sales, have done research. Purpose) - it goes along with, interest in sales is 3 fold. The first = connecting with customers, relationship building, building trust the second = helping people, can be a win win situation where you are helping developers run the SA tool on code, try to find where the vulnerabilities are and where libraries or APIs are causing vulnerabilities in code, win for company and customer, and third = the high if making the sale. Have done some cold calling when working for a fine foods company, 500 cold calls. There is that high when you get a call back and they're interested. Competitive sports, scoring a goal = getting a sale/win.

Long term vested interest in becoming an AE - think that sales is a good match for personality and interests, and Semgrep being technical pulls in the technical piece.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Intro - Didn't set an agenda or ask for introductions from stakeholders on the call

Discovery - Didn't demonstrate much active listening. For example, she didn't pick up on signals or clues given and instead chose to follow her set of scripted questions. For example, I mentioned that we had some intitatives we were prioritizing and she didn't take any action to learn about those initiatives.
She was researched technically - did a lot of good prep to have some buzzwords and value props ready. Also had a good understanding of our 3 products - Code, SCA & Secrets.

Traps - Didn't dive into the competition when prompted, just moved on. Pricing was shared but only for the team level pricing.

Next Steps - Didn't take much initiative in driving for a short term next step. Also didn't come up in her self review - giving me a sense she doesn't have the instinct to drive to a short term resolution. Said she "didn't think about why it would be important to meet soon after the discovery call." 

Overall I didn't see very developed sales instincts.

questions.answer: TC seems to have very good command of FE for company websites. As a bonus, they also spent some time as a product development (SW) engineer, however they prefer being on marketing teams and the associated creative challenges. I felt I could go deeply into a conversation about pros and cons of different FE frameworks for marketing sites, tradeoffs some make compared to others, and things that a marketing site should prioritize. TC’s stated reason for winding down their consulting business was because it was very difficult to carve out a profitable niche (meant going for very small customers or very big ones). Overall it was a very positive conversation.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Score: 2

I went back and forth between a 2 and a 3, but after discussing and calibrating my score with Ben and Vivek, we came to the decision that it was a 2. The main flag that I wanted to bring up (as discussed above) is culture-wise, regarding when I tried to bring up handling relative links to their attention, it was like they just dismissed the feedback. I think it was most likely due to nerves and how they were quite pressed on time, but that aside, since their code didn't meet the requirements for a 3 in the rubric, I decided to round my 2.5 down to a 2. 

Functionality: 2.5
Coding styles: 3
Development process: 3
Culture: 2.5

- append the new urls if they don't exist

questions.question: Functionality: notes
questions.answer:
Did not get really far in terms of functionality. While they were able to fetch the first page successfully, and it might be due to nerves, they spent a lot of time trying to figure out how to get even parse the results of the first page properly. Since we require them to handle relative links to give a 3 on functionality, they unfortunately did not pass in this category. 

One flag that I would like to make, and this might be a culture / feedback flag more than technical signal is that during the interview, I actually tried to bring up handing relative links up to them as an attempt to push them closer to a 3 functionality-wise. However, not sure if it's because we didn't have much time left (~15 mins before we were out of time) and because they were very engrossed in the question, they almost unintentionally dismissed my feedback and proceeded to continue with their refactoring. Not sure how big this flag was, but thought it was worth mentioning in my interview feedback.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Functionality: 3
Laid out events in a grid, he just forgot the ordered by date part (and I didn't get the chance to prompt him to complete it).
Coding style: 4
Used styled components, thoroughly thought through names for each component. Thought about how he wanted to write rules (put a rule for paragraph styling in its wrapper).
UI: 4
Only person so far to properly style images. Got truncation (but missed the ellipses) using fixed height and overflow: hidden. Thought about page responsiveness: set a max width on the grid and centered it on the page.
Culture: 3
No red flags, just didn't seek feedback. Though when I gave feedback he accepted it graciously.

Trevor really zoomed through this interview. What consumed the most time were a few things that he got stuck on (truncating the description, some React errors when first starting). He's clearly well versed in both React and css, evidenced by his component setup and how he was thinking through responsive design - he's the first candidate to mention responsive design. Definitely a strong yes from me. If we were hiring for a frontend dev on the engineering team I would also recommend him there, given his background and how well he did on this interview.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
tl;dr - I'm a yes but I do want to first flag is comp, see private notes. Sai is the candidate that reached out to both myself and Brian on LI. Has drive. 

Timeline: Argoneta part of coursework, that is finished. Available to work on June 3rd. Graduating in 7 days. Will need auth. Still in early-middle stages with other companies. 
Working Hours: hours are good - flexible, and on call rotation. 

Questions/Notes:
- Things mentioned, correlate to experience at SFDC very well
- Biggest challenge facing right now as a company, as a support team?

Career History:
- SFDC - youngest team lead in history. Helping customers, scaling applications, on the customer focused front as a support engineer. Later on collaborating with prod and eng and design in prioritizing product roadmap, hiring new folks on the team. MEng leadership - management focused, so leadership is a path that TC wants to take. 
- Why smaller company? TC likes more entrepreneurial opps, the culture. SFDC was large, multiple layers to everything - every layer decisions, approval, etc. Lots of bureaucracy. TC wants to collaborate directly, work with senior leadership. The opp to move fast and grow quickly is an opportunity that smaller companies provide. Take SFDC for example, to be a manager there = certain processes, wait time for tenure, etc. Growth = number one thing on TC's mind. 
- Entrepreneur feel - take ownership.

Three main things: 
1) Technical ability: (CI/CD environments) - Heroku platform product, integrates with SFDC, integrates with Circle CI, GitLab, etc. whatever new customer brings in, have experience with multiple CI/CD environments, concept same and tool is different 
2) Customer focus: - constantly maintained 94% CSAT on team. Story: favorite customer, VIP customer, notorious for raising multiple tickets and cases on a single day. Was assigned to this customer purposely. To build bond, proactively monitor and discover issues, what could pop up in the future. Customer liked skills, raised a ticket saying I want this CSE (Sai) for my case and I don't want anyone else. 
3) Leadership: (process improvement, documentation, projects) - lead at Salesforce, understand leading without title - support needed for the team. when started journey no onboarding framework in place timeline of 90 days to learn about platform and customer skills - after team lead put a framework in place, first week this, then this, etc. end of 90 days certification so you are set and can go from probation to actual work. Second = building bridge between product and eng teams and support. Disconnect, couldn't escalate to them - no proper way. Slack channels, ticket or case, but once made recommendation - went to management from teams, came up with Jira or Gus (? didn't get that clearly) - navigated the product features prioritization.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
CI/CD: 4
Rules: 3

Sai did quite well on both parts of the interview. 

He was fluent in navigating GitHub Actions (workflow file, logs, secrets), and able to use docs to quickly fill in gaps like not knowing what `workflow_dispatch` does. He did make the classic mistake of assuming that the `push` paths key is to tell you where the workflow file is, but this is quite minor. He was not familiar with how you would block a pipeline, but almost immediately found the relevant doc. However, he skimmed right past the section that explains what makes a finding blocking, and it required quite a bit of guidance from me to get him to slow down, back up, and look at it, rather than just reading off the other information in the doc.

For the rule-writing question, he had clearly prepared (he had a written-out guide he was referring to) and was familiar with metavariables, ellipses, and metavariable-regex. He quickly identified areas to substitute to generalize the rule, and mostly accurately. He periodically ran the revised rule to check the matches as well. He only struggled with two things: the exact syntax for metavariable-regex, and using the $ALGO metavariable from `String $ALGO` in the second line as well, to scope the match properly. These are both quite subtle issues, and we aren't really testing on YAML syntax, so needing a little guidance is not a problem there. But as with the first section, I found it difficult to get him to slow down, read the docs, and listen to what I was suggesting for those two points. The exact syntax for metavariable-regex is in an example in the doc, and he didn't even try copy-pasting it to sort out his syntax issues. He also had a lot of trouble understanding, even with fairly explicit guidance, that metavariables can't be used to match part of a string (no "DES/$VAR/PKC5Padding").

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Misc. Notes
4 years at Salesforce, entire experience here
PM was original desire, but really drawn to making customer's happy, "customer happiness is more important than my own growth"
Product is targeting the right audience, thinks we're unmatchable
Saw Brian's linkedin post, felt JD was relevant
Seems very willing to dive into unfamiliar technology and get really well-versed in it
Overall seems to have good mentorship and customer focus

Conclusion:
It's a 2 for me. Overall, I liked his customer-focus and mentorship, and I trust that he's willing to learn. But, I wasn't able to build strong enough conviction in this interview. There was trouble with communication and listening well to the question -- it took a lot of nudging in order for him to give specifics about examples he brought up, and he was speaking way too quickly. Because of this, I wasn't able to suss out how technical he was in the support cases he was in, and how impactful of a role he played in resolving them. 

I would change to a 3 only IF someone else on the panel gave a 4 and was able to give better examples of how he communicates well and why his technical strengths are unmatched.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to dive deep into a complex problem to understand its root cause?
questions.answer:
Raw Notes
  • Problem: customers always come and say "apps are down, app doesn't work" -- really vague issue, troubleshooting bare minimum foundation
  • Custom domains are not showing up
  • Making sure the IP address is matching the same name
  • Salesforce gave onboarding on how to use the tools to check uptime and domain name
  • Customer pays for DNS, attaches to Salesforce platform, and point to DNS pointer
  • Reachout to customer via email to give feedback, gather enough data and make sure
  • Would hop on calls with customers and troubleshoot
  • Team lead, was always the 2nd and 3rd layer of contact
  • Would take ownership of the tickets if team mates did it wrong
  • Try to understand their thought process

Overall Takeaways
  1. Speaking way too fast, didn't dive deeply into example, just giving me a lot of process
  2. Really leans into process and what was taught to solve issue --> worried about ability to be scrappy
  3. Great note on mentorship -- fix issue first for customer, then follow-up to help teammates who didn't do well on the case to teach

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to communicate effectively with other teams to resolve a complex problem or achieve a common goal?
questions.answer:
Raw Notes
  • Lots of cross collaboration with teams, bug reports coming stressing the customer down, report through Jira, biggest and most powerful thing where there are outages
  • One incident: Platform API was down, happened once when he was at Salesforce -- everyone all-hands on deck
  • How we resolve: 
    • To understand the trend: what customers, how many paid, what are they saying, pattern
  • Have to update to the customers on the customer status pages
  • How do you prioritize:
    • Don't be a hero, trust in the team
    • Break the tasks in chunks, let your team take up the parts they want to take care
    • Two medium: get on the bridge, report every 5-10 min, provide update, platform API incident, customer shouldn't be left hanging
    • Slack: anything stressing on one particular aspect
  • Outcome: Team discovered malicious attack on Platform API, RCA (root-cause analysis), resolved in 2-3 hrs, ask again if customers are still seeing the issue, posted with customers

Overall Takeaways
  1. Made sure to have timely check-ins, multiple mediums to communicate
  2. Always kept the customer priorities in-mind first
  3. Issue was still really vague, I had to ask a lot of follow-ups to dig into why this mattered, what was business impact, what was specifically wrong

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to learn a new skill or tool to solve a customer problem? OR Tell me about a time you found you needed a deeper level of subject matter expertise to do your job well.
questions.answer:
Raw Notes
  • Learning Apache Kafka and take some time to learn this
  • Want to understand what the hype and problem solved by using Kafka
  • Planned the content to learn Kafka, tracked his progress, and taught someone as well to really reinforce
  • Learning processes
    • Basic nuances
    • Find patterns
    • Tie it back to workflow
    • Teach someone
  • Kafka is only used on heavy Enterprise customers
  • How many partitions and how many consumers
  • Consumers are idle, not efficient
  • Would propose solution from new knowledge, but get verified from senior first to check understanding

Overall Takeaways
  1.  Methodical in learning, makes sure to balance theoretical and real experience
  2.  Again, didn't really quite get how this tied back to a specific problem solved at the company -- was a bit general and vague

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Really enjoyed my conversation with Al. Started off as a software developer and decided to go the Entrepreneurial route in starting her own company with a friend where she built out the Product, Sales, support and other G2M functionalities before they had to close down the company in 2022 due to funding drying up. She joined Snyk as a TAM for a year and moved into the SE role for the last 3 months. Has hands-on experience working with CI/CD providers and is comfortable reading/writing code in multiple languages. She's currently working as a SE on the Named Enteprise segment but has only been in the role for 3 months. Great communication skills and will work great with both practitioners and executive buyers. Talked through examples where she inherited an account as a TAM that was going to churn due to product gaps and how she persisted in reaching out to the customer, understanding their gaps and putting a plan together with the product team to address their requirements. They ended up renewing for 3 years. She also chatted about multiple examples where she's built slack bots to alert on licensing utilizations, pilots expiring and more which showed her ability to see opportunities to automate functionality that did not exist. My main concerns are she does not have a whole lot of formal SE experience but she's done a lot of founder led sales and selling in her own startup. She also has 1.5 year of experience with our biggest competitor Synk. She wants to be part of a company that's innovating rapidly and wants to be a SE to get experience of selling before she eventually gets back into being an entrepreneur down the line. Moving her to the technical screen.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Example was from when she was a TAM at Snyk. Had a Snyk customer coming up for renewal that was not happy with the Snyk Kubernetes integration which was open source. The integration was not updating the state if new clusters were added/deleted/archived and they had to manually reimport the clusters everytime to get 100% visibility which was a lot of work. The customer was frustrated and it was affecting their renewal of Snyk container scanning. Snyk container scanning was the least funded and did not have lot of resourcing for the functionality to be built out. Escalated to sales leadership and went all the way to the CEO who told her to just figure something out to save the renewal. She formed a team with the container specialist SA and an intern who specialized in Kubernetes to work together. She built out a script using the Snyk CLI tool that create a cron job of importing all the deployments on a daily basis. Worked with the product team, sales team and the customer as a design partner to build it out and get it deployed which helped save the renewal. Also looked through their feature request system to find other customers that had requested this functionality and worked with SE's and CSMs to get this rolled out to other ENT customers. Al is someone who's driven, motivated, great communicator and works great cross functionally to get shit done. She will be great in front of customers on demo calls and engagements. The only reason I was not a 4 is I felt she was not super confident when it came to talking through some of the design decisions and technical details which is my only concern with her and if she has the skillset to ramp up quickly, learn and succeed in the SE role with large ENT customers.

Working Hours: feasible, absolutely

Something that uses technical and people skills. If only just doing staright up engineering, farther from customers - motivated by helping people. Building things to make dev lives easier, or customer dev lives easier. Some support background IT in college and working at a call center around the same time. 

Career History:
- Common factor of last few jobs = automation, devops, was in CI/CD a lot mostly GitHub Actions and other things as well. Occasionally Jenkins, CircleCI. Themes = what can we do to make things easier for developers. OpenSesame - working on getting OIDC authentication from GHA to ___ accounts. It was an audit nightmare though. Roll out services in Terraform, walked out one of the most comfortable ppl on team vs. not knowing much about it at all going into it - loves learning. Slack was more testing focused, thesis of that role = so how would you automate testing things like native apps in windows or mac. Desktop tools don't exist, no access to APIs through OS. Got into CI tooling too. Graphical testing working in CI. 
- Sprint call center = worked that during college. Thought it was fun bc was new problem every 5 minutes, working with customers. Feels so good to fix problems and make the crazy thing going wrong to go away. 
- Looking for: casting a broad net, other direction outside of TSE is documentation. Done a lot of documentation work in eng roles, it's such a fore multiplier and so under recognized most of the time. Got to do a big project re-architecting internal box for engineers. Understanding own system, connections, etc. Would love to help out in that realm as well. If you can document yourself out of a job that is really rewarding. 

Three main things: 
1) Technical ability: (CI/CD environments): see above
2) Customer focus: see above
3) Leadership: (process improvement, documentation, projects): see above

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
10 out of 8 would hire!

OK just kidding, technically the rubric only goes up to 4. But seriously, 8/8, Finn did really well.

CI/CD knowledge was fluent - I almost didn't have to prompt them to go through the things we want to hear. They knew all the triggers, and they acknowledged that they weren't sure how the paths key interacts with push, which is cool because a lot of people assume they do know and are wrong. They were willing to test out some methods related to blocking, and didn't get too flustered when the first one didn't work. They accepted a small amount of guidance on search terms readily, although they didn't click through to look at the policies after realizing that was probably what was required, which was a bit surprising. But they knew all about logs, secrets, showed findings, explained the job and steps correctly, etc.

Rule-writing was also quite fluent - they quickly identified all the places that needed to be substituted, and chose the correct construct for each one (ellipsis, metavariables). They quickly realized they needed to pair the first $ALGO metavariable with the last. They didn't go down any rabbit holes but immediately thought of regex for the DES bit, and correctly searched the rule syntax doc for regex...as connected to metavariable -> metavariable-regex. Their regex was correct on the first try, but there were some syntax issues that prevented a match, and they were quite willing to simplify the regex to see if that would help. Once lightly guided through the YAML syntax, their rule matched perfectly.

Finn also had really thoughtful questions for us (Swati joined as a shadow on this interview) about supportability and company culture, and was pleasant to speak with. Based on what they said, they have a similar interest in support and documentation as we do - they enjoy talking to and helping the humans who use a product, and making that process easier. There can be lots of reasons to be interested in support engineering but that's definitely a good one in my mind. :) Zero reservations, I would love to have Finn on the team.

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to dive deep into a complex problem to understand its root cause?
questions.answer:
Raw Notes
Overhauling our documentation, feedback from engineers talking about how it's not serving us, offsite topic in breakout group
First step: Figure out what is actually wrong, what tangible thing can we do about it?
Did a lot of reading about user research, no budget to do real research
Took principles and did mini-study, internally, minimal set of questions to ask to get a dig at the problem
How do you ask documentation, when do you look for it
Separated qualitative vs quantitative data
It wasn't that they were enthusiastic or had time -- when folks write docs, they don't know where to put them
Also highlighted a trust issue -- people don't trust the freshness of the docs
Docs were organized by author, not by reader. Think about the audience
Lot of empathy: wants to make solution work for them, not against them
Communicated impact by reaching out to managers first, communicating impact, A/B testing with small volunteer group
Ended up delaying that part
Good communication, but can easily ramble
Surface Confluence pages that might need attention: is it outdated or does should it be deleted -- heuristics
Retrospective: would have gotten to people 1:1 to pitch this idea

Overall Takeaways
 Not a super technical example, but very thoughtful approach to solving the issue. Not just as simple as proposing a new template, but validating that it's accepted by folks on the team + surfaced in the right ways
 Excellent root cause analysis -- looked beyond surface level problems with Docs, saw that it was due to organization and trust.

Overall Takeaways

questions.answer:

Incident response, figure out what was breached, what we need to rotate

In practice, lots of things were touched by devsecops, had to focus on comms with letting impacted teams know what was wrong
Anything marked dev/staging, don't touch -- focus on everything that's prod-based first

Nothing crazy stood out here, consistently good practices with comms in this example and prior ones

questions.question: Describe a situation where you had to handle a high-stress or urgent customer issue

Source: 
Office: wants to go into office 
Timeline: graduating in about a week and a half, getting surreal. Really open to start as soon as possible. In SLO, from LA, looking to move to SF. Have a lot of friends there, wants to be where tech is.

Use it to support clients. Talked about earlier, everything is going digital needs security and as much as you can be a technical person behind copmuter, it's useful to have that knowledge but support people, sell products and solutions that help them. Wants to be in a position where TC ca help people with security and continue learning. Why sales? Likes sales because it aligns with a lot of who Mason is, consistently learning, being challenged, being competitive, and helping people. Was voted the most competitive negotiator among peers. What is the hardest problem you've faced? (frame however, academic, athletic, etc. Want to understand how do they problem solve. Bad = look at problem as problem. Good = solution oriented and wants to overcome challenge)  Internship, slower season for the company in IT consulting. It was really competitive with who would get assignments and projects, and it was hard to get placed on them. TC knew that they had to be proactive in reaching out to different people, following up time and time again to keep top of mind. Mason landed a project that was client service proposal, it was an opp to research client services and pitch a service to a client. Worked with someone there that had a vision that aligned to the project.  Used the project as a way to show the company - hey, here is a client that we can offer more services to, and really expand their security posture and went a step forward to show that this is something that we could do for many other clients as well, not a one time thing, supporting the vision the person had.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Cullen was a clear and engaging communicator, and I think captured attention well. Perhaps due to time constraints, I thought that the presentation didn’t have some of the a-ha’s (or, rather, things that I was attracted to) from our previous conversations because we only covered them at a not-deep level during the presentation (the specific example being how he used market research and positioning to make a business case recommendation about a new product that was ultimately very successful at Sonos).

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
- Strong security prioritization involving multiple stakeholders. Specifically, balancing time, money, volume estimation for logging and monitoring using a combination ELK+Splunk stack
- Clear technical ability to implement the strategy. Specifically, Som spoke on porting Fitbit's logging and monitoring infrastructure from Terraform to Borg? (whatever IaC stack Google used)
- Seems like the kind of person who can "figure it out" as they go, with good decision-making and relational skills to bolster the end result

Observations
- Som became visibly nervous during the Red Team portion of the interview and repeated throughout how he was more familiar with the defensive perspective. While not a problem by itself, it was difficult for me to draw out details about the broader techniques he was describing.



Conclusion
- Som seems like a good fit with good and demonstrable security skills and risk assessment experience. Som was very personable and gave me the impressions that he was willing to learn AND willing to execute. My biggest concern is noted in the Observations, where he became nervous during the Red Team portion of the interview. I don't think his performance here makes him any less capable in the security domain, but from a strict rubric evaluation he missed the marks here. Overall a regular "Yes" for me.
Rubric
These are cumulative. The more checkboxes the better.
Threat Modeling
[x] Can identify at least 3 cloud infra security concerns
[x] Priorities are reasonable for a 50-person startup
[x] Has worked with Infra Eng teams in the past on security issues
[x] Shows ability to self-analyze / criticize past work
Red Team
[ ] Can detail plausible ways to hack a 50-person startup 
key word is detail
[x] Demonstrates practical red team knowledge (e.g. buying domains, etc, etc)
[x] Can identify potential security controls ‘a hacker’ should be concerned about
[ ] Knows how to hack us, and we should be scared 😅
Blue Team
[x] Can link red team tactics to blue team concerns
[x] Can detail 3 relevant security concerns for a startup
[x] Has a plausible ‘cheap’ solutions for today’s problems
[x] Has a model of what ‘good’ looks like if ‘money is no concern’
Team Fit
[x] Empathetic communicator
[x] Low-ego
[x] Ability to work in the grey-zone

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Pros
  • Som has broad range of experience from endpoints, to infrastructure, and applications, making his responses complete and thoughtful. For example, one aspect we discussed was securing endpoints. He touched on establishing a well-known baseline model for operating system configurations, data security, patching, monitoring, and logging. 
  • We went further into describing the importance of training and educating the end-user base for constant awareness of security topics.
  • He is also cognizant of balancing security, productivity and cost, recognizing that risks sometimes must be accepted vs dealing in absolutes.
  • Som is a proponent of shift left to scale, which aligns well with our own internal philosophy. He mentioned empowering end-users to help themselves not only moves the burden from operational staff but also trains end-users in good access patterns which was rather insightful.

Cons
  • Som has been at the Google mothership for a long time now and has not been exposed to broad process/procedure, technology/solutions, and industry for a while. That being said his fundamentals and concepts are solid, he demonstrates thoughtfulness in his answers, and his thought process (security vs productivity, cost vs benefit, end-user training and communications) aligns well with the team.
  • He noted he's considering leaving Google for the very reasons mentioned above so his motivation to hit the ground running is high.

Take Aways
Som is a seasoned veteran of corporate security, straddling the world between IT and security. He has a good high level view of security as it pertains to endpoints, infrastructure, and networking. In addition, he advocates for the end-user and customers, emphasizing continuous communication and feedback. He may be challenged to "get back in the game" after his time at Google, but it's not a major concern as he can still describe technically what "good" looks like.

CorpSec priorities
  • [x] Reasonable priorities for a 50-person startup
  • [x] Shows ability to self-analyze / criticize past work
  • [x] Can describe how security appetite / maturity changes with org
  • [x] Can identify process that don’t scale
CorpSec capabilities
  • [x] Has a plausible approach to addressing security concerns
  • [x] Solutions include processes & people, as well as technology
  • [x] Has hands-on experience with some solutions
  • [x] Shows ability to self-analyze / criticize past work
TechOps collaboration
  • [x] Has concrete examples of working with IT / TechOps
  • [x] Demonstrates knowledge of IT priorities / concerns
  • [x] Shows ability to self-analyze / criticize past work
  • [x] Show an interest in solving TechOps problems ‘outside’ of just security
Company Culture
  • [x] Empathetic communicator
  • [x] Low-ego
  • [x] Ability to work in the grey-zone

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, I would say Som is a solid 3/4 yes. I think he would be able to do the role, he may be a bit junior in some areas so there are opportunities for him to grow in his role as a Senior.
 
He responses gave me the sense that he's adaptable, empathetic, and has that "service to others" sort of security attitude. He seemed a bit unexperienced in some areas - D&R, incidents, etc. His responses were mostly from the angle of corporate security. The incidents he said he worked on or found memorable were about red teaming or blocking a domain for a ransomware. I thought they were relatively straightforward incidents and not particularly complex (maybe a signal for how senior he is?).

Only small flag is that he said the red teaming felt a bit like hazing and fun, which was a bit odd (I wouldn't say hazing is fun during an interview). But I think his intention was to say it was just good natured fun and rivalry between the red and blue teams at fitbit, so not worried about this.

He said he wasn't interested in managing, but he asked how I became a manager.

Execution


[x] Reasonable priorities for a 100-person startup
[yes and no] Detection building blocks feel 'right-sized’
he seemed a bit new to D&R in general, but overall seemed to think about it the right way
[x] Shows ability to self-analyze / criticize
[x] Can make reasonable trade-offs (e.g. signal vs noise, build vs buy)

Threat modeling


[ ] Reasonable priorities for a 500-person startup
spoke about org wide priorities like benefits and MFA/SSO (which I would expect much before a 500 person startup) 
[x] Can describe a methodology for assessing security gaps / strengths
was informal in his approach here, said basically he would speak with his manager to understand priorities
 Honestly the more formal the worse this answer (e.g. parroting ATT&CK or NIST CSF is worse then an ad-hoc but actually used process)
[x] Capabilities include people, processes, and technologies
[ ] Can identify practices that don't scale
he didn't have solid answers for this question, but because he has experience with a growing org, I think he would be adaptable enough!

Incident response


[x] Has experience with formal incident response program
[x] Can talk in detail about specific incidents
[x] Able to self-analyze / criticize
[ ] Lists reasonable IR priorities for a small startup
Didn't get any signal for this, but seemed like something that could be mentored
 Better answers emphasize triage and risk; rather than completeness and paperwork

Company Culture


[ x] Empathetic communicator
 Flags: run-on info dumps; not clarifying questions; not allowing the interviewer to intercede/ask questions
[x ] Low-ego
Not afraid to do the glue work that early-stage security and techops teams need.
[x ] Ability to work in the grey-zone
 Security is a process, not an end-state. Startups are scrappy. Candidates need to be able to operate in the grey and be able to define a reasonable path to ‘better’.




Intro to yourself ~ 10 min
I did an intro to myself. 
Has a small interest in management, but not too much. 
The best managers were at smaller companies, they didn’t have time to hand hold they had to empower people to go for it.
Managers at larger companies (wouldn’t be interested in that). They aren’t able to make a lot of things happen because of the layers. Managers are more focused on people management. Making sure people are on track, working according to levels. 
Likes enablers


Intro
Started working in a tech ops role (IT support) 
When he was at fitbit, he got more opportunities (200 → 2k), business needed technical expertise, he was able to step into those roles
Switched into security ops and was more or less doing the same thing
Alerting and overseeing more security infrastructure
Fitbit -> Tesla 
Didn’t really enjoy his time, so moved back to fitbit
Tesla -> fitbit
D&R role, corp sec
Google
Migrating tech stack from fitbit -> google
Most of his time now is reviewing infra launch process before it goes to prod, secure reviews from an infra perspective
A lot of legacy fitbit stuff comes back to him 


Detection Engineering ~20 mins


What are the first 2 or 3 early security threats you’d want to address?
Imagine that there is not a lot of turn over yet, tribal knowledge exists
We don’t need to worry about losing that stuff or insider threat stuff yet
Recalled Fitbit experience
Didn’t have a lot of experienced managers (everything felt more reactive)
#1 - Logging or logging aggregation 
Standard images, docker configs
As things grow or infra grows, it’s already in place
Intention is to gather information, or for technical troubleshooting
#2 - vulnerability detection program
Fitbit didn’t really think about it
Vulnerability scanning, infra oriented
Bug bounty program or encouraging people to poke stuff
Different types of scanning, infra oriented
#3 - understanding what grants access to customer data or code
What service accounts exist and are allowed to get customer data
Orient it around customer data


How would you go about addressing these? 
logging/telemetry
Establish baseline, you can address
Vuln scanning
Establish SOP for what happens and establish expectations with eng teams
Focus on the highest priority
Access control
Identify crown jewels and understand controls/access
Limit who is in those groups


Doing these types of things in the past
What worked well?


What would you do differently?
Fitbit/logging - didn’t get in early and had to retrofit and make configs to get logs
Were blind for a few years and was tedious to troubleshoot
fitbit/Vuln detection and setting expectations
Eng would just get tickets in jira and low severity things were filing
No agreements in place with eng
Shared understanding of prioritization 


Detection engineering can span a lot of different disciplines
What areas are you the most confident working in?
Investigations and understanding of where to look 
Typically will try to have a bigger picture mentality and correlate things really well
Focusing purely on what to protect and working backwards
Doesn’t get distracted and wants to avoid daily toil 


What are some areas you feel less comfortable, or at least less experienced in?
Was part of a lot of compliance programs, but it was very ad-hoc
Wants to understand holistically what the compliance program is asking
Wants to automate more of compliance needs
Decent at coding, but not great at starting scripts from scratch
Has found AI really helpful
Not a regex whiz either (likes to use AI for this)


How do you balance catching important security risks vs alert fatigue?
Main thing is understanding priority from the business side
If the business doesn’t need us to do something, then we should consider avoiding it to avoid burnout
Balance what we have available and what resources/technologies do we have to utilize that
Going back and revisiting early decisions in detection or technologies we are using to ask if they are still the right decisions for us would be helpful
Proofpoint 


Org level Threat modeling ~10 mins
As a company grows, threats and security priorities change. Imagine going from a 100-person company to a 500-person company. What would you expect to look different?


What’s different in your priority list?
Start to have more people leaving
assets/secrets become more critical - people can walk away with those
A stronger offboarding process for individual teams based on access
Rotate secrets or things when people leave so they can’t take it with them
More processes and standards start falling into place
Applications that people are buying - make sure they integrate
Vendor review process
Vendor service agreement
Going to hire a lot more non-technical people and people who are not as familiar with security
Improve Email security
Improve Endpoint telemetry 
DNS layer of security - network traffic originating from endpoints
More centralized tools (MDM, RAT, etc) 
Ex. JAMF - managed
Google ex. Will build an MVP and everything else is a feature request and needs to be prioritized (so there is huge reliant on feedback on users vs using their own expertise) 
Sometimes people are not sure 
We spoke about eBPF for a little bit (told him about Capsule8/cloudflare's linux based network, etc) 


What are some methods you’d use to track / assess priorities over time?
Have regular alignments with management on prioritizations
Seemed like a growth opportunity as a senior to make suggestions on what should be prioritized as well
Security is expensive 
Tracking where the most time is spent and evaluating if that is good time spent
Seemed informal methods and aligned with our hope in this response


What capabilities would you expect a 500-person company to have?
Investing more into scalable and automated solutions where you don’t need a lot of people to support it
Standardized tech stack
SSO, MFA
Well defined benefits, prevent burnout, demands, and pace are high


Have you seen traps or bad practices that don't scale?
For logging, the thing that they did at fitbit, they ingested and paid a lot money for licensing (think Splunk) 
Removing unnecessary information 
Google 
Use their own logging aggregator for incidents or threat hunting
Parse things into splunk for alerting


Incident Response ~10 min
Incident response processes also need to change as an org grows. Let’s assume we’re a 100-person company again with a green-field incident response program.


Walk through what incident response processes look like at your previous company.
IR process was not that organized in fitbit
If something was down, they knew who to contact, so they weren’t well documented, we didn’t POCs for services
Things were done more informally, so when new people joined the team, they are trying to do their job, but they don’t have the right information
Didn’t have clear definitions of what was worthy of being paged for, 
Incident severity is almost purely based on customer impact (number of customers)
It didn’t work that well, because it was the biggest factor 
They were really focused about the brand or reputation being damaged
But they were organized on IC and who is responsible for what


What was a particularly memorable incident? Why was it memorable?
Wannacry
Only reason why it didn’t spread was because you can block the domain
Changes to windows policies 
Scanning machines
An incident that he was in charge of, crowdstrike gave an alert (CS overwatch) 
There was an alert about dumping windows credential on a machine 
We started looking into it right away so he was the first one to catch the alert and was briefing everyone
We saw that alert and saw a series of other things happening
Someone was able to push a PR into their bitbucket to change something basic
Prodsec team was doing a red team exercise
Once they discovered it was them, the taunting went up a lot more
They had a good relationship so it kind of felt like hazing and it felt normal/fun (hazing is kind of flag, but seemed good natured about it) 
Wasn’t the IC for this incident because google has a strong hierarchy
Sheer magnitude of the vulnerability was memorable 
All hands on deck for all security engineers at google
Problem was massive so it was a bit disorganized so people gravitated towards what they were familiar with
It wasn’t 3-4 days later, they got more organized (had a PM) 


What was working well? What would you do differently?


Given a green field IR program — what would be your top 3 early priorities?


Questions for me ~10 min
With my security background, coming into semgrep, what were 2-3 that you thought would be in place but aren’t?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
tl;dr - Atul is really, really nice. It was such a refreshing conversation. Engaging, excited about Semgrep. Comes off like a genuinely good person, low ego, joy to have at a company. Passionate about security, has used and is excited about Semgrep. Technical, but has worked consulting so has that customer communication piece down. 

Timeline: can join within two weeks, 
Compensation: no concerns with the range
Working Hours: EST

Questions/Notes:
- Have used Semgrep before, project for school. Loves it, thinks it's great. Finds Semgrep interesting bc of the ability to create rules, anyone can create rules. The reduction of false positives, would be really excited to work for Semgrep. 
- Recently graduate with MS in Cybersecurity
- Interview process?

Career History: started as a cyberseuciryt analyst and worked way up to a security engineer role. Started with secure code reviews (manual) when codebase got larger did automated security testing reviews. Experience has been working with clients (Deloitte) from different areas - from healthcare to finance and everything in between. The one thing TC loves = helping customers, and clients were like customers. If doing web app security or penn testing, really enjoyed whenever TC interacted with clients explaining the issue, how they can fix it. Now working at a small startup, com-sec, handling infrastructure security and working on webapp security testing, network penn testing.  Takeaway from work so far - erally enjoys interacting with customers, users and solving their problems. This is why TSE/TAM roles have caught TC's eye. Checks all the boxes TC wants. 

Three main things: 
1) Technical ability: (CI/CD environments) [when it comes to ci/cd experience have you configured yaml files or set up custom environment variables? this is a core part of troubleshooting this topic as it relates to semgrep] Working on GitLab, also used Jenkins previously. Very familiar with the core basics, have done those previously and very comfortable with that. 
2) Customer focus: have a ticket management system - also have a client Slack environment. Clear and concise, communicate with customer and be really clear, lots of communication. Find the root cause. Big problems = scoping call. If cannot resolve issue, then TC will fully understand issue go back do research and come back with final solution. Keep info in database so the information is there, more efficient.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
CI/CD Notes:
Already scanned some projects onboarded onto Semgrep
Very clear and thorough when talking through the onboarding flow
Knows where the file is placed within the repository
Able to click through the SCM environment very easily  
CI/CD Walkthrough:
on triggers were very clear and mostly correct; just a small mishap on the workflow dispatch and the commonly confused push/paths trigger
Left me without any questions on what features of the yaml were present
Talked about specifying different versions of semgrep via docker tags
Very good walkthrough
Understands where we grabbed the token from (repository secrets).
Knows the dangers of hardcoded secrets.
Knows where to find logs for the jobs - communicated smoothly from the CI/CD walkthrough
knows how to export the logs
Went over the different findings as well.  Seems to have great security context as well. 
Knows what branch protections are in general and was able to explain it cleanly


CI/CD Summary: I will give a 4/4 of this one, there were small mishaps here and there but overall went over every bullet point necessary for a 4. Complete, concise, and communicated very efficiently. All follow-up questions were answered very sufficiently



Rule Writing Notes:
Started by identifying commonalities between the examples that are needed to be matched
Immediately started using ellipses and metavariables
Seems to have spent a lot of prep time on this.
Was able to match all of the examples very simply and leveraged a pattern not to remove the AES pattern



Rule Writing Summary: I will give him a 3/4 for rule writing. Did not leverage any docs to get things solved and required minimal minimal assistance to get most things matched. 




Other Notes:
Overall there were minor things here and there that he tripped up on but overall came very prepared and very knowledgeable about the topics. I would have minimal issues about his ramp up speed and am overall very confident in Atul's technical abilities.
Was very interested in Semgrep. Has some friends on the end side that work with Semgrep.

Will give him a very strong yes for Atul. Very confident in his abilities.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Would be a strong yes but was missing some details in a couple answers 3.5/4


Why Semgrep: Configure rules and modify it accordingly so there is less burden for developers; want to be part of company that is OSS, and wants to be part of a startup
Hands on experience configuring CI
Best customer experience: Healthcare industry - interested in SAST/DAST; Responsible for implementing everything from scratch - creating infrastructure, procuring tool for them, creating operational process - lay down template where client will come; Implemented everything from scratch and give them final report
Asked for feedback at the end of the interview
Lots of security experience - cybersecurity analyst as part of deloitte - worked alongside security team - risk management to pen testing report, threat modeling, secure code assessment, SAST/DAST

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Case 1 Notes: 


Showed that finding was present and then fixed within the next screenshot however, the code snippet screenshot is hard to read. However, the explanation of using a parameterized SQL query is the correct fix for it. 
The explanation of the vulnerability is correct and went deep into how the rule itself works. Would've loved more direct code within what the rule matched but it is within the explanation paragraph (nit-picky).

Will give the score a 3 out of 4 for this one. Unsure if the fix actually fixes it 100%? It looks to be true but more marks off for the overall issue of visibility within the communication on some of the more code related aspects.

Case 2:


The script itself was great. Took into account all of the issues regarding hardcoded secrets and paginations and was even able to solve for SCA issues as well.
The communication is where I take some part of confusion with. Atul assumes Windows usage here instead of making system agnostic and is a tad bit overkill when trying to send this to a customer (nitpicky so not really put into score). 
The thought process as well if thoroughly worded and gives clear idea to what the script wants to do. No issues with what next steps should be as well.

Will give this score a 3 out of 4 as well due to mostly some weirdness with the communication thought process. Overall though impressed with the technical detail and can give this take home a yes.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Case 1 - 4
Case 2 - 3

On the whole the technical aspects of this assignment are very good.  It's one of the best scripts we've seen (I slightly suspect they used our utilities script to base it on, but there's nothing wrong with that if they did). The explanations are mostly good as well, but somewhat overdone (too many words for the amount of content) and there were a few misses:


I was particularly nonplussed that they went through the entire explanation of how to run the Python script on Windows, with only a short "Note" at the beginning. I would have preferred to see a clearly stated assumption in the thought process about what platform is being used, or an explanation of how to run the script itself, followed by a question about platform in case they need more details about setting up the environment.
The description of the action to take if the question comes up again is quite content-free - it's a lot of words to just say "I'd discuss a feature request with the product team".
There's also a sentence "I will also recommend that customers include it in their Continuous Integration and Delivery Workflow if they frequently need to export the results." which is not an appropriate recommendation for findings from the API (although it can be a reasonable suggestion about how to export findings from the CLI/CI run directly).

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Notes
- Security engineer working in IT
- Making apps and code secure for developers
- Heard of Semgrep from friends, used on own project and impressed
- Likes the gratification and diversity of interacting and fixing client issues
- Asked what his customer philosophy is: mentioned empathy as the biggest one, patience and staying vigilant, things don't always get solved always

Overall Takeaways
- Very soft 3. If I had to compare to Sai and Finn, I would say Atul is quite up there with them
- I liked his customer philosophy -- empathy and patience, really sticking it thru to the end especially when problems are tough
- Seems comfortable with technical ambiguity: will take time to learn, reach out to experts, self-sufficient. I liked his look at cpu usage to determine race condition example
- Communication: Takes a bit of time to get to the point, but I think he's shared examples where I believe he's proactive about it
- Learning: is a practical learner, believes in "learn by doing"
- What would make he a stronger candidate: more concise in his communication + better sense of how he used his technical knowledge. He shared a lot of "I just learned X and did Y with it". He mentioned empathy, but I kinda also wanted to hear why he thought certain problems were problems. Alexis does this well, helps with prioritization a ton.

- made detailed report and shared with product team

- Product team gave fix, he gave issue back to clients, things fixed!

- Maintain internal troubleshooting guide for himself

Overall Takeaways
- Comfortable with technical ambiguity, self-sufficient to learn, proactive with communication back to customers

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
tl;dr - wants to make the change, sees transferable skills between recruiting and sales. Understands the ups & downs, work that will need to be put in. Wants sales. 

Office: perfectly feasible - sees value in in person collaboration
Comp: feasible
Timeline: various stages with other opportunities, decisive and targeted in search. More early middle. 

Questions:
- First year in inbound, who are the major liaisons or xfunctional stakeholders 

Overview: thrives on building relationships helping candidates and hiring mangers hit their goals. Finds sales exiting - leverage strong problem solving and comm skills while learning and being pushed to adapt to diff challenges. motivated by direct impact sales can have, the goal oriented nature is exciting and rewarding - work put in affects results of success and growth. That is the key factor of why TC wants to make that transition. Isn't as much of a ceiling in sales. Financial cap not limited - that is enticing. Sees recruiting skills applicable to sales. Loves pitching during calls about company, the closing - the sales adjacent goals and skills. 
Looking for:

1. How well researched is the candidate? - Do they understand what the SDR position is? - Did they take the time to understand the role they are interviewing for? Pretty solid understanding of the expectations, you're going to face more rejection than success. Determination, grit, and grind. Get through ejection, grow and improve based on where failed earlier. Not opposed to losses - opportunities to win. Learn the most, gain growth. Learned from wins as well, but losses drive TC. Had done research on day to day. 
2. Why do they want to go into sales specifically and not another go-to-market function like success or marketing? - Looking for reasons why they want sales specifically and not just a “job in tech” 
3. Why do they want to work with a technical product specifically? (Why Semgrep?) - What is driving their excitement to work with a technical product as opposed to something easy to understand like Yelp or Salesforce (companies that also offer SDR programs). - Touched on that a little, the small team environment and the impact TC can make. The org is smaller - not a lot of resources, but not a turn off. To be able to come in, contribute, make an impact and have input. Medely - help create processes, drive growth. Being in security = specifically, given that tech industry is focused on optimization, automation, etc. the importance of security (observability issues, data leaks) is at the forefront. Data protection is more and more important. Step into org that helps with this, to secure code development, impactful and exciting.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Kenneth Liu - Inbound SDR interview notes


1 - Reason for this role / career trajectory
Growth opportunity. More earning potential, goal oriented. Help contribute to growth.

2 - Cross functional alignment - partnering with others to be successful
Medly - build and scale design team from scratch. VP product partnership alignment. Set expectations and clear goals.
Accountability - regular metrics updates 
Results - team grew to 11 ppl by end of yr
Weekly syncs to stay aligned 
Proactively seeking out feedback


3 - Time when barrier was faced / misalignment with another team or colleague - how did you resolve and what was the outcome?
Medly - filling more sr role. Dir product role for facilities team. Newer to working with marketplace> facilities team. Clearer internal expectations on what the product org evolution was going to look like.
Uncovering that there was a change in expectations and asking for a recalibration conversation.
Was able to source 25 new leads and get the role filled in 2 mos once recalibration occurred. 


4 - Staying energized and connected in remote/hybrid environments
Taking advantage of every opportunity to build deeper relationships. Start and end call with personal connection. Aim to leverage in person time strategically to deepen bonds with and learn from team members.

Overall -- nervous. fast talker. but good and positive energy. hungry.


 ______________________
Pros
Motivation for success / reasons for sales 
Good energy, self starter, goal oriented
Collaboration / internal partnerships 
Examples were specific and able to talk me through specific results, challenges, timing 
Knows tech audience well enough to recruit them; maybe this will help make him quick study?
Cons
He just needs to slow down, demonstrate a bit more confidence, think of how he wants to leverage his time most strategically

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall: Kenneth is an enthusiastic, relationship-focused individual. He brought very genuine and excited energy to our conversation, and was able to paint a detailed picture of what led him to this moment. He was easy to talk to, honest and would be a great addition to our team. 

Pros:
Has worked a wide range of customer-facing roles. Has worked with C-level execs and built out systems/processes in his last roles.
Brought a genuine curiosity about the wins and challenges we face at Semgrep.
Able to reflect on times when he benefited from feedback and bounced back from challenges. He also reflected on his past role when working in insurance/claims and mentioned that it did not contribute to his happiness. I appreciated his honesty and ability to reflect.
Comes from an immigrant family and is clearly passionate about creating a life for him and his future family. Eager to jump into a sales role and I sensed he would be excited to grow and start here at Semgrep.

Cons:
Did not ask a ton of questions about Semgrep in general or the product. However, this is understandable!

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Clara gave this interview, and I shadowed. Will defer to Clara on scoring and notes, but after a brief debrief, we agreed that it was 3+.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Kathryn ("Kat")

Summary: Kat was a very strong candidate, currently at the staff level and has been at Meraki for a long time. Experience leading projects and coordinating projects within org. She helped grow a POC team into a 30+ multi-team org and spoke about balancing maintainability and ease of onboarding with continuing to release features. 

My only concerns would be around the questions the candidate asked, which pointed towards being dissatisfied with the amount of support she's receiving for career growth at the staff+ level in current job, which I'm worried could frustrate her if she came here as well. Might want to dig in more around what she's looking for and needs to de-risk the chances of her joining and then leaving soon after.

---- RAW NOTES--------

Currently at Meraki and got promoted to staff? last year. Previously on data engineering. Kicked off a project team on Cloud Monitoring for Catalyst. Product to bring Cisco’s lines into Meraki Dashboards. Different hardware and firmware but how can we collect same data and give people same visibility even if they can’t configure. POC team of 3-5 into org of >30. Moved into a TL role and helping others onboard. Now helps sub-team and works with PM and UX to determine the best thing to be doing. “Keep things sustainable” is one of my goals trying to deliver features and also keep it sustainable. 

PROJECT 1

Most recent project was to transition devices that were previously communicating to Cisco CLI and now sending data to Meraki. Working on migration flow. Encourage customers to do it and stay transparent. 


Challenges - Meraki has different customer-base than Cisco. Catalyst have a lot of enterprise customers and larger networks. More policies and auditing. Originally doing the changes without telling customers, which broke trust. 


Measuring success - ask customers how comfortable they are with it. How quickly we can get this out and keep system understandable. Delivering in a quarter. Working with dev team and TL and manager. 


Challenges working with multiple teams - on the East Coast and China-based team is really hard. Priority of things can be hard to coordinate across teams. 

PROJECT 2

Added wireless networking to Cisco devices. 


Onboarding - adding a device to a dashboard network. WLC is the config center of wireless. Only have communication to the WLC. 


Traditional Meraki onboarding adds everything directly to the network. Customer turns it on and adds it to the network. Many pieces and steps that can fail. Knowing when and why something failed was really important. Added observability and logging really important. 


Customer Firewalls can introduce issues. Need to be able to pinpoint that for the customer. 


Customers can misconfigure. If you try to add something to a network but it’s already in the network.

PROJECT 3 -- (the one that didn't go so well)

First project I started on when I joined - track by unique client identifier. Originally was a single support case. Spiraled into multi-year project that I joined part way through. Stayed in beta for 3-4 years. Never went and improved that code or prioritized bug fixes. I still get questions about it.


System is so confusing that team that owns it has a hard time working with it. 


Understanding weight behind a project is helpful. PM left and project dissipated. Know who is asking for it and pushing for it so we could fill that gap when PM left. Cutting down milestones - what is acceptable for GA. 


Doesn’t have to be now, but we need a plan for it. 


Talking with PM or whoever is bringing up a project. Sit down and write out everything I understand about a project to make sure I’m not the only one who knows the information. Have them review it and make sure it’s correct. Separate requests into must-haves, nice-to-haves, and not-in-scope.

PROJECT 4

POC of current team. Instead of rebuilding everything. Take advantage of what exists and extend it in a way that can apply to new and old devices. Instead of building out new services, adjusted data fetching to be able to understand different devices’ languages or protobufs or whatnot. 


When you add a new feature for Meraki you can add it for catalyst which helps keep parity. Everyone wants to work on what they know already.

Would be a 3.5/4 for senior and shows great promise. Did not see any staff level markers. I prompted for a discussion about a concurrent implementation in any language, candidate chose to talk about Golang but said they had no recent experience with Go either. The explanation indeed was not very clear to me. Also made an interesting, likely wrong, assumption that HTML parsing and its parallelization would be the most critical (I expect us to be I/O bound a lot more.)

What I liked was nice self awareness, thinking about the cognitive effort going into coding, by refusing to name functions until after some implementation is done and it gets more clear what the name should be. Although the names did not turn out that impressive regardless of this process.
