questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Solid 3 at staff. Good communicator, and generally values aligned. My only hesitation is about whether she's excited about the chaos of a startup. She called out that she thinks Semgrep culture now is much more like the version of Meraki that she saw when she joined, but by then Meraki was already many years acquisition, was already >150 engineers, was way more siloed from GTM.

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback, Mentorship, and Sponsorship
questions.answer:
3.5. Real examples of growth, how she's changing her behavior, giving challenging feedback that meets the person where they are. Not quite a 4 because the examples were a little bit light - the feedback was not a single piece of feedback, the conflict wasn't a single conflict, examples were a bit more general than I would have liked.


"Don't do everything" - feedback is often not concrete, but being told to delegate, the more she mentors others, the more the feedback comes in - if you do everything, the knowledge isn't transferring
Delegating everything is overwhelming
First step was stopping answering all questions on slack, let other people answer
If there's a "first responder", pair with people to help them get to the same answers, rather than just telling them the answer
In incident response, goal is to help them figure things out without having them feel lost
Used to be the only person who would talk to PM and UX, started really involving 
When giving technical feedback, try to talk 1:1, give lots of context
Interpersonal feedback: QA engineer who is overeager to tell the team all of the bugs and tell folks that everything is bad
Just posts "this is a bug" on slack
Directly told the person "ideally we would fix all of these, but we just don't have time to do everything, could really use prioritization" - giving the feedback on how this is impactful to the team, trying to better understand where they are coming from, recognizing that the person had other needs
Likes writing a decision doc, write down the problem, the context, the proposed decision, and alternatives
Writing ^ helps make sure that we're disagreeing about the same thing; lots of disagreements are because of misunderstandings around context

questions.question: Part 1: Why Semgrep and values alignment
questions.answer:
3. I think strong on values alignment in general. Wants to work somewhere collaborative, creative, wants to be closer to customers again, wants to build. Not a 4 because I feel like there's signal that she's more running away from a bad culture. Would loved to have had more concrete, positive examples of what she's looking for.


Vibes at Semgrep are extremely positive, much more like their early experience at Meraki
Frustrated by the bureaucracy and slow pace of Meraki/Cisco; wants to be closer to the customer
Progress is really slow
"Seniority gets the authority at Cisco"
Looking for something smaller
Hard to quantify what she's looking for
One of the early reasons she chose Meraki was the interview process, being able to talk to people, feeling like what she said mattered and feeling heard
Strongest culture points: team is happy to be a team, team is good at iterating, self governs, good communication both about work and socially, people aren't worried about saying their opinions
Weaker culture points: when the team agrees or an argument feels valid but a senior leader just says no without explaining, feeling like you have no say in what happens, makes the team feel siloed, brings the team down, saps creativity

questions.question: Part 3: Growth
questions.answer:
3. Clearly focused on growth and growing, growth goals are aligned with the staff path, but no specific examples of things she's deeply focusing on.


"How can I actually make impact on others?"
Trying to figure out technical pieces she's missing, improving communication, writing has been helpful at the iteration before talking to someone
"I can have great ideas that won't matter" need to listen more to the business, understand what people need
Staying technical, doesn't want to be a manager, wants to stay on the staff track
Wants to grow technically, more technical skills, larger problems, larger scope, etc
Breadth type of staff engineer over the deep dive

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Summary: 1-2 is we're strictly hiring for Staff, 3-4 at Senior or Senior 2 level

I thought Kathryn was great, she definitely passes the vibe check ✅. I can see her being a mentor to more junior members of the team. She seems to be thorough and gives valuable, constructive feedback in her reviews.

That said, I don't think she meets Semgrep's criteria of a Staff engineer. I think she floundered a little bit in the part of the interview where I asked what kind of solution she would implement if she were given this ticket - she kind of gave a non-committal answer and didn't offer up any alternative solutions. I also asked if there was any way she could think of a malicious actor OOMing the backend and she sort of gave a half correct answer saying "that may be too much data for the frontend to handle in memory". I would expect someone at the staff level to be able to at least offer up a long term, viable solution for this particular problem.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall, I would rate this candidate at a 3.5 and recommend hiring at the staff level.

Outcome Oriented: 4
  - Asked many clarifying questions and drilled down on requirements
  - Included strategy from product AND eng perspectives
  - Discussed importance of testing, documentation, and well-defined specs

Makes good tradeoffs: 3
  - Provided many alternatives, discussed tradeoffs of each
  - Detailed tradeoffs for one point
  - Had to probe for tradeoffs for remaining points

Iterative: 4
  - Asked about resourcing
  - Break up system to parallelize development
  - Downscoped some of the features
  - At times, got too focused on implementation and made things overcomplicated

Cross-stack: 4
  - Competent in FE (admitted not an expert)
  - Very comfortable discussing architecture at high level

Also got the sense that she’s unhappy with current situation “too many decision makers in room make it hard”

Not a 4 for me because comms were a bit rambly at times, parts of the execution plan weren’t fully fleshed out

Too many decision makers in the room make it hard

Asked about what kind of things engineers have impact on

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: TLDR: I'd like to work with Kathryn -- I think she would be a good additional to the team! She seemed to care quite a bit about the user problems to solve, and asked about how the problem connected to broader company & product metrics. I was particularly impressed with her level of technical detail --  she got further into the technical implementation of this feature than the majority of candidates I've interviewed! I wasn't a 4 because I found her communication a bit hard to follow at times, (could've been due to interview nerves), but again I think she'd be a great addition to the team!

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
tl;dr - good convo with Justin, we need to fit things in next week. TC was very curious, had a lot of questions that were very thougthful about how finance is viewed outside of the direct team, how we integrate new people, etc. 

Timeline: talked to Sue-Wei, and gave schedule. Trying to get someone onboard asap. For TC soonest = probably the 23rd of September, if not week after. Reason = out of town, going to a work conference for financial planning tool which could be integrated to Semgrep. Available next week, will be abroad from 27th through 12th, week after could schedule a call - but it'll be volatile. Could make it next week. 
In Office: totally fine, current work 3x a week, was in SF two years ago - familiar with office location

Questions/Notes:
- Already chatted with Sue-Wei 
- Curious about outside perspective about finance, team and org - outside looking in 
- HRIS previous company was exploring Rippling, interesting 
- Involved with hiring accounting team, what helped drive their hiring - outside of Sue-Wei 
- Finance and HR have a close relationship, sometimes love/hate 
- Series C, boost of a lot of hires - that we're more remote/hybrid - thoughtfully think about getting new hires involved
- How does company handle annual reviews - development and growth 

Scheduling preferences = no preference at the moment whatever would give a fair assessment and work with schedules. 

Experienced working with CFO, so good senior leader partnership there. Has also worked with dept heads to manage budgets, P&L, work with them on the finance piece and helping them understand how the goals fit in with one another. Technicallky, dashboards and models = experience developing and maintaining. Has also hired and had a direct report, so when it comes time to build out the function has experience there.

questions.question: Private Notes
questions.answer: Compensation: at current work, at the very top end of that range in base. If base isn't negotiable, other parts. If at the ceiling would consider but would push for more than that candidly. The fact that Sue-Wei is hm that means a lot too, makes it easier to accelerate and push for this.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Justin is a great candidate. He knows the role and values the visibility into how the business works and exposure into higher levels in the org. He loves  the challenge of how to use info from execs and operationalize. He knows what it's like to quickly pivot from one company initiative to the next (CONVIVA has been undergoing a pivot into new markets that hasn't worked, so he's been very involved with a new initiative toward cash visibility and scenario planning for the CEO). He's eager to implement a financial planning tool. He was able to speak about overall company challenges well and framed in a similar way to Isaac; referenced Crossing the Chasm and the challenge with moving along the adoption curve. He would be a great partner - he communicates well and has a good feel for what's important to stakeholders and values those relationships.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
very dynamic, energized, friendly and excellent communicator which is a must for cross-departmental collaboration
hungry and eager to start at ground zero and take the company to the next level
I loved that he knows accounting from his prior roles and how to bridge the gap between accounting and FP&A, to bring efficiency to accounting
he asked some great questions about challenges in accounting and what are we focusing on besides the month-end close activities
fun fact: it seems like we both worked with the same CFO in the past at various companies
he asked me to describe the current team and a quick overview or their responsibilities and what are things that i like about working here and things that I don't ->he was interested to learn about company's culture
he is interested in Semgrep because he loves building stuff and he sees a lot of opportunities to improve since we are still a very early stage company
he emphasized the importance of proactive communication with accounting through regular check-ins during the month to ensure data is recorded correctly which reduces the need of last-min corrections during the month-end close  
Overall I really enjoyed my conversation with Justin and I think he would be a great addition to the team and company.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
The Candidate spoke about their experience working on a price tracker web application for online shopping. TC went into detail about how this required getting out of their frontend comfort zone and learning more about technologies like databases and Docker as well as the workflow of building a full-stack app from scratch, like where to start.

When presented with the SemVer exercise, TC said they may have seen concepts like SemVer described before but did not have direct experience with it. After reading the problem and skimming the specs, TC was able to dive right into the problem while only asking a few clarifying questions. TC was specific and pointed out direct passages in the spec when talking about wording and interpretation. TC produced a working and concise solution for part 1 in less than 5 minutes. TC was able to write mostly comprehensive tests and debug with minimal coaching. After being asked to refer back to the point about prerelease versions, TC correctly identified additional tests to write.

For the following two parts, TC wrote similar solutions and even went back to put the version parsing logic into a class for reuse. TC asked specific questions as needed and needed little coaching to reach comprehensive test coverage. After completing part 3 with some time left, TC identified logical ways to improve the code like adding comments, renaming variables, and moving reused logic.

TC spent the final 10 minutes asking questions:

- What is your typical day like
- What's a current or upcoming project at Semgrep you're excited about

TC seemed particularly interested when told about lockfileless scanning and asked intelligent questions. TC seemed interested in Semgrep. Overall recommend strong hire.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
RUBRIC: 


Communication - clear and skillful - 2 (some answers were very general - had to ask a lot of follow-up questions to get specifics)
Communication - respectful - 4


Values - care about culture - 4
Values - demonstrate alignment - 3 (seems interested in security but it's a recent issue, mentioned words in our values like honesty and respect but may have been coached on these - I didn't get stories that backed this up but also none that contradicted)


Feedback - as a gift - 3
Feedback - communicate challenges - 3
Feedback - conflict resolution - 2 (one of the more general answers - wasn't so much conflict as just a decision-making process)


Growth - intentional - 2
Growth - growth mindset - 4


3rd-year CS student at USC 
Passionate about wb development 
New technology and tools in software 
Last year worked for UCLA to rebalance website 
Course producer (TA?) for a few courses 
Love tinkering with websites 
Want to explore software security, recent interest 


Why Semgrep?


Familiarity with Semgrep through Sal. Got to try out the tool, opened up curiosity. 


How will you know it’s a good fit?


Close-knit design team. Form close relationships. Want to work at a start-up, very early in career and that seems like a great way to level up. Loved reading the intern blogs. 


Liked and not liked


As course producer with professor as boss. Loved boss constantly asking for feedback. He cared for that. Active slack channel with a lot of communication. 


Previous project


Each line of data had different parameters coming from an instrument. Each line would calculate other variables from each line. Make program to automatically calculate for them. 
Loved the impact and that it was being used.


Group project with disagreement 


Full-stack project with mentor and I believe 6 other people. Disagreed on what to base it on. Laid out requirements. I had some reservations about scope/not being able to deliver it all. Once we went through each thing and had projected timelines, pros/cons, I ended up enjoying an idea. We were just all honest and respectful. 


Feedback received that helped grow


Feedback on designs/content. This was my first individual project. It was my first time, but they were constantly providing feedback. Helped me hold myself accountable. 


Feedback on behavior?


Feedback on teaching ways as course producer. Use that to alter how I taught. Feedback really molded the way I teach.


Critical feedback you disagreed with?


No, always take it in and see if there’s something I could change or apply. I always enjoy receiving it, because it helps me improve.


Something unexpected coming up?


Not so far… website I made for UCLA lab there were some problems with SquareSpace I had to do custom. Email to them explaining the situation.


What are you looking to get out of internship?
 
 Typescript, learning tools, professional experience. Build relationships with other engineers. Want to be full-stack and want to be able to work with all the components. More comfortable with frontend. 


Prior experience?


Last Summer worked on an individual project learning CI/CD pipeline. Taught me a lot and taught me some backend. Originally I was more frontend but I learned SQL and Docker, etc. Website was input link and it tracks price over time. 


Hardest thing about that project?


Backend - always seen it as complex and not for me. Docker, learning images and virtualization.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Solid 3. Definitely has lack of experience in places but cares about learning and feedback. May need more support and coaching in how to break down projects and when to ask for help but I expect that is true of a lot of interns who haven't worked at a company before and they are well aware of this growth path. 

General:
Rubric: Yes + 
 Overall: Do they communicate at the right level of detail (e.g. do they provide examples and details when asked)? Do they refrain from rambling? Do they ask questions? Do they pause for questions or otherwise ensure their content is landing? -> Yes. Struggled with some examples but didn't get too in the weeds or too non concrete. I think the example trouble was more lack of experience. 
 Communication: Are they respectful? -> Strong yes. 

Part 1: Why Semgrep and Values
Rubric: Yes 
 Do they care about culture and values? Will they work to improve our culture and live our values? -> Yes. Cares about security and the people. Wants high collaboration and to celebrate wins. 
 Do they demonstrate values alignment? Do their answers to later questions align with their stated values? -> Yes. Mostly looking for a product they care about and learning and growth mindedness. 


Raw Notes:
There are lots of jobs out there, why Semgrep?
* Interest in security -> watches low level learning youtube channel 
* Likes the cybersecurity side 
* “This is a great product”
* Being proud of what you do is very important to me
* Likes the people who they have talked
What have you liked or not liked about your managers, teams, or workplaces in the past?
* Liked: 
  * School club
  * Moved project into server and client model as a group 
  * Planned out system design as a group
  * Collaborative and everyone was helping each other 
  * Celebrating wins as a group
* Not Liked:
  * Robotics Lab
  * Bigger projects with lots of schools breaks down the communication 
  * Relying a lot of info with details lost means the goal was hard

Part 2: Feedback and Communication
Rubric: Yes
Communication: Do they treat feedback as a gift? Can they give good examples of how feedback has helped them? -> Yes. Appreciates feedback. Learns from their "mistakes". No evidence of seeking out feedback
 Communication: Are they able to appropriately communicate challenges to team/manager? -> No. Mostly focused on learning to ask for help early in their research projects. Learned how to communicate but no signal on their learning since then
 Conflict Resolution: How do they respond to disagreements with peers or managers? -> Strong yes. Focused on the outcome of the ask (performance) and how to balance that with their other work. Worked with their manager to figure out how to get the time to investigate the issue and then brought back learnings

Raw Notes:
Tell me about a piece of feedback you received that helped you grow?
* Mistake someone called me out on
* Some bad habits -> not best practices
* Did a lot of amending of git commits 
* Didn’t scale to team dynamics 
* Overwrote their commit. Apologized and fixed it 
* Worked to change force pushes and others
Conflict is a natural and important part of working on a functional team. Tell me about a time you disagreed with another engineer or stakeholder. How did you resolve this disagreement and what would you do differently?
* Found a performance issue and brought it up
* Professor told me that it wasn’t worthwhile
* Trying to map where you were and the data we got was really large 
* Comparsion of algorithms and saw ours using 10X more memory
* Professor said it was okay
* “Didn’t seem right to me”
* Asked if I could finish what i was doing and then take some time to look into it
* Adjusted malloc and decreased memory by 90%
* Most threads were allocating memory for the whole object
* Professor appreciated the deep dive
Talk about a project that you worked on where something unexpected happened. How did you challenges or delays to your manager/mentor/team?
* Robotics Lab -> optimization stuff
* Started with an NP hard optimization but gave it my best shot
* Started really ambitious and lots of things I didn’t know
* Spent 3-4 days trying to understand and got not much out of it
* Hard to see progress after so long -> “that was upsetting”
* Updated approach and professor told me to look at the older papers (less vocab and math)
* Cares about learning and figuring it out

Part 3: Growth
Rubric:
 Career Growth: Are they intentional about their own growth? Do they have growth goals? -> No. But I think this is more due to lack of experience. Wants to grow on learning how to produce a product and what happens there
 Growth: Are they open minded? Do they have a growth mindset? -> Yes. Cares a lot about learning. Wants to collaborate with people to build something and learn from them

Raw Notes:
What is a skill you want to develop?
* Work in a corporate environment with deadlines, more stakeholders, etc
* “You don’t know what you don’t know” so want to learn more in general 
* Wants to branch out and work on multiple things
* How to deploy a product and work for a customer

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I liked this candidate. I found him quick-to-answer, thoughtful, and overall likeable.

His technical expertise was interesting, and pointed towards commonality, as he dealt with resource management and optimization in memory-constrained environments for an OCaml research project.

He did mostly express academic interest in Semgrep and fewer actual behavioral things, but when prodded was able to come up with a few. I'll give him a pass here because I think I was much the same when I was younger, and it's a university student kind of mindset.

He sounded like he had worked in projects where there was a communication bottleneck due to remote workers, and he told me about good strategies and outlooks he had adopted as a result, which were to be able to have multiple threads running, work on another when blocked, and switch when needed. He also described finding it important to be able to break problems down into concrete milestones, in order to make them digestible, which I found highly important.

He also sounded thoughtful, in that when I asked him to give feedback, he described a time that he gave feedback to a friend that wasn't as nice as he wished it was. I thought it was nice of him to bring up such a personal example, and also a positive sign, as I found that to be quite a mature mindset to take towards it. He expressed something similar too about teaching his brother, and dealing with getting negative feedback from him.

Overall, I find him to be agreeable and I think he said all the "right" things. I would give him a strong yes.

questions.question: Do the candidate's interests and values align with Semgrep?
questions.answer:
Candidate mentioned honesty and humbleness. 

Candidate was initially mostly interested in being able to work on programming languages, functional programming, and compilers. Seems very aligned in terms of academic interests. Mostly concerned about working at a company immersed in tech that he is interested in, so he can get better at it. But, also mentioned that friendly people who are down-to-earth is important.

questions.question: Which technical project did the candidate describe? (Not graded)
questions.answer:
The candidate described a research project he did in the previous summer, which involved implementing and writing programs in a novel programming language for cake cutting algorithms.

This project seemed to go well, and was mostly implemention-based. He did some interesting work in optimization, specifically memory usage, because they were running into resource constraints with generating constraints to be fed into a SAT solver. It sounds like he did some interesting work with developing clusters of constraints that could be solved in parallel across processes, in order to more efficiency do the SAT solving.

questions.question: Does the candidate execute on their work?
questions.answer: It sounds like the candidate has a solid head for responsibility. He is a teaching assistant and worked on research during his normal coursework, and developed strategies for making sure that he could make progress on projects even when his contributors are remote, even when potentially being blocked due to communication bottlenecks.

questions.question: Summary
questions.answer:
Values (5-10 mins)
Tell me about what made you look at Semgrep?
Really like programming languages, functional programming, want to learn about compilers
What’s important to you about where you work? What are you looking to get out of your internship?
Want to be spending time learning, pay bills practically
Even if working at other jobs, would want to study these things on his own
Working at a company immersed in stuff he's interested in is important, get better at things he's interested in. Lots of academic interest stuff
Like hearing about honesty, values that, learning opportunities (from Austin). Friendly people, down-to-earth and humble, hearing that is a 
(Optional) This is a good stopping point to “sell” the culture. How do we align with their goals and values?
Technical communication (15 mins)
Now please walk me through a technical project you’ve worked on with someone else
Give the candidate time to explain the motivation and goals of the work, then dive into technical details. You’re looking for them to explain at both a high and low level.
- Research when working on programming languages for verifying cake cutting
- Proofs for cake cutting are very tedious, papers are not often proved, kind of handwaved a lot.
- Would be a good target for an automated proof system. Designed a language to describe cake cutting algorithms, implemented a couple of features, but worked on implementing real algorithms as described in research papers.
- Verify a 4-person cake cutting algorithm.
- Ran into issues with memory, lots of state space explosion. Can handle for 3 people, but 4 agents is crazy bit. 50 gb just to run the program.
- Worked on solving memory issues using lazy evaluation, "chunk up" constraints
- Generate a bunch of constraints and feed into Z3, but the stage that was most expensive was running against Z3.
- Could chunk up constraints to feed them in several batches, on several processes.
- Working with 1 grad student who worked on the theory behind the language, the type system. 
- Wishes he had done a bit more theory rather than implementation of the language.
It’s also important to make sure this part is efficient. If the candidate is rambling or taking up too much time, you’ll need to interrupt to keep the interview going. Allow 10 minutes maximum here.
What was most challenging about this project? How did you deal with the challenges?
Did the language functionally, but most algorithms that were described were in an imperative style. Algorithms written in english.
Challenging to transcribe the algorithm, and the language it was implemented in was quite limited
How did you make sure, as the project progressed, that you were on track?
Record time spent on different tasks/projects, see time allocated per week to each thing. 
How did you coordinate work with others?
Weekly meetings to talk about stuff, more often slack messaging with the grad student. They were very remotely, PI was traveling, so not able to communicate often.
Check fairly often, one slack message a day.
Didn't think remote would be an issue, but it turned out to be one. Sometimes would find an issue with the language, but the debugging messages would be inaccurate or vague. Exceptions would raise the same string.
Maintained multiple branches, so he could work on one and then switch when blocked. Branches of the algorithm*
What is the biggest lesson you learned from this project and how have you applied it? (Looking for a behavioral, not technical answer. Push if they mention using a different technology or the like).
Working with uneasiness with not knowing if there is a solution, got comfortable with that, vs like psets. Don't know how long it will take to get there.
Better at heuristically guessing how much time a task will take
Finding ways to decompose the open-ended problem into concrete subtasks
Do one problem, verify its correct, then do separate ones separately
English -> imperative -> functional weird jump, so draw more graphs and convert things into maehtmatical models
Now, working on computer architecture, had a project for a multiplier with a test harness for a whole multiplier and not individual modules. He now realizes to test the individual modules, so hook up is easier.
When doing leetcode, chunk into stages
Interpersonal communication (15 mins)
Tell me about a time (maybe on this project or another) where you disagreed with another engineer. How did you resolve this disagreement? What would you do differently if you had this disagreement in the future?
Working on a group project with 3 people, ran into slight issues with 1 of them being a junior premed and the rest were freshmen. he didn't care about grades
Had to do an open ended project in OCaml, other guy wanted to do chess. 
Not super interesting or creative, others didn't like that
Knew he wanted something easy. Pointed out a bunch of edge cases, tried to convince him it was annoying
Afterwards, pivoted to doing a board game which was more fun than just chess.
Would try to coordinate times because this person was always busy, but knew that he had time based on associates.
Tried to set up async workflow with him, with Trello. 
For the most part, tried harder to be careful with teammate selection if doing again in the future. 
Try to meet up with 3 at once, so some progress could be done rather than waiting.
Sometimes, 2 that were good friends would do work and not communicate about that.
One time they redesigned a module while not giving a lot of notice.
If they still weren't communicating? Not much else to do other than raise it as a discussion topic. Hard time saying no to reasonable things, hopefully. Need to talk and discuss.
Worst case, if the junior person was still on this chess thing, maybe reach a consensus or compromise where he would be more fine with doing other things. There's a lines of code requirement, so would feel fine if he did more of the testing and not have to do too much work.
Maybe raise it as a problem with the class, or do chess with a bit more flair.
Tell me about a time that you changed your behavior based on feedback from a peer, your manager, or even someone you were mentoring. What did you learn?
In high school, ran a python programming class for middle schoolers
Middle schoolers didn't give much feedback (there wasn't a great feedback system implemented)
Had a little brother in the class, and got feedback from him (maybe harsh)
Had fun teaching but his brother was a bit older, so if he had trouble that would be harder also.
Have more examples, if people have questions try to answer them more, and not just questions that he comes up with that could feel unmotivated.
Class had a bit of structure but mostly was whatever instructors thought was good to teach
Hearing back from brother helped approach teaching from the perspective of the student. Learning it for the first time doesn't need to reflect how you think you should have learned 
Felt there was a lot of lack of feedback when having issues working with others. On both ends.
Tell me about a time when giving feedback didn’t go well for you. What would you do differently? What about a time when giving feedback was successful? What made it successful?
Trying to work on feeling not comfortable giving negative feedback, especially in teaching scenarios
Someone feels like giving up, trying to give feedback on approach 
Have a friend trying to learn computer science but getting into it late, giving them advice but it seems that what he's saying might be demotivating
Work on being more careful with how to give the feedback
Sometimes feel like he is prickly and say things how it is, depending on his mood. Wouldn't say things intentionally mean, but would say as it is. Friend did things questionable and wanted feedback and he wanted to know what he should do, and candidate was in a poor mood and said shouldn't have done xyz for this reason, and was very logical, but should have been phrased more softly perhaps. Be more constructive. More encouraging.
Sounds like he has self-awareness.
Realizes that it's difficult to think of interesting examples, seems boring because it's kind of just received well.
Brings up an example with friends, talking to someone about what he appreciates about them. Saying that helps to get closer with people.
I asked about giving feedback to someone and it went well. Feels like he gives feedback and don't get the follow-up often.
Had friends go through depressive episodes and try to give them feedback, but doesn't necessarily see if had a good impact afterwards.

questions.question: Does the candidate communicate effectively?
questions.answer:
The candidate sounds like they are relatively introspective, self-aware, and good at communicating with others. The candidate described good strategies for dealing with others in adversarial cases, such as when others have strongly held beliefs or when others have negative feedback. Overall, he gives the impression that he understands when to apply compromise, and how to tactfully deal with potential stakeholders to achieve an outcome that everyone is happy with.

Some of his examples entailed him having made a realization in the past that he needed to see more through others' eyes, in teaching or in giving feedback, which was a positive, mature signal to me.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I was the only person who could make this team lunch interview. Candidate came prepared with a lot of questions. Many of them were business and strategy related. Lots of the usual questions about "how did you get to where you are" and "why did you choose Semgrep". Candidate seemed aligned with Semgrep's mission and interested in learning more about blue team work.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Was feeling ill that day. Spoke to Kurt, and we both agreed for me not to attend the interview. I'll reiterate my recommendation from the previous interview.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Interview verbatim notes available for reference here:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Xk1oIuijGhZyfkaH79PqDGpbD5RRvwGrIg52kze7rSU/edit?usp=sharing

BLUF: I really enjoyed chatting with Garrett and found him personable and approachable. Very low ego on this one. No red flags other than the orange flags he called out himself, which is that he may or may not have the depth of experience needed to be a CRO but really wanted to take a shot at it. A few observations:

High humility - quick to admit that he is likely not the most qualified candidate due to his experiences (or lack of global experience, as he pointed out)
Has worked to get to where he is - cited numerous times in his career where he focused on learning and personal development to advance himself
MICE initial assess - E
People-focused - but mostly with regard to his growth - talked about mentors that helped him along the way and peers, but didn't talk as much about the teams he built or the people that he mentored and brought along for the journey - would love for someone else to dive into this bit.


Experience as a sales leader or sales rep at a high quality company that grew from $10M-$100M at 2x annual growth rate - YES
Built a business plan for a three-year GTM strategy either as an individual contributor with a territory or set of accounts or as a leader running a district or region - YES
“Experience working at one or more of the following companies. Hashicorp, Mongo, Elastic, Sentry, RedPanda, Segment, Grafana, DBT Security: Snyk, Auth0, Tanium (?), Lacework (?), 1Password” - NO

Track record of building successful internal cross functional relationships with exec team, HR, Sales Ops, Marketing, Finance - YES
Demonstrated track record of building and scaling repeatable sales processes - YES
Security or OSS / developer company g2m experience - YES

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Jah-wid 

tl;dr - I had a good conversation with Jaweed, really lively, energetic, and just fun person to interact with. Relevant security expereince, but also an engineering background which is helpful in our space. Thoughtful as well. Get's the stamp of approval from background + interaction from me, will defer to Pablo on the PMM centric pieces. 

Office: yeah, not a problem at all. Lafayette area, coming in isn't an issue 20min BART into the city. Prefer 2-3 days a week. Lots of synergy when you go into the office. 
Timeline: flexible in that sense middle or end of Oct works for TC. No deadlines currently. 

Questions:
- Funding, maturity of company
- Marketing org
- Throwing people in the deep end, due diligence while interviewing 
- Top 2-3 things that Aaron + Pablo are looking for in candidate  + 30/60/90 
- Our timeline, where we are in the search

Overview: real passion for PM, fortunate to be doing that for most of life. has done other aspects of marketing as well, but passion is PMM. Launches, GTM plans, IC and has also managed, ultimately a team player. Delivered assets, sales enablement, partner enablement, PMM drives pipeline, sales, and revenue. Results, data driven & metrics driven background. Working well? Double down. Not working well? Stop it. Fail Fast to use a SV term. Background: looking at role and team, engineering background - bringing that technical background. Taking complex tech and distilling that into salient points. Godo aptitude for creating impactful content, collateral, and repurposes technical side. Coupling with PM is paramount. To be successful PMM - two key stakeholders: sales + product. Understands appsec space, API space, static and dynamic, SAST DAST. Worked with industry analysts, Forrester, Gartner, ESG. Exciting: good background to leverage. Adding to dev community at Harness, has CI/CD tool for developers - security component. 

Process in line, high profile role so meeting with stakeholders makes a lot of sense, and is great to get to know folks as well. Presentation, can see an example of success and communication skills. Timing looks good, only thing to mention, out T/W this week. There is a generative AE how to do marketing better using AI session. If we can start on Th/Fr that would be great. 

Looking for: eluded to it when talking about compensation, when folks think about comp it's salary. + equity, peppered answer with a few other things. Did due diligence on leadership, who is at the company. Looking at management, product + offerings, all very interesting in terms of technology - technologist at heart. Product fit, place in market, management, total comp. 4 legged stool, all things in line. Wants to leverage exp. and expertise into something innovative, new, challenging. 

Contrast - closest in terms to work at Semgrep. Joined contrast employee #48/50. VP of marketing was doing everything from events to product marketing to everything. Diluted in terms of what VP could do. First PMM hire there, enjoyed it - basic messaging + positioning. Talk to analysts, customers, had new messaging + positioning. Revamped all of that. Strategy: no concerted effort to look at dev community and analyst community. Latter part of tenure there =. customer advocacy. Lots of work done with customers, dev events, customer events. Just shy of 5 years passed, wanted to try something new. Harness came around and said hey we acquired a company called zero north (?), would like you to come in and help launch. In Alpha when came on board. Get new product to market in a company more known to be CI/CD integrator. Opp opened up at WallArm and they approached TC, more API space higher level position thought it would be a good step in the next direction. Company didn't get latest round of funding. RIF, deprioritize sales + marketing until next round of funding. 

Looking for something a bit more mature, Wallarm was a Series A - big risk, but willing to take risk. Contrast was B -> C.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: TC described two important product launches they’ve been a part of, one at Contrast Security and one at Harness. We spent most of the time discussing the one at Harness, which was the launch of STO (security test orchestration). This was an important launch for Harness and that product has gone on to have some success today (to the best of my understanding). Interestingly, there was a delay in launching STO between its alpha stage and GA date, so TC and I discussed the pros and cons of this, and how it impacted the launch and overall GTM. I think this was a solid interview overall and TC seems very capable; my only flag is that at times TC comes across as having so much knowledge about PMM that it’s almost as if TC doesn’t have to explain things in detail because certain things are already understood. Maybe that makes sense interviewing with me but I’m curious if that would come out in other interviews.

questions.answer:
Drive pipeline, sales and revenue
Very results driven and data driven
Fail fast
Need to distill very complex things
Knows quite a bit about our space
2 key stakeholders: PM and Sales (minor one is CS)
Shared goals, clear comms, etc
Making sure we’re in alignment on messaging and launch deliverables
Messaging of zeronorth (harness acquisition)
Amplification of voice: analyst comms
Product launch: sales decks, content, architecture slides, etc
2 SKOs every year; beta product was buggy; delayed launch by 3-4 months
Spoke about positioning AI around value
Trying to ask about past work with partners but didn’t really answer that question; spoke about joint webinars and shared customers

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Agile Mindset:
He advocates for a “work fast, fail fast” mentality, which is crucial for innovation. His willingness to experiment, learn from failures, and pivot quickly demonstrates a proactive approach to challenges.
Enjoys Challenges:
His enthusiasm for tackling challenges and using guerrilla marketing techniques suggests he is not only creative but also resilient in the face of obstacles.
Experience with High-Growth Startups:
His background in high-growth environments equips him with the agility and insight needed to thrive in a fast-paced setting.
Data-Driven Approach:
He emphasized the importance of being data-driven, which aligns with our goals at Semgrep. His focus on metrics can help us measure success effectively.
Philosophy on Differentiation:
He has a thoughtful approach to understanding competitive advantage. He raised critical questions about how we can differentiate ourselves and articulated the importance of our unique value. I asked him what he thought we were missing at Semgrep, I think he hit the nail on the head. Jaweed believes in empowering our users, encapsulated in his phrase "empower confidence." That really resonated with me.

Overall, I believe Jaweed's skills and mindset is a great fit for Semgrep. His combination of strategic thinking, data-driven insights, and a focus on empowering users would make him a valuable addition to our team. I recommend moving forward with him in the hiring process.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
-Great energy
-Very concise speaker, great job establishing problem and context 
-Love the inclusion of messaging 
-Overall spoke at a level of detail where it was clear he "did the work"
-Obsessed with the fact that he wrote SDR sequences! 
-Loved the dedicated portion of the presentation focused on feedback 
-Companies need a lot of help and they just want to be told what to do 
-Would understand a lot of the issues we are facing on the GTM side

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Weak No.

Pros
Demonstrated that he knows PMM from an academic stand point. Has the frameworks and structures that we need to implement. And he clearly knows how to apply them; while I didn't like the end result on the Semgrep messaging he did, I see how he got there and the value of the exercise.
Is not starting from scratch. Has cursory knowledge of what we do and the audience we need to speak to.
"Top stakeholders are Sales and PM"- loved this.
Exposure to analyst relations

Cons
Concerned about culture fit. "Hold all questions" and didn't take a moment to learn the audience (critical PMM skill!)
Very rigid and preachy in the approach. Like this all came from the book on PMM
Disconnect between presentation and voice over. The presentation was good. The words were good. Felt the two were disconnected and that's a flaw in storytelling.
Great at dodging the question- didn't like the answer to Chushi nor Aaron (curious if they felt satisfied)

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I'm a strong 4 on Jaweed. He has the exact skillset we're looking for -- has a strong marketing background, extremely familiar with our space, and demonstrated enough of a "roll-up your sleeves" attitude that made me think he's excited about a startup. As a highlight, I liked how when he discussed Harness' Unscripted event, he mentioned that he was at the event all day, talking to customers and getting an opportunity to understand their pain points. 
I deeply appreciated that he put together Semgrep-specific taglines as part of his presentation, it was clear he had researched us and had put thought into us vs. any other company.

If I had any nits, I'd would've appreciated if some of his responses were a bit shorter, but nothing I'd block on.

Questions:
- What skills (hard or soft) is the team focused on when finding someone 
- What's the KPIs and quotas 
- Process
- Growth + longevity

Overview: started at WeWork, mostly in - started out as a customer support role. Fielding inbound phone calls, emails, tickets. Quickly evolved into team also taking on product support. All internal requests from marketing, sales, etc. Wide understanding of diff functions and diff departments. What really got TC interested in sales = often times get calls from potential new customers thinking they were sales team - took down info, specs like office size, budget, location, etc. First inbound-like experience. Got TC excited to explore sales. At DocuSign, a pure outbound role. On the local gov't, local edu, etc. team. Pure outbound motion, got a lot of experience cold calling, emailing, LI research. Local gov't doesn't have large budgets, great navigating tricky conversations and overcoming objections. Impacted by a RIF at DocuSign, got TC looking for next role and how ended up at Squarespace. Inbound and outbound. Had fun with inbound, taking a more consultative approach - felt like TC got a bit more of that. At the same time, their team was really trying to drill down ICP, not totally defined yet. Figure out what key accounts, personas would look lie. Was able to get involved in convos with sales leadership, marketing, product to brainstorm ideas and come up with diff approaches on how to navigate setting meetings with potential ICPs. Liked opp to get more involved in a sales team in early development stages. RIF again - that is where TC is at currently. Moving forward, really honing in on a place where TC can grow - long term. Develop career and be there for awhile. 

Outbound experience at Docusign: had some pretty specific KPIs wanted them hitting weekly, came down to phone calls and contact adding. Emails = made sure sending out consistently. Emphasizing phone calls and more account researching and finding contacts. 150 a week, about 30 calls a day. Similar with contact adding, 150-200 a week. Quota was split and weighed both meetings/SQLs and MRR tied to deals. Took into account two factors, but would say that attainment wise TC was consistent with team members across the board - team was encountering some challenges. Given the sector they were in, SLED, longer sales cycles, smaller budgets. Having to navigate a lot of tricky conversations, budget top of mind. Lots of great experience having hard conversations. Being up front, ensuring that no time was wasted on any end - ensure alignment in discovery.  What problems can we help them solve, remove burden re: cost. Pushed TC to dig deeper in conversations. Squarespace: quota was based on SQLs. Took into consideration number of meetings set. 

What stood out = has awlays enjoyed digging deeper and getting better understanding of product selling. With DocuSign was always looking for more info, play with dev sandbox, wanted to get that user experience as much as possible. To navigate technical questions. Squarespace conversations were sometimes with IT or devs and enjoyed getting the best understanding of the more technical details. It always helps. Having as much PK as possible = great for SDRs.

scorecards_data.interview: SDR - In Person Onsite - Culture Interview
scorecards_data.interview_step: SDR - In Person Onsite - Culture Interview

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overview: Hannah is a personable and passionate candidate. She spoke of her experience as an SDR thus far, and asked pointed questions about Semgrep's culture and the SDR team. I sensed a true desire to grow here at Semgrep and that Hannah would be a valuable team player.

Pros:
Had a strong passion about being a woman in sales. Spoke of how sales has made her more confident as a person and has helped her become a sharper communicator. 
There was a clear path of how she got to this interview at Semgrep. Sadly was affected by two layoffs but spoke of her work at both places with pride.
Extremely personable and easy to talk to. Our conversation flowed and I did not feel as though I had to grasp for more questions to fill the time.
Really took advantage of the culture interview, was genuinely curious about how the SDR team works together and what an average day at the office looks like. Was very excited about the potential of in-person work.

Cons:
Did not ask about day-to-day SDR work, which is okay. She went with the route of asking more cultured-centered questions which is fine!

scorecards_data.interview: SDR - In Person Onsite - Mock Call
scorecards_data.interview_step: SDR - In Person Onsite - Mock Call

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Very clear Hannah has had good sales training from orgs like Docusign & Squarespace. She presented as a very polished SDR/sales woman in a mock customer interaction. 

What I enjoyed most was her ability to be coached. During the mock call, I gave her feedback on her approach and she was very quick to understand what I was looking forward and what she could change should she re-do the exercise. 

Strong yes!

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
High level feedback is that while I enjoyed meeting Hannah, she seemed a bit nervous and hadn't done too much research on Semgrep. The lack of detail in her responses and her reasons for why Semgrep were lacking for me.

Pros
Her thoughtfulness around desire to progress into an Enterprise role
Seemed collaborative and brought a positive energy to the conversation 

Cons
Both examples for the situational questions were pretty basic and didn't incorporate any kind of meaningful business results.
The reason for why Semgrep was moreso focused on being fatigued with larger organizations facing layoffs/RIFFS - also maybe a pink flag she's been impacted twice back to back?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: A little underwhelmed by this candidate. I drove the interview a little harder than I would like to, even with Gonen asking some good questions. Usually the mark of a good interview is that I learn something from a candidate, or they say something that I think "wow, that's really good advice", but this interview was a little meh. He also suggested moving our backend to Nextjs, which was kind of weird. I take it that was something that has helped his teams in the past, but I'm always a little concerned when candidates suggest improvements to our tech stack without taking a look first. I think it's a common downfall for even more senior engineers where they move companies and want to bring the tech stack with them, but seeing that in an interview was interesting.

questions.question: Does this person enable growth through coaching?
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: Does the candidate give feedback clearly and appropriately?
questions.answer: Gonen did give some positive feedback - he said that my goal was a good one for working towards promotion, and after the unblocking section of the interview, he said that I was showing good ownership over the project by bringing the issue to him. I think that was good.

questions.question: Does the candidate help the interviewer set SMART goals?
questions.answer: Gonen was very ambiguous with his ideas for goals. Pretty much told me that my idea of working on system design was a good one, suggested that I maybe take a course and work on a project. I would've liked to see more specific goals like "for next time, I'll send you some courses to look into, and you can chat with so-and-so staff eng to learn about how they designed X project".

questions.question: Does the candidate employ a variety of management/growth tactics?
questions.answer: I would say Gonen mostly deployed advice-giving? But in a more hands-off way than other candidates.

questions.question: Does the candidate build trust and psychological safety?
questions.answer: The candidate was a good listener and gave positive feedback, which contributed to me feeling comfortable speaking with him.

questions.question: Does the candidate help the interviewer hold themselves accountable for their goal?
questions.answer: Like mentioned in the previous question, no.

questions.question: Does the candidate help the interviewer be creative in setting their goals?
questions.answer: We didn't chat too much about different ways I could achieve my goal, so not really. However, Gonen did ask good questions in the beginning, like "what is your current level" (I would usually mention this to the candidate but I'm rusty on this interview so I forgot) and "where do you see yourself in 2 years". Also after I mentioned some stuff about our backend architecture, he asked "are you interested in learning about that". So he asked good questions, but we didn't drill down enough to make those answers worth something.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Med/high 3, almost a 4. Deep dive was kind of meh (weird to use Snowflake as datastore for product-facing code, but he acknowledged it was non-standard / just doing the best with what they got on a short timeframe). Project was using ML to generate insurance estimates for drivers. I really liked how he approached the problem from a personnel and managerial sense -- it sounded like it was a team of new and low-performers that he whipped into shape. I just really liked his personality / attitude / communication style. I think he'd be a good fit, but I'm happy to be overridden if he doesn't pass the technical bar.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
TLDR: I'd like to work with this candidate, I got the impression that they were a very strong EM. They asked all the "right" questions, to properly scope out a project, considering the team size, the urgency of the project, and where this fits in the broader team priorities. They were able to break this down very well. My highlight was that they had a clear security "spike" they mentioned an additional feature where they'd build a "correlation graph, to recommend rules -- for example if I added an XSS rule in Flask, here's 5 other rules I can suggest". I'd go up to a 4, depending on how the more technical interviews went.

raw

why semgrep?
one of these most security minded EMs -- wanted to bring security earlier into the dev process
liked growth stage, nerd wallet was like that when i joined
double it's size since joined -- more of a mid-size company, works more slowly. ipo'd & company culture took a turn, past the golden age. eng has a lot more restrictions
good dx -- developer experience

well apis, simple to use -- mak

not revenue impacting -- doesn't need to have same level of uptime as scanning
"what happens if the system is down? if there's a degraded experience?'
"xss correlation graph for flask, are there additional rules we should suggest" -- i really liked this! clearly demonstrates knowledge of our space


team & talent composition (!!) woo! 
"how big is the team?"

nice, asked about where we are in the quarter & if the team has other priorities, holidays/PTO. Very practical. "i like to keep room for the known unknowns" and "unknowns that can creep up"

great week by week breakdown, very strong. 

tradeoff in terms of scope -- treat it like a hackathon, try to push to make it, "pizza & drinks" <3 depends on the culture of the company. get more people on board. buying some of it. -- (milan: this has been the most nuanced answer about, how to make it happen, i've heard!)

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall a solid 3/Yes from me!


Context/Overview
Eng manager at NerdWallet for past 5 years, across insurance, banking, and investing verticals
Had to dual EM/PM particular teams since there was no PM presence in some
Brings security knowledge + focus: mentioned always thinking about how to build securely given they were fintech product, have used Snyk and has opinions on what could be better for developers
Interview itself:
I thought great communication. Took time to jot down notes on google docs: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1wd6ahMH6LWIzunmnytk44sHkTSRb9mfv4uIAe4HctCc/edit?tab=t.0 
Flow was good -- broke down high-level mental model, naturally moved on to inquire about team + milestones unprompted
Seems very people-oriented. Parallelize workstreams so every engineer can lead + own something, but also balance seniority and timeline (e.g pair junior with senior)
Had great ideas for how to reach quicker deadline
hackathon, make trade-offs (more easily done if planning done well), borrow engineers, and consider buy vs built
Why 3 not 4: 
I wish they were more product-focused and asked more questions about the user and if their use cases were met
Slightly low-energy, but this one is really more a nit
Didn't get to see how technical they really are

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall: Thumbs up. I thought Gonen had some of the best answers yet for the Managing Execution interview. Almost a strong yes (but its hard to give a strong yes in the Managing Execution interview). 

It be interesting to see Gonen talk to Annika. I feel like they have very similar orientation. 

Pros:
Strong combination of 'soft' people orientated manager strategies with also focus on 'hard' metrics.  
Was clearly motivated by being an EM. Had some clear examples of throwing himself into 'problem' teams to push himself and improve the outcomes of the company. 
Good mix of formal processes + removing things that aren't productive. We could benefit from a lot of his experience here.

Cons:
One minor nit is that NerdWallet was a very formal / mature engineering team. Lots of SCRUM and Agile etc. Obviously very different to Semgrep. However, he didn't seem attached to that type of environment and we could definitely use more formality and rigor in our engineering efforts.

questions.question: Execution
questions.answer: 3. Solid understanding of how to run a team and hold folks accountable under various circumstances

questions.question: Identifying Performance Issues
questions.answer: 3.Knows how to keep the pulse of a team and identify and differentiate poor/good/great performance, curious when investigating and creative in improving performance

questions.question: Process
questions.answer: 4. Deep understanding of the principles behind existing processes, minimizes process overhead and quick to remove an unhelpful process, helps guide team to create best practices and share knowledge instead of relying on required processes

questions.question: Execution Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Lots of detailed examples of planning, tracking work. 
Clear experience balancing competing demands of various stakeholders. 
Very metrics-centric, but not dogmatically so. 
Could talk concretely about trade-offs, cutting scope, outsourcing to different groups. 
Gave examples of times to use different quality of work. MVPs vs engineering excellence. 

Some key quotes:
"my job as an eng leader created predictability" 
"nothing is impossible. but we can make trade offs"
"brought in focus by using various engineering frameworks"
"once you have a GANTT chart in front of you, you can model changes to engineering timelines"

questions.question: Identifying Performance Issues Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Very metrics-driven performance mgmt. Tracking PRs, Lines in PRs, hitting milestones, development velocity.
But it was not exclusively metrics-focused. I spoke a lot about having conversations with engineers, daily standups, encouraging radical transparency, and working on 'draft / partial' solutions. 
Spoke concretely about working with an engineer falling behind for personal reasons. Had a clear plan to give them space to focus on 'family' but clear goals and performance goals to hold themselves to.
Also had examples of turning a team around. "Insurance team" with poor performance. Poor Tech Lead. To strong growth and org impact.

Key quotes
"I worked hard on getting engineers to be more open and share where they are at a lot earlier"
"engineers are not happy when they dont know what is expected of them and dont have the tools for meeting those expectations" 
"replacing the TL early who'd lost confidence of the team"
"setting clear expectations for levels is a core aprt"
"having clarity was a big morale boost. there was a major shift when the team felt listened to, supported, and had a  manager who cared about their growt"
"giving people ownership. assigning DRIs. was another way.  didnt matter if it was a jr or sr engineer"

questions.question: Process Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Had a great example of process change
A team that had a workflow that was highly demoralizing for engineers.
Two releases per week. less than ideal but we would only mark a ticket as done when code was in prod..
Code could only go to prod if it passed regression tests, which included lengthy manual QA. 
In a typical 2 weeks sprint, you'd need to get your changes commited in the first week.
This was hard. A lot of the time time features took longer to implement.
Caused lots of scrambling in the first week of the sprint and engineers tended to be burnt out in the second.
Caused lots of 'carry overs' to the following sprint which no one liked. 
Regression testing was a corp mandate and "how we've always done it"
Gonen tried a few things
Structing the sprint. New features first. Tech debt and bug feature second. Improved things but not by much.
Better fix: worked with QA to get daily regression testing. Allowed daily releases. Required more budget and horse trading.
Even better: Getting releases out pre-QA. Meant accepting more risk.  Features could be live for 24 hours before testing. High chance of breaking things in production. But sped up developer velocity. Improved moral. Meant that QA was no longer a gate. 
Another process change:
Split the 'insurance' team. Development and Data Engineering.
Made the case the Data Engineering didnt need the overhead of SCRUM. Moved to KANBAN instead. 
Improved moral and made the team more efficient.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Solid 3+. Feels like he's spiky on honesty and digging in and figuring out how to get groups of people moving in the right direction. I'd love a little bit of signal on how he's handled remote teams, since I didn't have time there. Also, he was pretty long winded - the charitable interpretation is that he is reflective and wanted to make sure I had all of the context. But I'd love to see if anyone else had that feedback.

questions.question: Honest
questions.answer: 4. Honest and extremely reflective. Takes an active role from day one in building trust. Gives feedback early and often. Helps those around them give feedback.

questions.question: Growth-minded
questions.answer: 3. Real reasons for startup with some depth or experience, took feedback and ran with it, solid examples of what they learned, why, and how

questions.question: Inclusive
questions.answer: 3. Values inclusion and has taken steps to improve inclusion on their teams, has remote experience and has helped remote teams build culture, has worked through a real culture issue on their team

questions.question: Passionate
questions.answer: 3. Active participant in building and maintaining culture, helps people see the impact of their work, tries to make team a fun and desired place to be, sets expectations around quantity and pace of work

questions.question: Passionate Raw Notes
questions.answer: He said all of the right things here, but I think he's still learning to connect the frameworks in his head to action, he didn't have a ton of examples here. Still, I think this is an area he can easily be coached in.

questions.question: Honest Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Definitely his strongest area. Seems extremely honest and reflective. Works with senior leaders, has hard conversations with peers, gives feedback and seeks feedback. Was very impressed here.


Talented but challenging
Senior engineer who was very challenging technically but difficult to work with
Has some issues with the PM, communications get blocked or things don’t happen b/c they don’t work well together
Reenforced that he needs to communicate issues he’s having with the PM
Engineer asked for clear artifacts, PM didn’t provide, but didn’t give the feedback directly
Made sure to be clear that his job as EM is to help make the team work better
This has helped things improve quite a bit, still challenges but moving in the right direction
Disagreement with manager/leadership
Finding balance between engineering excellence and delivering product improvements
Business impact of tech debt vs delayed release
More specific example: took over leadership of a very low morale team, a staff engineer who didn’t want to do leadership
3 teams working on a project with no clear requirements, project was a mess - someone had been working for 8 months in the wrong direction on database changes
Started to get folks aligned on requirements, was super clear that the tech they’d built so far was not right for what they needed
Hard conversation with the manager of that team; team either needs to pivot, or deliver something bad that would have to be rebuilt
Wasn’t able to put egos aside to build the right thing

questions.question: Growth-minded Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Likes security, likes taking on new challenges, has lots of experience working at both tiny startups and larger companies. Not a four because the examples he gave on the feedback he received was fairly standard, and I worry he didn't internalize the tradeoff of speed vs quality of communication, where quality is very important but sometimes you really do need to move quickly to give feedback.



Cares about security a lot - one of the most security focused engineering managers at NerdWallet, really likes our product and mission, likes the dev focus and the education focus
Likes being a part of “the good guys”, likes making it easier to do the right thing
“Making it cheaper to write secure code” mission resonates a lot
At a startup, easier to make a significant impact and see that impact
Has experience working at a much bigger company, and has built a few startups from the ground up that didn’t take off
Feedback
Workflow challenge: corporate mandate to release things before we call them done
Development cycle, had only two releases per week, became really difficult to handle
Engineers would send feature to QA, wouldn’t have time to get feedback from QA into release, would force us to ship too fast or miss deadline
Tried to reduce feedback loop, but ICs pushed back that he was micromanaging or forcing folks to work more quickly than was possible
Stepped back: tried to understand what he could have done differently and where the gap in communication was
Framing conversations with honesty and putting egos aside: “here’s what we are trying to do as a team, what we are trying to achieve”
Worked with QA teams; how could we improve that relationship and improve the release cycle
Now: when he writes a message to the team, tries to think about how it could be perceived
1:1s with folks on the team, or sometimes chatgpt
Has helped avoid his own preconceived notions

questions.question: Inclusive Raw Notes
questions.answer:
Unfortunately we ran out of time to get to the remote part, I had to speed through the last two sections because he was very verbose. Has solid foundations and understanding of the depth of inclusion. My only concern is that he may swing too far toward the "everyone on the team must be friends" - at the end of the day, we require respect, but not everyone is going to be friends.



People on the team are the most important thing, people should be supportive, they should like each other, and care about each other
Very diverse workforce, people come from all over the place, folks from different countries, contract engineers, how do you make sure folks feel included even when company policy prevents folks from feeling included?
Making sure teams celebrate each other's cultures
Sprint names were different countries or cities, learn a little bit about the culture, celebrate with some foods, tried to prioritize places where people were from
Did lightning talks, have people do intro presentations about themselves, who they are, their roots, what got them into software engineering - helped the team understand each other better
Company didn’t allow the team to spend money on contract engineers, so got creative with the budget to be able to include them more easily

questions.question: Raw Notes
questions.answer:
tl;dr - I had a really good chat with Bianca. TC was very thoughtful, prepared, and did their research. TC comes off as curious, driven, and thorough. 


Office: absolutely, loves the thought of working in person and learning from others 
Timeline: flexible and open right now, early stages of talking with companies. Located in SF, have a car, can get around no issues. 


Questions:
Inbound, to outbound what that pipeline looks like how an inbound SDR isr recognized as ready to move 
Process
Growth
Learning/onboarding/education


Overview:
Started out in the East Bay, Cal Poly economics and entrepreneurship. Discovered how much TC loved technology - opp to work with performance management startup. World with co-founder to build out GTM strategy, ICP, and that is where TC realized the impact that sales has. How much impact you can have at a startup. That was such a great privilege and exciting to see what loved about tech. Upon graduating, realized wasn’t sure where TC wanted to be yet. Joined a sales team at commercial real estate company working in operations as a revenue coordinator. Tough market downturn, great challenge to see how they could navigate the adversity and find ways to be successful in inopportune times. Worked on projects that would really streamline processes, ensure brokers had every opportunity to to make their commissions and achieve what they set out to do. After 1.5 years there, got a great understanding of how sales team and rev org play into leading the rest of the company to achieve long term goals and vision. Knew wanted to be a part of that, wanted to be in sales, what TC I meant to do. Wants to build out pipeline, make the deals, wanted to transition FTE to working in tech and in sales. Have been focused on finding a position in tech sales. Some familiarity with dev tools after left previous job worked vacation coverage at ReTool, realized the impact dev tools have on eng lives. SO happy to see Semgrep is looking for someone to hire. Eng = more being asked of them. So eager and hungry. 
Something that really differentiates itself with dev tools vs other tech sales, it’s the complex negotiation of it all. Explain the product, value it can bring and quantify it, but also different because as someone who comes from a. Family of engineers knows how much the scope of work is. Getting more and more asked of them, tools like Semgrep will differentiate engineers. Allow them to focus on wha they do best in craft. Create more value for users, care out new space, vs something more maintenance or admin. 
Missions statement (stated it) aligns - TC behind it and excited about what we’re doing. Passionate about complex dev tools. 


1. How well researched is the candidate? - worked closely, saw sales in the coordinator role. That's where TC fell in love with the thought of going down that career path - knows it is hard/challenging, still wants this path. 
3. Why do they want to work with a technical product specifically? (Why Semgrep?) - in the Overview we dove into this

Quota

-He built out the payroll function, implementing all new tools and systems.
-Has also has implemented 
-Has managed 401k and benefits. 
-Has used paychex, and paylocity.
-He did not manage equity directly but recorded it correctly as it relates to payroll. Not handling the cap table.
-He was there from ISO and switch to RSUs.
