questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
pros
- experience with scaling companies, esp sentinel one
- experience in HR, empathetic and could be a great thought partner with HR team
- likes working hard, explaining things to employees (esp social security, 401k) so people have confidence in their paychecks and deductions
- seemed to have good understanding of right amount of process needed for startup at our size/stage v. being a public company with extensive controls and documentation

cons
- struggled a bit to give detailed examples; tendency was to give high-level, vague answers
- struggled to understand WHY Semgrep; did he do much research to see if we are the right place for him, besides just being a scaling company that is raising its Series D

---> Worth setting up a call with Crina, Rachel - I'd be curious if they are able to get any strong reads on him or if anyone would champion as a candidate.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I was very pleased with my conversation with Jason, very knowledgeable in the payroll field, well rounded, great communicator and process driven. I found him easy to talk to and, I think, work with, he brings a lot of advice including international payroll, tax compliance with various states, and as we grow and we open subsidiaries abroad, he seems to know what needs to be done. I really think he would be a great addition to our team.

questions.question: How do you ensure the accuracy and completeness of payroll data before and after processing, including reviewing the payroll preview, payroll register, reconciling accounts and validating pay period changes?
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: What internal controls would you implement to maintain the security of payroll data and ensure only authorized personnel can access or modify payroll information?
questions.answer: He walked me through some of the internal controls that he put in place at Sentinel One where he created rules for any changes submitted by EEs and HR, he wrote processes and created clear flow charts with approvals

questions.question: Describe your experience with general ledger reconciliations for payroll liability accounts such as benefits, accruals and clearing accounts.
questions.answer: This seem like a no brainer to him. He said: it is only difficult in the beginning until I make sense of each account balance, but once I understand the process, I document the steps and then everything flows easily as most of the time in payroll, things are just repetitive.  I loved his confidence.

questions.question: How do you ensure accurate allocation of payroll expenses at the departmental level?
questions.answer: Documentation of all departmental changes submitted in the payroll system with dept head approval

questions.question: Can you walk me through your approach to ensuring payroll tax compliance and handling payroll tax verification?
questions.answer: He gave me many examples of various states which all have different requirements, he seemed very knowledgeable about each states regulations. I was very impressed.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Strictly from a "can this person do the job" perspective I'm a 4. He had a strong point of view. He's worked with most payroll systems before. I would be curious how others who were assessing for culture fit felt about him. He spoke fast, very to the point - not a bad thing - but I don't think he would be the type of person to come in and add something special to the culture. 



Raw notes:
Systems Expertise: 
Which payroll and accounting systems are you most familiar with, and how have you used them to streamline payroll processes? Can you describe a time when you integrated a new system, and what challenges you faced during implementation?
Managed payroll (ADP, paychecks, HRISs) I think workday is the most robust. Paylocity is what I would recommend for a smaller company, all the tools you need, check to see which taxes to add for which state. 
I’ve audited lots of companies, especially sentinel one - I was doing the state registration, talked about monopolisitic states, some states have their own STD 
Have you ever led an evaluation of payroll tools? 
What criteria did you use in making your decision?
I’ve done this twice - moved from paychex to paylocity, most important is the features that allows for segregation of duties (different access for payroll and HR).
Was there anything about the solution that didn’t function as expected? How did you work around it?
With ADP, they were great during the sales pitch, when you start using it it’s a nightmare. Working through issues was painful. Asked for a dedicated rep in the US 
Data Integrity: 
How do you ensure data accuracy across payroll, accounting, and benefits systems? Can you give an example of a time when you identified a significant discrepancy and how you resolved it?
Do an xlookup if things are not integrated. On a semi monthly basis I run a report to check against terms, new hires, etc. For integrated systems. For integrated systems, I would still run the report (HC and actual salary, compare against actuals to see if there are differences - maybe they’re on leave, termed, etc)
Automation Experience: 
Have you worked on automating payroll processes? If so, what tools or methods did you use, and what were the time savings or error reduction achieved?
Workday - set up earning codes, geo accounts, don’t need to do a lot of manual edits. 
For small companies, have a call with the HR team, make sure we’re aligned and have the same expectations, set up cut offs/calendar events
Compliance:
We have employees in many states. How do you stay on top of tax compliance when managing multiple states?
Check with google on different states, every time someone is terminated I will check when I can pay them
If time allows, default to how california law is - the most strict
Overpayments?
Try not to let that happen, it’s a nightmare. When it does happen, reach out to the employee and get them on a call. Make it simple for them. You can generally claw money back but you need to have their consent. From my experience, it’s about 50/50 - some people know that you don’t need to give 
Current employees is easier - work on a 4-5 payroll payment plan

questions.answer: I will start off by saying that he did not understand the instructions for the presentation very clearly which is something I need to improve for future SE candidates. Shi spoke about an ongoing customer opportunity that was still in progress. Existing customer who was sold a 2 year license and were coming up on their renewal in year 2. Unhappy customer with multiple challenges and not getting value for what they paid. Shi stepped in to work with the renewal manager and involved the account team. There were different points of view on what the issues were internally and Shi stepped in and worked with the customer to align everyone on what the main challenges were. Shi also proposed the customer moving from their current on-prem solution to their SaaS offering which was recently launched to help alleviate some of their challenges. Shi put together a presentation highlighting his plan and also setup a custom demo to showcase some of this functionality. Unfortunately as mentioned this is still in progress and there were no quantifiable metrics or outcomes. The example definitely showed Shi taking initiative and ownership and working with multiple stakeholders internally to come up with a plan to help save the account from churning.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall a 3.5

Erik had good technical knowledge and experience around the AI field, with work preciously in both traditional ML environments at LinkedIn, and GenAI at his startup. He had good intuition and thoughts around testing and evaluation, relying on his background in ML but acknowledging the differences that lie in the GenAI landscape. 

Additionally, Erik was able to clearly walk through the processes of how they approached a problem statement, engineered the solution, and then evaluation and tested before releasing, all of which had similar problem areas that we have faced at Semgrep, such as “this doesn’t have a definitive answer, how do we know that the model output is good before deploying in front of users”. 

Overall, I think Erik is knowledgeable enough technically in the GenAI space to be successful in the role.

questions.question: Private Notes

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Really enjoyed my conversation with Erik, really tough call between Yes and Strong Yes. I think he has the right attitude and technical background to be successful here. I don't think the career overview interview is the best champions interview, but would consider championing. We'll need to get more signal on leveling, but I think he's at least an M6, potentially an M7 based on his scope at LinkedIn and exp as a founder.

======

Product we built at LinkedIn (Senior EM):
Product externally was called intelligent matches, you could type in a structured query and get results back. Pseudo-monopoly, definitely a dominant player in the space. Steep learning curve, took people a long time to learn the query engine. We wrote a recommendation engine to recruiters find candidates.

How large was the team working on this?
This is something we started working on as a startup, and then we got acquired by LI. When we got acquired there were about 5 people working on this, once we got acquired by LI there were about 15 across two teams.

How were the two teams divided?
LI had a unique and controversial organization structure where they had an App team and a Data Science team (called the Relevance Team at the time). We had 6 people on the AI side, and ~10 reporting to the application engineering manager. Eventually the App team split into FE and BE.

If you had full control over the org structure, how would you have designed it?
I advocated for this change across engineering, my rec was to have teams working on the same problem work more closely. I wasn't able to get them closer organizationally, I was able to get them to physically move closer to each other which helped. After I left LinkedIn, the head of AI and the head of Eng, they re-orged so that AI was more distributed throughout the organization.

<I shared some of my thoughts on the org's AI usage - mostly around wanting more teams to use AI>
Eric commented on the benefits of a centralized team, and having people that speak a "Shared language," this allowed LI to attract elite AI people. But other teams felt hesitant because they "weren't AI people"

<We also talked about the Secrets team size, and how having 1 eng of each "shape" was challenging
Eric started AI academy: https://www.linkedin.com/learning/paths/linkedin-s-ai-academy     originally as an internal project. Goal was to help folks outside of the AI group feel proficient w/ AI

What were some of the metrics used to determine if this project was successful?
Worked on this project primarily from 2015 to 2019. This was a brand new project a LI
- adoption
- we didn't directly charge for the project, but did try to tie revenue to increases in seats in the main product
- got repackaged with the "jobs" product
- we also tracked thumbs up/down ratio of candidates that we surfaced to recruiters.
- we also tried experimented w/ suggesting messages for recruiters to reach out with. We experimented with auto-sending reach outs vs. queuing up a group of people and sending messages in bulk after review
- a lot of metrics at the time were pretty shallow "did someone click a button" and less focused on the actual outcome, e.g. "did someone get hired." We started tracking metrics that started w/ did we recommend someone -> did that person get hired
- also measured if people were receptive to the messages

Product that didn't go so well (Closefactor):
Mission statement was to help revenue teams to empower them with data to sell more effectively. One feature was we could help customers figure out what technologies their prospects were using. We had PMF and then the market shifted, and we lost PMF. We got to a few mil in revenue but couldn't get to 10

What do you think changed with the market that caused you to lose PMF?
The end of ZIRP, a lot of our customers were tech companies selling to other tech companies. We relied too much on a human-led sales motion, the space got crowded and it was hard for us to get attention, even if we had the best product. We couldn't grow sales fast enough

What's something you could have experimented with earlier that could've changed the trajectory?
I think about this a lot, we did experiment with PLG, but too late. 2 of the 3 founders were more salesy, and we played to their strengths of calling people and demoing. If we had held a higher bar where more people could've experimented on their own and gotten value.

The tradeoff here was we built more features w/ less polish and depth, which did help us land customers.

<We talked about their own pipeline problem>
We didn't loose too many deals, we just didn't have enough total deals to win.

If you find a submarket within the market, we can go both wide and deep as an experiment to see if we can win big in this area. Make these core customers really happy.

<we talked a little bit about how it probably isn't enough for us to "build C# LFS", we really need a suite of windows features, and how these should be packaged>
 
What's something you're looking to learn or accomplish in your next role?
I really like that the role at Semgrep would allow me to make people's experience better with AI, and allows me to get back into security since that is something I did in school. I am also passionate about building products that people like.

Mindset has always been to take mental ownership over things that I think I can make work well, "let me see if I can help with x," "how can I fix something that is broken"

Questions for me?
Eric: What could I do after joining that would be the most impactful

<we talked about joining as an IC on assistant to start, getting context on the team and org, and how we've had a lot of success doing this with other EMs at the company. I told him I could see him leading the SAST 3.0 efforts and redefining the Code team. I shared with him my aspirations around leading the AI-powered "fix" workflow and why that was personally motivating to me>

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Would be very good to work with 
I appreciated the specific examples of product thinking 
Clear communicator answered in depth but didn't ramble
I liked his definition of a good PM/EM relationship: if we agree at the high level objective and the goals and how we'll measure that then everything else falls into place
Leif vibes - cares about the product side, the GTM side
He asked good clarifying questions: Is the question ship or not ship? Or invest energy or no energy? What are the most critical business needs?
Self-aware: made sure to not ramble as much as possible. 
One of the most valuable things is helping people working on the right things.
Show me the cost benefit analysis of continuing this work.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion: overall I thought Erik would be a strong addition to the Assistant Team and to Semgrep overall.  I think he would help us mature into a stronger AI organization with more formality around what we ship and why, how we're meeting the business needs of our customers, what our model quality is, and more.

Positives:
- seems very focused on results rather than ownership and ego
- seems to have strong relationship-building skills
- loved his answer about how you respond to an exec or other leader who asks for something you simply cannot deliver - e.g. the model won't be predictive (you figure out what their actual need is and try to solve it another way)
- understands some of the key gotchas when building (or using) black-box ml models (model drift, bias, building something that's not really needed, etc) and has some good understanding of the differences between building in-house ml models and using a 3rd party llm.
- The words he used to describe model bias implies he understands some of the inherent biases of the world and our responsibility in counteracting them.
- He treated me with respect / I felt he woudl treat me as a valued partner if here
- Good response to how you figure out if something is shippable
- I liked his answer about figuring out whether something is in scope (just cause its on the roadmap someday doesn't mean we should prioritize it this quarter b/c some customer asked for it).

Negatives:
- First 15 minutes I thought for sure I'd be a no.  He was not succinct and did not tell a clear story at the beginning.  This got a lot better once he warmed up, and we have a lot of other eloquent people if needed (like the PM team).

Lack of signal:
- I did not get signal on wether he truly wants to be a front-line manager for a small team
- i did not get signal on his interest in developing the careers of his reports
- I only asked a few questions, and they were definitely targeted at the manager level.  Did not get very much signal on technical competence around ML / AI.

questions.answer:
A+, we should hire him.

Only caveats: hasn't written much code recently, will need time to spin back up. Remote, so we'll need to figure that out.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways

I'm a solid 3, seems very culturally aligned and excited about the problem space. My biggest flag is technical, especially as a contractor.

Values: 3+ borderline 4. Strong alignment high on respect, psychological safety, productive disagreements, giving feedback.
Technical: seems to have solid breadth of experience. My flag is that their python experience seems a lot more scripting related than building scalable applications, working in web app frameworks, working with databases, etc. For a FTE, I'd care about this less; if someone has experience building scalable systems in hacklang, they should be able to translate those to python in a reasonable time frame. As a contractor, I'd want this person to spin up faster, so this gives me a little bit of pause.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways

Mark is a really strong candidate for this senior position.

Great energy, would always aside his solution with "i know this isn't great, if we were to do this in the real world i would do this: {some solution}"
Good post interview questions
Seems like candidate is interested with working with people he can learn from and working for a company that cares about customers.

Part 1 - 2/2 points
Finished under targe time of 10 minutes
Leveraged external resources well

Part 2 - 4/4 points
Clarified any confusing points of the problem (even offered feedback to improve the question!)
Leveraged python sets and set operations to come up with a concise, clean solution
Improved boilerplate types, and went on an insightful tangent on how he would approach it in another language using the pipe operator `|>`


Part 3 - 8/9 points
Candidate gave an all python solution, and used basic sqlalchemy for DB fetching
Focused on writing a clean solution, but unfortunately was running on time and opted to quickly get a solution
Used TODOs effectively to remember to revisit a section (candidate actually went back through code and clean up TODOs)

Overall, I am a strong yes for this candidate. I was impressed on the technical insights he offered and his upbeat energy. He's assertive, and able to give feedback in a positive way. I can easily see him in a staff role in the future (although I only saw technical signal).

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Raw notes:
Before the call
Set the call up yesterday with a mock email to Jason and I, which outlined how he was excited to talk to us about Netflix and our security program there
The call
Very strong intro and executive presence: Was personable, confident and gave a great outline of how the call was going to go
His questions hit all of the necessary points for qualification, and he dug further into questions in order to find the pain. Jason and I use the same formula for everyone, and we generally use false positives as the pain, and one-dimensional questions don't get to it. He did, which was essential.
He kept Jason engaged, which is something that we look for specifically.
Thoughts
As far as the mechanics of a qual call, he performed very well. The amount of call training would be minimal. I think he'd make a great remote addition to the team.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Started off in SWE -> dev rel. Joined Dolby, in that first year - joined to help kickstart dev platform. How do we build a dev community, use as an opportunity - business case = large part of business is licensing. Had all this tech on shelf, make this available to market. Deals won’t be huge, but maybe there are a lot of them bc we can help a lot of people. Aaron joined that first year, helped unite a lot of separate programs. Transferred, reported to him directly. Spent years together building out programs rowing a team, and trying to make things a success for the business. 

Still with Dolby, things have changed. Moved into new role as a product manager. Reluctant move. Dolby has changed business strategy a little bit. Didn’t want to pursue dev community anymore. Whatever needs to be done needs to be done, happy that has a job. Folks who do well in devrel are on generalist side, part of that generalist is also observing a community or group and understanding motivation, what you need to succeed in environment adapting to it. In most parts of the business doing a dev rep function, e act one a diff type of role. Based ons Tate of product, objectives of org, and that’s what they did. 

What enjoys - never quite the evangelist, for TC its as how do I help someone else in company be the person who is there on the stage and do the keynote? Not going to win through my influences alone, have all of us be influential. Even people not employed by us me influential. 

Yesterday, met with Drew and chatted with Drew for almost an hour. 

Did he actually, is there a JD?  Has it gotten to that point? Role can be ambiguous, and it changes over time. 

Prides self on being hammer or tool, point to problem and that is problem to solve. Need a little pointer. 

Did have a job offer a few months back, turned it down - timing wasn’t quite right. Also maybe truth too - being at Dolby the proverbial golden handcuffs. Looking more towards - with the new year, new opps, new beginnings. Start to get to that point now. 

Timeline: January. Holidays, slow time. But, threading the needle as well. 

Commute: Didn’t talk about that explicitly. Not surprised, and generally agree - intention behind it. When joined Dolby going into Sf daily. Partially remote, but going in daily for first Q. Not surprised bc sees that as a way of ensuring success.

questions.question: Private Notes
questions.answer: Asked the Comp question - motivated to change for diff reasons. Int his scenario not a comp based decision to move, bc there is more out there. It’s more of a problem to solve, the problem space, and team to a certain extent. Handcuffs aside, that is a sliding down scale bc of where the business is. More of looking at the longterm play this case. But it is hard to accept less than making now, that would be hard to justify. Job offer had before turned down bc the base salary was more than what make but were missing a few of the other things. Equity component. Have learned from some past mistakes like 401K match it’s not 0 but some companies do and some do not as long as it all balances out. When joined GE, sort of surprised ended up taking payout not realizing making payout. Offer recently was a good offer can point out where that was at. Will send me offer.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Has a ton of drive. Thoughtful, wants to dig, seems like someone who pushes hard. Pragmatic, not looking for perfection, looking for stuff that works well and solves the problem holistically ("not done when the code ships, need to think about logging, monitoring, whether users like it and use it"). Based on her interests, I'd still level her a little on the early side of senior 1, but 100% agree that she has the right mentality to grow very quickly. She's someone I'd take a bet on.



I love problem solving
At the start of my career, so many things are black boxes, it can become anxiety producing to not know so many things
Trained the muscle to go in with curiosity, treat every problem as a learning opportunity
The more she works with different technologies or on new problems, gets better and better at learning and connecting dots
Loves working with peers, learning from others, getting more perspectives
Loves joint architecting or designing stuff with other people right before starting to build, loves that part of collaboration
Loves picking up new tools - docs, chatgpt 
When you’re using a new tool, often there are a million things going on - figuring out the few things that are the most important, finding expert blogs, seeing if a peer has any experience and trying to crowdsource expertise from there
 
Example: rabbitmq or using aws sqs, could hook up more easily with the rest of the aws toolkit 
Once she understands it enough, sets up a PoC in a few minutes and trying to probe how it works, what are the edges, etc
 


Kinds of problems 
Database optimizations 
Basic indexes, different kinds of columns, getting better at writing sql queries
 
After a couple of months here, definitely wanted to go back to broader
 


Figuring out tricky bugs, race conditions, 


What makes us a good fit for her? 
Collaboration - wants to make sure folks aren’t siloed, has worked places where it’s hard to get help
 
People and the culture, people are uplifting each other
 
Projects - low red tape, good ownership over projects, if they have an idea or want to bring in a new tool, they can - can own projects end to end, strong feedback loops, work isn’t done when you ship code, you’re also responsible for logging, monitoring, talking to users, etc, things aren’t just handed off to PMs
 
Work Life balance 
There are times when you need to go above and beyond working hours and that’s ok, but making sure that normally

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
TL;DR
In Matthew's words - "if you hire me, it will be for my product experience and not for my technical engineering experience."
While the experience he has aligns very well with our needs, I feel like he will need a long time to ramp up on the technical side to be able to perform effectively at the Senior I level.
I'll be happy to be proven wrong in his technical interviews :)

Pros
Product management experience
Experienced challenges with team scaling up
Identified a need and pitched a solution to get executive buy-in
Worked cross-functionally, influencing decisions and affecting change
Engineer at heart
Self-taught
Designed a game rating/classification system that had a difficult rollout

Cons
Hasn't been hands-on coding in a while with the exception of a hobby project
Not enough signal on concrete technical expertise. The new backend question will be useful here, as well as the system design question.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
OVERALL:

Matthew has an unconventional background, having spent most of his career as a PM. However, his passion for coding definitely came through in the interview and he shared several personal projects that were pretty impressive. I would be a "yes" (at Senior 1 level) if he does well on other interviews, but also won't be super surprised if he has some unusual holes in his knowledge since it looks like he hasn't been a developer at a company in quite a few years. I'd be much more enthusiastic at a SWE III level (the one below senior). It's hard to level given the unusual background, so I would recommend leveling low-ish and watching closely for chances to level them up quickly, since it's easy to level someone up/promote them but hard to level down.

----

SUMMARY:

Mission alignment: Matthew's passion for building games came through very strong and some security awareness came through too. He also said he was passionate about developer tools, though that didn't come through in the examples/history as clearly.

Values: Matthew had a good answer for how he recognized others' work (had PM he was managing present directly to CEO to give credit)

Project management: has strong experience as a PM, getting promoted up quickly at Roblox, and I'd expect much of this to transfer. I would have liked more technical detail in the "how" for risk mitigation: they had to throw out a fair bit of work on one project and Matthew didn't seem like he had tangible things he'd do differently from a technical perspective as opposed to just setting expectations differently, but I do think that there probably would have been a way to do a short technical spike to mitigate the incompatibility they encountered with other systems earlier rather than later.

Metrics: I wasn't 100% convinced by Matthew's answer about metrics and impact. He seemed driven more by user feedback than by quantitative data, at least on the project he first talked about (public API for external game devs). Other projects might have been better for this, but he didn't pivot away from the API project to give details about another project that had really impressive results from a metrics perspective, which would have been nice. I should have asked for that and didn't.

Technical: Demo'd a couple of personal projects that looked good on the frontend and probably involved learning a lot of different tech. Didn't seem intimidated by unknown problems or unknown technical stack - seemed very comfortable teaching himself and passionate about developing.

Risks: Doesn't seem to have experience breaking down technical projects into milestones for delivery or dividing up work to more junior developers, etc. since his background is as a PM. So, I'd be slightly hesitant to hire at the senior 1 level and very hesitant at the senior 2 level (but would be in favor of hiring below that level). Giving a 3 because I think the candidate is interesting and potentially could be very very strong, just hard to know from this interview.


RAW NOTES:


HS interested in game development and played around with Yoyo games, game maker, Roblox. Full-stack platform to code games, publish, and play them on any device.


3D digital equivalent of “can do anything” and could share with the world. Made a very popular game, which cemented desire to learn how to program and pay for college.


Finding exploits in the Roblox system??


Internships at Roblox during college and then worked on dev environment where people make the games. IDE and cloud platform. 6 months as an engineer - got recruited as a PM. Switched to have more impact, but afraid of not coding, but did well and enjoyed it and rose through the ranks quickly. 


Managing a team of PM’s but wasn’t in the nitty-gritty and missed that. Left around a year ago to work on personal projects. Open Source shockwave emulator. Might be good to talk about these. Developer tools are particular of interest. 


PROJECT WITH OTHERS


Players turn into developers 


When started, 2 separate products for the Roblox Client and the Roblox Studio IDE


WWW website 


Split monolith out into separate modules/services which led to more autonomy but standards for API’s were up to the teams (inconsistent maybe?)


Devs using Roblox Studio developers would sometimes 


Pitch: opening up the API’s will let developers do more than we can


Allow direct access from developers to services through API keys. 


Main technical challenge: API’s set up as different subdomains, standards all over the place, no centralized documentation. Built up a central domain to reverse proxy the services under that. Not all API’s are open yet. Teams had to opt their API’s in. At least 5 services are open now and can use API keys or OAuth. 


Learning: moving towards opt-out model. 


Impact: heard from developers 


Metrics: hard to measure. Service coverage and endpoint coverage. Services developers are asking for. How many requests are we able to fulfill? 


Challenges working with other people on this project?
Explaining strategic benefit of open platform. Engineers get it, but not all others get it. They see downsides (company velocity or security or privacy). 


How? Concrete examples. One team that was most reluctant to (economy team). Here are the top requests. 


TECHNICAL PROJECT LARGEST IN SCOPE/NEEDED TO BREAK DOWN 


Played games growing up from Lego. Created a catalog of which ones are playable or not on modern browsers. Have to download untrusted executables. Archives incomplete and missing images from the WayBack machine. Wanted to be able to play these without compromising my security. 


Very self-directed. Learned Rust, performantly display game tiles. Contributed to open-source project. Shockwave documentation is quite bad, so the one involved a lot of testing on Windows and comparing with behavior elsewhere. 


Knowing end-user impact and being able to build things people can expand upon and use. 


Scaling challenges: a lot of service-specific throttling and global throttles (per IP). Didn’t see a lot of abuse and it was easy to fix when needed. 


PM PROJECT THAT DIDN’T GO SO WELL


Built the game-rating system. About 3 years ago, has users of all ages. Wanted some parental control over things like violence level. Had to build classification system, prompt developers to give information, have reviewers play game, compare against what dev told us, verify results are reasonable. 


Started with industry standards for rating industries by country, but it was very hard to show this. Engineering team spent 1 year building system, the we then had to tear apart and rebuild our own version. Risk we knew up front. 


Delayed the launch. Could have done better if used policy and business development teams who would have helped me understand and mitigate the risks. Told the team long-term vision was build our own. But within 3 months of release we took some shortcuts even though we didn’t have signs. 


Learning: spend more time with the team to make sure they understand the plan up front. Rebuilding trust with the team after the first misfire. 


Small team building out most of the feature. Worked with BD, policy, Trust & Safety, teams on the app side to display the rating. Mandate from CPO to make this happen. Some amount of conflict because teams were worried that showing age rating would be problematic. 


Recognizing others: first half I was sole PM, latter half had a PM under me managing it. Lots of spotlighting the team leads by name, post in Slack channels during releases to congratulate the team. Had executive review with CEO and had PM represent the project there. I trusted her and knew she knew the material, even though CEO was blunt and intimidating so some others were more micro-manage-y.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Overall recommend hire. TC may need some more experience handling systems of larger scales, but has good app development and customer instincts. Would also like to see more data on how they work with a team before making a final decision.

questions.question: (5 min) Initial Presentation
questions.answer: The Candidate took about 5 minutes to explain their project and went into detail about what existed prior and the need for the project to exist (Roblox has no in-game way to browse or try on cosmetics for sale, wanted to give users the ability to do that). TC had went through the system diagram end to end and explained the purpose of each system and how it worked together. Some particular design decisions, such as long polling, had good explanations (Roblox game server only allows outbound HTTP requests). The reasoning behind the MySql server required some followup questions to clarify, as well as some general background about the game.

questions.question: What stood out about this candidate
questions.answer: TC's passion for the game and its userbase stood out as this was a self-motivated project. Even though TC eventually earned money from Roblox for it, TC seemed motivated by the users for this. This likely translates well into customer-centricity.

questions.question: Raw notes
questions.answer: Did not answer question

questions.question: (10 min) How did the project go?
questions.answer:
As TC did this project as a 3rd party developer and not a Roblox employee, it was on their own timeline and was a solo effort. That being said, it achieved success from a usage and user feedback standpoint and it worked well for its stated purpose. 
TC explained several iterations the project went through, from initially being able to look up item IDs to try them on in game, to eventually building an in-game browser which was a better experience. TC clearly cared about the user experience, as he collected feedback and fixed issues after they were reported by users immediately. However it is hard to gauge how they would work with another team member from this solo project.

questions.question: (10 min) Technical details
questions.answer: TC was able to explain the architectural decisions made during the project as well as some alternatives considered, such as flatfiles vs a relational database and the pros and cons of each. TC shows that they considered the scalability of the service and made adjustments as needed, for example the size of the connection pool so that more than 300 concurrent long polling requests could happen. TC recognized that eventually more parallelism might be needed, and was able to describe a general strategy for scaling horizontally, but didn't get into many specifics about how that would be accomplished or what problems might arise (like data consistency)

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Soft yes, project was quite old (originally from 2011) and in a kinda crazy environment (roblox game server), but generally had some good ideas around making the service highly available. Missed a softball re: using redis or memcache as a stand-in for pub sub but generally he seems like a solid engineer with a non-traditional background. More importantly, I think he has good taste (which is very hard to teach), is gritty, and could be a fast learner.

questions.question: Part 1: Why Semgrep and values alignment
questions.answer:
4. Has been on the smaller company -> larger company journey, likes smaller companies a lot. Cares about values deeply, inquisitive, thought about starting own company but very excited about the space the opportunity, etc.



Eng -> PM because of more impact
Saw the transition from small where everyone understands the product and the customers -> bigger company where it’s harder to see the strategy, understand the product direction, harder to feel the impact
Thought about possibly starting own company, feels like being at a smaller growth stage company will fit his life better (not trying to work 80 hours per week)
Likes that Semgrep’s customers are mostly engineers, Vivek described the culture as fast paced, collaborative
Evaluating us:
Met with Tom and Drew prior to interviewing; there’s chaos and things change regularly, would want to know that changes won’t be disruptive, can still focus
Good team:
Everyone cares intrinsically about what we’re building and the customer impact, ideas come from everywhere, singular decision makers, wants to be on a team where everyone really cares, lots of passion, everyone wants to learn and adapt and pick up new technologies, kindness, blameless, helping each other, high respect
Not good team:
Everyone is siloed, folks don’t go outside of their boxes, folks don’t think bigger

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback, Mentorship, and Sponsorship
questions.answer:
4. One of the best received feedback answers I've ever heard - dug into the nuance of how it was tough feedback to hear, tough feedback to act on, and how he learned he had feedback to give as well about the systemic issues that resulted in him being overworked and it being tough to speak up. Learned a lot about giving feedback from his experience receiving feedback. Extremely thoughtful and pragmatic in general here.



Feedback received:
PMing a lot of different product areas and features - was managing a team of five PMs, 60 engineers, kept getting handed stuff because he was doing well, was getting overwhelmed
Got feedback that he can sometimes lack follow through - better to say no or set the right set of expectations
Communicating updates, even if the update is “nothing has changed”
Had to get better at deligating as well
Would do redundant, sometimes “shameful” communication - “nothing has changed” - better to provide the same information, absence of information can make people make assumptions
Was very stressed, overwhelmed, felt like he wasn’t being heard, had to have a more productive conversation with his manager
Giving feedback
If they are stressed, when you give feedback, that might cause feedback to come back, need to make space for the person you’re giving feedback to to give feedback back
Start with observations, ask clarifying questions
Have to have built trust ahead of time, have to make sure that they believe you’re on their side, much more receptive to hearing feedback
Conflict
Working on a project to add game ratings to games on roblox - building a questionnaire
Lots of policy and safety and legal implications
We’d have these larger meetings to get everyone on board, certain people wouldn’t speak up, or senior people in the room would shut down dissent
Could bulldoze by doing ^, but folks would burn out and their concerns would never be addressed, they’d never feel heard
Would take people aside separately and try to hear feedback 1:1 or in small groups - had really good feedback, was able to incorporate it into the plan

In the next 5-10 years, security engineering will be super prestigious, be super critical to the success of most companies, as security becomes more top of mind, more penalties for companies that don’t do security well

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusion: Very solid 3 (on both the senior 1 and 2 scale), maybe more like a 3.5 but rounding down. Overall, this was probably one of the strongest mock reviews I've given in a long time. 

Clear communication: 4
Super strong signal on this as everything was crystal clear and easy to understand. They flagged the bad PR description + commit title from the get-go and offered suggestions for alternatives. They also were very clear with their overall asks, in terms of setting the tone at the beginning of the interview to figure out how the junior dev approached the problem, going through the PR itself to give suggestions and then finally wrapping up the session by giving them clear action items on what to tackle first. 

Technical expertise: 3
This would've been closer to a 3.5/4 given that they caught most, if not all of the bugs in the PR. They also pointed out that the user would still have a slow experience waiting for the page to load given the current implementation and it might be worth considering other methods if we wanted to improve the user experience in terms of load time. The reason why I bumped this down to a 3 is because there were 2 things that they pointed out that wasn't quite correct, so I wanted to cover that in the feedback, but definitely not a big deal overall:
- When I asked if a malicious actor could still OOM us, they said yes, because the API isn't rate limited. This isn't quite right since the reason is due to how the param doesn't have a limit set.
   - I will note that this might just be because we were talking about monitoring / alerting right before so they might've gotten confused when they tried to answer this question
- They pointed out a logic error where we may have been incorrectly checking whether or not to return a pointer to the next page i.e. tried to point that we may be terminating the pagination too early and not fetch the next page properly, which isn't quite correct.
  - I will note that while they incorrectly pointed this out, they did mention that something like this would be caught by unit tests, so we will get verification with tests whether or not this read on the code is correct or not, so I don't think this is too big of a deal overall. 

Respect and collaboration: 4
Probably one of the strongest interviews I've given that spiked in this category. Throughout the whole interview, they always had very great and collaborative phrasing to their suggestions such as "if I'm reading this correctly...", "I know you didn't write this function, but the naming is confusing", or "I know I'm giving you a lot of feedback, but just kinda wanted to go line-by-line". I felt like they overall demonstrated that they cared a lot about having a two-way conversation about the PR, and wanting to work with the junior dev in order improve on the PR together, and understand how the junior dev landed on the current implementation rather than just giving suggestions without understanding where the junior dev is coming from. 

Distinguishing between large and small issues: 3
Pretty solid in this category. They definitely knew that the implemented solution wasn't the right way to go long-term and wanted to get a better understanding of how we landed on the current implementation. and why other solutions were not attempted as outlined in the issue. Having said that, it was clear that they wanted to still give good feedback so they walked me through the PR to go over the comments they left. Even though they didn't necessarily spend time talking about how they would've approached the problem differently from the get go, they spent a lot of time talking about testing and putting emphasis on what tests could help bring to validate the functionality and correctness about the PR. They also gave me clear action items on their expectations in testing before the "next meeting" and that it will help identify and fix the potential bugs in the code, which I thought was good since it also indicated to me that they cared a lot about code quality. 

Tradeoffs: 3.5
In the initial PR review, they placed a lot of heavy emphasis on the benefits of test writing and that they would require testing given that there seemed to be some number of logic bugs that would be considering blocking for the PR to go out. However, once I gave them a bit more context about the situation that this was blocking a high priority customer, they were fine with it going in once the logic bugs were fixed, functionality was correct and there was some manual testing to verify that the solution worked. They also explicitly mentioned requiring a fast follow with testing to ensure that we got all the edge cases and to prevent bugs from occurring in the future if the logic was modified as well. 

Tests: 4
Flagged lack of tests from the get go. Also put a lot of emphasis in general around how the logic bugs that were introduced can be easily verified by unit tests, and can verify whether or not their read on the code was correct or not. When I gave them more context about the PR in that this was blocking the customer, they were happy to let it be merged after the logic bugs were fixed with manual testing, and then an immediate fast follow with automated tests.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
- Empathetic and technical in both PR review and in the video call, a bit more instructive than collaborative but not necessarily in a bad way
- Good customer focus, emphasis on end user experience of these changes
- Called out multiple times that cannot merge without tests fixing logic bugs that he spotted
- Called out abstractions + naming improvements + PR hygiene

Only nit: maybe would need to have a better sense of what a better implementation might look like - this limits to a 3

Only reason not a 4 is that he hasn't worked as an engineer for some time and we are taking a bet that he will ramp up technically here

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Conclusions:

Amy's background is strong from an IMM perspective and also well rounded given her experience in brand and partner marketing, so she has a great perspective on different ways of approaching campaign strategy. She provided thorough / relevant examples of integrated campaigns she's led (from strategy to execution) which aligned well with our process, and also has a creative energy that we'd benefit from to really start branching out on how we approach campaign development.

Pros: Extensive experience in integrated marketing management (IMM) with a well-rounded skill set from brand and partner marketing. She also brings a unique perspective on various approaches to campaign strategy. Her creative mindset would help diversify and improve your approach to campaign development. Her experience from her time at Segment, which was at a similar size and growth stage will be valuable to where we're at / where we're going.

Cons / Follow up:

Ask about specific challenges she’s faced in campaign strategy or execution and how she overcame them and dig deeper into her approach to creating industry-specific marketing strategies, including examples and measurable outcomes.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Overall felt like the answers demonstrated effectiveness oriented campaigns around pipe gen. What I will say is that while I think she knows "what to do" she definitely seems to have had a lot of resources and support to help her drive campaigns (for example, an large operations team to help with setting up campaigns and tracking data). If she's expected to be able to source her own data, I'd worry about her. If this role is meant to be project & creativity based then she could be a good fit. Light yes on my end. 
-


Can you explain the steps you take to gain a deep understanding of the ICP/persona and determine the most effective ways to reach them?
Has used tools like 6sense to understand audience targeting. 
Would also listen to AE sales calls & talk to sales to see what they are hearing. 
Competitive info - who our competitors are targeting
What methodologies do you employ to identify and understand customer segments, their unique needs, and behaviors? How do you leverage this understanding to tailor customer marketing initiatives?
Messaging should be segment dependent
Example from RingCentral - wanted to penetrate top ENT & MM accounts. She came up with top 200 accounts, then segmented by value props. The campaign also came with a "sales enablement kit." The kit would have different content tailored to each segment. 
Describe a successful customer marketing campaign or program you spearheaded. What were the key elements that contributed to its success? How did you measure the impact and effectiveness of the campaign?
"CRM is not Enough" Segment Campaign 
Leading indicators - number of PR articles, unique social posts, website traffic, impressions, etc 
Lagging indicators - 6 months after event looked at pipeline influenced & sourced (MQLs & trial sign ups). Measured through salesforce campaigns for each sub campaign 
Could you provide examples of successful collaborations you've had across different marketing functions? Looking back, what aspects would you handle differently?
Share your experience in developing customer advocacy programs and leveraging customer testimonials and case studies. How have you used these assets to drive brand awareness, credibility, and lead generation?
In your opinion, what are the shared objectives that a Product Marketing and Demand Generation team should strive for?
Really comes down to revenue & pipeline - need to be able to track campaign influence
She likes to revisit old campaigns to see what cold leads remain that can be "warmed up" by new campaigns 
Have you played a role in content strategy development in your previous positions? If so, what types of content have you focused on and how have they supported your marketing efforts?
Doesn't write whitepapers but she does own email & call script writing. More "operational content"

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I like the holistic marketing experience that Amy has- time in PR, as a coordinator, in Field Marketing-- and it seemingly was buoyed by a roll up her sleeves attitude. While a little light on examples of where she has jumped in, she had enough of the right language to prove the points. The top thing that jumped out to me though was her desire to scale teams and programs, while also knowing there are a lot of teams to navigate and align with. She provided good examples of where she started with a brief, where she was able to iterate, and the tools she used. My only flag there is that all of her examples were reliant on enterprise tools that we don't have at our disposal.

Overall, I believe that we could work together, and that our strengths and weaknesses would align well. I am a light yes because nothing wow-ed me, but also have confidence in her delivery and ability.

jobs: Staff Software Engineer, Full Stack- Frameworks and Services

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
TL;DR
Rex is a seasoned engineer with valuable experience, both in hard skills and in soft skills.
His technical experience will be valuable for F&S, having gone through some of the challenges we're facing - SCM connections, AuthX, full-stack expertise, etc.
His soft skills will be invaluable here as we're growing the team in size and into a more mature state. His approach to planning, executing, launching, and maintaining projects is exactly what we need to foster here.

Pros
Experience connecting to SCMs (!!!)
Designed the SSO migration at Segment
Worked on AuthZ migration project at Segment
Ported the auth service from Go to TS
Built a responsible migration/launch process - launching the new service side-by-side the legacy one, and reconciled every request between the two, measuring the differences before completely switching over to the new one
Progressive planning approach
Starts with ironing out requirements before designing anything
Planned parallel work streams (!)
Great communicator - coherent, detailed, dove into sections of the design and the project with ease
Strong full stack engineer
Strong FE, with years of experience
Strong BE and system design - shared clean diagrams during the interview
Security-focused
Monitoring and observability
Shared detailed feedback for teammates, recognizing/celebrating their impact

Cons
Seemed to shy away from (our) big challenges

Rex's Questions
What are the team's biggest challenges?
What are the expectations of this role?
What scale are we facing? (Asked after I mentioned that our biggest challenges are scaling and enterprise-readiness)

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
TLDR: seemed technically strong, kind personality, seemingly good values shown in the anecdotes they shared. Some weak examples of projects that didn't go well: "what went wrong" was merely "the project dragged on a bit" rather than anything more substantial, e.g "I failed to uncover some serious uncertainties and the team suffered for it". That and a lack of mentorship signal keeps me from saying "strong yes".

Rubric:
What size projects have they been working on recently? 
several months-long (sometimes >1Q), high risk, cross-functional (good for staff level)
Based on the way they talk about their career, do you think they’d be effective within our engineering/company culture and processes?
Yes. Rex appeared modest and thoughtful, caring deeply about the impact his work had on the end users.
How does the candidate talk about other people involved in their projects? Are they collaborative or is it “I did, I did, I did?” 
Specifically think about the differences in language/attitude when they talk about projects that went well vs. projects that didn’t go well
Rex seemed collaborative and modest. Did not show any signs of being "hard on people" rather than "hard on problems", and took responsibility for failures.
How does the candidate reflect on highlights and lowlights throughout their career. Do you think they’d be able to help us avoid similar mistakes and replicate successes?
Didn't get a good sense of lowlights in their career. Examples of challenges faced were weak, albeit relatable. I certainly think he's help semgrep replicate his successes, and perhaps he'd help avoid mistakes made in the past, but I wonder how he'd handle more serious challenges (e.g. personal conflicts or an urgent project whose complexity balloons 10x after he misses an important detail)
Do they measure success or is this left to EMs/PMs? How do they think about success?
yes -- for the projects Rex shared, success was based on usability and performance (since their projects were highly technical and, in one case, the goal was to have no noticeable impact on the UX).
How do they think about project planning?
methodically -- explores options, seems to have a high bar for product requirements and digs for more when they're too thin, values exploration and iteration, values estimating complexity/unknowns to appropriately pad estimates
For senior+ candidates, do you think they’d be a good technical leader?
100%

- Short stint at Change healthcare

PROJECTS

    1. Can use to build data models
    2. Segment: Reverse ETL, take data in warehouses and send out (contrary to normal)
    3. Would run dat queries, compile models, export SQL models, and export data using THAT
    4. Involved supporting SCMs, Github, gitlab, bitbucket
    5. Various auth schemes for each vendor
    6. note: seems very technically competent
    7. What did planning look like?
        1. Initial problem was vague
        2. Someone wrote a quick PRD, Rex asked “what’s this project about” and signed up for a competitor’s app to play around with their version
        3. Rex analyzed what the competition did, documented what he’d like to make and improve
        4. Sync’d with PM on what to do and what not to do
        5. Rex deep-dived into DBT and data warehousing in general, using in-house experts for learning
        6. Started with GitHub as a first vendor
        7. Divided the project into parallel workstreams: one on all git-related work, another with DBT, another on the segment side and reverse ETL’ing
        8. Rex did the last ^, asked his manager for peeps to help, each person picked a work stream
        9. Weekly project syncs
        10. After a couple of sprints, integration phase began, followed by an integration phase
        11. Iterative bre-beta testing
    8. How did you measure success?
        1. There are hard metrics
        2. We set the bar pretty low as it’s not a widely used feature
        3. One metric: need to be able to handle malicious attacks
        4. One metric: need to be able to handle 10 concurrent requests
        5. One metric: need to clone fast and not use too many resources (compilation duration capped at a certain amount)
        6. A lot of these were not predetermined but discovered
        7. From business side, at least 10 customers adopting it during beta phase was a goal
            1. tedious: needed to set up DBT params and other things
            2. Managed to do it! Painful though. But insightful and a good learning experience, made him want to build things for easier setup in the future
            3. By the end: “let’s make this as automated as possible” (subscribe to commit events)
    9. Was there anything you would’ve changed?
        1. It was a Greenfield project - rare
        2. Thought we’d have more time for more iterations
        3. Wished product had more concrete requirements
        4. Wish we spent a little less time exploring options, but it paid off
    10. How did you celebrate success?
        1. Didn’t really celebrate, since it dragged on
        2. The next team took it over and did their own thing
        3. Rex personally thanked the other staff engineers for taking on their parts of the work, especially since they had to go on vacation

Did not go so well: porting an auth service from Go to TS
    1. Assigned a project 3 months after joining segment
    2. Didn’t lead the project: was a contributor
    3. Standardized auth service that nobody wanted to touch (segment was 5 years old at this point but peeps still didn’t want to touch it)
    4. Anytime you touch anything in auth, you risk opening a security hole, and people were allergic to p0s
    5. To help others feel more comfy with working on the auth service, eng decided to port it from Go to TS (oof)
    6. “Was a scramble”
    7. No p0s introduced, but we ran into a lot of mismatch:
        1. Had a verifier layer check the output differences between legacy and new systems
        2. At first, had a TON of differences. Overwhelming amount of work
        3. Some difficult-to-reproduce issues, some because of replication delay (ooooof)
    8. About 3 weeks after starting (after porting all the code and plugging it into the permission check flow), realized the volume of effort ballooned
    9. Had to extend the project from 2 months 3 months
    10. Were lucky: management understood that this was an all-or-nothing project that had to be done right. They were fortunate to have the backing of leadership to be patient, but the project required extensions
    11. What would you do differently?
        1. Not assuming a code port would be simple
        2. Pad the estimates for sure
    12. How did you handle times at which you fixed bugs unexpectedly as a result of the port?
        1. Was honest
        2. If a fix allows more access, it’s a security problem. If it prevents more access, it’s an engineering bug.
    13. How big was the team?
        1. 2 others, including a tech lead
    14. How did you celebrate success?
        1. Was a big relief to finish
        2. Cooking class!
        3. Laughed about how risky the project was

Q&A!
- Problems you’re facing? How will I contribute to those initiatives?
    - (Cross the enterprise chasm! Eventually lead your own team.)
- What kind of scale do you want to go to?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Disclaimer: I've worked with Rex on a prior team. I try my best to avoid bias. 


Rex performed well in the interview, demonstrating strong debugging skills, self-sufficiency, and a clear focus on code readability. They continuously tested their code, quickly resolved issues, and required little guidance throughout. However, they relied on a naive solution for part 2 without mentioning a more optimal approach, and while they made strong progress on part 3, they didn’t have time to apply the prioritization rules, leading to an incorrect final output. My recommendation is a "yes." For a strong yes, I would have liked to see them proactively mention an optimal solution for part 2 and output the correct display id for part 3.


Pros:
Strong focus on code readability 
Made intentional efforts to write clear and maintainable code, even modifying the boilerplate to improve readability.
Continuously tested code 
Regularly printed intermediate results while solving problems, allowing for quick course corrections.
Quick to debug issues 
Never got stuck for long; efficiently unblocked themselves when encountering unexpected behavior.
Self-sufficient and required minimal help 
The interviewer (Maggie) remained mostly hands-off. The candidate only asked for necessary clarifications and leveraged external resources appropriately.


Cons:
Struggled with the final question (part 3) 
Made strong progress but didn’t have time to implement prioritization rules, leading to an incorrect display ID.
Relied on a naive solution for part 2 
Would have liked to hear them at least acknowledge a more performant approach.


Part 1: 2/2 points
Used external resources effectively.


Part 2: 4/4 points
Implemented a naive solution. 
Would have liked to see a mention of a more efficient approach.
Debugged effectively and quickly resolved issues.


Part 3: 6/9 points
Correctly queried the database, grouped issues by parent IDs, and formatted the output.
Did not have time to apply prioritization rules, leading to an incorrect display ID. 
Given more time, I am confident they would have completed it successfully.

Highly product-minded, my favorite quote from the interview re: product development "Ideally one of the engineers would join you on a call, sitting in to see how customers react". Clearly has a real passion for security & Semgrep (mentioned the Playground + already using the Registry).

"at twilio, we were blessed with good pms" i appreciate pms that work with the eng perspective & can fight for you with more time. "knew that it takes time to do cool stuff". very knowledgable about the product domain

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Rex had great clear communication and both technical and product sense. Would have liked him to dig in more into alternative solutions to the problem - considered FE pagination a "one month" thing and not a fast-follow, with a different solution maybe being best with more time (maybe query debugging) but also offered other options for fast solutions. Caught the core logic bugs and the off-by-one pagination bug. Acted as a great mentor in the call

Probably needs to adopt a little more of a startup mindset in terms of delivery speed and what exists for testing infra (we have no big staging or automated load testing) but could be good to lead that kind of vision as we mature.

Seems like a really strong staff candidate. Overall 3

---

Clear communication - Strong 4

Nailed all the objective criteria, actively avoided jargon and explained abbreviations, clearly explained the why between each change, clearly explained how the problem connected to the customer and to others in the org, very easy to understand explanations, and very kind in call and in PR review.

Demonstrates Expertise - Mid/strong 3

Caught everything but the backend page size decision. In a roundabout way, understood that the frontend loop was not ideal but figured that adding pagination would be a "if you had one month" project instead of something right now to solve the problem. Also flagged that adding more memory could solve the issue, but required knowledge of the infra. Talked about load testing as part of a suite to maybe go along with this, at least to validate the problem and solution - interestingly made assumptions that this existed (coming from a bigger tech world). Caught argument swap, pagination validation DRY.

Respect and Collaboration - Strong 3

Very kind, great explanations, asked for clarification from the interviewee for things he wasn't sure on (why ignore types?) - so assuming he may not have all the context he needed. Would be a strong mentor

Distinguishing between large and small issues - Mid 3

Understood the nits vs real concerns pretty well, did spend a good chunk of time explaining them but at least in an instructive way (I did try to dig in softly on one or two of them but for the others he spent a lot of time on by his own volition). On his own mentioned what was important for the customer and the product

Tradeoffs - Mid 3

Sort of considered adding frontend pagination to be a one month project instead of something we could add in right now or fast follow - definitely separated out the nits and the important things, and noted that increasing memory could be a faster fix.

Lack of tests - Strong 3

Called out lack of tests, didn't explicitly block the review for tests but had some opinions on what tests would be most useful to add for validating the issue. Good long-term vision for setting up "big staging" for these kinds of tests, but didn't necessarily offer to manual test as a quick fix to get things out. Overall fine

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Solid 3 for staff (refer to Zack's score card for notes)

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Previous score: Strong 3+. In retrospect after reviewing my feedback, I'm a 4. Seems like a really solid builder, someone who cares a lot, sets good foundations, works well with others. I don't think he's the kind of person who will grow into a principal - I think he really likes what it means to be a tech lead. But also that's 100% ok, staff is a terrific terminal level.

questions.question: Part 3: Growth
questions.answer:
4. Good examples of both technical and interpersonal growth. Clearly he's a builder, I think he would develop into a really talented TL for a tough technical part of the product.



Technical
Was not very well versed in TF, TL told him it would help him grow there, helped unlock more projects - asked a staff engineer to give him some pointers, took more tickets
Interpersonal
Got feedback: seemed to be good at leading projects, got pushed to lead more projects
Tried to lead more projects, was good, but stressful
Learning with every project
Has a lot to do with the team members he’s working with
EM assigned other experienced engineers to the project
Went from just taking tickets and doing well, to lots of coordination, working with stakeholders, talking to other engineers
Not much time to do the engineering work, that felt really stressful
Had to learn how to balance delegating out more tickets, while still understanding the bigger picture and the direction
Still need to be able to dig in when a problem arises
Was frustrated that people didn’t care, people would clock-in and clock-out
Made a comment trying to push folks to care, another engineer took him aside that he had pushed too hard
Was unfortunate, but learned that he can only do so much
Growth
Short term
Ramping up on python, frameworks, ecosystem
Establish good relationships with people
Long term
Likes to run projects, launch products
Likes to run projects and digging deep on technical stuff
TL kind of role, not as an EM
Twilio has “architects” and it is lots of bureaucracy, so much red tape, it’s bad

questions.question: Part 1: Why Semgrep and values alignment
questions.answer:
4. Very into security, thinks being on a frameworks team at a security company is the dream job. Wants to work with folks who care, who like getting stuff done, wants to cut through red tape. so so solid.



Why startups? Why Semgrep?
Very interesting to join a startup prior to hypergrowth
Very interested in the problems that Semgrep is facing, will be excited to dive in and contribute
Attracted to “chaos” or helping to build some of that structure
Likes digging into problems, likes understanding core problems, likes thinking about how to scale through tooling, making things self service for other developers
Been through many teams, understand different frameworks, can help us figure out how to apply them
Loves security, loves that he’ll get to work at a security company
Was at Security team at Segment
Has worked on scanning products/tools before
Evaluating us
Many rounds of interviews, has gotten to meet so many folks
Get curious with each interviewer, try to better understand the vibes, see if he can imagine working with them, collaborate well
Company culture - seems very hands on, likes that, lots of doing
Team examples
Good: everyone respects each other, can have opinions but aren’t forcing them onto others, discussion atmosphere - “we have problems, let’s discuss them, your approach, my approach”, people don’t hold grudges, everyone has the same goals, can move forward if there’s conflict, people explain their thought, open understanding of intent, if there’s a problem people are proactive and surfacing it
Bad: too many things going on that nobody is proactively seeing or paying attention to, people don’t have a say in what’s going on, people don’t really care, or people are very hard headed and there’s no room for discussion, people use titles to win arguments - very demotivating

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback, Mentorship, and Sponsorship
questions.answer:
3.5 Seems like a very very skilled mentor. Really interesting conflict example - he kind of initiated it and realized he'd made a mistake, learned a lot from it. Not a 4 because the feedback examples were little shallow. But I really liked how pragmatic his approach to interpersonal communication is, and he has a thick skin and is willing to admit his mistakes and learn from them.



Mentorship
Mentored folks at different levels
For junior folks, give them very well defined problems and expectations
Depends on personality
For some people, need lots of hand holding, will be very hands on
For some folks, you see that they are very self driven, can give them some nudges and hints and they can fill in the blanks very very well
For experienced folks, more open ended
In conversation, never telling, always discussing
Talking through pros and cons
Offering a different perspective
There’s always something to learn, no matter experience level, always looking to learn
Conflict
Migration of Okta instance
Quickly glanced through apps that need to migrate
Another engineer said “no you can’t do it that way, you should do it this other way”
Accidentally “banned” his idea on slack
Other engineer pointed out that he had missed something
Realized he might not know everything, was useful to learn something, saw the other approach was better
Didn’t feel great because it was in a public channel, but realized it’s ok - not about his ego, learned something, with age has learned to brush it off
Feedback giving
Every year, peer reviews 2x per year
Tries to point out strong and exceptional bits that they do
Some interactions he might not like - if it’s small, he tries to brush it aside
For bigger things, tries to give suggestions on how to improve instead of just pointing out the problem
Suggestions are usually like “hey, this was hard for me when I was working with you, it would be great if you could improve in this area”
Tries to be data driven or concrete, pick an objective area where improvement can be made

She had all the right answers when it came to her approach to messaging and positioning, what's important, what the deliverables are and how do you get there working cross-functionally with multiple stakeholders. Strong yes on hiring Wendy.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Rough notes:

Started on the agency side, super comfortable learning in a fast paced environment and excited by using new technologies, wanted to go in-house to have more ownership and longer term projects that involved more strategy. Led web team at Braze 
Project management to scope web projects
Resource management 
Prioritizing projects
Getting acclimated to a new company and their stack - reads docs but actually take the time to go through page by page to understand how routes work (learns by doing)

Pros
Wants to continue building her skills and grow her career
Seems very thorough and detail oriented
Business oriented from a web design perspective - gave an example of the importance of forms being above the fold, have standard web layouts
Used to having a lot of ownership and is excited to bring that to us
examples she gave shows she's proactive, a team player and is always looking for ways to streamline work - even if it's for another team
big on learning from mistakes or things that go wrong and building permanent solutions


Cons
I asked if there was one thing she would change about our website (big or small) what would it be and she seemed caught off guard / didn't want to give an opinion...assume she didn't want to give a "wrong answer" or offend anyone but would have liked her to have something to say 


______


Questions I asked:


What’s a particularly challenging project you’ve worked on? How did you approach solving the problem, and what was the result?


-first web redesign and Braze, solo developer, trying to get a grasp of the stack, blockers around the CMS and code base being in two different places, worked closely with C-suite leaders, dealing with personalities and communication
-design phase went long and when close to approval it would get shut down by the CEO
-had to rework schedules and was concerned with timelines, flagged to manager to understand what flexibility there was - came up with different scenarios and proposed to stakeholders - rolling release or break up features into different releases 
-for the next time, she had the insight into flags to look for, what to flag, etc 


Describe a time when you had to troubleshoot and resolve a significant issue with a live website. How did you handle it, and what did you learn?
-UTM tracking not stamping properly, asked what pages it’s happening on, looked at template and no issues, then noticed the UTMs were duplicated and stamping incorrectly, proposed rebuilding in house to have full control in one quarter   


What web project are you most proud of and can you walk me through the end to end process of how it went from idea through execution?
-website performance, site load speeds were slow, had a datadog dashboard for website vitals, highest converting pages, user frustration section (people clicking repetitively due to a bug)
-knew there was an SEO taskforce and knew that web vitals play a part so proactively joined the two projects together and able to help the other team, content team project to improve SEO, built an exporter for meta information for the team so they didn’t have to manually go through everything
