questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
- start at 2:32, writing code at maybe 2:35
- candidate seems ot have a solid handle on the problem, conceptually
- immediately imports dataclasses to make ADTs easier
- comments on how the AST is untyped, given the natureo f the question
- starts by writing an EBNF for the grammar!
- comments on how he has his leaves and intermediary nodes
- uses python, defines a hierarchy of Expr and classes inheriting from that
- because of dataclasses, has to write much less oilerplate
- realizes could type dispatcho n Python's type system values, but decides to just proceed with his repr
- asks a clarifying question on the expected failure behavior
- defines an Environment type for his context
- realizes he forgot a class for `Val` for mapping variables to values, uses a union type to map the str to StrLit or IntLit
- realizes he needs to represent his types
- finishes his signature for the typechecker at 2:39
- "a structural traversal of the expr"
- uses `match`! knows that python 3.10 has `match` stmts
- knows that you can pattern match out the `name` field from the dataclass, which i didn't even know
- realizes he could have checked for equality, but it is explicit
- mixes up the types for ToInt, reverses them by accident
- 2:45 finishes the typechecker 1
- clarifies the signature of the function 
- asks if we want TypeVar as the type if it were `a {}`
- writes some test cases to "get his feet wet"
- hasn;t yet written a test case which exercises nonlocal constraints
- starts p2 at 2:50
- changes his env to map to types
- his initial type is insufficient because it has the environments as result, but not the actual type
- decides to do it as an iteratble with `yield`
- Var case, "there's a possibility here"
- "if aything is not in v morally it is a type var"
- finds out in the REPL live you can't `+` dictionaries but you can `|` them
- writes code with yield to handle the multiplicity
- "if this typechecks at all, how did it typecheck"
- walks through his example by running the code in his head and keeping notes
- does a + b by looking at the results on a + b 
- realizes its not quite cross product
- does a product but realizes its not a product on pairs of constraints, its a product on pairs of envs
- writes some code to determine if the envs are compatible, does an intersection on the sets of the envs
- is still missing the fact that not only are the envs there, but they agree with each other
- his env matching code is a bit hard to reason about but i think its incomplete, i think he always returns the env unions
- realizes at the ToIntCall case that hen eeds to reutrn the type as well
- can't immediately come up with an example for when the lhs type and the rhs type are not the same, but that's actually pretty simple
- he finishes his first draft at 3:10 or so
- we walk through the code together because i dont think hes handling the conflicting bindings case
- has yet to come up with a + toInt(a), but he is now looking for an example where the two envs disagree
- i point out what happens if you mention a variable on two sides of the +
- tries out a + a
- actually i get confused and forget whether his code will fail on this so i just tell him about a + toInt(a)
- actually i missed something where teh arg needs to be at str

type solver:
```
def typecheck_v2(e: Expr, env: EnvironmentV2) -> Iterable[tuple[Type, EnvironmentV2]]:
    match e:
        case IntLit(_): yield (IntegerType(), env)
        case StrLit(_): yield (StringType(), env)
        case Var(v):
            if v in env: yield (env[v], env)
            else:
                yield (IntegerType(), env | {v: IntegerType()})
                yield (StringType(), env | {v: StringType()})
        case Plus(lhs, rhs):
            for (lhs_t, lhs_env), (rhs_t, rhs_env) in itertools.product(typecheck_v2(lhs, env), typecheck_v2(rhs, env)):
                if lhs_t != rhs_t: continue

                for binding in set(lhs_env.keys()) & set(rhs_env.keys()):
                    if lhs_env[binding] != rhs_env[binding]: break
                else:
                    yield (lhs_t, lhs_env | rhs_env)
        case ToIntCall(arg):
            for arg_t, arg_env in typecheck_v2(arg, env):
                if isinstance(arg_t, StringType):
                    yield (IntegerType(), arg_env)
```

Final verdict:
I am giving this candidate a "strong yes". The candidate started out by cognizing the problem statement very quickly, first writing an EBNF grammar for the language, and then defining a very canonical representation in Python (even teaching me some things about the language I did not know in the process). The candidate proceeded to write the type-checker in ten minutes, and the type-solver in twenty. There was a serious moment where I thought we were going to get to the bonus round with user-defined functions.

We only didn't get to it because the candidate had a few bugs that they hadn't ironed out that I needed to point out, but they seemed very meticulous in thinking things through, and had a good understanding of how to conceptually model things in their head so that they could ingest the problem properly. The candidate was clear to define the interfaces of their functions before they began writing code, and was able to iterate on this and improve it when needed, writing extremely canonical code along the way to the point where I began questioning if something I thought was a bug was really a bug.

I am giving this candidate a "strong yes" over a "yes" because they were extremely pleasant to work with. Any time I pointed out a potential flaw I saw, the candidate was extremely quick to recognize it and incorporate my feedback enthusiastically, and to brainstorm with me to think of other possible mistakes. Several times, the candidate corrected their own work, and recognized things they had done which were incorrect, and seemed to be very good at logically thinking through the problem space.

I am also giving a "strong yes" because of our chat at the end of the interview. I described the PA work we do (compilers, dataflow, tainting), and then launched a secondary explanation of the "systems" requirements (distribution, scalability, reliability), and the candidate actually said they were more interested in the second category. I had only explained it that way because I assumed a PhD student would be more interested in the former. The candidate explained that they were interested in building "tools that matter to people", and seemed enthusiastic to deal with systems-level concerns to improve a tool. I think they are the candidate we have been looking for.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Strong yes. Strong builder profile, thoughtful and empathetic, and extremely pragmatic and practical (especially for someone with an academic background). Would be a terrific fit.

questions.question: Part 3: Growth
questions.answer: 4. High growth, extremely curious, keeps pushing himself and wanting to learn. Has good frameworks for how much "scaffolding" he needs to learn - learning with constraints being more successful vs totally open ended learning.

questions.question: Part 2: Feedback, Mentorship, and Sponsorship
questions.answer: 3.5. Deeply empathetic, tons of experience as a mentor, giving feedback etc. Good thoughts around how when done well, conflict doesn't feel like conflict it feels like problem solving, and with feedback, the best feedback is small, frequent nudges in the right direction. Has been a lecturer/teacher, lots of similar energy to many of the former TAs here. Learned a lot about how to give feedback from receiving feedback, likes feedback to be direct, practical, actionable. Not a 4 because he talked about "consensus" a little bit more than I would have liked, never found an example that got all the way to "disagree and commit", instead he has a lot of trust with folks and once he realized he had missed some assumption, he would default to their view, but that's not all the way to disagree and commit.

questions.question: Part 1: Why Semgrep and values alignment
questions.answer: 4. Has worked at big companies, small companies, and done research. Loves the impact that comes with a big company. Thought he was going to have to step away from PL work after research, is so excited that there could be a job, doing real impactful work, doing systems engineering and PL work. Seems like its his dream job. Extremely values aligned on impact, cares about teams working together, has a good sense of what a high performing team really looks like. A *little* big "academic" in some of his answers, but he's non dogmatic, wants to get into the particulars, wants to dig for nuance, wants to ship. Likes engineering over research, wants to build.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
4

+ Clearly explained the context and problem to be solved (outage in custom CDN serving stack)
+ Design made sense, simple and got the job done ("could have used Kafka but Postgres was good enough for us")
+ Breadth and depth of knowledge on cloud engineering - complimentary set of skills to SAF (could be a great counterpart for Amarin), liked that he called out components and alternate implementation (LXC container - spiritually Kubernetes Pod)
+ Good familiarity with operational excellence - observability and metrics, discussion on blue/green (by design, no - wanted uniformity of system behavior) and rollbacks (keep old versions around, can specify previous id to run)
+ Awareness of people factor in project success ("I have to sell not only the impact of the company but that it would be fun to work on")
+ Good signal on execution (tickets, sprints, delivery graphs)
+ Honest about shortcoming and lessons learned - frontloaded technical design but got snagged on rollout, didn't talk with SRE early enough

My first project deep dive interview but this seemed super solid.

Nathan Taylor Project Deep Dive
Feb 11, 2025


I can kick things off
I’m an engineer at semgrep for X years
I focus on


Nathan
Last few years doing PhD, now dropping out
Applied PL
Currently in Austin or East coast move or back to Seattle


I’ll start by giving a high level overview of problem domain
The project is underlying infra for that system


CDN
We have two main actors


Client -> http request to edge node
There’s something like a serverless function on an edge node


Customer says run this function on this request in a SDL


They post to a management server which copies DSL to every edge node


Problem is 
Dynamically shims compiled code into running http server


Escape into C on edge node
gcc caused metrics to drop


NYT rate limiting
Hundreds to thousands of edge nodes in POPs worldwide


Dlopen crash in serverless code kills nodes
NULL to strlen causing global segfaults


Edge nodes should consume binary directly
Push compiled artifact rather than compiling on the node


Alleviate the performance hit
I was TL on the project
1-2 engineers working on the project


From the customer’s perspective, nothing changes
Includes statsd for metrics!


Assign a dsl to a compilation node, node writes to storage
We were running bare metal


Edge nodes from shared storage


Dig into a compiler replica
Job status (postgres)
Artifact storage (S3)


Mentioned Kafka, but keep it simple


Comp previously synchronous
Now async
Customer can check status by polling


LSC workers - containers, technially chrootjails with network namespace


Say they are all busy, worker can now pick up a new job


Mostly talking about happy path, we can talk about unhappy path later


Scp the script into a worker, worker becomes busy, update status, store compiled object into S3


Fanout listening like CDC on job database, diff previous and current state, read blob name from S3 and fan out to S3



Tom:
blue/green
Build - dirft of libraries between workers and edge nodes


Did not have to worry about ABI compat when compiling on edge nodes
That’s now a thing we have to think about when rolling edge nodes
SRE had to be involved towards the end of the work
LXC workers for each worker node configuration


Blue green - non feature, edge nodes sharded geographically
We wanted consistent behavior regardless of edge node
It would add non-determinism


You could home roll in DSL if you wanted


Push time important
Original p99 in ~10seconds


Chris - rollbacks?
We keep all recent N artifacts
Customer can specify which version to run
Observability infra helped us find which customer’s scripts were failing


Deep dive into LXC container
After compilation with gcc, want something to prevent obvious security and crash code


Add fuzzed, syscall monitor - partnership with security team


We spun up a mini edge within the worker to
I didn’t build any of this - I did the platform piece, they did heavy lifting and 
Assert expectations around syscalls, 15 seconds
This affects latency of rollout


Passes verdict - pass/fail
Exposed similarly as compilation error to end user


What metrics are we collecting as the application runs?
500s
Queue depth


We didn’t because we were running on bare metal, wasn’t in Kub
We didn’t ever hit this in prod but setup a NACK on job submission


Lessons learned
My first TL experience a few jobs ago
Like talking about it thing because it is straightforward


Big issues were “Layer 8” - people


Had never led an engineering effort before
First charge on “P” product


Shipped, and more or less on time
Had to sell that this is good for the company and you’ll have a good time working on it with me
Table stakes previously, now putting more weight


I was not talking to SRE as I was building this
We wanted it to be done by Q3
Can we have a meeting to talk about how to deploy it
Why are you just coming to us right now


Frontloaded the design, didn’t want to get tripped over technical issues
Ended up tripping over people issues
We did a rough deploy in tandem with legacy system, dry running


Verify LXC did the same thing as the edge nodes


That’s me spinning as more validation


> How did you manage comms with different stakeholders
Tell me about everyone else


Impetus for this work was the outage
Had buy in from upper leadership
Interested in how things were progressing
They were naturally in the loop


Project management - assigned tasks to engineers
Issues in GitHub, epic/sprint in Jira
Did my best to keep it update to date

Maybe 150 eng - wouldn’t work at a bigger company


No secret sauce
I like writing things downs


“I’m not very smart - I don’t lose track of it”
Written down in the wiki


Final question
> What gives you energy and takes energy away?


I like working on a thing that helps people
Language and PL stuff - tooling that helps other developers
I love hearing: “That thing you’re building is really going to help me”


Take away energy:
Working by myself, prefer a collaborative environment
Personality wise - introvert, do my best work in conversations with others
Don’t like noodling in a corner


Don’t want to work on something that matters to no one
Has to be somewhat interesting


I took a job “Distributed Systems” engineer - was only writing puppet configs
It was a miserable slog



What keeps you up at night?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I really enjoyed hearing the conversation between him and Nat, especially when Taylor clocked many of the abstractions/challenges the semgrep engine has (common IR, interporc analysis, taint flows, parallelism, fixpoint timeouts) very naturally after Nat asked him one of the probing interview questions (i.e "Ask them what kind of stability and performance issues they might expect when productionizing such an analysis").

He also seemed, in his line of thinking, to always stumble upon incremental scanning like mechanism to address perf issues or soundness issues, which is interesting since it isn't something we have yet to put on our roadmap. I am not sure if there's any cons I could have spotted: He is a disgruntled academic, but it seems like he really does hate being in academia; the pattern in his resume and the conversation he had with Nat seems to support that.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
It's clear that he has experience with program analysis both in an academic setting and in a more practical industry setting. He often didn't arrive at exactly the answers I was hoping to hear, but I think that was more of an issue with the interview I planned. All of his answers were very thoughtful and he did well at narrating the issues he expected to face and brainstorming solutions to them. It was clear that he has a strong background and is very smart. For example, in a discussion on productionizing dataflow analysis, he noted the need for a language-agnostic representation of code upon which we can perform an analysis, and he correctly guessed that module resolution would be an area where it is difficult to do things in a language-agnostic way. He also suggested breaking up the analysis into smaller chunks (e.g. file-by-file) and even speculated that for large functions we could analyze subgraphs of the CFG separately. He discussed the possibilities of speedups we could gain by doing an incremental analysis and only re-analyzing things that could have been affected by the change at hand. He expressed excitement around moving our analysis to multicore, which I suspect would be a great fit for him based on his recent experience with parallelizing Paxos. He was also friendly, enthusiastic, and easy to talk to.

I would be thrilled to have him on my team.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
* Summary
** Culture
 * Wants to work somewhere it's ok to ask about dumb stuff
 * Feel, and be seen, as productive
 * Have momentum
 * Reflective! Would give an answer and would say "hmm I should meditate on that more"
 * Would double check he understood the question/he was giving the answer I was asking for
 * Assume everyone is smart/competent, treat them like it
** Mentorship
 * Doesn't like Apple where principle engineers do hard stuff, and anyone below doesn't have the opportunity to do the hard stuff
 * "Quiet confidence" -> make sure he sees the whole picture, and set up junior devs to do the hard stuff (similar to pad/tom)
 * Step X -> ok what's next?
 * Not being prescriptive, make sure support is felt but not breathing down neck
** Engineering
 * Knows his shit
 * Strong understanding/experience with systems/OS <-> compilers
 * Could easily explain tough topics, like an issue in the JVM due to logging when the GC would stop the world
 * Knows his OS stuff, has the experience
** Debugging/problem solving
 * Can get told something is "just not working", and dive deep to figure it out
 * Starts at basics, make sure he understands all inputs, starts at easy cases first
 * Remediates by reflecting what he can do to stop it from happening again
 * Strongly believes in writing tests that exercise the failure paths
 * Start with small tests, they can even be removed after awhile
 * Communication and context is key -> easier to solve hard problems if they have an "embedded" engineer
 * Less theory, just try it see if it works
 * Good management of bug squashing, understands that a bug is always someone's top priority, so first try and understand what is best for company before taking it on
 * Constantly re-evaluate where bug is
 * Bug squashing like a doctor "try this and see if it helps, if not come back later"
 * Not afraid to ask senior engineers for sanity checks/help
 * Good reflection on how to improve things going forward, but needed a bit of prompting
 * Did not talk much about improving things at scale/testing at scale without prompting
 * Can totally handle and solve systems issues and make those improvements
 * Good resource for testing/debugging knowledge, but not going to actively drag us into a better state
 * Documentation is great for passing knowledge, long comments in code
** Scaling
 * PBT testing, fuzz testing etc.
 * Has done some A/B testing, loves unit tests
 * Understands gradual roll out and sharding testing by behavior
 * Test the happy paths, AND the failure paths
 * Used to working on "monoliths" that are hard to write unit tests for, a lot of integration tests
 * Tried to push him to talk about experience on how these monoliths are tested, and his experience, he has used them and understands them but hasn't done the work himself as much
 * Did not talk much about observability/release cycle/testing
 * Definitely can do scaling/perf feature work e.g. Um/Tom, but not observability/release e.g. Chris
 * Does not seem like he's going to significantly improve our release process
 * Has reliability/testing knowledge, not sure about experience/how active he'll be

* Technical project - Challenging - time crunch/fog of war (e.g. segfault, compiler bug, hard to reproduce locally)
** Summary
*** JVM - behavior changes, tail effeciency jvm logging
*** lock free data structures for blocking reading
*** "Just not working"
** How did you identify?
*** Checking JVM version
*** next checking deadlocks
*** took snapshots
*** checking if threads parked
*** Check jvm changelog - runtime behavior
*** What about compiler
*** Checked assembly for misaligned jump
*** Realized they should have asked them what they changed
*** Ring buffer - didn't test - blocking
*** Every step wrong question
** How did you know you were making process
*** Always up against a wall
*** Scary senior engineers - db that holds the tweets
*** Keep in mind all inputs
*** Next time test things instead of theorizing
*** Have I exercised the rare failure path thing I think is fine
*** Neglect to test failure paths vs happy paths
*** Call in senior engineers
*** Wrong hat

** What was the remediation - reporting, standards, sharing info?
*** Hard because it's a monolith that's impossible to write unit tests for
*** If they had a better relationship with the storage team, someone embedded - could have been anticipated
*** Easier said than done due to large orgs
*** Contrast - optimizing regexps - fast deploy - knew within 10 minutes that something went wrong
** When do you give up?
*** If there's no choice - buckle in
*** Constantly re-evaluate how critical it is for them
*** "It's always someone's top priority"
*** How good does it have to be
*** How to just unblock - doctors office, come back if it doesn't

*** What to measure

**** gradual rollout
**** sharding behavior
** How do you make it easy to solve these problems at scale (i.e. classes of errors)
** How do you make it easy to fearlessly contribute? (testing, debugging)
*** Documentation
*** code changes, comments don't
*** Why not how comments
*** Likes small tests
*** Tests well designed to enable change
*** Small tests -> then remove
*** Tutorials
* Mentorship

*** gnarly things
*** how to make it comfortable
*** breaking it down piecemeal -> incremental
*** giving examples
*** Assume best intentions -> not condescending
*** Quiet confidence you know how it works
*** Ensuring there's context
*** Not breathing down your neck vs far away
*** Not adversarial
*** Getting people working on the hard stuff
*** Apple -> PE
* Bonus nerd snipe: Time travel debugger, how do you automate/do it at scale? Or why not do that, what's the alternative
* Their questions

candidate_data.title: Events Marketing Manager - Brand and Creative

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
-Lives in Oakland. 
-Doesn't like to get bored, would rather be overwhelmed. 
-Doesn't have domain experience, but this is my only flag. Other than that I was really impressed. 

General:
-Has been at Coursera for 2.5yrs.
-Within her first 5 weeks her whole team got laid off and she moved into the Global Events Lead.
-Does all events, strategy, and processes from scratch.
-Did her first in-person conference in Vegas back in September. 
-Currently in the middle of Coursera's SKO, 260 attendees. 
-She managed a team of 9 and was responsible for global events at CareDX.

What they're looking for:
-Wants to move on to something new. 
-Loves moving fast.
-She's a builder. Wants to be able to implement her frameworks.
-Targeting startups.

Interview Status:
-Just started her search. 
-She got an offer which she turned down on Monday. The company didn't provide any equity, and wanted her to commute from Oakland to San Mateo.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: I really enjoyed my conversation with Caitlin. She was very confident, experienced, and organized. She has run everything from large 3rd party tradeshows, to hosted tradeshows, to global SKOs. I felt she was organized, could come in and immediately make an impact, and someone we wouldn't need to worry about executing at the level we need her to. I would be excited if she was hired.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Caitlin has clear communications and could bring a strong energy to the team.

From a PMM partnership standpoint; she relies on clear communications and set dates. Big believer in update, update, update. When we talked about struggles with PMM partnerships in the past, she had a great example of when a team or person comes from a place of "no" and how her deep seeded curiosity helps her to get around that.

When we spoke about the ICP though, she had far less to contribute. We did get to some nuances about differences and details from the theoretical to the practical and what it looks like to build an anecdotal data loop. But it is my top flag for her specifically in the role- large scale companies with very defined and set profiles. 

Moving on to Brand, she had a great definition for brand implementation "consistent, clarity, ubiquitousness" and that "brand is a feeling". Our conversation here centered on not just knowing what it is, but how to evoke it and how to use it to draw people in. 

Overall, I liked her energy and the approach she laid out to events. My concerns keeping me from strong yes are the size and scale of companies she has worked with.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Summary:
Overall Caitlin is my top choice for this role. She blends both the Project Management and Creative prowess into one. Here reasons for why Semgrep align with our culture - high growth, in office relationships, opportunity to manage scaling events program and potentially a team down the road.

Permanent remote - doesn’t love it, wants a team, in office presence, Oakland, values events (her current org went through massive budget cuts), 20 yrs in - growth
Coursera - stepped in right as vaccine was available, 3rd party trade show events/activations, scaled own events first and foremost, CAB/investigator meetings/medical specific meetings, standard processes and checklists, 300% growth while there, plug in right folks at right time, brain works in excel, their brains in ppt, central calendar color coded by type of event, proper workbook schedule, gave notice already
Distinction btw project manager vs great event person, contracts w legal, finance
Standardizing - process
Most successful activation — brand awareness 
Wifi sponsorship, negotiating things not on prospectus, think of splash page - QR codes to book meetings, creativity on shoestring budget, brand and style guide just for the event (10>1 concept) custom booth build, messaging, etc — connect (movement, visual support of the concept), social media exposure 
Sales - end user vs buyer, goals for events; thinking through 
Talk to her about structure - field and sr events/ how it’ll feed
CAB Too many other things going on — separate it!!! Regional CABs, CAB over user conf if pipeline more important

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
I'm a strong yes at IC5. I would be a soft yes at staff (potential is there but I don't have good examples for everything to say that level). Clear mentorship ability and very very strong values alignment. Only flag is that they want to be an IC for a "while" but I think they would be a very successful TL or TLM for this team and that the desire is to stay close enough to IC work to grow there. I think if we needed them to take on a report or two, they might be open to that as they want to be between the IC <> manager life for a bit. 

General:
Rubric: Strong Yes
 Overall: Do they communicate at the right level of detail (e.g. do they provide examples and details when asked)? Do they refrain from rambling? Do they ask questions? Do they pause for questions or otherwise ensure their content is landing? -> Strong yes. Even confirmed after talking that they were getting at what I was hoping to get at. 
 Communication: Are they respectful? -> Yes. 

Part 1: Why Semgrep and Values Alignment
Rubric: Yes + 
 Do they care about culture and values? Will they work to improve our culture and live our values? -> Yes+. Cares a lot about autonomy and being in a culture of high trust that is earned. Has some experience building it as a manager, but some of the things were a bit high level on how they built it so not fully a strong yes
 Do they demonstrate values alignment? Do their answers to later questions align with their stated values? -> Strong Yes. This is one of the few times I've had strong values alignment. Saw it through conflict resolution (customer feedback, low ego, mutual respect, and disagree and commit). They also understand the tradeoffs made there.  

Raw Notes:
* Been at panther for 6 years in many roles
* Started at cyber security (employee number 5)
* Then internal sec, compliance, and SR role for content creation
* Helped build out CSPM product line
* Started more product engineering that way
* 17 different jobs (good at wearing multiple hats)
* Been mostly focused on sales

There are lots of jobs out there, why Semgrep?
* KK made the recommendation and spoke highly
* Highlighted high performing work culture but less burn out expectations
* “Healthy and productive work environment”
* Gotten more interested as learning about the teams and the roles

What have you liked or not liked about your managers in the past?
* Liked:
  * Gave me higher autonomy -> high level goals but freedom to figure out the right solution
  * Start by completing tasks and then growing bigger scope
* Not Liked:
  * Overbearing or micromanaging
  * Manager would tell me I was doing good, but criticism on my reports and micromanaged them (around me)
  * Trust but needs to be earned
* Saw a lot of examples of management
* Difficult to learn lesson but got a good mentor for it
  * If you delegate a task, it’s not about getting them to do the way you would do it

Part 2: Feedback, Mentorship, and Sponsorship
Rubric: Yes + 
 Communication: Are they skilled at delivering feedback? ->  Yes. Had multiple examples of building trust and giving difficult feedback. Conflict resolution is easier for them on two way doors (let's measure and figure out). Only not a yes cause all of it was mostly technical (in this section). 
Communication: Do they treat feedback as a gift? Can they give good examples of how feedback has helped them? -> Strong Yes. Looks now to get feedback from both sides. Has a great story on upwards feedback that really changed both how they approach management and also how they gather feedback. Learns from the feedback (both explicit and implicit) and grows from it 
Leadership: Are they intentional about how they mentor? Do they have a history of successful mentorship? -> Strong yes. Experienced mentor who has mentored people through multiple roles and levels. Learned the art of coaching vs teaching and how that works and needs to work with different people. I got to do a bit of mentorship with my coaching <> learning spectrum idea and they were really appreciative of that take too. 

Raw Notes:
Tell me about your experience mentoring others. What do you think makes you a successful mentor?
* Moved to a TL and hired two people onto my team (wasn’t doing performance but doing roadmap and scope)
  * At the time I thought of them as extensions of myself rather than guide them to things I wasn’t doing
  * Found that I needed to be too specific about what to do and so I was forced to give more delegation and approach the problem and coaching and feedback
  * Focused more on explaining the problem and problem solving
  * One woman struggled with getting her voice heard (and some of that wasn’t her fault) so worked to make space and give her the tools (notes). She grew from that coaching
  * Other person didn’t work out and he eventually left. I didn’t set him up for success

At Semgrep, we like to say that conflict is good. If nobody disagrees, it often means folks aren’t engaged or that folks don’t feel comfortable disagreeing. Conflict helps bring out the best ideas. So long as it’s done with respect. Can you tell me about some time that you and someone else disagreed about what to do, and how you moved forward?
* Mentioned one way door vs two way doors
* If its two way doors, it is easier for me to disagree and commit (let’s measure against it and see how it goes)
* “Those are the easier ones”
* “People get really hung up on internal process here”
* Customers won’t see that and we can handle that thrash
* When customers are involved, there is a risk to reputation
* Working on a new search language (panther flow)
* “I was very against the idea” No DSLs was what we were built on. There was an internal push for it for control reasons
* Worked through it with internal conversations (when we realized we gridlocks we just went to customers to get feedback)
* “What information do we need to find to gather the data we need”
* Decided to invest a small amount of feedback to get a POV while it isn’t too much of a distraction and then really see how customer feedback is and we got good feedback and now we are adopting it. “I still think it’s the wrong thing to invest it, but the organization has commited to giving it so I tried to be a supporter of it”

Tell me about a piece of feedback you received that helped you grow?
* Most helpful: upwards feedback
* Woman from earlier who I was mentoring and reported to me
* Gave her a performance review with feedback on representing the team in some of the cross team meetings
* 6 months later, I was really busy and gave her similar feedback
* She was quiet during the meeting and asked to talk
* She was upset cause I thought I did all these actions and changes and then I got the same feedback “I felt like you didn’t pay any attention to the progress I made”
* It felt casual to me but it was a really big deal for her
* In a manager position, what you say it can really impact -> people management is not just directing, but also helping them

Part 3: Growth 
Rubric: Strong Yes
 Leadership: Are they intentional about their own growth? Do they have growth goals? -> Strong Yes. Defined goals (wants to learn more IC work and be hands on for a bit). Is actively moving roles within the company (and now outside) to get that experience. Grew into management fast and might want to go back but wants to be closer to a TL or IC for a while. 

Raw Notes:
Where do you want to grow next and where do you see your career going?
* Progressed really quickly into management. Enjoyed that very much
* Want to spend a serious amount of time in an IC
* Feel like I missed out on the opportunity for the IC skills on execution of projects
* Go back to IC and then manager and go back
* Concentrated IC work for a while
* Continue technical work in security and be a dedicated IC for a while
* Do a little more research

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
Copied over for Clara. 4 for ic5 3 for ic6


Pros
- Incredibly clear communication
- Made sure others were recognized
- Very applicable experience to Semgrep
- Proactively talked about tradeoffs
- Experience not just executing himself but fighting for projects, acting as PM/EM, breaking down problems, etc.

Neutral
- Pretty good visual aid

Cons
- Didn't actually write code on this project, but had good answers to technical questions about the technology and said he has used these technologies as an IC on other projects


(5 min) Initial PresentationGreat explanation of the problem and context as well as a high-level of the "what"
(10 min) How did the project go?Well - explained why the added delay was fine in this case and how the design of the system meant that old logs/signals were unimpacted.
(10 min) Technical detailsLambda feeds logs to S3, lambda converts logs to alerts (now signals and alerts after this project). Wrote a UI -> SQL query engine to help users who weren't sql experts combine things.
What stood out about this candidateVery clear communication and articulation of the problem. 
Raw notesClara/Amarin shadowGoes by Nick Has been at Panther around 6 years (5th to join company, everyone was an engineer back then)Helped build out product as well as security EM role for awhile - managed team that built out platform Last 6 months more of an IC role again Very customer-focused, extremely good at keeping business needs in Project at Panther “Correlation Rules” Panther is a Security Event Management platform. Analyze logs and look for malicious activity. Examining each event as it enters system. Low delay was great. Problem identified frequently by customers: some types of attacker behavior are very difficult to identify with a single log event. Only in conjunction of 2 things do you want an alert. Came up in deal cycles as an objection from customers. Goal: provide users the ability to define relationships between atomic actions to describe a bigger picture of attacker behavior. Another interesting thing we found was we could also use this to reduce noise (initially it was just used for missing detections). Rules: atomic rules and correlation rules can come from the out-of-the-box suite or be written by customers themselves. Requirements:
Old thing scaled well (one log at a time), but a combination system would need this performance and not necessarily have it without designing for that intentionally.
Must be easy to understand without high degree of SQL expertise (users could also do this if they were SQL experts)
Possible but too hard to do means impossible 
Needs to work across log types and time 


What do you mean by log types?
Several hundred out-of-the-box schemas and one schema maps to one log type. Logs from AWS cloud trail, logs from Okta, logs from point security solutions like Sublime, push.io, logs from Semgrep or other security tools. 
Format of data from different places is quite different. Correlating on e.g. IP address can be challenging since it may show up in different places or under different labels, etc. 
User ID might be formatted in many different ways (user, user@email, first.last name, etc.)


Log processor processes logs in AWS lambda, dumps in S3. We’d get notified, process in batches and then write out rule matches to a datalake in snowflake. 


New component: system that would run SQL queries. 
Web UI -> Correlation Rule conversion (AWS lambda) -> Scheduled query engine (AWS lambda)


Engine reconfigured so rules could write signals but not alerts 
Scheduled query would only query the signals DB, not the alerts
Needle in a haystack becomes needle in a stack of needles 


No longer real-time, for cost optimization we introduced 30 min (ish) delay for these queries. Many things folks wanted to correlate on might be hours apart anyway so people didn’t care so much. Atomic actions you want to know as soon as possible. 


Most difficult things about project?
About environment it happened in. Right before this we had second round of layoffs in a year. Project manager was brand new, so was VP product. Myself and principal engineer on my team were scoping this out. 


Me and principal engineer working to push this through because we said that it should happen. Not ideal, but org was kind of dysfunctional at the time. 


I was closely involved throughout. Large customer with high risk of churn was demanding capability like this. Principal engineer demonstrated via prototype. Customer said looks great. We then ID’d a senior engineer on the team and PE said it would be a great opty for senior engineer to lead this. 


Project manager was great, we’re still good friends, but he had his hands full, so I did the PM and EM roles more. Sam and another engineer worked on backend and 2 FE on frontend at the end. 


Didn’t write any code on this project, but was figuring out tradeoffs and all that. Breaking down milestones, getting feedback early from customers. 


Have worked on lambdas, S3, snowflake as an IC. Built out the platform management tool. Entirely on AWS lambdas with DynamoDB storage layer. 


When to use AWS lambdas?
Lambda limitations: maximum 15 minute runtime for single invocation. Can hack a long-lived thing but it’s not a great fit. 
If you need elasticity to scale, that can be a really good candidate. That’s why we chose this, logs can be quite spiky. 


Backwards compatibility issues?
Concern for the data lake component. But log data was a JSON. 


If contracts changed. Most of the time SQS queue in the middle to get things like retries. 


Either backwards compatible staged queue rollout or drain queue, push changes, resume.


Recognition?
Got some “great job Nick” and “great job Zach” - made sure Sam got credit too. Sam got promoted to staff SWE. A more frontend, junior engineer, Aliska. I had to do lay-offs and lay off one of the FE engineers, but Aliska is still there and got promoted.


——


Questions


Brought this same project up with Jarryd. Start-to-end was a 10 month project including the spit-balling. What is typical project length?

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer:
With the rubrics I see, I don't think there's any follow up. But one thing to note is that the candidate has been a manager for a while and recently switched to IC. I would probably want to learn more about individual contributions. This interview it sounded like the candidate led a team.


(5 min) Initial PresentationI think the initial presentation went a bit overtime but it was engaging and I didn't keep track of the time. The project was explained quite clearly, leading with the problem, the state of the world, and then the solution. Solid story telling skills.
(10 min) How did the project go?There were hiccups at the beginning because the company just had a layoff round and some re-orgs. It sounded like the candidate is able to mitigate risks through incremental milestones. At first the candidate worked with a principal engineer to prototype things out, then later on the team got an extra engineer, at which point the candidate supported to lead the effort. The project helped with one big client, and after that it was easier to get buy in and it sounded like it was a success.
(10 min) Technical detailsThe candidate seemed to have a good grasp of the pros and cons of the technology they used. Two discussions that I think were important. The first was about AWS lambdas. The candidate was able to articulate that it's good for spiky, short-live tasks but the generally perceived notion is that it's not good for continuous influx of work. The candidate expressed that the log ingestion is a continuous influx of work and AWS lambda still worked quite well. The other discussion was about the tradeoff between being able to quickly get some signals from events with false positives vs waiting a bit longer with less false positives. The candidate seems to be able to look at a problem in a wider context and talk about technical tradeoffs effectively.
What stood out about this candidateTo me, it was how well the candidate could articulate complex ideas, understand questions, and giving well-thought answer to those questions.
Raw notesSorry, I was shadowing and I didn't realize I'm also expected to take notes and give feedback.

questions.question: Key Take-Aways
questions.answer: Came prepared for the presentation round and talked about a high impact project that he volunteered himself for. Worked on an application called Filebird that he put together to replace a legacy Oracle application that was delaying data validation for a lot of folks on the GM Finance team. Clearly articulated the current architecture, pain points both technical and business and walked through the new architecture he helped build. Was able to confidently talk about the technical details and choices he made and also put together a ROI of how much money ($200K) that would help save if they retired the oracle licenses and also reduce the time the teams would have to wait for getting the data validated and ingested into the systems. JP is highly curious, humble and a hustler who really wants to join Semgrep and accelerate his professional career. He would be a great addition as an associate SE and would make an immediate impact to the SMB sales team.
